commit_message:
[lite] Update TfLiteIntArrayCreate to return size_t

PiperOrigin-RevId: 416439896
Change-Id: I847f69b68d1ddaff4b1e925a09b8b69c1756653b
cve_desc: Tensorflow is an Open Source Machine Learning Framework. An attacker can craft a TFLite model that would cause an integer overflow in `TfLiteIntArrayCreate`. The `TfLiteIntArrayGetSizeInBytes` returns an `int` instead of a `size_t. An attacker can control model inputs such that `computed_size` overflows the size of `int` datatype. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.8.0. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.7.1, TensorFlow 2.6.3, and TensorFlow 2.5.3, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

vulnerabilities.description: If the `size` parameter is large enough, the computation inside TfLiteIntArrayGetSizeInBytes (typically something like `sizeof(int) * (size + 1)`) can suffer an integer overflow, wrapping around to a small positive value. This small positive value passes the `alloc_size <= 0` check, causing malloc to allocate a much smaller buffer than expected. The large original `size` is then stored in `ret->size`, leading callers to believe the array can hold `size` elements, resulting in heap buffer overflows on subsequent writes.

step_by_step_execution.description: The function is called with a very large `size` value, e.g. size = 0x3FFFFFFF or larger.

TfLiteIntArrayGetSizeInBytes(size) computes the allocation size, which typically involves a multiplication like sizeof(int) * (size + 1). With a large size, this overflows the int range and wraps to a small positive number.

state.value: small positive integer due to overflow, e.g. 4 * (0x3FFFFFFF + 1) = 4 * 0x40000000 = 0x100000000 which truncates to 0 on 32-bit int — but other nearby values produce small positive results

step_by_step_execution.description: The check `alloc_size <= 0` is evaluated. Because the overflowed value can be a small positive number (for slightly different large size values), the check passes.
malloc(alloc_size) allocates a very small buffer on the heap.

state.value: pointer to a small heap allocation (e.g. 8 bytes)

step_by_step_execution.description: ret->size is set to the original large `size` value, recording that this array has a huge number of elements despite the tiny allocation.
The returned TfLiteIntArray is used by callers who trust ret->size and write up to `size` int elements into the array, causing a heap buffer overflow.

commit_message:
Prevent nullptr deref in SparseTensorSliceDataset

The arguments must determine a valid sparse tensor. This means that when indices are empty then the values must be empty too (and the reverse).

Also added test, by modifying existing test with empty sparse tensor to now run with an invalid sparse tensor input.

PiperOrigin-RevId: 388562757
Change-Id: Id8b54cd7c2316025b4f9a77292c8fb5344d17609
cve_desc: TensorFlow is an end-to-end open source platform for machine learning. When a user does not supply arguments that determine a valid sparse tensor, `tf.raw_ops.SparseTensorSliceDataset` implementation can be made to dereference a null pointer. The [implementation](https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorflow/blob/8d72537c6abf5a44103b57b9c2e22c14f5f49698/tensorflow/core/kernels/data/sparse_tensor_slice_dataset_op.cc#L240-L251) has some argument validation but fails to consider the case when either `indices` or `values` are provided for an empty sparse tensor when the other is not. If `indices` is empty, then [code that performs validation](https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorflow/blob/8d72537c6abf5a44103b57b9c2e22c14f5f49698/tensorflow/core/kernels/data/sparse_tensor_slice_dataset_op.cc#L260-L261) (i.e., checking that the indices are monotonically increasing) results in a null pointer dereference. If `indices` as provided by the user is empty, then `indices` in the C++ code above is backed by an empty `std::vector`, hence calling `indices->dim_size(0)` results in null pointer dereferencing (same as calling `std::vector::at()` on an empty vector). We have patched the issue in GitHub commit 02cc160e29d20631de3859c6653184e3f876b9d7. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.6.0. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.5.1, TensorFlow 2.4.3, and TensorFlow 2.3.4, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

commit_message: Improve handling of Gopher responses (#1022)
cve_desc: In Squid 3.x through 3.5.28, 4.x through 4.17, and 5.x before 5.6, due to improper buffer management, a Denial of Service can occur when processing long Gopher server responses.

vulnerabilities.description: Off-by-one error when null-terminating `line`. The code writes the null terminator at `line[llen + 1]` instead of `line[llen]`. When `llen` is at its maximum allowed value of `TEMP_BUF_SIZE - 1` (just passing the overflow check `gopherState->len + llen >= TEMP_BUF_SIZE`), writing to `line[llen + 1]` means writing to `line[TEMP_BUF_SIZE]`, which is one byte past the end of the buffer. Additionally, `line[llen]` contains stale/uninitialized data that will be processed by subsequent string operations (strchr, strtok, etc.), potentially leading to information disclosure or incorrect parsing.

step_by_step_execution.description: The `line` buffer is allocated as a local array of TEMP_BUF_SIZE bytes.
Assume `gopherState->len` is 0 (no leftover from previous call) and input contains a line of length `TEMP_BUF_SIZE - 1` ending with '\n'.

state.value: TEMP_BUF_SIZE - 1 (the distance from pos to lpos which includes the \n)

step_by_step_execution.description: The overflow check: `gopherState->len + llen >= TEMP_BUF_SIZE` evaluates to `0 + (TEMP_BUF_SIZE - 1) >= TEMP_BUF_SIZE` which is false, so llen is NOT capped.
Since `gopherState->len == 0`, `memcpy(line, pos, llen)` copies TEMP_BUF_SIZE - 1 bytes into `line`. This fits within the buffer.
The null terminator is written at `line[llen + 1]` which is `line[TEMP_BUF_SIZE]`. This is a 1-byte out-of-bounds write past the `line` buffer.

Furthermore, `line[llen]` (i.e., `line[TEMP_BUF_SIZE - 1]`) is not zeroed and contains stale data from previous iterations or the initial memset, which subsequent string functions (strchr, strtok) will process as part of the line content.

state.value: stale/uninitialized byte, not null-terminated at correct position

commit_message:
Ensure num_streams >= 0 in tf.raw_ops.BoostedTreesCreateQuantileStreamResource

PiperOrigin-RevId: 387452765
Change-Id: I9990c760e177fabca6a3b9b4612ceeaeeba51495
cve_desc: TensorFlow is an end-to-end open source platform for machine learning. In affected versions an attacker can cause a denial of service in `boosted_trees_create_quantile_stream_resource` by using negative arguments. The [implementation](https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorflow/blob/84d053187cb80d975ef2b9684d4b61981bca0c41/tensorflow/core/kernels/boosted_trees/quantile_ops.cc#L96) does not validate that `num_streams` only contains non-negative numbers. In turn, [this results in using this value to allocate memory](https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorflow/blob/84d053187cb80d975ef2b9684d4b61981bca0c41/tensorflow/core/kernels/boosted_trees/quantiles/quantile_stream_resource.h#L31-L40). However, `reserve` receives an unsigned integer so there is an implicit conversion from a negative value to a large positive unsigned. This results in a crash from the standard library. We have patched the issue in GitHub commit 8a84f7a2b5a2b27ecf88d25bad9ac777cd2f7992. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.6.0. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.5.1, TensorFlow 2.4.3, and TensorFlow 2.3.4, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

commit_message:
[lite] Update TfLiteIntArrayCreate to return size_t

PiperOrigin-RevId: 416439896
Change-Id: I847f69b68d1ddaff4b1e925a09b8b69c1756653b
cve_desc: Tensorflow is an Open Source Machine Learning Framework. An attacker can craft a TFLite model that would cause an integer overflow in `TfLiteIntArrayCreate`. The `TfLiteIntArrayGetSizeInBytes` returns an `int` instead of a `size_t. An attacker can control model inputs such that `computed_size` overflows the size of `int` datatype. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.8.0. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.7.1, TensorFlow 2.6.3, and TensorFlow 2.5.3, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

vulnerabilities.description: If `size` is a very large positive integer or a negative integer, the computation inside `TfLiteIntArrayGetSizeInBytes(size)` (typically `sizeof(int) * (size + 1)` or similar) can experience integer overflow/wrapping, producing a small `alloc_size` value that is non-zero. The `alloc_size <= 0` check cannot catch this because `size_t` is unsigned, so any non-zero wrapped value passes the check. `malloc` then allocates a small buffer, but `ret->size` is set to the original large/negative `size`, causing subsequent accesses to the array's `data` member to read/write far beyond the allocated heap buffer.

step_by_step_execution.description: Function is called with a very large `size`, e.g. size = 0x3FFFFFFF on a system where sizeof(int) == 4.

`TfLiteIntArrayGetSizeInBytes(size)` computes something like `sizeof(int) * (size + 1)`, which is `4 * 0x40000000 = 0x100000000`. On a 32-bit system (or if intermediate arithmetic is 32-bit), this overflows to 0.

state.value: 0 (after overflow, or a small wrapped value depending on exact formula)

step_by_step_execution.description: The check `alloc_size <= 0` catches the zero case and returns NULL. However, with slightly different large values of `size` (e.g., size = 0x40000001), the overflow result may be a small non-zero value like 8, passing the check.

state.value: small non-zero value (e.g., 8) due to integer overflow wrapping

step_by_step_execution.description: `malloc(alloc_size)` allocates a very small buffer (e.g., 8 bytes).

state.value: pointer to heap buffer of ~8 bytes

step_by_step_execution.description: `ret->size` is set to the original large `size` value. Callers that iterate over `ret->data[0..size-1]` will access memory far beyond the allocated buffer, resulting in a heap buffer overflow.

commit_message:
btrfs: fix NULL pointer dereference when deleting device by invalid id

[BUG]
It's easy to trigger NULL pointer dereference, just by removing a
non-existing device id:

 # mkfs.btrfs -f -m single -d single /dev/test/scratch1 \
				     /dev/test/scratch2
 # mount /dev/test/scratch1 /mnt/btrfs
 # btrfs device remove 3 /mnt/btrfs

Then we have the following kernel NULL pointer dereference:

 BUG: kernel NULL pointer dereference, address: 0000000000000000
 #PF: supervisor read access in kernel mode
 #PF: error_code(0x0000) - not-present page
 PGD 0 P4D 0
 Oops: 0000 [#1] PREEMPT SMP NOPTI
 CPU: 9 PID: 649 Comm: btrfs Not tainted 5.14.0-rc3-custom+ #35
 Hardware name: QEMU Standard PC (Q35 + ICH9, 2009), BIOS 0.0.0 02/06/2015
 RIP: 0010:btrfs_rm_device+0x4de/0x6b0 [btrfs]
  btrfs_ioctl+0x18bb/0x3190 [btrfs]
  ? lock_is_held_type+0xa5/0x120
  ? find_held_lock.constprop.0+0x2b/0x80
  ? do_user_addr_fault+0x201/0x6a0
  ? lock_release+0xd2/0x2d0
  ? __x64_sys_ioctl+0x83/0xb0
  __x64_sys_ioctl+0x83/0xb0
  do_syscall_64+0x3b/0x90
  entry_SYSCALL_64_after_hwframe+0x44/0xae

[CAUSE]
Commit a27a94c2b0c7 ("btrfs: Make btrfs_find_device_by_devspec return
btrfs_device directly") moves the "missing" device path check into
btrfs_rm_device().

But btrfs_rm_device() itself can have case where it only receives
@devid, with NULL as @device_path.

In that case, calling strcmp() on NULL will trigger the NULL pointer
dereference.

Before that commit, we handle the "missing" case inside
btrfs_find_device_by_devspec(), which will not check @device_path at all
if @devid is provided, thus no way to trigger the bug.

[FIX]
Before calling strcmp(), also make sure @device_path is not NULL.

Fixes: a27a94c2b0c7 ("btrfs: Make btrfs_find_device_by_devspec return btrfs_device directly")
CC: stable@vger.kernel.org # 5.4+
Reported-by: butt3rflyh4ck <butterflyhuangxx@gmail.com>
Reviewed-by: Anand Jain <anand.jain@oracle.com>
Signed-off-by: Qu Wenruo <wqu@suse.com>
Reviewed-by: David Sterba <dsterba@suse.com>
Signed-off-by: David Sterba <dsterba@suse.com>
cve_desc: A NULL pointer dereference flaw was found in the btrfs_rm_device function in fs/btrfs/volumes.c in the Linux Kernel, where triggering the bug requires ‘CAP_SYS_ADMIN’. This flaw allows a local attacker to crash the system or leak kernel internal information. The highest threat from this vulnerability is to system availability.

commit_message: fixed #1897
cve_desc: The binary MP4Box in Gpac 1.0.1 has a double-free vulnerability in the gf_text_get_utf8_line function in load_text.c, which allows attackers to cause a denial of service, even code execution and escalation of privileges.

vulnerabilities.description: Stack-based buffer overflow in szLineConv[1024]. The input szLine can be up to lineSize bytes (which can exceed 1024), and the conversion loop writes to szLineConv indexed by j without any bounds checking. Furthermore, when unicode_type==0 and non-UTF8 bytes are encountered, the code expands single bytes into two bytes, so j can grow even larger than the input length len.

step_by_step_execution.description: Function is called with lineSize > 1024 (e.g., 2048), unicode_type == 0.
szLine is zeroed and then filled via gf_fgets with up to lineSize (2048) bytes of data from the file. Attacker controls the file content.
unicode_type <= 1, so the conversion branch is entered. len = strlen(szLine) could be up to 2047.
The loop iterates i from 0 to len-1, copying bytes from szLine to szLineConv[j]. Even without character expansion, when i reaches 1024, j also reaches 1024 and szLineConv[j] writes out of bounds.

If the input contains bytes with the high bit set (0x80) that are not valid UTF-8 continuations, each such byte is expanded into two bytes in szLineConv, making j grow even faster than i, reaching the overflow sooner.

vulnerabilities.description: If gf_utf8_wcstombs returns a negative error value (e.g., -1), casting to u32 yields a very large value (e.g., 0xFFFFFFFF). Using this as an index into szLineConv[i] causes a massive out-of-bounds write.

step_by_step_execution.description: Function is called with unicode_type == 2 (or 3 depending on endianness), so the UTF-16 conversion path is taken.
gf_utf8_wcstombs is called to convert the UTF-16 data. If the input contains invalid sequences, the function may return -1 (a common error return for wcstombs-like functions).
The return value is cast to u32, yielding 0xFFFFFFFF, and stored in i.
szLineConv[i] = 0 writes a null byte far beyond the stack buffer bounds.

commit_message:
Merge pull request from GHSA-p6g5-v97c-w5q4

* Prevent heap buffer overflow when parsing DNS packets

* Make sure packet parsing doesn't advance beyond max/end

* Update checks

* Remove  check

Co-authored-by: sauwming <ming@teluu.com>
cve_desc: PJSIP is a free and open source multimedia communication library written in C. A buffer overflow vulnerability in versions 2.12 and prior affects applications that use PJSIP DNS resolution. It doesn't affect PJSIP users who utilize an external resolver. This vulnerability is related to CVE-2023-27585. The difference is that this issue is in parsing the query record `parse_rr()`, while the issue in CVE-2023-27585 is in `parse_query()`. A patch is available in the `master` branch of the `pjsip/pjproject` GitHub repository. A workaround is to disable DNS resolution in PJSIP config (by setting `nameserver_count` to zero) or use an external resolver instead.

commit_message: Fix update session when auth fail
cve_desc: Glewlwyd 2.0.0, fixed in 2.6.1 is affected by an incorrect access control vulnerability. One user can attempt to log in as another user without its password.

code_snippets: if ((session_uid = get_session_id(config, request)) != NULL && user_session_update(config, session_uid, u_map_get_case(request->map_header, "user-agent"), issued_for, json_string_value(json_object_get(j_param, "username")), NULL, 1) != G_OK) {
vulnerabilities.description: When password authentication fails (G_ERROR_UNAUTHORIZED or other error), the code still calls user_session_update with the last argument set to 1 (same value used in the successful authentication path, likely meaning 'authenticated'). If an attacker already has a valid session cookie, this could add the target username to the session as authenticated even though the credentials were invalid. In the successful auth path, the identical call uses `1` for authenticated. In the no-password refresh path, `0` is used. This inconsistency on the failure path constitutes an authentication bypass.

code_snippets: if ((session_uid = get_session_id(config, request)) != NULL && user_session_update(config, session_uid, u_map_get_case(request->map_header, "user-agent"), issued_for, json_string_value(json_object_get(j_param, "username")), NULL, 1) != G_OK) {

step_by_step_execution.description: The function receives a JSON request body containing a 'username' and an incorrect 'password'. The attacker also has a pre-existing valid session cookie.
j_param is not NULL, and json_string_length of 'username' is non-zero, so we enter the username branch.
scheme_type is NULL so we enter the password authentication branch. json_string_length of 'password' is non-zero.
auth_check_user_credentials is called and returns G_ERROR_UNAUTHORIZED because the password is wrong.
check_result_value(j_result, G_OK) is false, so the else branch is entered.

get_session_id returns a valid session_uid from the existing session cookie. The condition (session_uid != NULL) is true, so user_session_update is called with the victim's username and 1 as the last parameter (the 'authenticated' flag).

user_session_update associates 'victim_user' as authenticated in the attacker's existing session, despite the credentials being invalid. The response returns 401, but the session state has already been modified.

state.value: 'victim_user' marked as authenticated in the session

commit_message:
Prevent memory leak in decoding PNG images.

PiperOrigin-RevId: 409300653
Change-Id: I6182124c545989cef80cefd439b659095920763b
cve_desc: Tensorflow is an Open Source Machine Learning Framework. When decoding PNG images TensorFlow can produce a memory leak if the image is invalid. After calling `png::CommonInitDecode(..., &decode)`, the `decode` value contains allocated buffers which can only be freed by calling `png::CommonFreeDecode(&decode)`. However, several error case in the function implementation invoke the `OP_REQUIRES` macro which immediately terminates the execution of the function, without allowing for the memory free to occur. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.8.0. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.7.1, TensorFlow 2.6.3, and TensorFlow 2.5.3, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

commit_message:
crypto: digest: use crypto_memneq()

When verifying a digest it is important not to leak timing information
through memcmp(). Use crypto_memneq() instead.

Signed-off-by: Sascha Hauer <s.hauer@pengutronix.de>
cve_desc: crypto/digest.c in Pengutronix barebox through 2021.07.0 leaks timing information because memcmp is used during digest verification.

commit_message: Improve handling of Gopher responses (#1022)
cve_desc: In Squid 3.x through 3.5.28, 4.x through 4.17, and 5.x before 5.6, due to improper buffer management, a Denial of Service can occur when processing long Gopher server responses.

vulnerabilities.description: Off-by-one buffer overflow when writing the null terminator. After the overflow check caps `llen` to `TEMP_BUF_SIZE - gopherState->len - 1`, and when `gopherState->len != 0` the code does `llen += gopherState->len`, making `llen` equal to `TEMP_BUF_SIZE - 1`. The null terminator is then written at `line[TEMP_BUF_SIZE]`, which is one byte past the end of the `line` buffer (size TEMP_BUF_SIZE). Even when `gopherState->len == 0`, the adjusted `llen` can be `TEMP_BUF_SIZE - 1`, leading to a write at index `TEMP_BUF_SIZE`.

step_by_step_execution.description: Function is called with inbuf containing data with a newline, and gopherState->len is non-zero from a previous partial line.

state.value: large enough to contain a newline

step_by_step_execution.description: In the while loop, `lpos` finds a newline, so `llen` is computed as the distance to the newline.
The overflow check fires: `gopherState->len + llen >= TEMP_BUF_SIZE`. llen is adjusted to `TEMP_BUF_SIZE - gopherState->len - 1`.
Since lpos is non-NULL, the code does not break. Since gopherState->len != 0, it copies gopherState->buf then pos data into line, then does `llen += gopherState->len`.
The code writes `line[llen + 1] = '\0'`, which is `line[TEMP_BUF_SIZE]`, one byte past the end of the buffer.

vulnerabilities.description: Format string argument mismatch: the format string contains 5 format specifiers (%s, %s, %c, %s, %s) but only 3 arguments are provided (icon_url, rfc1738_escape_unescaped(selector), html_quote(name)). This causes the function to read uninitialized stack values for the missing arguments, leading to undefined behavior, potential crashes, or information disclosure.

code_snippets: outbuf.appendf(" %s\n", icon_url, rfc1738_escape_unescaped(selector), html_quote(name));

step_by_step_execution.description: The function processes a gopher directory line where gtype is GOPHER_WWW and selector does not start with 'GET /'.
The code enters the `else if (gtype == GOPHER_WWW)` branch and calls appendf with a format string expecting 5 arguments but only 3 are passed.
The printf-like function reads from the stack for the missing 2 arguments (%c and one %s), causing undefined behavior — reading arbitrary stack data.

state.value: 2 arguments read from uninitialized stack memory

commit_message:
Add negative bound check for row and column pooling_sequence in FractionalAvgPoolGrad op to avoid out of bound heap access

PiperOrigin-RevId: 413837346
Change-Id: I2b86034101df31bee161abcb781755e236c7bccd
cve_desc: Tensorflow is an Open Source Machine Learning Framework. The implementation of `FractionalAvgPoolGrad` does not consider cases where the input tensors are invalid allowing an attacker to read from outside of bounds of heap. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.8.0. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.7.1, TensorFlow 2.6.3, and TensorFlow 2.5.3, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

vulnerabilities.description: The code iterates d from 0 to out_depth when accessing in_backprop_tensor_temp_mat, which has in_depth rows. There is no validation that out_depth == in_depth. If out_depth > in_depth, the coeffRef(d, in_index) call on in_backprop_tensor_temp_mat writes out of bounds.

step_by_step_execution.description: orig_input_tensor_shape is read, providing in_batch, in_rows, in_cols, in_depth. Suppose in_depth=2.
out_backprop is read. Its dimensions can differ from orig_input_tensor_shape. Suppose out_depth=10.
in_backprop_tensor_temp is allocated with shape in_shape (1,4,4,2). The EigenDoubleMatrixMap is created with in_depth=2 rows.
In the inner loop, d iterates from 0 to out_depth (10). When d >= 2 (in_depth), coeffRef(d, in_index) on in_backprop_tensor_temp_mat accesses memory beyond the allocated buffer.

state.value: 2..9 (out of bounds for matrix with 2 rows)

vulnerabilities.description: The code loops b from 0 to out_batch and computes in_index using in_rows/in_cols, but never validates that out_batch == in_batch. If out_batch > in_batch, in_index = (b * in_rows + in_r) * in_cols + in_c will exceed in_cols * in_rows * in_batch, causing an out-of-bounds write on in_backprop_tensor_temp_mat.

step_by_step_execution.description: orig_input_tensor_shape specifies in_batch=1, in_rows=4, in_cols=4, in_depth=2.
out_backprop has out_batch=5, out_rows=2, out_cols=2, out_depth=2. No check verifies out_batch <= in_batch.
in_backprop_tensor_temp_mat has in_cols*in_rows*in_batch=16 columns.
When b=1 (second iteration), in_index = (1*4 + in_r)*4 + in_c which can reach 19. This exceeds the 16 columns of the matrix, causing out-of-bounds write.

commit_message: add assert for addition overflow on queue creation (#225)
cve_desc: The kernel in Amazon Web Services FreeRTOS before 10.4.3 has an integer overflow in queue.c for queue creation.

code_snippets: pxNewQueue = ( Queue_t * ) pvPortMalloc( sizeof( Queue_t ) + xQueueSizeInBytes );
vulnerabilities.description: Integer overflow in the size argument to pvPortMalloc. Even if the multiplication overflow check passes (via configASSERT which may be a no-op in release builds), the addition of sizeof(Queue_t) + xQueueSizeInBytes is never checked for overflow. If xQueueSizeInBytes is large enough (close to SIZE_MAX), the addition wraps around to a small value, causing a small heap allocation. The code then treats this undersized buffer as if it can hold sizeof(Queue_t) plus uxQueueLength*uxItemSize bytes, leading to a heap buffer overflow.

code_snippets: pxNewQueue = ( Queue_t * ) pvPortMalloc( sizeof( Queue_t ) + xQueueSizeInBytes );
prvInitialiseNewQueue( uxQueueLength, uxItemSize, pucQueueStorage, ucQueueType, pxNewQueue );

step_by_step_execution.description: Function is called with large uxQueueLength and uxItemSize whose product does not overflow (e.g., on a 32-bit system, uxQueueLength=1 and uxItemSize=(SIZE_MAX - sizeof(Queue_t) + 1)).
xQueueSizeInBytes is computed as uxQueueLength * uxItemSize. With uxQueueLength=1, no multiplication overflow occurs.

The configASSERT multiplication overflow check passes (1 == xQueueSizeInBytes / uxItemSize). Even if configASSERT is compiled out in release, the check is irrelevant here since the multiplication itself didn't overflow.
pvPortMalloc is called with sizeof(Queue_t) + xQueueSizeInBytes. This addition overflows: sizeof(Queue_t) + (SIZE_MAX - sizeof(Queue_t) + 1) = SIZE_MAX + 1 = 0 (wraps to 0 or a very small value).

state.value: 0 or very small due to integer wrap-around

step_by_step_execution.description: pvPortMalloc returns a pointer to a very small (or zero-size) allocation. pxNewQueue is not NULL.

pucQueueStorage is set to point past the Queue_t header inside the undersized buffer. prvInitialiseNewQueue is called, which will configure the queue to use uxQueueLength items of uxItemSize bytes in the undersized storage area, leading to heap buffer overflow on subsequent queue operations.

state.value: points past Queue_t in undersized buffer

commit_message: Merge pull request from GHSA-r374-qrwv-86hh
cve_desc: PJSIP is a free and open source multimedia communication library. In version 2.11.1 and prior, if incoming RTCP XR message contain block, the data field is not checked against the received packet size, potentially resulting in an out-of-bound read access. This affects all users that use PJMEDIA and RTCP XR. A malicious actor can send a RTCP XR message with an invalid packet size.

vulnerabilities.description: Out-of-bounds read when a report block has a valid block type (e.g., BT_STATS or BT_VOIP_METRICS) but a crafted small rb_len. The code stores a pointer to the block header cast to a large structure type without validating that rb_len is large enough to cover all fields of that structure. Later, when accessing fields of rb_stats or rb_voip_mtc, reads occur well beyond the actual packet data buffer.

step_by_step_execution.description: The function receives a crafted RTCP XR packet where size is, say, 20 bytes (enough for a common header + one report block header with a small rb_len of 1, and bt set to BT_STATS).
pkt_len is read from rtcp_xr->common.length via pj_ntohs. For a 20-byte packet, pkt_len = 4 (since (4+1)*4 = 20 <= 20).
The bounds check `(pkt_len + 1) > (size / 4)` is evaluated: (4+1) > (20/4) => 5 > 5 is false, so execution continues.
rb_hdr is initialized to rtcp_xr->buf (offset after the common header). The while loop checks rb_hdr < pkt + pkt_len (pkt + 4 int32s = pkt + 16 bytes). rb_hdr is within bounds.

state.value: points to byte offset ~4 of the packet (start of first report block)

step_by_step_execution.description: rb_len is read from rb_hdr->length via pj_ntohs. The attacker sets this to 1 (a very small value). rb_hdr->bt is set to BT_STATS.

Since rb_len != 0, the switch statement executes. rb_stats is set to (pjmedia_rtcp_xr_rb_stats*) rb_hdr. No validation that rb_len is large enough to cover the full pjmedia_rtcp_xr_rb_stats structure.

state.value: points to byte offset ~4, only 1 word (4 bytes) of actual block content after header

step_by_step_execution.description: rb_hdr advances to rb_hdr + rb_len + 1 = rb_hdr + 2 int32s = rb_hdr + 8 bytes, which is now >= pkt + pkt_len, so the loop ends.

After the loop, since rb_stats is non-NULL, the code reads many fields like rb_stats->lost, rb_stats->dup, rb_stats->jitter_min, rb_stats->jitter_max, etc. These fields are at offsets well beyond the 8 bytes (header + 1 word) of actual valid data, causing out-of-bounds reads from beyond the 20-byte packet buffer.

state.value: read from memory beyond the packet buffer (out-of-bounds)

commit_message:
Fix potential divide by zero error when executing FractionalMaxPool, when pooling ratio is higher than input size for a particular dimension.

PiperOrigin-RevId: 412151722
Change-Id: I06e57cbb8eca43816eff79eac264fa7aae8f7163
cve_desc: Tensorflow is an Open Source Machine Learning Framework. The implementation of `FractionalMaxPool` can be made to crash a TensorFlow process via a division by 0. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.8.0. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.7.1, TensorFlow 2.6.3, and TensorFlow 2.5.3, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

vulnerabilities.description: DCHECK_GT is a debug-only assertion that is compiled out in release/production builds. If pooling_ratio_ values are large enough relative to input dimensions (e.g., pooling_ratio > input_size), output_size can become 0 or negative. This leads to allocating a zero/negative-sized output tensor but then passing it to GeneratePoolingSequence and using the result to index into the input tensor, causing out-of-bounds memory reads and writes.

step_by_step_execution.description: The input tensor is read and verified to be 4-dimensional.
input_size is populated from the tensor dimensions. Suppose input_size[1] = 2, input_size[2] = 2.

output_size is computed as floor(input_size[i] / pooling_ratio_[i]). If pooling_ratio_[1] = 3.0, then output_size[1] = floor(2/3.0) = 0. DCHECK_GT is a no-op in release builds, so execution continues.
GeneratePoolingSequence is called with input_size[1]=2, output_size[1]=0. The resulting height_cum_seq may have unexpected content or minimal size.
An output tensor is allocated with shape containing a zero dimension (output_size[1]=0). The Eigen matrix map out_mat is constructed with output_size[2]*output_size[1]*output_size[0] = 0 columns.

In the nested loop, if height_cum_seq or width_cum_seq has entries, the code computes out_offset and in_offset and accesses out_mat.col(out_offset) and in_mat.col(in_offset). With output_size[1]=0, out_offset calculation uses output_size[1]=0 leading to incorrect offsets, and col() access on a 0-column matrix is out-of-bounds.

state.value: potentially out-of-bounds depending on sequence values

commit_message:
Prevent memory leak in decoding PNG images.

PiperOrigin-RevId: 409300653
Change-Id: I6182124c545989cef80cefd439b659095920763b
cve_desc: Tensorflow is an Open Source Machine Learning Framework. When decoding PNG images TensorFlow can produce a memory leak if the image is invalid. After calling `png::CommonInitDecode(..., &decode)`, the `decode` value contains allocated buffers which can only be freed by calling `png::CommonFreeDecode(&decode)`. However, several error case in the function implementation invoke the `OP_REQUIRES` macro which immediately terminates the execution of the function, without allowing for the memory free to occur. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.8.0. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.7.1, TensorFlow 2.6.3, and TensorFlow 2.5.3, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

commit_message:
Properly validate sparse tensor in `SparseTensorSliceDataset`

Existing validation was incomplete.

PiperOrigin-RevId: 415375048
Change-Id: I14cd18f29ede73286f3ffac35171bd15828997e9
cve_desc: Tensorflow is an Open Source Machine Learning Framework. The implementation of `SparseTensorSliceDataset` has an undefined behavior: under certain condition it can be made to dereference a `nullptr` value. The 3 input arguments to `SparseTensorSliceDataset` represent a sparse tensor. However, there are some preconditions that these arguments must satisfy but these are not validated in the implementation. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.8.0. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.7.1, TensorFlow 2.6.3, and TensorFlow 2.5.3, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

code_snippets:
for (int64_t i = 0; i < indices->dim_size(0); ++i) {
      int64_t next_batch_index = indices->matrix<int64_t>()(i, 0);
vulnerabilities.description: Out-of-bounds read when the `indices` tensor is a matrix with shape [N, 0] (N > 0). The check `num_indices == num_values` passes because both are 0 (N*0 == 0), but the loop iterates `indices->dim_size(0)` times (N > 0) and accesses column 0, which does not exist.

step_by_step_execution.description: The `indices` input tensor is obtained. An attacker provides a tensor with shape [N, 0] where N > 0 (e.g., [5, 0]).
The `values` input tensor is obtained. An attacker provides a tensor with shape [0].
The IsMatrix check passes because indices has rank 2.
num_indices and num_values are computed. Since indices is [N, 0], num_indices = N*0 = 0. Since values is [0], num_values = 0.
The empty-check condition `num_indices == 0 || num_values == 0` is true, and the inner requirement `num_indices == num_values` (0 == 0) passes.
IsVector check on values passes ([0] is a vector). IsVector check on dense_shape passes.
The batch-ordering loop runs with `i` from 0 to `indices->dim_size(0) - 1`. dim_size(0) is N (e.g. 5), so the loop body executes.
Inside the loop, `indices->matrix<int64_t>()(i, 0)` is accessed. The matrix has 0 columns, so accessing column 0 is an out-of-bounds read.

authfile != authkey

commit_message: fixed #2175
cve_desc: MP4Box is a component of GPAC-2.0.0, which is a widely-used third-party package on RPM Fusion. When MP4Box tries to parse a MP4 file, it calls the function `diST_box_read()` to read from video. In this function, it allocates a buffer `str` with fixed length. However, content read from `bs` is controllable by user, so is the length, which causes a buffer overflow.

vulnerabilities.description: The loop reads bytes from the bitstream into a stack buffer `str[1024]` without checking if the index `i` exceeds 1023. If the bitstream contains more than 1023 non-null bytes before a null terminator, the write `str[i]` will overflow the stack buffer.

step_by_step_execution.description: A stack buffer `str` of size 1024 is declared and index `i` is set to 0.
The while(1) loop begins reading bytes from the bitstream one at a time into str[i], incrementing i for each non-null byte.

state.value: increments with each non-null byte read

step_by_step_execution.description: If the bitstream contains 1024 or more non-null bytes before a null byte, i reaches 1024 and str[1024] is written, which is out of bounds.
Further iterations continue writing beyond the buffer, corrupting the stack.

commit_message: fixed #1905
cve_desc: The binary MP4Box in Gpac 1.0.1 has a null pointer dereference vulnerability in the mpgviddmx_process function in reframe_mpgvid.c, which allows attackers to cause a denial of service. This vulnerability is possibly due to an incomplete fix for CVE-2021-40566.

vulnerabilities.description: Integer overflow in hdr_store reallocation. If ctx->hdr_store_size + pck_size overflows a u32, the comparison and allocation use the wrapped (small) value, while the subsequent memcpy writes the full pck_size bytes into a too-small buffer, causing a heap buffer overflow.

step_by_step_execution.description: The function is called repeatedly. On a prior invocation, header data was accumulated (e.g., because gf_m4v_parse_config returned GF_EOS), resulting in ctx->hdr_store_size being set to a large value.

state.value: Large value, e.g., close to UINT32_MAX / 2

step_by_step_execution.description: A new packet is received from gf_filter_pid_get_packet and its data and size are obtained.

state.value: Large value, e.g., close to UINT32_MAX / 2

step_by_step_execution.description: The condition (!ctx->resume_from && ctx->hdr_store_size) is true, so the aggregation block is entered.

The check (ctx->hdr_store_alloc < ctx->hdr_store_size + pck_size) is evaluated. The addition ctx->hdr_store_size + pck_size overflows u32, wrapping to a small value. If ctx->hdr_store_alloc is already >= this small wrapped value, the realloc is skipped entirely; otherwise a too-small buffer is allocated.

state.value: Wrapped small value due to u32 overflow, e.g., 0 or a small number
Previous allocation size, likely >= the wrapped value

step_by_step_execution.description: memcpy writes pck_size bytes from data into ctx->hdr_store at offset ctx->hdr_store_size. Since the buffer was not resized to accommodate the true sum, this writes far beyond the allocated heap buffer.

state.value: ctx->hdr_store + ctx->hdr_store_size (past end of allocated buffer)

commit_message:
Fix crash in MatrixSolve when inputs have different batch dimensions.

Before, the process would crash or certain elements would be silently ignored. Now an InvalidArgument is raised.

PiperOrigin-RevId: 384844020
Change-Id: Iba44417e383bdd0e1abc4012bfca83b2377dd335
cve_desc: TensorFlow is an open source platform for machine learning. In affected versions several TensorFlow operations are missing validation for the shapes of the tensor arguments involved in the call. Depending on the API, this can result in undefined behavior and segfault or `CHECK`-fail related crashes but in some scenarios writes and reads from heap populated arrays are also possible. We have discovered these issues internally via tooling while working on improving/testing GPU op determinism. As such, we don't have reproducers and there will be multiple fixes for these issues. These fixes will be included in TensorFlow 2.7.0. We will also cherrypick these commits on TensorFlow 2.6.1, TensorFlow 2.5.2, and TensorFlow 2.4.4, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

commit_message: fixed #1897
cve_desc: The binary MP4Box in Gpac 1.0.1 has a double-free vulnerability in the gf_text_get_utf8_line function in load_text.c, which allows attackers to cause a denial of service, even code execution and escalation of privileges.

vulnerabilities.description: Stack buffer overflow in szLineConv. When unicode_type is 0 and the input contains many non-UTF8 high-bit-set bytes (win-CP characters), each such input byte produces 2 output bytes in szLineConv (one 0xc0-prefix byte and the modified original byte). Since szLineConv is fixed at 2048 bytes but the input szLine can be up to lineSize bytes, if lineSize > 1024, the index j can exceed 2048, overflowing the stack buffer.

step_by_step_execution.description: Function is called with lineSize > 1024 (e.g., 4096) and unicode_type = 0.
gf_fgets reads a line into szLine. The line consists of, say, 2048 bytes all with high bit set (e.g., 0xA0) followed by a non-high-bit-set byte pattern, making strlen return >= 2048.

The loop iterates with i from 0. For each byte 0xA0: (szLine[i] & 0x80) is true, and the next byte is also 0xA0 so (szLine[i+1] & 0xc0) == 0x80 is false (0xA0 & 0xc0 = 0x80... actually let me reconsider). For the non-UTF8 win-CP branch to trigger, we need (szLine[i+1] & 0xc0) != 0x80. Use bytes like 0xC1 where next byte is also 0xC1: (0xC1 & 0xc0) = 0xc0 != 0x80, and (0xC1 & 0xe0) = 0xc0 which triggers the UTF8 2-byte branch first. Instead use 0x80 where next is 0xC0: (0xC0 & 0xc0) = 0xc0 != 0x80, and (0x80 & 0xe0) = 0x80 != 0xc0, (0x80 & 0xf0) = 0x80 != 0xe0, (0x80 & 0xf8) = 0x80 != 0xf0, so it falls to else and does i+=1; continue. Better: use bytes like 0x80 followed by 0x40: (0x40 & 0xc0) = 0x40 != 0x80. Then check: (0x80 & 0xe0) = 0x80 != 0xc0, falls through all multi-byte checks to else: i+=1; continue. For the expansion, we need to avoid UTF8 patterns. Use alternating 0x91, 0x41 pairs. For 0x91: high bit set. Next byte 0x41: (0x41 & 0xc0)=0x40 != 0x80 → enters non-UTF8 branch, writes 2 bytes. Then 0x41: no high bit, writes 1 byte. Each pair of input bytes yields 3 output bytes.

With 2048 input bytes structured so roughly every other byte triggers the non-UTF8 expansion (each expanding byte writes 2 to szLineConv), j grows to approximately 1.5 * 2048 = 3072, well exceeding szLineConv's 2048-byte capacity.
Writes to szLineConv[j] occur past the end of the 2048-byte stack buffer, causing a stack buffer overflow.

vulnerabilities.description: When lineSize < 2048, the strcpy from the fixed 2048-byte szLineConv back into szLine (of lineSize bytes) can overflow szLine. In the unicode path, gf_utf8_wcstombs can write up to 2048 bytes into szLineConv, which is then copied to szLine without size checking.

step_by_step_execution.description: Function is called with a small lineSize (e.g., 256) and unicode_type = 2 or 3 (UTF-16).
gf_fgets reads up to 256 bytes of UTF-16 data into szLine.

After byte-swapping if needed, gf_utf8_wcstombs converts UTF-16 to UTF-8 into szLineConv (up to 2048 bytes). UTF-8 can use up to 3 bytes per UTF-16 code unit, potentially producing more bytes than the original lineSize.
strcpy(szLine, szLineConv) copies the result back to szLine which is only 256 bytes, causing a heap/stack buffer overflow.

commit_message:
Fix tf.raw_ops.StagePeek vulnerability with invalid `index`.

Check that input is actually a scalar before treating it as such.

PiperOrigin-RevId: 445524908
cve_desc: TensorFlow is an open source platform for machine learning. Prior to versions 2.9.0, 2.8.1, 2.7.2, and 2.6.4, the implementation of `tf.raw_ops.StagePeek` does not fully validate the input arguments. This results in a `CHECK`-failure which can be used to trigger a denial of service attack. The code assumes `index` is a scalar but there is no validation for this before accessing its value. Versions 2.9.0, 2.8.1, 2.7.2, and 2.6.4 contain a patch for this issue.

commit_message:
Merge pull request from GHSA-p6g5-v97c-w5q4

* Prevent heap buffer overflow when parsing DNS packets

* Make sure packet parsing doesn't advance beyond max/end

* Update checks

* Remove  check

Co-authored-by: sauwming <ming@teluu.com>
cve_desc: PJSIP is a free and open source multimedia communication library written in C. A buffer overflow vulnerability in versions 2.12 and prior affects applications that use PJSIP DNS resolution. It doesn't affect PJSIP users who utilize an external resolver. This vulnerability is related to CVE-2023-27585. The difference is that this issue is in parsing the query record `parse_rr()`, while the issue in CVE-2023-27585 is in `parse_query()`. A patch is available in the `master` branch of the `pjsip/pjproject` GitHub repository. A workaround is to disable DNS resolution in PJSIP config (by setting `nameserver_count` to zero) or use an external resolver instead.

vulnerabilities.description: After parsing the DNS name, the code reads 4 bytes (2 for type, 2 for class) from the packet without verifying that `p + 4 <= max`. If the packet is truncated such that `start + name_part_len` is close to `max`, the `pj_memcpy` calls will read beyond the valid packet buffer, causing an out-of-bounds read.

step_by_step_execution.description: get_name_len is called, which validates the name portion of the query and returns name_part_len. This only accounts for the name bytes, not the 4 additional bytes for type and class.

state.value: length of DNS name in the raw packet (validated to be within bounds for name only)

step_by_step_execution.description: Memory is allocated for the name and get_name parses it successfully.
p is set to start + name_part_len, pointing just past the name. If the packet is crafted so that max - (start + name_part_len) < 4 (e.g., 0, 1, 2, or 3 bytes remaining), p is near or at max.

state.value: start + name_part_len, where (max - p) can be < 4

step_by_step_execution.description: pj_memcpy(&q->type, p, 2) reads 2 bytes from p. If p + 2 > max, this reads beyond the packet buffer.

state.value: points at or past max, causing out-of-bounds read of up to 2 bytes

step_by_step_execution.description: p is incremented by 2, then pj_memcpy(&q->dnsclass, p, 2) reads another 2 bytes, potentially further out of bounds.

state.value: start + name_part_len + 2, potentially well past max

commit_message:
Fix potential divide by zero error when executing FractionalMaxPool, when pooling ratio is higher than input size for a particular dimension.

PiperOrigin-RevId: 412151722
Change-Id: I06e57cbb8eca43816eff79eac264fa7aae8f7163
cve_desc: Tensorflow is an Open Source Machine Learning Framework. The implementation of `FractionalMaxPool` can be made to crash a TensorFlow process via a division by 0. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.8.0. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.7.1, TensorFlow 2.6.3, and TensorFlow 2.5.3, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

code_snippets:
ConstEigenMatrixMap in_mat(tensor_in.flat<T>().data(), input_size[3],
                               input_size[2] * input_size[1] * input_size[0]);

EigenMatrixMap out_mat(output_tensor->flat<T>().data(), output_size[3],
                           output_size[2] * output_size[1] * output_size[0]);
vulnerabilities.description: The products `input_size[2] * input_size[1] * input_size[0]` and `output_size[2] * output_size[1] * output_size[0]` are computed using `int` arithmetic, which can overflow for large tensor dimensions (e.g., three dimensions of ~1300 each exceed INT_MAX). An overflowed value creates an Eigen map with incorrect (smaller or negative) column count, and subsequent `in_mat.col(in_offset)` / `out_mat.col(out_offset)` accesses use offsets computed from the original (non-overflowed) dimensions, leading to out-of-bounds heap reads and writes.

step_by_step_execution.description: Input tensor dimensions are read into `input_size` (type std::vector<int>). An attacker provides a tensor with large but valid dimensions, e.g. input_size = {1300, 1300, 1300, 1}.
The check `input_size[i] >= pooling_ratio_[i]` passes since dimensions are large.
output_size is computed via floor(input_size[i] / pooling_ratio_[i]). Assuming pooling_ratio ~1.0, output_size is similar to input_size.

Eigen map for input is constructed. The column count is `input_size[2] * input_size[1] * input_size[0]` computed as int: 1300 * 1300 * 1300 = 2,197,000,000 which overflows signed 32-bit int (max 2,147,483,647), producing a negative or small positive value.

state.value: overflowed int value (e.g. negative or wraps around)

step_by_step_execution.description: The nested pooling loops compute `in_offset = (b * input_size[1] + h) * input_size[2] + w` using int64_t arithmetic, producing correct large offsets like up to ~2.19 billion.

`in_mat.col(in_offset)` accesses the Eigen map using the large offset, but the map was created with an overflowed (incorrect) column count. This leads to out-of-bounds memory read. Similarly, `out_mat.col(out_offset)` leads to out-of-bounds write.

state.value: exceeds the overflowed column count of the Eigen map

commit_message:
Merge pull request from GHSA-5x45-qp78-g4p4

* Prevent infinite loop in scanning xml content

* Simplify scanning method

* Optimization
cve_desc: PJSIP is a free and open source multimedia communication library written in the C language. Versions 2.12 and prior contain a denial-of-service vulnerability that affects PJSIP users that consume PJSIP's XML parsing in their apps. Users are advised to update. There are no known workarounds.

vulnerabilities.description: Multiple locations dereference *(scanner->curptr+1) without first verifying that the scanner has at least 2 bytes remaining. If the scanner buffer ends exactly after a '<' character, reading curptr+1 accesses memory one byte past the end of the valid buffer, causing an out-of-bounds read.

step_by_step_execution.description: Function entry: scanner->curptr points to '<' which is the last valid byte in the scanner buffer.

state.value: points past end of valid buffer

step_by_step_execution.description: The first check passes (*scanner->curptr == '<'), then the code accesses *(scanner->curptr+1) to check if it equals '?'. This reads one byte beyond the end of the valid buffer.

Similarly, in the sub-nodes while loop, after parsing child content, if scanner->curptr points to '<' at the very end of the buffer, *(scanner->curptr+1) is read without any bounds or EOF check preceding it.
In the CDATA check section, the code checks *scanner->curptr == '<' && *(scanner->curptr+1) == '!' without verifying there is more than one byte remaining after the content parsing stage.

vulnerabilities.description: The attribute-parsing while loop checks *scanner->curptr without verifying EOF. If the input is truncated (e.g., '<tag attr' with no closing '>' or '/'), the loop will keep reading and dereferencing scanner->curptr past the end of the buffer.

step_by_step_execution.description: A malformed XML input such as '<tag attr' is provided, where the buffer ends without a '>' or '/' character.
pj_scan_get_char consumes '<', pj_scan_get_until_chr parses 'tag' as node name. Scanner now points to ' attr'.

The while loop condition checks *scanner->curptr != '>' && *scanner->curptr != '/'. Since the buffer never contains '>' or '/', if pj_scan_get_until_chr does not throw an exception on EOF, the loop continues reading past the buffer.

state.value: past end of buffer — out-of-bounds read

commit_message:
Prevent segfault in `GetSessionHandle{,V2}`.

In eager mode, session state is null.

PiperOrigin-RevId: 332548597
Change-Id: If094812c2e094044220b9ba28f7d7601be042f38
cve_desc: In eager mode, TensorFlow before versions 1.15.4, 2.0.3, 2.1.2, 2.2.1 and 2.3.1 does not set the session state. Hence, calling `tf.raw_ops.GetSessionHandle` or `tf.raw_ops.GetSessionHandleV2` results in a null pointer dereference In linked snippet, in eager mode, `ctx->session_state()` returns `nullptr`. Since code immediately dereferences this, we get a segmentation fault. The issue is patched in commit 9a133d73ae4b4664d22bd1aa6d654fec13c52ee1, and is released in TensorFlow versions 1.15.4, 2.0.3, 2.1.2, 2.2.1, or 2.3.1.

commit_message: fixed #2175
cve_desc: MP4Box is a component of GPAC-2.0.0, which is a widely-used third-party package on RPM Fusion. When MP4Box tries to parse a MP4 file, it calls the function `diST_box_read()` to read from video. In this function, it allocates a buffer `str` with fixed length. However, content read from `bs` is controllable by user, so is the length, which causes a buffer overflow.

vulnerabilities.description: Integer overflow in the allocation size calculation `s->size+1`. If `s->size` is the maximum value for its type (e.g., 0xFFFFFFFF for a 32-bit value or SIZE_MAX), adding 1 wraps the value to 0. `gf_malloc(0)` may return a small valid allocation, after which `gf_bs_read_data` writes `s->size` bytes into it, causing a massive heap buffer overflow.

step_by_step_execution.description: The function casts the input box `s` and reads its `size` field, which is attacker-controlled from the parsed file.

state.value: 0xFFFFFFFF (or other large value causing wrap on +1)

step_by_step_execution.description: Allocation is performed with `gf_malloc(sizeof(char) * (s->size+1))`. The expression `s->size+1` overflows to 0.
`gf_malloc(0)` returns a small but valid heap pointer (implementation-defined, often a minimum-size allocation). The NULL check passes.

state.value: valid heap pointer to a very small buffer

step_by_step_execution.description: `gf_bs_read_data` is called to read `s->size` (0xFFFFFFFF) bytes into the small buffer, causing a heap buffer overflow.

commit_message:
Add negative bound check for row and column pooling_sequence in FractionalAvgPoolGrad op to avoid out of bound heap access

PiperOrigin-RevId: 413837346
Change-Id: I2b86034101df31bee161abcb781755e236c7bccd
cve_desc: Tensorflow is an Open Source Machine Learning Framework. The implementation of `FractionalAvgPoolGrad` does not consider cases where the input tensors are invalid allowing an attacker to read from outside of bounds of heap. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.8.0. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.7.1, TensorFlow 2.6.3, and TensorFlow 2.5.3, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

code_snippets: const int64_t in_index = (b * in_rows + in_r) * in_cols + in_c;
vulnerabilities.description: There is no validation that out_batch == in_batch or out_depth == in_depth. The outer loop iterates b from 0 to out_batch and the inner loop iterates d from 0 to out_depth, but in_backprop_tensor_temp_mat is sized as (in_depth, in_cols * in_rows * in_batch). If out_batch > in_batch, in_index can exceed the number of columns. If out_depth > in_depth, d can exceed the number of rows. Both lead to out-of-bounds read/write on the matrix.

step_by_step_execution.description: The orig_input_tensor_shape is read, giving in_batch, in_rows, in_cols, in_depth. Suppose in_batch=1, in_rows=4, in_cols=4, in_depth=1.
out_backprop dimensions are read. Suppose out_backprop has shape [2, 2, 2, 3], so out_batch=2, out_rows=2, out_cols=2, out_depth=3.
in_backprop_tensor_temp is allocated with in_shape [1,4,4,1], total 16 elements of double. The EigenDoubleMatrixMap maps it as (in_depth=1 rows, in_cols*in_rows*in_batch=16 columns).
The nested loop iterates b from 0 to out_batch=2. When b=1, in_index = (1 * 4 + in_r) * 4 + in_c. With in_r=3, in_c=3, in_index = 7*4+3 = 31, but the matrix only has 16 columns.

coeffRef(d, 31) is called on a matrix with only 16 columns, causing an out-of-bounds write. Additionally, d can go up to out_depth=3, but the matrix only has in_depth=1 rows, causing further OOB access.

code_snippets: const int64_t in_index = (b * in_rows + in_r) * in_cols + in_c;
vulnerabilities.description: The values from row_seq_tensor and col_seq_tensor are used as row/column start indices without any lower-bound validation. A negative value in the sequence tensor leads to negative in_r or in_c, causing a negative in_index and out-of-bounds memory access on in_backprop_tensor_temp_mat.

step_by_step_execution.description: Inputs are set up: in_shape=[1,4,4,1], out_backprop shape=[1,2,2,1]. row_seq_tensor and col_seq_tensor have enough elements to pass the checks.
row_seq_tensor contains a negative value, e.g. row_seq_tensor_flat(0) = -5.
In the loop with r=0, in_row_start = row_seq_tensor_flat(0) = -5. in_row_end is clamped to at most in_max_row_index=3, so the loop iterates in_r from -5 to some positive value.
With b=0, in_r=-5, in_c=0: in_index = (0 * 4 + (-5)) * 4 + 0 = -20. This is a negative index used in coeffRef, causing out-of-bounds memory access.

commit_message:
Prevent a division by 0 in division ops.

PiperOrigin-RevId: 385223169
Change-Id: Ia4228960b5d2aa44480385f74bdd70d21a3613c3
cve_desc: TensorFlow is an end-to-end open source platform for machine learning. In affected versions the implementation of division in TFLite is [vulnerable to a division by 0 error](https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorflow/blob/460e000de3a83278fb00b61a16d161b1964f15f4/tensorflow/lite/kernels/div.cc). There is no check that the divisor tensor does not contain zero elements. We have patched the issue in GitHub commit 1e206baedf8bef0334cca3eb92bab134ef525a28. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.6.0. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.5.1, TensorFlow 2.4.3, and TensorFlow 2.3.4, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

commit_message:
Prevent nullptr deref in SparseTensorSliceDataset

The arguments must determine a valid sparse tensor. This means that when indices are empty then the values must be empty too (and the reverse).

Also added test, by modifying existing test with empty sparse tensor to now run with an invalid sparse tensor input.

PiperOrigin-RevId: 388562757
Change-Id: Id8b54cd7c2316025b4f9a77292c8fb5344d17609
cve_desc: TensorFlow is an end-to-end open source platform for machine learning. When a user does not supply arguments that determine a valid sparse tensor, `tf.raw_ops.SparseTensorSliceDataset` implementation can be made to dereference a null pointer. The [implementation](https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorflow/blob/8d72537c6abf5a44103b57b9c2e22c14f5f49698/tensorflow/core/kernels/data/sparse_tensor_slice_dataset_op.cc#L240-L251) has some argument validation but fails to consider the case when either `indices` or `values` are provided for an empty sparse tensor when the other is not. If `indices` is empty, then [code that performs validation](https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorflow/blob/8d72537c6abf5a44103b57b9c2e22c14f5f49698/tensorflow/core/kernels/data/sparse_tensor_slice_dataset_op.cc#L260-L261) (i.e., checking that the indices are monotonically increasing) results in a null pointer dereference. If `indices` as provided by the user is empty, then `indices` in the C++ code above is backed by an empty `std::vector`, hence calling `indices->dim_size(0)` results in null pointer dereferencing (same as calling `std::vector::at()` on an empty vector). We have patched the issue in GitHub commit 02cc160e29d20631de3859c6653184e3f876b9d7. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.6.0. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.5.1, TensorFlow 2.4.3, and TensorFlow 2.3.4, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

vulnerabilities.description: Out-of-bounds read when `indices` is a matrix with shape [N, 0] where N > 0. The code checks that indices is a matrix and handles the case where NumElements() is 0, but it iterates based on `indices->dim_size(0)` (number of rows). A matrix with shape [N, 0] has 0 elements but N rows, so the loop runs N times and accesses column 0 which does not exist, causing an out-of-bounds memory read.

step_by_step_execution.description: Input `indices` tensor is obtained with shape [N, 0] where N > 0 (e.g., [5, 0]). This is a valid matrix shape.
Input `values` tensor is obtained with shape [0], a valid vector.
IsMatrix check passes because indices has rank 2.
num_indices == 0 and num_values == 0, so the emptiness check passes (both are zero).
IsVector checks pass for values and dense_shape.

The loop iterates from i=0 to i < indices->dim_size(0) which is 5. On the first iteration, `indices->matrix<int64>()(0, 0)` is accessed, but the matrix has 0 columns, resulting in an out-of-bounds read.

commit_message:
Fix tf.raw_ops.EditDistance vulnerability with negative indices.

Check that indices are non-negative. Fix several identical code sites.
Clean up grammar in error message.

PiperOrigin-RevId: 445442017
cve_desc: TensorFlow is an open source platform for machine learning. Prior to versions 2.9.0, 2.8.1, 2.7.2, and 2.6.4, the implementation of `tf.raw_ops.EditDistance` has incomplete validation. Users can pass negative values to cause a segmentation fault based denial of service. In multiple places throughout the code, one may compute an index for a write operation. However, the existing validation only checks against the upper bound of the array. Hence, it is possible to write before the array by massaging the input to generate negative values for `loc`. Versions 2.9.0, 2.8.1, 2.7.2, and 2.6.4 contain a patch for this issue.

code_snippets:
OP_REQUIRES(
            ctx, loc < output_elements,
            errors::Internal("Got an inner product ", loc,
                             " which would require in writing to outside of "
                             "the buffer for the output tensor (max elements ",
                             output_elements, ")"));
        output_t(loc) =
            gtl::LevenshteinDistance<T>(truth_seq, hypothesis_seq, cmp);
vulnerabilities.description: The bounds check on `loc` only verifies `loc < output_elements` but does not verify `loc >= 0`. Since `loc` is computed via `std::inner_product` using group indices from user-supplied sparse tensor indices, a malicious input with negative indices can produce a negative `loc` value. This negative value passes the `loc < output_elements` check (since negative < positive is true for signed int64_t) and is then used to index into the output tensor buffer, causing an out-of-bounds write.

step_by_step_execution.description: User provides sparse tensor inputs. The hypothesis_indices or truth_indices contain negative values in the group dimensions (dims 0..RANK-2).

state.value: Contains rows with negative index values, e.g. [[-1, 0, 0]]

step_by_step_execution.description: ValidateShapes is called but does not reject negative index values in the sparse tensor indices.
SparseTensor objects are created from the user-supplied indices and values.

state.value: SparseTensor with negative indices in group dimensions

step_by_step_execution.description: group_dims is set to [0, ..., RANK-2]. The grouper iterates over groups based on these dimensions. A group with negative index values is encountered.
loc is computed as std::inner_product(g_truth.begin(), g_truth.end(), output_strides.begin(), 0), yielding a negative value.
The bounds check `loc < output_elements` is evaluated. Since loc is -1 and output_elements is positive, the check passes (true).
output_t(loc) is written with the negative index, causing an out-of-bounds memory write before the start of the output buffer.

commit_message:
Add more validation to `RequantizationRangePerChannel`.

cve_desc: TensorFlow is an end-to-end open source platform for machine learning. In affected versions due to incomplete validation in MKL implementation of requantization, an attacker can trigger undefined behavior via binding a reference to a null pointer or can access data outside the bounds of heap allocated arrays. A similar issue occurs in `MklRequantizePerChannelOp`. The [implementation](https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorflow/blob/460e000de3a83278fb00b61a16d161b1964f15f4/tensorflow/core/kernels/mkl/mkl_requantize_per_channel_op.cc) does not perform full validation for all the input arguments. We have patched the issue in GitHub commit 9e62869465573cb2d9b5053f1fa02a81fce21d69 and in the Github commit 203214568f5bc237603dbab6e1fd389f1572f5c9. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.6.0. commit_message:
CVE-2022-21654

tls allows re-use when some cert validation settings have changed

Signed-off-by: Yan Avlasov <yavlasov@google.com>
cve_desc: Envoy is an open source edge and service proxy, designed for cloud-native applications. Envoy's tls allows re-use when some cert validation settings have changed from their default configuration. The only workaround for this issue is to ensure that default tls settings are used. Users are advised to upgrade.

commit_message:
Validate and require subkey binding signatures on PGP public keys

All subkeys must be followed by a binding signature by the primary key
as per the OpenPGP RFC, enforce the presence and validity in the parser.

The implementation is as kludgey as they come to work around our
simple-minded parser structure without touching API, to maximise
backportability. Store all the raw packets internally as we decode them
to be able to access previous elements at will, needed to validate ordering
and access the actual data. Add testcases for manipulated keys whose
import previously would succeed.

Depends on the two previous commits:
7b399fcb8f52566e6f3b4327197a85facd08db91 and
236b802a4aa48711823a191d1b7f753c82a89ec5

Fixes CVE-2021-3521.
cve_desc: There is a flaw in RPM's signature functionality. OpenPGP subkeys are associated with a primary key via a "binding signature." RPM does not check the binding signature of subkeys prior to importing them. If an attacker is able to add or socially engineer another party to add a malicious subkey to a legitimate public key, RPM could wrongly trust a malicious signature. The greatest impact of this flaw is to data integrity. To exploit this flaw, an attacker must either compromise an RPM repository or convince an administrator to install an untrusted RPM or public key. It is strongly recommended to only use RPMs and public keys from trusted sources.

commit_message: vm.c: check if target_class is NULL (when prepended).
cve_desc: Out-of-bounds Read in mrb_obj_is_kind_of in in GitHub repository mruby/mruby prior to 3.2. # Impact: Possible arbitrary code execution if being exploited.

vulnerabilities.description: In the OP_SEND/OP_SENDB handler, the local variable 'blk' is declared but never initialized before being passed to 'prepare_missing()' and later checked with 'mrb_proc_p(blk)'. This is undefined behavior in C. The uninitialized stack memory in 'blk' could be interpreted as a valid mrb_value, potentially leading to type confusion, information leakage, or memory corruption when dereferenced via mrb_proc_ptr().

code_snippets: m = prepare_missing(mrb, recv, mid, &cls, a, &c, blk, 0);

step_by_step_execution.description: Execution reaches the OP_SEND/OP_SENDB handler (via L_SENDB or L_SENDB_SYM).
Local variables 'recv' and 'blk' are declared on the stack but 'blk' is never assigned a value. It contains whatever garbage was on the stack.

ARGUMENT_NORMALIZE is called, recv and cls are set up, and mrb_method_search_vm looks up the method. If the method is not found (MRB_METHOD_UNDEF_P(m) is true), prepare_missing is called with the uninitialized 'blk'.

After a C function method call returns, the code checks mrb_proc_p(blk) on the still-uninitialized 'blk'. If the garbage memory happens to have a type tag matching MRB_TT_PROC, mrb_proc_ptr(blk) will interpret arbitrary memory as an RProc pointer, leading to potential memory corruption when writing p->flags.

state.value: uninitialized; if garbage type tag matches MRB_TT_PROC, p is a wild pointer
pointer derived from garbage memory, potentially pointing anywhere

vulnerabilities.description: In the OP_SUPER handler, the local variable 'blk' is declared but never initialized before being passed to 'prepare_missing()'. Same class of vulnerability as in the SEND handler - use of uninitialized variable leading to undefined behavior.

code_snippets: m = prepare_missing(mrb, recv, mid, &cls, a, &b, blk, 1);

step_by_step_execution.description: Execution reaches the OP_SUPER handler.
Local variables 'recv' and 'blk' are declared. 'recv' is later assigned but 'blk' is never assigned a value.
If mrb_method_search_vm fails to find the method (MRB_METHOD_UNDEF_P(m) is true), prepare_missing is called with the uninitialized 'blk' value, resulting in undefined behavior.

commit_message:
Prevent dereferencing of null pointers in TFLite's `add.cc`.

PiperOrigin-RevId: 387244946
Change-Id: I56094233327fbd8439b92e1dbb1262176e00eeb9
cve_desc: TensorFlow is an end-to-end open source platform for machine learning. In affected versions an attacker can craft a TFLite model that would trigger a null pointer dereference, which would result in a crash and denial of service. The [implementation](https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorflow/blob/149562d49faa709ea80df1d99fc41d005b81082a/tensorflow/lite/kernels/internal/optimized/optimized_ops.h#L268-L285) unconditionally dereferences a pointer. We have patched the issue in GitHub commit 15691e456c7dc9bd6be203b09765b063bf4a380c. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.6.0. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.5.1, TensorFlow 2.4.3, and TensorFlow 2.3.4, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

commit_message:
Fix for bug #72790 and bug #72799

(cherry picked from commit a14fdb9746262549bbbb96abb87338bacd147e1b)

Conflicts:
	ext/wddx/wddx.c
cve_desc: ext/wddx/wddx.c in PHP before 5.6.25 and 7.x before 7.0.10 allows remote attackers to cause a denial of service (NULL pointer dereference and application crash) or possibly have unspecified other impact via an invalid wddxPacket XML document that is mishandled in a wddx_deserialize call, as demonstrated by a stray element inside a boolean element, leading to incorrect pop processing.

vulnerabilities.description: php_base64_decode can return NULL if the input is not valid base64 or is empty. The return value is used directly in ZVAL_STR without a NULL check, setting the zval's string pointer to NULL. Subsequent operations on ent1->data (e.g., the object __wakeup path, or hash insertions) will dereference this NULL pointer, leading to a crash or potentially exploitable condition.

step_by_step_execution.description: Function is called with an EL_BINARY closing tag name. stack->top > 0.
ent1 is retrieved from the top of the stack. Z_TYPE(ent1->data) is IS_STRING (not IS_UNDEF), so we don't return early.
The name matches EL_BINARY, so php_base64_decode is called. With invalid base64 input, php_base64_decode returns NULL.
zval_ptr_dtor frees the old string in ent1->data. Then ZVAL_STR sets ent1->data to contain the NULL zend_string pointer.

state.value: zval with IS_STRING type but NULL zend_string* pointer

step_by_step_execution.description: Execution continues past the EL_BINARY block. If Z_TYPE(ent1->data) triggers any string operation (e.g., when it is inserted into a target hash via zend_symtable_str_update or zend_hash_next_index_insert), the NULL string pointer is dereferenced, causing a crash.

commit_message:
Fix for bug #72790 and bug #72799

(cherry picked from commit a14fdb9746262549bbbb96abb87338bacd147e1b)

Conflicts:
	ext/wddx/wddx.c
cve_desc: ext/wddx/wddx.c in PHP before 5.6.25 and 7.x before 7.0.10 allows remote attackers to cause a denial of service (NULL pointer dereference and application crash) or possibly have unspecified other impact via an invalid wddxPacket XML document that is mishandled in a wddx_deserialize call, as demonstrated by a stray element inside a boolean element, leading to incorrect pop processing.

vulnerabilities.description: php_base64_decode can return NULL if the input is invalid or memory allocation fails. The original zval data is destroyed via zval_ptr_dtor, and then ZVAL_STR is called with a potentially NULL zend_string pointer. Any subsequent access to ent1->data (e.g., during __wakeup call, hash insertion, or zval_ptr_dtor) will dereference this NULL pointer, leading to a crash or potential further exploitation.

step_by_step_execution.description: The function is called with an EL_BINARY closing element. stack->top > 0, so we proceed.
The element name matches EL_BINARY, so we enter the main branch and retrieve ent1 from the stack top.
Z_TYPE(ent1->data) is not IS_UNDEF, so we skip the early return.
The name matches EL_BINARY, so php_base64_decode is called on the string data. If the input contains invalid base64, the function returns NULL.
zval_ptr_dtor(&ent1->data) destroys the original string data.
ZVAL_STR(&ent1->data, new_str) sets ent1->data to a zend_string pointer that is NULL.
Subsequent code accesses ent1->data (e.g., the __wakeup check reads Z_TYPE, or later zval_ptr_dtor / hash insertion dereferences the NULL string pointer), causing a NULL pointer dereference.

commit_message: codegen.c: fix a argument generation bug in array assignment.
cve_desc: Out-of-bounds Read in GitHub repository mruby/mruby prior to 3.2.

commit_message: codegen.c: fix a argument generation bug in array assignment.
cve_desc: Out-of-bounds Read in GitHub repository mruby/mruby prior to 3.2.

commit_message: vm.c: check if target_class is NULL (when prepended).
cve_desc: Out-of-bounds Read in mrb_obj_is_kind_of in in GitHub repository mruby/mruby prior to 3.2. # Impact: Possible arbitrary code execution if being exploited.

vulnerabilities.description: In the OP_SEND/OP_SENDB handler, the local variable `blk` is declared but never initialized before being used. It is passed to `prepare_missing()` when the method is undefined, and later used in `mrb_proc_p(blk)` to check if the block is an orphan proc after a C function call returns. Reading this uninitialized stack variable can lead to use of indeterminate values, potentially causing incorrect control flow (e.g., treating garbage memory as a valid proc pointer and writing to its flags).

code_snippets: m = prepare_missing(mrb, recv, mid, &cls, a, &c, blk, 0);

step_by_step_execution.description: Execution enters the OP_SEND or OP_SENDB handler (via L_SENDB). Local variables `recv` and `blk` are declared on the stack without initialization.
ARGUMENT_NORMALIZE is called, which normalizes arguments but does not assign a value to the local `blk` variable.
If the method lookup fails (MRB_METHOD_UNDEF_P(m) is true), `blk` is passed by value to `prepare_missing()`. This reads an uninitialized value.

state.value: uninitialized value read and passed to prepare_missing

step_by_step_execution.description: After a successful C function method call returns, the code checks `mrb_proc_p(blk)`. Since `blk` was never assigned, this evaluates the type tag of indeterminate memory. If the garbage value happens to match MRB_TT_PROC, the code dereferences it as a proc pointer and writes to its flags field.

state.value: uninitialized; if type bits match MRB_TT_PROC, treated as a valid object pointer
mrb_proc_ptr(blk) - pointer derived from uninitialized data

vulnerabilities.description: In the OP_SUPER handler, the local variable `blk` is declared but never initialized before being passed to `prepare_missing()` when the super method is not found. This is the same class of uninitialized variable use as in the SEND handler.

code_snippets: m = prepare_missing(mrb, recv, mid, &cls, a, &b, blk, 1);

step_by_step_execution.description: Execution enters the OP_SUPER handler. Local variables `recv` and `blk` are declared without initialization.
After method search, if the super method is not found (MRB_METHOD_UNDEF_P(m)), `blk` is read and passed to `prepare_missing()`.

state.value: uninitialized value read and passed to prepare_missing

commit_message:
Secure tf.raw_ops.QuantizeV2

Validate size and shape of min_range and max_range
Ensure axis is within input dims limits

PiperOrigin-RevId: 387232799
Change-Id: I36975281f7b5758e9e31a8dcc73fe610ef456318
cve_desc: TensorFlow is an end-to-end open source platform for machine learning. In affected versions due to incomplete validation in `tf.raw_ops.QuantizeV2`, an attacker can trigger undefined behavior via binding a reference to a null pointer or can access data outside the bounds of heap allocated arrays. The [implementation](https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorflow/blob/84d053187cb80d975ef2b9684d4b61981bca0c41/tensorflow/core/kernels/quantize_op.cc#L59) has some validation but does not check that `min_range` and `max_range` both have the same non-zero number of elements. If `axis` is provided (i.e., not `-1`), then validation should check that it is a value in range for the rank of `input` tensor and then the lengths of `min_range` and `max_range` inputs match the `axis` dimension of the `input` tensor. We have patched the issue in GitHub commit 6da6620efad397c85493b8f8667b821403516708. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.6.0. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.5.1, TensorFlow 2.4.3, and TensorFlow 2.3.4, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

commit_message:
avformat/adtsenc: return value check for init_get_bits in adts_decode_extradata

As the second argument for init_get_bits (buf) can be crafted, a return value check for this function call is necessary.
'buf' is  part of  'AVPacket pkt'.
replace init_get_bits with init_get_bits8.

Signed-off-by: Michael Niedermayer <michael@niedermayer.cc>
cve_desc: adts_decode_extradata in libavformat/adtsenc.c in FFmpeg 4.4 does not check the init_get_bits return value, which is a necessary step because the second argument to init_get_bits can be crafted.
