commit_message:
Prevent use after free in `DecodePng` kernel.

We are cleaning up the memory in `decode` and then we are using an `OP_REQUIRES` to check an invariant on the `decode` data.

PiperOrigin-RevId: 409299145
Change-Id: I4eb93aaca52483eb202e89b78df07fbb2f6cb254
cve_desc: Tensorflow is an Open Source Machine Learning Framework. A malicious user can cause a use after free behavior when decoding PNG images. After `png::CommonFreeDecode(&decode)` gets called, the values of `decode.width` and `decode.height` are in an unspecified state. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.8.0. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.7.1, TensorFlow 2.6.3, and TensorFlow 2.5.3, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

vulnerabilities.description: When op_type_ is "DecodeBmp" or "DecodeAndCropJpeg", the OP_REQUIRES macro causes the function to return early without calling png::CommonFreeDecode(&decode), leaking resources allocated by png::CommonInitDecode. This can be triggered repeatedly to cause a denial of service through resource exhaustion.

code_snippets:
OP_REQUIRES(context, false,
                  errors::InvalidArgument(
                      "Trying to decode PNG format using DecodeBmp op. Use "
                      "`decode_png` or `decode_image` instead."));

OP_REQUIRES(context, false,
                  errors::InvalidArgument(
                      "DecodeAndCropJpeg operation can run on JPEG only, but "
                      "detected PNG."));

step_by_step_execution.description: png::CommonInitDecode is called, allocating internal libpng decode resources into the 'decode' struct.

state.value: initialized with internal libpng resources (memory, state)

step_by_step_execution.description: Width and height bounds checks pass for a valid PNG image.
context->allocate_output is called to allocate the output tensor. status may be ok or not.

state.value: pointer to allocated tensor or nullptr

step_by_step_execution.description: op_type_ is checked. If op_type_ == "DecodeBmp", OP_REQUIRES(context, false, ...) is executed, which sets an error on the context and causes the function to return immediately.

state.value: still holds allocated resources, CommonFreeDecode never called

step_by_step_execution.description: The function returns without calling png::CommonFreeDecode(&decode), leaking all resources held by the decode context.

state.value: leaked — internal libpng resources not freed

commit_message:
Fix a null pointer exception caused by branching on uninitialized data.

This is due to not checking that the params for the quantization exists. If there is no quantization, we should not access the `.params` field.

PiperOrigin-RevId: 385163909
Change-Id: I2beb8d50649b6542db224c163033fbcbaa49314f
cve_desc: TensorFlow is an end-to-end open source platform for machine learning. In affected versions all TFLite operations that use quantization can be made to use unitialized values. [For example](https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorflow/blob/460e000de3a83278fb00b61a16d161b1964f15f4/tensorflow/lite/kernels/depthwise_conv.cc#L198-L200). The issue stems from the fact that `quantization.params` is only valid if `quantization.type` is different that `kTfLiteNoQuantization`. However, these checks are missing in large parts of the code. We have patched the issue in GitHub commits 537bc7c723439b9194a358f64d871dd326c18887, 4a91f2069f7145aab6ba2d8cfe41be8a110c18a5 and 8933b8a21280696ab119b63263babdb54c298538. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.6.0. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.5.1, TensorFlow 2.4.3, and TensorFlow 2.3.4, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

commit_message:
Fix tf.raw_ops.EditDistance vulnerability with negative indices.

Check that indices are non-negative. Fix several identical code sites.
Clean up grammar in error message.

PiperOrigin-RevId: 445442017
cve_desc: TensorFlow is an open source platform for machine learning. Prior to versions 2.9.0, 2.8.1, 2.7.2, and 2.6.4, the implementation of `tf.raw_ops.EditDistance` has incomplete validation. Users can pass negative values to cause a segmentation fault based denial of service. In multiple places throughout the code, one may compute an index for a write operation. However, the existing validation only checks against the upper bound of the array. Hence, it is possible to write before the array by massaging the input to generate negative values for `loc`. Versions 2.9.0, 2.8.1, 2.7.2, and 2.6.4 contain a patch for this issue.

vulnerabilities.description: When truth_st_shape has only 1 dimension (truth_shape is a vector with a single element), group_dims has size 0, output_shape has 0 dimensions, and output_strides is an empty vector. The line `output_strides[output_shape.dims() - 1]` becomes `output_strides[-1]`, which is an out-of-bounds write on the vector's underlying buffer. Similarly, if truth_st_shape.dims() is 0, `truth_st_shape.dims() - 1` is -1, which when cast to size_t for the vector constructor becomes an enormous value, causing a massive allocation attempt or crash.

step_by_step_execution.description: An attacker provides truth_shape as a 1-D tensor containing a single element (e.g., [5]), making truth_st_shape a 1-dimensional TensorShape.
group_dims is constructed with size truth_st_shape.dims() - 1 = 0. The output_shape loop runs 0 times, so output_shape has 0 dimensions.
output_shape.num_elements() returns 1 for a scalar shape, so the check `output_elements > 0` passes.

output_strides is constructed as an empty vector (size 0). Then the code attempts output_strides[output_shape.dims() - 1] = output_strides[0 - 1] = output_strides[-1] (interpreted as a very large index due to unsigned conversion), causing an out-of-bounds memory write.

commit_message:
Fix a null pointer exception caused by branching on uninitialized data.

This is due to not checking that the params for the quantization exists. If there is no quantization, we should not access the `.params` field.

PiperOrigin-RevId: 385163909
Change-Id: I2beb8d50649b6542db224c163033fbcbaa49314f
cve_desc: TensorFlow is an end-to-end open source platform for machine learning. In affected versions all TFLite operations that use quantization can be made to use unitialized values. [For example](https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorflow/blob/460e000de3a83278fb00b61a16d161b1964f15f4/tensorflow/lite/kernels/depthwise_conv.cc#L198-L200). The issue stems from the fact that `quantization.params` is only valid if `quantization.type` is different that `kTfLiteNoQuantization`. However, these checks are missing in large parts of the code. We have patched the issue in GitHub commits 537bc7c723439b9194a358f64d871dd326c18887, 4a91f2069f7145aab6ba2d8cfe41be8a110c18a5 and 8933b8a21280696ab119b63263babdb54c298538. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.6.0. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.5.1, TensorFlow 2.4.3, and TensorFlow 2.3.4, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

commit_message: Prevent malformed upload path causing arbitrary write (#1174)
cve_desc: This affects the package drogonframework/drogon before 1.7.5. The unsafe handling of file names during upload using HttpFile::save() method may enable attackers to write files to arbitrary locations outside the designated target folder.

vulnerabilities.description: The member variable `fileName_` (originating from user-uploaded file data) is used directly in constructing the final file path without sanitization. If `fileName_` contains path traversal sequences such as `../`, the resulting path `fsPath / fsFileName` can escape the intended upload directory, allowing an attacker to write files to arbitrary locations on the filesystem.

step_by_step_execution.description: The function is called with a valid `path` argument (e.g., "uploads").
`fileName_` is not empty, so the early return is skipped. However, `fileName_` is attacker-controlled and contains a traversal payload.
`fsPath` is constructed. Since the path is not absolute and has no parent, it is joined with the configured upload path.
`fsFileName` is constructed directly from the unsanitized `fileName_`.
Directories are created if `fsPath` doesn't exist.
The final path `fsPath / fsFileName` resolves to a location outside the intended directory, and the file is saved there via `saveTo`.

commit_message:
Prevent overflow in sparse dense cwise ops.

PiperOrigin-RevId: 415543171
Change-Id: I22dab7c41be2121ab5efe5403ca0e2f9b7cb24b8
cve_desc: Tensorflow is an Open Source Machine Learning Framework. The implementations of `Sparse*Cwise*` ops are vulnerable to integer overflows. These can be used to trigger large allocations (so, OOM based denial of service) or `CHECK`-fails when building new `TensorShape` objects (so, assert failures based denial of service). We are missing some validation on the shapes of the input tensors as well as directly constructing a large `TensorShape` with user-provided dimensions. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.8.0. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.7.1, TensorFlow 2.6.3, and TensorFlow 2.5.3, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

commit_message:
Merge pull request from GHSA-5x45-qp78-g4p4

* Prevent infinite loop in scanning xml content

* Simplify scanning method

* Optimization
cve_desc: PJSIP is a free and open source multimedia communication library written in the C language. Versions 2.12 and prior contain a denial-of-service vulnerability that affects PJSIP users that consume PJSIP's XML parsing in their apps. Users are advised to update. There are no known workarounds.

vulnerabilities.description: The attribute parsing loop checks for '>' and '/' but never checks for EOF. If a malformed XML input like `<foo bar` is provided (no closing '>' or '/'), the loop condition `*scanner->curptr != '>' && *scanner->curptr != '/'` will continue to be true past the end of valid buffer data (since '\0' matches neither character), leading to out-of-bounds reads.

step_by_step_execution.description: The function is called with a scanner whose buffer contains a truncated/malformed XML tag, e.g., `<foo bar`.
The initial check passes (`*scanner->curptr == '<'`). The PI and comment checks do not match. A node is allocated, '<' is consumed, and the node name 'foo' is parsed.
The attribute while loop begins. `*scanner->curptr` is ' ' which is neither '>' nor '/', so the loop body executes. `pj_scan_get_until_chr` parses 'bar' as the attribute name.

state.value: points at or past end of buffer data

step_by_step_execution.description: The loop condition is checked again. Since the buffer is null-terminated, `*scanner->curptr` is '\0', which is neither '>' nor '/'. The loop continues, and subsequent reads from `scanner->curptr` go beyond the valid buffer, causing an out-of-bounds read.

state.value: at or past the end of valid buffer data

vulnerabilities.description: When parsing sub-nodes, `*(scanner->curptr+1)` is dereferenced without first checking for EOF or that at least two bytes remain in the buffer. If the scanner is positioned at the last byte '<' in the buffer, accessing `curptr+1` reads one byte past the allocated buffer.

step_by_step_execution.description: The function processes a valid opening tag (e.g., `<a>`) and reaches the sub-nodes while loop.
The condition checks `*scanner->curptr == '<'` which is true, then accesses `*(scanner->curptr+1)`. If '<' is the very last byte of the scanner buffer, `curptr+1` is past the buffer boundary.

state.value: points to last valid byte in buffer
one byte past end of allocated buffer

vulnerabilities.description: The CDATA detection reads `*(scanner->curptr+1)` without verifying there are at least 2 bytes remaining. If the scanner is at the last byte '<', this is an out-of-bounds read.

step_by_step_execution.description: After parsing an opening tag and potentially content, the function reaches the CDATA check.
The check `*scanner->curptr == '<'` succeeds, then `*(scanner->curptr+1)` is accessed. If only one byte remains in the buffer, this reads out of bounds.

state.value: points to last byte in buffer

commit_message: fixed #2163
cve_desc: GPAC 2.1-DEV-rev87-g053aae8-master. has a Null Pointer Dereference vulnerability in gf_isom_parse_movie_boxes_internal due to improper return value handling of GF_SKIP_BOX, which causes a Denial of Service. This vulnerability was fixed in commit 37592ad.

vulnerabilities.description: In the MOOF handler cleanup section, the code checks `mov->root_ssix` but frees `mov->seg_ssix` instead. This is a copy-paste/logic bug that causes: (1) `mov->root_ssix` to be leaked (never freed), and (2) `mov->seg_ssix` to be freed while its pointer is NOT set to NULL, creating a dangling pointer. If a subsequent loop iteration or later code accesses `mov->seg_ssix`, it results in a use-after-free.

step_by_step_execution.description: During parsing, an SSIX box is encountered in the SIDX/SSIX case with `signal_frag_bounds` set and not in debug mode.
The SSIX box is stored in mov->seg_ssix.

state.value: pointer to valid GF_Box (the parsed SSIX box)

step_by_step_execution.description: Assume mov->root_ssix was set to a non-NULL value previously (e.g., from another code path or prior state).
A MOOF box is encountered next in the while loop.
The MOOF cleanup code checks `mov->root_ssix` which is non-NULL, so it enters the block.

gf_isom_box_del(mov->seg_ssix) is called, freeing the seg_ssix object. However, mov->seg_ssix is NOT set to NULL afterward, leaving it as a dangling pointer. Meanwhile, mov->root_ssix is set to NULL but never freed (memory leak).

If the loop continues and encounters another SSIX box, the code `if (mov->seg_ssix) gf_isom_box_del(mov->seg_ssix);` will attempt to free the already-freed dangling pointer, causing a use-after-free / double-free.

vulnerabilities.description: In the MOOV box handler's non-debug path, the code dereferences trak->Media->information->sampleTable without any NULL checks. A malformed ISO file could have a track box where Media, information, or sampleTable is NULL, leading to a NULL pointer dereference.

step_by_step_execution.description: A MOOV box is parsed from a malformed ISO file. The moov box contains a track that has a NULL Media, information, or sampleTable field.

state.value: valid pointer to parsed MOOV box

step_by_step_execution.description: The code enters the else branch (non-debug mode) and iterates through the track list.

state.value: index of a track with missing sub-boxes

step_by_step_execution.description: trak is retrieved from the track list. trak->Media or trak->Media->information or trak->Media->information->sampleTable could be NULL in a malformed file.
The code dereferences trak->Media->information->sampleTable->sampleGroups without null checks, causing a NULL pointer dereference crash.

commit_message:
Fix segmentation fault in shape inference logic.

When running shape functions, some functions (such as `MutableHashTableShape`)
produce extra output information in the form of a `ShapeAndType` struct.  The
shapes embedded in this struct are owned by an inference context that is
cleaned up almost immediately; if the upstream code attempts to access this
shape information, it can trigger a segfault.

`ShapeRefiner` is mitigating this for normal output shapes by cloning them
(and thus putting the newly created shape under ownership of an inference
context that will not die), but we were not doing the same for shapes and
types.  This commit fixes that by doing similar logic on output shapes and
types.

PiperOrigin-RevId: 384761124
Change-Id: I07c0c42d29dfbb55bfa13ec1f09ef825fb0a1a1d
cve_desc: TensorFlow is an end-to-end open source platform for machine learning. In affected versions when running shape functions, some functions (such as `MutableHashTableShape`) produce extra output information in the form of a `ShapeAndType` struct. The shapes embedded in this struct are owned by an inference context that is cleaned up almost immediately; if the upstream code attempts to access this shape information, it can trigger a segfault. `ShapeRefiner` is mitigating this for normal output shapes by cloning them (and thus putting the newly created shape under ownership of an inference context that will not die), but we were not doing the same for shapes and types. This commit fixes that by doing similar logic on output shapes and types. We have patched the issue in GitHub commit ee119d4a498979525046fba1c3dd3f13a039fbb1. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.6.0. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.5.1, TensorFlow 2.4.3, and TensorFlow 2.3.4, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

commit_message:
Fix segmentation fault in shape inference logic.

When running shape functions, some functions (such as `MutableHashTableShape`)
produce extra output information in the form of a `ShapeAndType` struct.  The
shapes embedded in this struct are owned by an inference context that is
cleaned up almost immediately; if the upstream code attempts to access this
shape information, it can trigger a segfault.

`ShapeRefiner` is mitigating this for normal output shapes by cloning them
(and thus putting the newly created shape under ownership of an inference
context that will not die), but we were not doing the same for shapes and
types.  This commit fixes that by doing similar logic on output shapes and
types.

commit_message: Merge pull request from GHSA-r374-qrwv-86hh
cve_desc: PJSIP is a free and open source multimedia communication library. In version 2.11.1 and prior, if incoming RTCP XR message contain block, the data field is not checked against the received packet size, potentially resulting in an out-of-bound read access. This affects all users that use PJMEDIA and RTCP XR. A malicious actor can send a RTCP XR message with an invalid packet size.

Fix tf.raw_ops.DeleteSessionTensor vulnerability with invalid `handle`.

Check that `handle` input is actually a scalar before treating it as such.

PiperOrigin-RevId: 445228994
cve_desc: TensorFlow is an open source platform for machine learning. Prior to versions 2.9.0, 2.8.1, 2.7.2, and 2.6.4, the implementation of `tf.raw_ops.DeleteSessionTensor` does not fully validate the input arguments. commit_message: fixed #2163
cve_desc: GPAC 2.1-DEV-rev87-g053aae8-master. has a Null Pointer Dereference vulnerability in gf_isom_parse_movie_boxes_internal due to improper return value handling of GF_SKIP_BOX, which causes a Denial of Service. This vulnerability was fixed in commit 37592ad.

vulnerabilities.description: When processing a MOOF box, the code intends to free `mov->root_ssix` but mistakenly calls `gf_isom_box_del(mov->seg_ssix)` instead. This frees `mov->seg_ssix` without setting it to NULL, and leaks `mov->root_ssix`. In subsequent loop iterations, if another SSIX box is encountered, the code `if (mov->seg_ssix) gf_isom_box_del(mov->seg_ssix);` will attempt to free the already-freed `mov->seg_ssix`, causing a double-free.

step_by_step_execution.description: The parser encounters a SIDX/SSIX box in the signal_frag_bounds path (not READ_DEBUG). The SSIX box is assigned to mov->seg_ssix.

state.value: pointer to allocated SSIX box A

step_by_step_execution.description: A SIDX box is also encountered and assigned to mov->root_sidx, and some other box sets mov->root_ssix to non-NULL.

state.value: pointer to allocated SSIX box A

step_by_step_execution.description: The parser encounters a MOOF box. After processing, the cleanup code checks mov->root_ssix which is non-NULL, and frees mov->seg_ssix instead of mov->root_ssix. mov->root_ssix is set to NULL, but mov->seg_ssix is NOT set to NULL.

state.value: dangling pointer to freed SSIX box A

step_by_step_execution.description: The loop continues. Another SSIX box is parsed in the signal_frag_bounds path. The code checks `if (mov->seg_ssix)` which is non-NULL (dangling pointer), and calls gf_isom_box_del on the already-freed memory, causing a double-free.

vulnerabilities.description: When processing the MOOV box in non-debug mode, the code dereferences a chain of pointers (trak->Media->information->sampleTable) without null checks. A malformed ISO file with a track missing any of these sub-boxes (Media, information, or sampleTable) will cause a NULL pointer dereference, leading to a crash.

step_by_step_execution.description: A MOOV box is parsed from a crafted input file. The MOOV box contains a track box (trak) but with a missing or NULL Media, information, or sampleTable sub-box.

state.value: valid pointer to parsed MOOV box

step_by_step_execution.description: The code enters the else branch (not GF_ISOM_FRAG_READ_DEBUG) and iterates over trackList.

state.value: pointer to a GF_TrackBox from the track list

step_by_step_execution.description: The code dereferences trak->Media->information->sampleTable without checking if Media, information, or sampleTable are NULL. If any is NULL (malformed file), a NULL pointer dereference occurs.

This is because the implementation(https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorflow/blob/36229ea9e9451dac14a8b1f4711c435a1d84a594/tensorflow/core/kernels/reverse_op.cc#L75-L76) performs a division based on the first dimension of the tensor argument. commit_message:
crypto: ccp - fix resource leaks in ccp_run_aes_gcm_cmd()

There are three bugs in this code:

1) If we ccp_init_data() fails for &src then we need to free aad.
   Use goto e_aad instead of goto e_ctx.
2) The label to free the &final_wa was named incorrectly as "e_tag" but
   it should have been "e_final_wa".  One error path leaked &final_wa.
3) The &tag was leaked on one error path.  In that case, I added a free
   before the goto because the resource was local to that block.

Fixes: 36cf515b9bbe ("crypto: ccp - Enable support for AES GCM on v5 CCPs")
Reported-by: "minihanshen(沈明航)" <minihanshen@tencent.com>
Signed-off-by: Dan Carpenter <dan.carpenter@oracle.com>
Reviewed-by: John Allen <john.allen@amd.com>
Tested-by: John Allen <john.allen@amd.com>
Signed-off-by: Herbert Xu <herbert@gondor.apana.org.au>
cve_desc: A memory leak flaw was found in the Linux kernel in the ccp_run_aes_gcm_cmd() function in drivers/crypto/ccp/ccp-ops.c, which allows attackers to cause a denial of service (memory consumption). This vulnerability is similar with the older CVE-2019-18808.

vulnerabilities.description: When the AES action is decrypt, `ilen` is computed as `aes->src_len - authsize`. Both values are unsigned integers. If `aes->src_len` is smaller than `authsize`, this subtraction wraps around to a very large value (close to UINT_MAX). There is no check that `aes->src_len >= authsize` before the subtraction. This massive `ilen` value is then used in `scatterwalk_ffwd`, `ccp_init_data`, and length calculations, leading to out-of-bounds memory access and other undefined behavior.

code_snippets: ret = ccp_init_data(&src, cmd_q, p_inp, ilen, AES_BLOCK_SIZE, in_place ? DMA_BIDIRECTIONAL : DMA_TO_DEVICE);

step_by_step_execution.description: The function validates the key length, IV, and authsize. Suppose authsize resolves to 16 (default).
The action is CCP_AES_ACTION_DECRYPT and aes->src_len is, for example, 8 (less than authsize).
The subtraction `aes->src_len - authsize` underflows because both are unsigned, wrapping to a huge value.
scatterwalk_ffwd is called with this enormous ilen value, causing out-of-bounds access on the scatterlist.

state.value: result of scatterwalk_ffwd with massive offset

step_by_step_execution.description: Later, ccp_init_data is called with the wrapped ilen, attempting to set up DMA for ~4GB of data, leading to out-of-bounds reads/writes.

commit_message: Skip MNG CLIP chunk with out-of-range object IDs
cve_desc: In ImageMagick before 6.9.9-0 and 7.x before 7.0.6-1, the ReadOneMNGImage function in coders/png.c has an out-of-bounds read with the MNG CLIP chunk.

vulnerabilities.description: In the mng_LOOP chunk handler, `loop_level` is read directly from `chunk[0]` (a byte, range 0-255) with no bounds check against the size of the `loop_active`, `loop_jump`, `loop_count`, and `loop_iteration` arrays in `MngInfo`. If these arrays are smaller than 256 entries, an attacker can supply a crafted MNG file with a LOOP chunk whose first byte exceeds the array bounds, causing out-of-bounds writes.

step_by_step_execution.description: The image format is MNG, so the code enters the MNG chunk reading loop.
A chunk of type mng_LOOP is read from the input. The chunk length is > 4.
loop_level is set from chunk[0], which is an attacker-controlled byte with no validation against array bounds.
mng_info->loop_active[loop_level] is written to with value 1. If loop_level exceeds the array size, this is an out-of-bounds write.
mng_info->loop_jump[loop_level], loop_count[loop_level], and loop_iteration[loop_level] are also written, all out-of-bounds.

vulnerabilities.description: In the mng_ENDL chunk handler, `loop_level` is read from `chunk[0]` (range 0-255) with no bounds check, leading to out-of-bounds read/write on `mng_info->loop_active[loop_level]`, `loop_count[loop_level]`, and `loop_iteration[loop_level]`.

step_by_step_execution.description: The image format is MNG, so the code enters the MNG chunk reading loop.
A chunk of type mng_ENDL is read. length > 0.
loop_level is set from chunk[0] without bounds validation.
mng_info->loop_active[loop_level] is read and potentially written, causing out-of-bounds access.

commit_message:
patch 8.2.5050: using freed memory when searching for pattern in path

Problem:    Using freed memory when searching for pattern in path.
Solution:   Make a copy of the line.
cve_desc: Use After Free in GitHub repository vim/vim prior to 8.2.

vulnerabilities.description: When the reallocation of the 'bigger' array fails (returns NULL), the code does not bail out; it falls through to access files[depth + 1] where depth + 1 == old_files == max_path_depth. Since 'files' was allocated with max_path_depth elements (indices 0 to max_path_depth-1), accessing files[max_path_depth] is an out-of-bounds write.

step_by_step_execution.description: The files array is allocated with max_path_depth elements and old_files is set to max_path_depth.
Through repeated processing of include directives, depth is incremented up to 49 (depth + 1 == old_files == 50). The front stack (0..depth) and back stack (old_files..max_path_depth-1) have collided.

state.value: 49, then condition depth+1 == old_files triggers on next include

step_by_step_execution.description: The condition depth + 1 == old_files (50 == 50) is true, so the code attempts to allocate 'bigger' with ALLOC_MULT(SearchedFile, max_path_depth * 2). This allocation fails and returns NULL.
Since bigger is NULL, the entire if (bigger != NULL) block is skipped. The files array, old_files, and max_path_depth remain unchanged.
Code falls through to files[depth + 1].fp = mch_fopen(...), which accesses files[50]. This is one element past the end of the 50-element array, causing an out-of-bounds write.

commit_message:
patch 8.2.4219: reading before the start of the line

Problem:    Reading before the start of the line.
Solution:   Check boundary before trying to read the character.
cve_desc: Heap-based Buffer Overflow in GitHub repository vim/vim prior to 8.2.

vulnerabilities.description: The while loop condition checks `VIM_ISWHITE(*(bd->textstart + s - 1))` before checking `s > 0`. Due to C's left-to-right short-circuit evaluation, when `s` reaches 0, the code dereferences `bd->textstart - 1`, which is an out-of-bounds read. If that out-of-bounds byte happens to be whitespace, the loop body executes again, decrementing `pnew` below the allocated buffer, and the subsequent `*pnew = NUL` becomes an out-of-bounds write.

step_by_step_execution.description: Function is called with `exclude_trailing_space` set to a non-zero value, and `bd->textstart` points to a string of all whitespace characters (e.g., "   ") with `bd->textlen` = 3, `bd->startspaces` = 0, `bd->endspaces` = some value.
`bd->endspaces` is set to 0 because `exclude_trailing_space` is true.
Buffer `pnew` is allocated with size `0 + 0 + 3 + 1 = 4`. The text is copied in. After copies, `pnew` points to `allocated_start + 0 + 3 = allocated_start + 3`.
In the trailing-space removal block, `s` is set to `bd->textlen + bd->endspaces = 3 + 0 = 3`.
Loop iteration 1: `VIM_ISWHITE(*(bd->textstart + 2))` is true (whitespace), `s > 0` is true. `s` is decremented to 2, `pnew` decremented to `allocated_start + 2`.
Loop iteration 2: `VIM_ISWHITE(*(bd->textstart + 1))` is true, `s > 0` is true. `s` is decremented to 1, `pnew` decremented to `allocated_start + 1`.
Loop iteration 3: `VIM_ISWHITE(*(bd->textstart + 0))` is true, `s > 0` is true. `s` is decremented to 0, `pnew` decremented to `allocated_start + 0`.

Loop iteration 4: The condition first evaluates `VIM_ISWHITE(*(bd->textstart + 0 - 1))`, i.e., `*(bd->textstart - 1)`. This is an out-of-bounds read. The `s > 0` check that would prevent this is evaluated second (short-circuit). If the OOB byte is whitespace, the loop continues, decrementing `pnew` to `allocated_start - 1`.
`*pnew = NUL` writes a NUL byte one byte before the allocated buffer, causing a heap out-of-bounds write.

commit_message:
Fix NPE in restoring code.

PiperOrigin-RevId: 388303253
Change-Id: Ia8c68568cb854bca538909a182b31a618d68ce55
cve_desc: TensorFlow is an end-to-end open source platform for machine learning. When restoring tensors via raw APIs, if the tensor name is not provided, TensorFlow can be tricked into dereferencing a null pointer. Alternatively, attackers can read memory outside the bounds of heap allocated data by providing some tensor names but not enough for a successful restoration. The [implementation](https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorflow/blob/47a06f40411a69c99f381495f490536972152ac0/tensorflow/core/kernels/save_restore_tensor.cc#L158-L159) retrieves the tensor list corresponding to the `tensor_name` user controlled input and immediately retrieves the tensor at the restoration index (controlled via `preferred_shard` argument). This occurs without validating that the provided list has enough values. If the list is empty this results in dereferencing a null pointer (undefined behavior). If, however, the list has some elements, if the restoration index is outside the bounds this results in heap OOB read. We have patched the issue in GitHub commit 9e82dce6e6bd1f36a57e08fa85af213e2b2f2622. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.6.0. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.5.1, TensorFlow 2.4.3, and TensorFlow 2.3.4, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

This leads to a negative impact to application availability or other problems related to undefined behavior.

commit_message:
Reorganize and add more validation to MKL requantization

PiperOrigin-RevId: 387901341
Change-Id: I2515b9034c64e113db0bcec8337d30643ab0a0f1
cve_desc: TensorFlow is an end-to-end open source platform for machine learning. In affected versions due to incomplete validation in MKL implementation of requantization, an attacker can trigger undefined behavior via binding a reference to a null pointer or can access data outside the bounds of heap allocated arrays. The [implementation](https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorflow/blob/460e000de3a83278fb00b61a16d161b1964f15f4/tensorflow/core/kernels/mkl/mkl_requantization_range_per_channel_op.cc) does not validate the dimensions of the `input` tensor. A similar issue occurs in `MklRequantizePerChannelOp`. The [implementation](https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorflow/blob/460e000de3a83278fb00b61a16d161b1964f15f4/tensorflow/core/kernels/mkl/mkl_requantize_per_channel_op.cc) does not perform full validation for all the input arguments. We have patched the issue in GitHub commit 9e62869465573cb2d9b5053f1fa02a81fce21d69 and in the Github commit 203214568f5bc237603dbab6e1fd389f1572f5c9. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.6.0. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.5.1, TensorFlow 2.4.3, and TensorFlow 2.3.4, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

vulnerabilities.description: Division by zero occurs when both input_requested_min_float and input_requested_max_float are 0 (or very close to 0). The variable `requested_min_max` is computed as `std::max(std::abs(input_requested_min_float), std::abs(input_requested_max_float))`, which would be 0.0f. This value is then used as a divisor in the scales computation loop without any check, leading to floating-point division by zero.

step_by_step_execution.description: The input_requested_min tensor is read and its scalar value extracted.
The input_requested_max tensor is read and its scalar value extracted.
requested_min_max is computed as max(abs(0.0f), abs(0.0f)).
The validation checks pass: input is 4D, input_min_vec and input_max_vec sizes match depth. The DCHECK for qint8 is a no-op in non-debug builds when out_type_ == DT_QINT8.

In the for loop, the code divides min_max_from_vec by requested_min_max (0.0f), causing a floating-point division by zero producing inf or NaN, which is then passed to the MKL-DNN reorder primitive potentially causing undefined behavior.

commit_message:
crypto: ccp - fix resource leaks in ccp_run_aes_gcm_cmd()

There are three bugs in this code:

1) If we ccp_init_data() fails for &src then we need to free aad.
   Use goto e_aad instead of goto e_ctx.
2) The label to free the &final_wa was named incorrectly as "e_tag" but
   it should have been "e_final_wa".  One error path leaked &final_wa.
3) The &tag was leaked on one error path.  In that case, I added a free
   before the goto because the resource was local to that block.

Fixes: 36cf515b9bbe ("crypto: ccp - Enable support for AES GCM on v5 CCPs")
Reported-by: "minihanshen(沈明航)" <minihanshen@tencent.com>
Signed-off-by: Dan Carpenter <dan.carpenter@oracle.com>
Reviewed-by: John Allen <john.allen@amd.com>
Tested-by: John Allen <john.allen@amd.com>
Signed-off-by: Herbert Xu <herbert@gondor.apana.org.au>
cve_desc: A memory leak flaw was found in the Linux kernel in the ccp_run_aes_gcm_cmd() function in drivers/crypto/ccp/ccp-ops.c, which allows attackers to cause a denial of service (memory consumption). This vulnerability is similar with the older CVE-2019-18808.

vulnerabilities.description: When `aes->action` is decrypt, `ilen` is computed as `aes->src_len - authsize` using unsigned arithmetic. If `aes->src_len` is less than `authsize` (which can be up to 16), this subtraction wraps around to a very large value (close to UINT_MAX). There is no check that `aes->src_len >= authsize`. This wrapped value is subsequently used in `scatterwalk_ffwd`, `ccp_init_data`, and loop operations, leading to out-of-bounds memory access.

code_snippets: ret = ccp_init_data(&src, cmd_q, p_inp, ilen, AES_BLOCK_SIZE, in_place ? DMA_BIDIRECTIONAL : DMA_TO_DEVICE);

step_by_step_execution.description: Function is called with `aes->action != CCP_AES_ACTION_ENCRYPT` (i.e., decrypt mode) and `aes->src_len` set to a small value, e.g. 0, while `aes->authsize` is 0 (defaulting to 16).
The `authsize` variable is computed. Since `aes->authsize` is 0, it defaults to `AES_BLOCK_SIZE` (16). This passes the switch validation.
Since the action is decrypt, `ilen = aes->src_len - authsize` is computed. With unsigned arithmetic, `0 - 16` wraps to `0xFFFFFFF0` (4294967280).
`scatterwalk_ffwd(sg_tag, p_inp, ilen)` is called with the wrapped huge `ilen` value, attempting to skip ~4GB into the scatterlist, causing out-of-bounds access.

If execution continues, `ccp_init_data(&src, cmd_q, p_inp, ilen, ...)` is called with the wrapped `ilen` value, attempting to initialize a data operation over ~4GB of data, causing further out-of-bounds memory operations.

commit_message: fixed #1659
cve_desc: An issue was discovered in GPAC version 0.8.0 and 1.0.1. There is an invalid pointer dereference in the function SetupWriters() in isomedia/isom_store.c.

vulnerabilities.description: When allocation of writer->stsc or writer->stco fails, the function returns GF_OUT_OF_MEM directly without freeing the already-allocated `writer` object (and in the stco case, also leaking the already-allocated `writer->stsc`). The `writer` has not yet been added to the `writers` list, so it cannot be cleaned up by a subsequent call to CleanWriters. Additionally, writers already added to the list from previous loop iterations are also leaked since CleanWriters is not called (unlike the `goto exit` path for writer allocation failure).

step_by_step_execution.description: The function enters the for loop and allocates a TrackWriter via GF_SAFEALLOC.

state.value: valid pointer to newly allocated TrackWriter

step_by_step_execution.description: Writer fields are populated from trak->Media.
writer->stsc is allocated via gf_isom_box_new(GF_ISOM_BOX_TYPE_STSC). Suppose this succeeds.
writer->stco is allocated via gf_isom_box_new. Suppose this fails and returns NULL.

The check `if (!writer->stco) return GF_OUT_OF_MEM;` triggers, returning immediately. The `writer` object and `writer->stsc` are never freed. The writer was never added to the `writers` list, so CleanWriters cannot reach it. Any writers added in previous iterations are also not cleaned.

state.value: leaked - not freed, not in writers list

commit_message:
Add more validation to `RequantizationRangePerChannel`.

PiperOrigin-RevId: 387693946
Change-Id: Ife8dcbdb021bec4787eef6a4361dd08f17c14bd6
cve_desc: TensorFlow is an end-to-end open source platform for machine learning. In affected versions due to incomplete validation in MKL implementation of requantization, an attacker can trigger undefined behavior via binding a reference to a null pointer or can access data outside the bounds of heap allocated arrays. The [implementation](https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorflow/blob/460e000de3a83278fb00b61a16d161b1964f15f4/tensorflow/core/kernels/mkl/mkl_requantization_range_per_channel_op.cc) does not validate the dimensions of the `input` tensor. A similar issue occurs in `MklRequantizePerChannelOp`. The [implementation](https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorflow/blob/460e000de3a83278fb00b61a16d161b1964f15f4/tensorflow/core/kernels/mkl/mkl_requantize_per_channel_op.cc) does not perform full validation for all the input arguments. We have patched the issue in GitHub commit 9e62869465573cb2d9b5053f1fa02a81fce21d69 and in the Github commit 203214568f5bc237603dbab6e1fd389f1572f5c9. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.6.0. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.5.1, TensorFlow 2.4.3, and TensorFlow 2.3.4, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

vulnerabilities.description: The variable 'depth' is derived from input_max.NumElements(), but there is no validation that it matches the inner dimension of the 'input' tensor. When the loop iterates up to 'depth', transposed_input.chip<0>(i) accesses the i-th slice along what was originally the inner dimension of 'input'. If an attacker provides an input_max tensor with more elements than the input tensor's inner dimension, the chip operation will read out of bounds.

step_by_step_execution.description: The input tensor, input_min tensor, and input_max tensor are read from the OpKernelContext.
depth is set to input_max.NumElements().
OP_REQUIRES checks that input_min.dim_size(0) == depth (10 == 10, passes) and input_max.dim_size(0) == depth (10 == 10, passes). No check relates depth to the input tensor's dimensions.
input_matrix = input.flat_inner_dims<qint32>() creates a 2D view with shape [outer, 3] where 3 is the inner dimension.
transposed_input = input_matrix.shuffle({1, 0}) transposes to shape [3, 4].

The loop iterates for i = 0 to depth-1 (0 to 9). When i >= 3 (the first dimension of transposed_input), transposed_input.chip<0>(i) accesses out-of-bounds memory, reading data beyond the allocated tensor buffer.

commit_message:
The code now checks for consistency of the MCU sizes across
hierarchical levels, and fails in case they are different.
cve_desc: libjpeg 1.63 has a heap-based buffer over-read in HierarchicalBitmapRequester::FetchRegion in hierarchicalbitmaprequester.cpp because the MCU size can be different between allocation and use.

commit_message:
Disallow division by zero FPE in `tf.raw_ops.ResourceScatterDiv`

Had to update a test that was broken.

PiperOrigin-RevId: 388516976
Change-Id: Ic358e6bf0559e011539974d453fc7aa18b427e9c
cve_desc: TensorFlow is an end-to-end open source platform for machine learning. In affected versions the implementation of `tf.raw_ops.ResourceScatterDiv` is vulnerable to a division by 0 error. The [implementation](https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorflow/blob/8d72537c6abf5a44103b57b9c2e22c14f5f49698/tensorflow/core/kernels/resource_variable_ops.cc#L865) uses a common class for all binary operations but fails to treat the division by 0 case separately. We have patched the issue in GitHub commit 4aacb30888638da75023e6601149415b39763d76. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.6.0. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.5.1, TensorFlow 2.4.3, and TensorFlow 2.3.4, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

commit_message: fixed #1782 (fuzz)
cve_desc: Memory leak in the afra_box_read function in MP4Box in GPAC 1.0.1 allows attackers to read memory via a crafted file.

commit_message:
Validate and require subkey binding signatures on PGP public keys

All subkeys must be followed by a binding signature by the primary key
as per the OpenPGP RFC, enforce the presence and validity in the parser.

The implementation is as kludgey as they come to work around our
simple-minded parser structure without touching API, to maximise
backportability. Store all the raw packets internally as we decode them
to be able to access previous elements at will, needed to validate ordering
and access the actual data. Add testcases for manipulated keys whose
import previously would succeed.

Depends on the two previous commits:
7b399fcb8f52566e6f3b4327197a85facd08db91 and
236b802a4aa48711823a191d1b7f753c82a89ec5

Fixes CVE-2021-3521.
cve_desc: There is a flaw in RPM's signature functionality. OpenPGP subkeys are associated with a primary key via a "binding signature." RPM does not check the binding signature of subkeys prior to importing them. If an attacker is able to add or socially engineer another party to add a malicious subkey to a legitimate public key, RPM could wrongly trust a malicious signature. The greatest impact of this flaw is to data integrity. To exploit this flaw, an attacker must either compromise an RPM repository or convince an administrator to install an untrusted RPM or public key. It is strongly recommended to only use RPMs and public keys from trusted sources.

vulnerabilities.description: If the loop breaks after `selfsig` is allocated (via `pgpDigParamsNew`) but before it is freed (via `pgpDigParamsFree`), the `selfsig` object is leaked. This can happen when `pgpPrtPkt` returns an error, or when the subkey binding signature type check fails, or when `pgpVerifySelf` fails — in all these `break` paths, `selfsig` has been allocated but is never freed before the function returns.

step_by_step_execution.description: The loop processes packets. At some iteration, `expect` was set to `PGPTAG_SIGNATURE` from a previous PGPTAG_PUBLIC_SUBKEY packet.
A new packet is decoded. Its tag matches `expect`, so `selfsig` is allocated via `pgpDigParamsNew`.
`pgpPrtPkt` is called with the newly allocated `selfsig`. It returns a non-zero error value.
The `break` statement is executed, exiting the while loop. `selfsig` is still allocated and holds a valid pointer.
After the loop, `all` is freed and `digp` is handled, but `selfsig` is never freed, causing a memory leak.

commit_message:
Fixed typo while calculating module path length.

The issue was introduced in 77c398f26d7e (not released yet).
cve_desc: Nginx NJS v0.7.3 was discovered to contain a stack overflow in the function njs_default_module_loader at /src/njs/src/njs_module.c. NOTE: multiple third parties dispute this report, e.g., the behavior is only found in unreleased development code that was not part of the 0.7.2, 0.7.3, or 0.7.4 release

vulnerabilities.description: When `dir` is not NULL, the `length` variable is set to `dir->length` (plus an optional trailing slash), but `info->name.length` is NOT added. The bounds check `length > NJS_MAX_PATH` therefore only validates the directory portion. The subsequent copy of `info->name` data into `src` can overflow the stack buffer `src[NJS_MAX_PATH + 1]` because the total bytes written is `dir->length + trail + info->name.length`, which can exceed `NJS_MAX_PATH + 1`.

step_by_step_execution.description: Function is called with a non-NULL `dir` (e.g., length 10, ending in '/') and `info->name.length` set to NJS_MAX_PATH.
`length` is initially set to `info->name.length` (NJS_MAX_PATH), then overwritten to `dir->length` (10) because `dir != NULL`. `trail` is 0 since dir ends with '/'.
Bounds check: `length > NJS_MAX_PATH` evaluates to `10 > 4096`, which is false, so execution continues.
Directory bytes are copied into `src`: `njs_cpymem(p, dir->start, 10)`. Pointer `p` now points to `src[10]`.

`info->name` (NJS_MAX_PATH bytes) is copied into `src` starting at offset 10: `njs_cpymem(p, info->name.start, NJS_MAX_PATH)`. This writes up to `src[10 + 4096 - 1]`, well beyond the `src[NJS_MAX_PATH + 1]` buffer boundary.

commit_message:
Secure tf.raw_ops.QuantizeV2

Validate size and shape of min_range and max_range
Ensure axis is within input dims limits

PiperOrigin-RevId: 387232799
Change-Id: I36975281f7b5758e9e31a8dcc73fe610ef456318
cve_desc: TensorFlow is an end-to-end open source platform for machine learning. In affected versions due to incomplete validation in `tf.raw_ops.QuantizeV2`, an attacker can trigger undefined behavior via binding a reference to a null pointer or can access data outside the bounds of heap allocated arrays. The [implementation](https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorflow/blob/84d053187cb80d975ef2b9684d4b61981bca0c41/tensorflow/core/kernels/quantize_op.cc#L59) has some validation but does not check that `min_range` and `max_range` both have the same non-zero number of elements. If `axis` is provided (i.e., not `-1`), then validation should check that it is a value in range for the rank of `input` tensor and then the lengths of `min_range` and `max_range` inputs match the `axis` dimension of the `input` tensor. We have patched the issue in GitHub commit 6da6620efad397c85493b8f8667b821403516708. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.6.0. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.5.1, TensorFlow 2.4.3, and TensorFlow 2.3.4, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

vulnerabilities.description: Out-of-bounds read on input_min_range and input_max_range tensors. The loop iterates `num_slices` times (derived from `input.dim_size(axis_)`), but there is no validation that the min/max range tensors actually contain `num_slices` elements. An attacker can provide min/max range tensors with fewer elements than `num_slices`, causing `min_ranges(i)` and `max_ranges(i)` to read out of bounds.

step_by_step_execution.description: axis_ is set to a valid axis (e.g., 1) during construction. The input tensor has a dimension size > 1 along that axis.
num_slices is computed from input.dim_size(axis_), e.g. 10.
input_min_range and input_max_range are attacker-controlled tensors, e.g. with only 1 element each.
Since num_slices != 1, the code falls through to the per-slice quantization path. min_ranges and max_ranges are obtained via vec<float>(), mapping to the small input tensors.
The loop iterates with i from 0 to 9. At i=1, min_ranges(1) and max_ranges(1) access beyond the single-element buffer, causing an out-of-bounds read.

vulnerabilities.description: No validation that axis_ is less than the number of dimensions of the input tensor. If axis_ >= input.dims(), then input.dim_size(axis_) performs an out-of-bounds access on the tensor's shape array.

step_by_step_execution.description: axis_ is set to a value >= 0 during construction, e.g. 5.
The input tensor is provided with fewer dimensions than axis_, e.g. a 2-D tensor.
The check axis_ > -1 passes since axis_ is 5. input.dim_size(5) is called on a 2-D tensor, reading out of bounds from the shape array.

commit_message: fixed #1659
cve_desc: An issue was discovered in GPAC version 0.8.0 and 1.0.1. There is an invalid pointer dereference in the function SetupWriters() in isomedia/isom_store.c.

vulnerabilities.description: When `gf_isom_box_new` fails for `writer->stsc` or `writer->stco`, the function returns `GF_OUT_OF_MEM` directly instead of jumping to the `exit` label. This leaks the currently allocated `writer` (which has not yet been added to the `writers` list) and also leaks all previously added `TrackWriter` objects in `writers` because `CleanWriters` is never called on these paths.

step_by_step_execution.description: The function begins iterating over tracks. In the first iteration (i=0), a TrackWriter is successfully allocated and added to the `writers` list.
In the second iteration (i=1), a new TrackWriter `writer` is allocated via GF_SAFEALLOC and its fields are populated.

state.value: newly heap-allocated TrackWriter, not yet in writers list

step_by_step_execution.description: `writer->stsc` is allocated via `gf_isom_box_new(GF_ISOM_BOX_TYPE_STSC)`. Suppose this succeeds.
`writer->stco` allocation via `gf_isom_box_new` fails and returns NULL.

The code executes `return GF_OUT_OF_MEM` directly, bypassing the `exit` label. The current `writer` (with its allocated `stsc`) is never freed, and `CleanWriters(writers)` is never called, so the TrackWriter added in iteration 0 is also leaked.

state.value: leaked heap allocation (not in writers list, never freed)
contains TrackWriter from iteration 0, never cleaned up

commit_message:
Fixed frame allocation from an awaited frame.

njs_function_frame_save() is used to save the awaited frame when "await"
instruction is encountered. The saving was done as a memcpy() of
existing runtime frame.

njs_function_frame_alloc() is used to alloc a new function frame, this
function tries to use a spare preallocated memory from the previous
frame first.  Previously, this function might result in "use-after-free"
when invoked from a restored frame saved with njs_function_frame_save().
Because njs_function_frame_save() left pointers to the spare memory of
the original frame which may be already free when saved frame is
restored.

The fix is to erase fields for the spare memory from the saved frame.

This closes #469 issue on Github.
cve_desc: nginx njs 0.7.2 is affected suffers from Use-after-free in njs_function_frame_alloc() when it try to invoke from a restored frame saved with njs_function_frame_save().

commit_message: Skip MNG CLIP chunk with out-of-range object IDs
cve_desc: In ImageMagick before 6.9.9-0 and 7.x before 7.0.6-1, the ReadOneMNGImage function in coders/png.c has an out-of-bounds read with the MNG CLIP chunk.

vulnerabilities.description: In the mng_LOOP chunk handler, `loop_level` is read directly from user-controlled chunk data (`chunk[0]`, range 0-255) without any bounds checking against the size of the `loop_active`, `loop_jump`, `loop_count`, and `loop_iteration` arrays in `MngInfo`. If these arrays are smaller than 256 entries, an attacker can craft a MNG file with a large loop_level value to write out-of-bounds.

step_by_step_execution.description: The image format is MNG, so the MNG chunk processing path is entered.
A LOOP chunk is read from the file with length > 4.
loop_level is set from chunk[0] which is attacker-controlled (0-255).
mng_info->loop_active[loop_level] is written to with value 1, which is an out-of-bounds write if loop_level exceeds the array size.
Additional out-of-bounds writes occur on loop_jump, loop_count, and loop_iteration arrays at the same unchecked index.

vulnerabilities.description: In the mng_ENDL chunk handler, `loop_level` is read from user-controlled `chunk[0]` without bounds checking, leading to out-of-bounds read/write on `mng_info->loop_active[]`, `mng_info->loop_count[]`, and `mng_info->loop_iteration[]` arrays.

step_by_step_execution.description: The image format is MNG, entering MNG chunk processing.
An ENDL chunk is read with length > 0.
loop_level is set directly from chunk[0], attacker-controlled byte.
mng_info->loop_active[loop_level] is read and written without bounds validation, causing out-of-bounds access.

vulnerabilities.description: In the mng_CLIP chunk handler, `first_object` and `last_object` are read from user-controlled data and can be up to 65535. The loop iterates from `first_object` to `last_object` accessing `mng_info->exists[i]` and `mng_info->frozen[i]` without checking if `i` is within the bounds of MNG_MAX_OBJECTS.

step_by_step_execution.description: MNG chunk processing reads a CLIP chunk with length > 3.
first_object and last_object are read from attacker-controlled bytes.
The loop iterates with i from first_object to last_object, accessing mng_info->exists[i] far beyond MNG_MAX_OBJECTS.

commit_message:
Fixed frame allocation from an awaited frame.

njs_function_frame_save() is used to save the awaited frame when "await"
instruction is encountered. The saving was done as a memcpy() of
existing runtime frame.

njs_function_frame_alloc() is used to alloc a new function frame, this
function tries to use a spare preallocated memory from the previous
frame first.  Previously, this function might result in "use-after-free"
when invoked from a restored frame saved with njs_function_frame_save().
Because njs_function_frame_save() left pointers to the spare memory of
the original frame which may be already free when saved frame is
restored.

The fix is to erase fields for the spare memory from the saved frame.

This closes #469 issue on Github.
cve_desc: nginx njs 0.7.2 is affected suffers from Use-after-free in njs_function_frame_alloc() when it try to invoke from a restored frame saved with njs_function_frame_save().

commit_message:
Ensure non-empty rt_nested_splits in tf.raw_ops.RaggedTensorToVariant

PiperOrigin-RevId: 387664237
Change-Id: Ia1700c34b5610873d63561abc86e23b46ead93b3
cve_desc: TensorFlow is an end-to-end open source platform for machine learning. In affected versions an attacker can cause undefined behavior via binding a reference to null pointer in `tf.raw_ops.RaggedTensorToVariant`. The [implementation](https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorflow/blob/460e000de3a83278fb00b61a16d161b1964f15f4/tensorflow/core/kernels/ragged_tensor_to_variant_op.cc#L129) has an incomplete validation of the splits values, missing the case when the argument would be empty. We have patched the issue in GitHub commit be7a4de6adfbd303ce08be4332554dff70362612. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.6.0. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.5.1, TensorFlow 2.4.3, and TensorFlow 2.3.4, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

commit_message:
Prevent unitialized variable use in grappler.

PiperOrigin-RevId: 399702928
Change-Id: Id7e75451fbff297692dfb687f60ea04b25c96b24
cve_desc: TensorFlow is an open source platform for machine learning. In affected versions TensorFlow's Grappler optimizer has a use of unitialized variable. If the `train_nodes` vector (obtained from the saved model that gets optimized) does not contain a `Dequeue` node, then `dequeue_node` is left unitialized. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.7.0. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.6.1, TensorFlow 2.5.2, and TensorFlow 2.4.4, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

commit_message:
Make Transpose's shape inference function validate that negative `perm` values are within the tensor's rank.

PiperOrigin-RevId: 403252853
Change-Id: Ia6b31b45b237312668bb31c2c3b3c7bbce2d2610
cve_desc: TensorFlow is an open source platform for machine learning. In affected versions the shape inference function for `Transpose` is vulnerable to a heap buffer overflow. This occurs whenever `perm` contains negative elements. The shape inference function does not validate that the indices in `perm` are all valid. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.7.0. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.6.1, TensorFlow 2.5.2, and TensorFlow 2.4.4, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

commit_message:
Prevent heap OOB read in TFLite's `gather_nd.cc`.

Passing negative indices is illegal but there was a missing check so that resulted in OOB accesses.

PiperOrigin-RevId: 387208551
Change-Id: I6b7a8a62d3e7c13a16d81619e5bc23ae2cdbc7fd
cve_desc: TensorFlow is an end-to-end open source platform for machine learning. In affected versions TFLite's [`GatherNd` implementation](https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorflow/blob/149562d49faa709ea80df1d99fc41d005b81082a/tensorflow/lite/kernels/gather_nd.cc#L124) does not support negative indices but there are no checks for this situation. Hence, an attacker can read arbitrary data from the heap by carefully crafting a model with negative values in `indices`. Similar issue exists in [`Gather` implementation](https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorflow/blob/149562d49faa709ea80df1d99fc41d005b81082a/tensorflow/lite/kernels/gather.cc). We have patched the issue in GitHub commits bb6a0383ed553c286f87ca88c207f6774d5c4a8f and eb921122119a6b6e470ee98b89e65d721663179d. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.6.0. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.5.1, TensorFlow 2.4.3, and TensorFlow 2.3.4, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

commit_message:
Avoid buffer overflow when loading tensors with insufficient data from checkpoints.

`CopyDataFromTensorSliceToTensorSlice` does not (and cannot conveniently)
provide any bounds checking on its own, so the size is instead checked prior
to passing unvalidated data to that function.

PiperOrigin-RevId: 392971286
Change-Id: If2073b36d4d5eedd386329f56729395fd7effee1
cve_desc: TensorFlow is an open source platform for machine learning. In affected versions an attacker can trigger undefined behavior, integer overflows, segfaults and `CHECK`-fail crashes if they can change saved checkpoints from outside of TensorFlow. This is because the checkpoints loading infrastructure is missing validation for invalid file formats. The fixes will be included in TensorFlow 2.7.0. We will also cherrypick these commits on TensorFlow 2.6.1, TensorFlow 2.5.2, and TensorFlow 2.4.4, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

commit_message:
Avoid buffer overflow when loading tensors with insufficient data from checkpoints.

`CopyDataFromTensorSliceToTensorSlice` does not (and cannot conveniently)
provide any bounds checking on its own, so the size is instead checked prior
to passing unvalidated data to that function.

PiperOrigin-RevId: 392971286

In affected versions an attacker can trigger undefined behavior, integer overflows, segfaults and `CHECK`-fail crashes if they can change saved checkpoints from outside of TensorFlow. commit_message:
Fixed typo while calculating module path length.

The issue was introduced in 77c398f26d7e (not released yet).
cve_desc: Nginx NJS v0.7.3 was discovered to contain a stack overflow in the function njs_default_module_loader at /src/njs/src/njs_module.c. NOTE: multiple third parties dispute this report, e.g., the behavior is only found in unreleased development code that was not part of the 0.7.2, 0.7.3, or 0.7.4 release

vulnerabilities.description: When `dir` is not NULL but `dir->length` is 0 and `info->name.length` is greater than 0, the combined `length` is non-zero so the `length == 0` early return is not taken. The expression `dir->length - 1` on an unsigned `size_t` wraps around to `SIZE_MAX`, causing an out-of-bounds read at `dir->start[SIZE_MAX]`.

step_by_step_execution.description: Function is called with `dir != NULL`, `dir->length == 0`, and `info->name.length > 0` (e.g., 5).
`length` is initialized to `info->name.length`.
Since `dir != NULL`, enter the if-block. `length += dir->length` keeps length at 5.
The check `length == 0` is false, so execution continues.
`dir->length - 1` is computed. Since `dir->length` is `size_t` (unsigned) with value 0, the subtraction wraps to `SIZE_MAX`. `dir->start[SIZE_MAX]` is accessed, reading far out of bounds.

commit_message:
Reorganize and add more validation to MKL requantization

PiperOrigin-RevId: 387901341
Change-Id: I2515b9034c64e113db0bcec8337d30643ab0a0f1
cve_desc: TensorFlow is an end-to-end open source platform for machine learning. In affected versions due to incomplete validation in MKL implementation of requantization, an attacker can trigger undefined behavior via binding a reference to a null pointer or can access data outside the bounds of heap allocated arrays. The [implementation](https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorflow/blob/460e000de3a83278fb00b61a16d161b1964f15f4/tensorflow/core/kernels/mkl/mkl_requantization_range_per_channel_op.cc) does not validate the dimensions of the `input` tensor. A similar issue occurs in `MklRequantizePerChannelOp`. The [implementation](https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorflow/blob/460e000de3a83278fb00b61a16d161b1964f15f4/tensorflow/core/kernels/mkl/mkl_requantize_per_channel_op.cc) does not perform full validation for all the input arguments. We have patched the issue in GitHub commit 9e62869465573cb2d9b5053f1fa02a81fce21d69 and in the Github commit 203214568f5bc237603dbab6e1fd389f1572f5c9. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.6.0. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.5.1, TensorFlow 2.4.3, and TensorFlow 2.3.4, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

vulnerabilities.description: The validation check for `input_requested_max` mistakenly checks `input_requested_min.NumElements() == 1` instead of `input_requested_max.NumElements() == 1`. This means `input_requested_max` is never validated to be a scalar. If an attacker provides an empty tensor for `input_requested_max`, the check passes (since `input_requested_min` was already validated), but accessing `input_requested_max.flat<float>()(0)` results in an out-of-bounds read.

step_by_step_execution.description: The input tensor is validated to be 4D.
input_min_vec and input_max_vec are read and their sizes are validated against each other.
input_requested_min is read and validated to have exactly 1 element. The check passes.

input_requested_max is read from the context. The OP_REQUIRES check erroneously validates input_requested_min.NumElements() == 1 again, instead of input_requested_max.NumElements() == 1. Since input_requested_min already passed this check, this always passes regardless of input_requested_max's shape.
input_requested_max.flat<float>()(0) is accessed, reading element at index 0 of an empty tensor, causing an out-of-bounds read.

state.value: out-of-bounds read from empty tensor buffer

commit_message:
Prevent heap OOB read in TFLite's `gather.cc`.

Passing negative indices is illegal but there was a missing check so that resulted in OOB accesses.

PiperOrigin-RevId: 387231300
Change-Id: I3111b54b2f232638d795be17efc46abe4ede6bf8
cve_desc: TensorFlow is an end-to-end open source platform for machine learning. In affected versions TFLite's [`GatherNd` implementation](https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorflow/blob/149562d49faa709ea80df1d99fc41d005b81082a/tensorflow/lite/kernels/gather_nd.cc#L124) does not support negative indices but there are no checks for this situation. Hence, an attacker can read arbitrary data from the heap by carefully crafting a model with negative values in `indices`. Similar issue exists in [`Gather` implementation](https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorflow/blob/149562d49faa709ea80df1d99fc41d005b81082a/tensorflow/lite/kernels/gather.cc). We have patched the issue in GitHub commits bb6a0383ed553c286f87ca88c207f6774d5c4a8f and eb921122119a6b6e470ee98b89e65d721663179d. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.6.0. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.5.1, TensorFlow 2.4.3, and TensorFlow 2.3.4, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

commit_message:
Disallow division by zero FPE in `tf.raw_ops.ResourceScatterDiv`

Had to update a test that was broken.

PiperOrigin-RevId: 388516976
Change-Id: Ic358e6bf0559e011539974d453fc7aa18b427e9c
cve_desc: TensorFlow is an end-to-end open source platform for machine learning. In affected versions the implementation of `tf.raw_ops.ResourceScatterDiv` is vulnerable to a division by 0 error. The [implementation](https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorflow/blob/8d72537c6abf5a44103b57b9c2e22c14f5f49698/tensorflow/core/kernels/resource_variable_ops.cc#L865) uses a common class for all binary operations but fails to treat the division by 0 case separately. We have patched the issue in GitHub commit 4aacb30888638da75023e6601149415b39763d76. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.6.0. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.5.1, TensorFlow 2.4.3, and TensorFlow 2.3.4, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

vulnerabilities.description: If the 'params' variable/resource tensor is a scalar (0 dimensions), accesses to params->dim_size(0) and params->flat_outer_dims<T>() are performed without first verifying that params has at least 1 dimension. The rank check allows updates.dims() == 0 unconditionally, which means a scalar params (0 dims) can pass validation. Calling dim_size(0) on a 0-dimensional tensor is an out-of-bounds read on the internal shape array.

step_by_step_execution.description: The function looks up the resource variable and obtains its tensor. An attacker provides a resource variable containing a scalar (0-dimensional) tensor.
The function reads the indices input (e.g. shape [1], containing one index) and updates input (a scalar tensor).
The rank check evaluates: updates.dims() == 0, which is true. The OP_REQUIRES passes unconditionally regardless of params->dims().
N_big = indices.NumElements() = 1, which passes the index-space check. N is set to 1.

The code calls params->dim_size(0) to check it fits in Index type. Since params is 0-dimensional, this accesses the shape array out of bounds (there is no dimension 0), resulting in an out-of-bounds read.

If execution continues, params->flat_outer_dims<T>() is called on a 0-dimensional tensor, which is also undefined behavior, and params->dim_size(0) is used again in the error-reporting path after the scatter functor.

commit_message:
patch 8.2.4219: reading before the start of the line

Problem:    Reading before the start of the line.
Solution:   Check boundary before trying to read the character.
cve_desc: Heap-based Buffer Overflow in GitHub repository vim/vim prior to 8.2.

vulnerabilities.description: Integer overflow in the size calculation passed to alloc(). If bd->startspaces, bd->endspaces, and bd->textlen are sufficiently large int values, their sum can overflow, resulting in a much smaller allocation than needed. Subsequent vim_memset and mch_memmove calls then write beyond the allocated buffer.

step_by_step_execution.description: The function receives a block_def where bd->startspaces, bd->endspaces, and bd->textlen are large int values (e.g., startspaces=0x40000000, textlen=0x40000001, endspaces=0).

The allocation size is computed as bd->startspaces + bd->endspaces + bd->textlen + 1. This integer arithmetic overflows: 0x40000000 + 0 + 0x40000001 + 1 = 0x80000002, which as a signed 32-bit int is negative (~-2147483646). When cast to size_t for alloc, on some platforms/configurations this may wrap or be treated as a small value.

state.value: pointer to a small or incorrectly-sized buffer

step_by_step_execution.description: vim_memset writes bd->startspaces (0x40000000) bytes of spaces into the undersized buffer, causing a massive heap buffer overflow.
mch_memmove then copies bd->textlen (0x40000001) bytes from bd->textstart into the buffer, further overflowing the heap.

commit_message:
Prevent unitialized variable use in grappler.

PiperOrigin-RevId: 399702928
Change-Id: Id7e75451fbff297692dfb687f60ea04b25c96b24
cve_desc: TensorFlow is an open source platform for machine learning. In affected versions TensorFlow's Grappler optimizer has a use of unitialized variable. If the `train_nodes` vector (obtained from the saved model that gets optimized) does not contain a `Dequeue` node, then `dequeue_node` is left unitialized. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.7.0. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.6.1, TensorFlow 2.5.2, and TensorFlow 2.4.4, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

vulnerabilities.description: The pointer `dequeue_node` is declared without initialization. If the loop over `train_nodes` never finds a dequeue op (i.e., `IsDequeueOp` is never true), `dequeue_node` remains uninitialized. The subsequent `if (dequeue_node)` check reads this uninitialized pointer, which is undefined behavior. An indeterminate pointer value could evaluate to a non-null value, causing a dereference of a garbage pointer in `dequeue_node->name()`.

step_by_step_execution.description: The function begins. `train_nodes` is populated by `ComputeTransitiveFanin`.

state.value: a vector of NodeDef pointers from the graph

step_by_step_execution.description: `dequeue_node` is declared as a local pointer variable without being initialized.
The loop iterates over `train_nodes`. If none of the nodes satisfy `IsDequeueOp`, the loop completes without assigning `dequeue_node`.
The code evaluates `if (dequeue_node)`. Reading the uninitialized pointer is undefined behavior. If the indeterminate value happens to be non-zero (likely on the stack), the branch is taken.
`dequeue_node->name()` is called, dereferencing the garbage pointer, leading to a read from an arbitrary/invalid memory address.

commit_message:
Validate that matrix dimension sizes in SparseMatMul are positive.

PiperOrigin-RevId: 401149683
Change-Id: Ib33eafc561a39c8741ece80b2edce6d4aae9a57d
cve_desc: TensorFlow is an open source platform for machine learning. In affected versions the code for sparse matrix multiplication is vulnerable to undefined behavior via binding a reference to `nullptr`. This occurs whenever the dimensions of `a` or `b` are 0 or less. In the case on one of these is 0, an empty output tensor should be allocated (to conserve the invariant that output tensors are always allocated when the operation is successful) but nothing should be written to it (that is, we should return early from the kernel implementation). Otherwise, attempts to write to this empty tensor would result in heap OOB access. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.7.0. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.6.1, TensorFlow 2.5.2, and TensorFlow 2.4.4, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

code_snippets: const int m = transpose_a_ ? a.dim_size(1) : a.dim_size(0);
const int k = transpose_a_ ? a.dim_size(0) : a.dim_size(1);
const int n = transpose_b_ ? b.dim_size(0) : b.dim_size(1);
const int k2 = transpose_b_ ? b.dim_size(1) : b.dim_size(0);
vulnerabilities.description: dim_size() returns int64 but the results are stored in int (32-bit) variables m, k, n, k2. If a tensor dimension exceeds INT_MAX (~2.1 billion), the value silently truncates or wraps, potentially resulting in a small or negative value for m or n. This corrupted value is then used to allocate the output tensor via TensorShape({m, n}), leading to an undersized output buffer. Subsequent matrix operations write to this undersized buffer, causing an out-of-bounds write. The k == k2 check can also be bypassed if both values overflow to the same truncated int.

step_by_step_execution.description: Tensors a and b are read from context. Suppose a has shape [1, 2^31+1] and b has shape [2^31+1, 1] (both valid matrices with int64 dimensions).
Matrix shape checks pass since both tensors are 2D matrices.

Dimension values are narrowed from int64 to int. With transpose_a_=false: m = (int)a.dim_size(0) = 1, k = (int)a.dim_size(1). The int64 value 2147483649 truncates to a negative or small int value when cast to 32-bit int.
Similarly for b with transpose_b_=false: n = (int)b.dim_size(1) = 1, k2 = (int)b.dim_size(0) which also truncates from 2147483649.
The check k == k2 passes because both were truncated from the same int64 value to the same int value.
Output tensor is allocated with TensorShape({m, n}) = TensorShape({1, 1}), a very small buffer.
The matrix contraction or DoMatMul operates on the original large tensors and writes results into the undersized output buffer, causing an out-of-bounds write.

commit_message:
Add a check for pad width to be a positive value.

PiperOrigin-RevId: 413275853
Change-Id: I261a8db9dabf5ce48a806a9e58129080c9fac619
cve_desc: Tensorflow is an Open Source Machine Learning Framework. The implementation of `StringNGrams` can be used to trigger a denial of service attack by causing an out of memory condition after an integer overflow. We are missing a validation on `pad_witdh` and that result in computing a negative value for `ngram_width` which is later used to allocate parts of the output. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.8.0. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.7.1, TensorFlow 2.6.3, and TensorFlow 2.5.3, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

vulnerabilities.description: Integer overflow in the accumulation of ngram counts can cause the total output size (ngrams_splits_data[num_batch_items]) to wrap to a small or negative value. This leads to under-allocation of the output tensor, while the subsequent loop writes ngram data at indices that exceed the allocated buffer, resulting in an out-of-bounds write. The overflow can occur in the local `int num_ngrams` variable (which accumulates across multiple ngram widths for a single batch), or in the SPLITS_TYPE accumulator across batches (especially if SPLITS_TYPE is int32). Additionally, `int length = splits_vec(i) - splits_vec(i-1)` truncates a potentially int64 difference to int, further contributing to incorrect size calculations.

step_by_step_execution.description: The input data tensor has a large number of string elements (e.g., close to INT_MAX), and splits define batch items with large lengths. SPLITS_TYPE is int32.
Splits validation passes because each split is a valid index and they are non-decreasing, with first=0 and last=input_data_size.
num_batch_items is computed from splits_vec.size() - 1. The ngrams_splits output tensor is allocated with splits->shape().
The early-return check for empty data/splits is not triggered since both are non-empty.

In the accumulation loop, for each batch item, `int num_ngrams` is computed. With large lengths and multiple ngram_widths, or across multiple batch items, the accumulated sum in ngrams_splits_data overflows int32, wrapping to a small positive value.

state.value: small positive value due to int32 overflow (e.g., 5)

step_by_step_execution.description: The output ngrams tensor is allocated with size ngrams_splits_data[num_batch_items], which is much smaller than the actual number of ngrams needed.
In the output loop, output_start_idx grows as ngrams are written. Since the allocated tensor is too small, writes to ngrams_data[output_start_idx] go out of bounds.

commit_message:
password: Use crypto_memneq() to compare hashes

Cryptographic verifications should be time-constant so that an attacker
cannot get information about the secrets used by observing the system,
so use crypto_memneq() rather than memcmp() to compare password hashes.

Signed-off-by: Sascha Hauer <s.hauer@pengutronix.de>
cve_desc: common/password.c in Pengutronix barebox through 2021.07.0 leaks timing information because strncmp is used during hash comparison.

commit_message:
Fix a null pointer exception in SVDF

This is due to not checking that `GetVariableInput` returns non-null tensor.

Also fix a potential null pointer exception in `GetVariableInput`.

PiperOrigin-RevId: 385160147
Change-Id: Iadf3f0705b036a9014d27caa5a8bbd91f4c4c401
cve_desc: TensorFlow is an end-to-end open source platform for machine learning. In affected versions the implementation of SVDF in TFLite is [vulnerable to a null pointer error](https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorflow/blob/460e000de3a83278fb00b61a16d161b1964f15f4/tensorflow/lite/kernels/svdf.cc#L300-L313). The [`GetVariableInput` function](https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorflow/blob/460e000de3a83278fb00b61a16d161b1964f15f4/tensorflow/lite/kernels/kernel_util.cc#L115-L119) can return a null pointer but `GetTensorData` assumes that the argument is always a valid tensor. Furthermore, because `GetVariableInput` calls [`GetMutableInput`](https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorflow/blob/460e000de3a83278fb00b61a16d161b1964f15f4/tensorflow/lite/kernels/kernel_util.cc#L82-L90) which might return `nullptr`, the `tensor->is_variable` expression can also trigger a null pointer exception. We have patched the issue in GitHub commit 5b048e87e4e55990dae6b547add4dae59f4e1c76. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.6.0. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.5.1, TensorFlow 2.4.3, and TensorFlow 2.3.4, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

commit_message:
Fix heap OOB in `tf.raw_ops.RaggedGather`

PiperOrigin-RevId: 388355464
Change-Id: If14d96231d1cd7aad7c4d1c22c1bab1576b75717
cve_desc: TensorFlow is an end-to-end open source platform for machine learning. In affected versions if the arguments to `tf.raw_ops.RaggedGather` don't determine a valid ragged tensor code can trigger a read from outside of bounds of heap allocated buffers. The [implementation](https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorflow/blob/8d72537c6abf5a44103b57b9c2e22c14f5f49698/tensorflow/core/kernels/ragged_gather_op.cc#L70) directly reads the first dimension of a tensor shape before checking that said tensor has rank of at least 1 (i.e., it is not a scalar).
