commit_message:
Fix tf.raw_ops.QuantizeAndDequantizeV4Grad vulnerability with invalid input_min or input_max.

Check that argument is actually a scalar before treating it as such.

PiperOrigin-RevId: 445198280
cve_desc: TensorFlow is an open source platform for machine learning. Prior to versions 2.9.0, 2.8.1, 2.7.2, and 2.6.4, the implementation of `tf.raw_ops.QuantizeAndDequantizeV4Grad` does not fully validate the input arguments. This results in a `CHECK`-failure which can be used to trigger a denial of service attack. Versions 2.9.0, 2.8.1, 2.7.2, and 2.6.4 contain a patch for this issue.

vulnerabilities.description: When axis_ is 0, the else branch is taken (since axis_ != -1), and flat_inner_outer_dims is called with axis_ - 1 = -1. The validation allows axis_ == 0, but flat_inner_outer_dims expects a non-negative index. Passing -1 leads to undefined behavior, potentially an out-of-bounds read/write or crash.

step_by_step_execution.description: The axis_ member variable is set to 0 (a valid value per the checks).
The check `axis_ >= -1` passes since 0 >= -1.
The check `axis_ == -1 || axis_ < input.shape().dims()` passes since 0 < dims (assuming at least 1 dimension).
The code reaches the else branch because axis_ != -1.
flat_inner_outer_dims<T, 3>(axis_ - 1) is called with argument -1, which is out of the valid range for this function, causing undefined behavior (out-of-bounds memory access).

commit_message:
Fixed Array.prototype.slice() with slow "this" argument.

Previously, when "this" argument was not a fast array, but the "deleted" array
was a fast array, the "deleted" array may be left in uninitialized state if
"this" argument had gaps.

This fix is to ensure that "deleted" is properly initialized.

This fixes #485 issue on Github.
cve_desc: Nginx NJS v0.7.2 was discovered to contain a segmentation violation in the function njs_value_own_enumerate at src/njs_value.c.

vulnerabilities.description: A Time-of-Check to Time-of-Use (TOCTOU) vulnerability exists. The object's length is captured once at the beginning, and `start` and `delete` are computed from this cached length. However, calls to `njs_value_to_integer()` on user-supplied arguments (args[1], args[2]) can invoke JavaScript valueOf/toString callbacks. These callbacks can modify the underlying array (e.g., shrinking it). After the callbacks execute, the stale `length` is used to compute `start` and `delete`, which may now exceed the actual array bounds. In the fast array path, `array->start[n]` is accessed directly without re-validating against the current `array->length`, leading to an out-of-bounds read. Similarly, in the second fast array block, `memcpy(&array->start[start], &args[3], items * sizeof(njs_value_t))` could write out of bounds if the array was shrunk.

step_by_step_execution.description: The function retrieves the length of the object/array via njs_object_length.

njs_value_to_integer is called on args[1] to compute `start`. If args[1] is an object with a valueOf method, that callback is invoked. The callback shrinks the array (e.g., by setting array.length = 2), reducing array->length.
Since nargs == 2, delete is computed as `length - start` using the stale length.
njs_array_alloc allocates the deleted array with size 10.

The fast array path is entered. The loop iterates from n=start(0) to n=start+delete-1(9), reading array->start[n]. Since array->length is now 2, accesses to array->start[2] through array->start[9] are out-of-bounds reads.

commit_message:
Fix heap OOB in `tf.raw_ops.RaggedGather`

PiperOrigin-RevId: 388355464
Change-Id: If14d96231d1cd7aad7c4d1c22c1bab1576b75717
cve_desc: TensorFlow is an end-to-end open source platform for machine learning. In affected versions if the arguments to `tf.raw_ops.RaggedGather` don't determine a valid ragged tensor code can trigger a read from outside of bounds of heap allocated buffers. The [implementation](https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorflow/blob/8d72537c6abf5a44103b57b9c2e22c14f5f49698/tensorflow/core/kernels/ragged_gather_op.cc#L70) directly reads the first dimension of a tensor shape before checking that said tensor has rank of at least 1 (i.e., it is not a scalar). Furthermore, the implementation does not check that the list given by `params_nested_splits` is not an empty list of tensors. We have patched the issue in GitHub commit a2b743f6017d7b97af1fe49087ae15f0ac634373. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.6.0. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.5.1, TensorFlow 2.4.3, and TensorFlow 2.3.4, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

commit_message:
Make Transpose's shape inference function validate that negative `perm` values are within the tensor's rank.

PiperOrigin-RevId: 403252853
Change-Id: Ia6b31b45b237312668bb31c2c3b3c7bbce2d2610
cve_desc: TensorFlow is an open source platform for machine learning. In affected versions the shape inference function for `Transpose` is vulnerable to a heap buffer overflow. This occurs whenever `perm` contains negative elements. The shape inference function does not validate that the indices in `perm` are all valid. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.7.0. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.6.1, TensorFlow 2.5.2, and TensorFlow 2.4.4, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

vulnerabilities.description: The validation of `in_idx` only checks for values >= rank but does not check for negative values. Since `in_idx` is `int64_t` and sourced from user-controlled tensor data, a negative value passes the bounds check and is then used as an index in `c->Dim(input, in_idx)`, leading to an out-of-bounds read.

step_by_step_execution.description: The function reads the input shape and the permutation tensor from the inference context.
rank is determined. Suppose input rank is known and equals 3.
The permutation tensor data is extracted via AsInt64. Suppose the user supplies perm = [-5, 1, 2].
In the loop iteration i=0, in_idx = data[0] = -5. The check `in_idx >= rank` evaluates to `-5 >= 3` which is false, so the check is bypassed.
c->Dim(input, in_idx) is called with in_idx = -5, which is an out-of-bounds negative index, causing an out-of-bounds memory read.

commit_message:
Fix EinsumHelper::ParseEquation to avoid uninitialized accesses.

EinsumHelper::ParseEquation is supposed to return true or false in
input_has_ellipsis and output_has_ellipsis to indicate whether there is
ellipsis in the inputs and output. Previously, when there is no ellipsis in the
inputs or output, the routine doesn't assign false to the variables. This
change initializes the two variables with false to fix the problem.
PiperOrigin-RevId: 391772004
Change-Id: I17b6c88aadef4131470378e48cced054bf252e86
cve_desc: TensorFlow is an open source platform for machine learning. In affeced versions during execution, `EinsumHelper::ParseEquation()` is supposed to set the flags in `input_has_ellipsis` vector and `*output_has_ellipsis` boolean to indicate whether there is ellipsis in the corresponding inputs and output. However, the code only changes these flags to `true` and never assigns `false`. This results in unitialized variable access if callers assume that `EinsumHelper::ParseEquation()` always sets these flags. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.7.0. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.6.1, TensorFlow 2.5.2, and TensorFlow 2.4.4, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

vulnerabilities.description: When num_inputs is 0, the short-circuit evaluation of `num_inputs == 1` is false, causing an out-of-bounds read on `(*input_label_counts)[0]` (which has been resized to 0 elements). When num_inputs is >= 3, only inputs 0 and 1 are checked, but this is a logic issue. The critical case is num_inputs == 0 leading to an out-of-bounds access.

step_by_step_execution.description: ParseEinsumEquation parses the equation and populates input_str. If the equation has no inputs (e.g., just '->abc' or some edge case), input_str could be empty.
num_inputs is set to the size of input_str.
input_labels is resized to 0, and the for loop for mapping input labels does not execute. However, MapToLabels on the output_str can still populate label_mapping with labels.
input_label_counts is resized to num_inputs (0 elements). The input counting loop does not execute.
output_label_counts is resized to num_labels (>0). Output labels are counted normally.

label_types is resized to num_labels and the for loop iterates over labels. For a non-ellipsis label, `num_inputs == 1` evaluates to false, so the next operand `(*input_label_counts)[0][label]` is evaluated, reading from index 0 of a vector with 0 elements.

commit_message: Fix fido2 signature validation bug
cve_desc: scheme/webauthn.c in Glewlwyd SSO server through 2.5.3 has a buffer overflow during FIDO2 signature validation in webauthn registration.

code_snippets: unsigned char data_signed[200], client_data_hash[32], cert_export[32], cert_export_b64[64];
vulnerabilities.description: The function copies multiple caller-controlled buffers (rpid_hash, client_data_hash, credential_id, cert_x, cert_y) into the fixed 200-byte stack buffer 'data_signed' without checking that the total length (1 + rpid_hash_len + 32 + credential_id_len + 1 + cert_x_len + cert_y_len) fits within 200 bytes. A large credential_id (up to 255 bytes in FIDO2) alone can push the total well over 200 bytes, causing a stack buffer overflow.

step_by_step_execution.description: The stack buffer data_signed is declared with a fixed size of 200 bytes.
data_signed[0] is set to 0x0, offset becomes 1.
rpid_hash is copied into data_signed. Assuming typical rpid_hash_len=32.
client_data_hash (32 bytes) is copied into data_signed.
credential_id is copied into data_signed. credential_id_len is attacker-controlled and can be up to 255 bytes (or more). Suppose credential_id_len=200.
The memcpy writes 200 bytes starting at offset 65 in a 200-byte buffer, overflowing by 65 bytes past the end of data_signed, corrupting the stack.

commit_message: Fix handling of APNG with 0 delay_den (#313)
cve_desc: libjxl v0.3.7 is affected by a Divide By Zero in issue in lib/extras/codec_apng.cc jxl::DecodeImageAPNG(). When encoding a malicous APNG file using cjxl, an attacker can trigger a denial of service.

vulnerabilities.description: Integer overflow in the computation of `imagesize`. Both `rowbytes` and `imagesize` are `unsigned int` (32-bit). If `w` and `h` are large (but still within `cMaxPNGSize`), the multiplication `h * w * 4` can wrap around, resulting in a much smaller allocation than expected. Subsequent writes into `frameRaw.p` via the PNG decoding callback will overflow the heap buffer.

step_by_step_execution.description: The function reads the IHDR chunk and extracts width `w` and height `h` as unsigned 32-bit integers.
The dimensions are checked against cMaxPNGSize. Assuming cMaxPNGSize is large enough (e.g., >= 65536), the check passes.

`rowbytes = w * 4` is computed as unsigned int. For w=65536, rowbytes = 262144, which fits. Then `imagesize = h * rowbytes = 65536 * 262144 = 17179869184`, which overflows a 32-bit unsigned int and wraps to 0.
`frameRaw.p = new unsigned char[imagesize]` allocates a buffer of size 0 (or implementation-defined minimal allocation).
The loop `frameRaw.rows[j] = frameRaw.p + j * rowbytes` sets row pointers that point far beyond the allocated buffer.
When PNG image data is decoded via processing_data/processing_finish, the decoded pixel data is written through frameRaw.rows pointers, causing a massive heap buffer overflow.

vulnerabilities.description: Division by zero if `delay_den` is 0. The value is read directly from the fcTL chunk data without any check for zero before being used as a divisor.

step_by_step_execution.description: A fcTL chunk is encountered, and `delay_den` is read from the chunk data.
After processing_finish succeeds, the code computes `bundle.duration = delay_num * 1000 / delay_den`, dividing by zero.

commit_message:
Validate that matrix dimension sizes in SparseMatMul are positive.

PiperOrigin-RevId: 401149683
Change-Id: Ib33eafc561a39c8741ece80b2edce6d4aae9a57d
cve_desc: TensorFlow is an open source platform for machine learning. In affected versions the code for sparse matrix multiplication is vulnerable to undefined behavior via binding a reference to `nullptr`. This occurs whenever the dimensions of `a` or `b` are 0 or less. In the case on one of these is 0, an empty output tensor should be allocated (to conserve the invariant that output tensors are always allocated when the operation is successful) but nothing should be written to it (that is, we should return early from the kernel implementation). Otherwise, attempts to write to this empty tensor would result in heap OOB access. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.7.0. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.6.1, TensorFlow 2.5.2, and TensorFlow 2.4.4, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

code_snippets: const int m = transpose_a_ ? a.dim_size(1) : a.dim_size(0);
const int k = transpose_a_ ? a.dim_size(0) : a.dim_size(1);
const int n = transpose_b_ ? b.dim_size(0) : b.dim_size(1);
const int k2 = transpose_b_ ? b.dim_size(1) : b.dim_size(0);
vulnerabilities.description: Tensor dimension sizes (returned as int64 by dim_size()) are truncated to int. A carefully crafted tensor with a dimension slightly above INT_MAX (e.g., 2^31) would wrap to a negative value or a small positive value when cast to int. While the check `m >= 0 && n >= 0 && k >= 0` catches negative values, certain large int64 values truncate to small positive ints (e.g., dim_size = 2^32 + 1 truncates to 1). This causes a small output buffer to be allocated via TensorShape({m, n}), while the actual matrix multiplication operates on the real, much larger tensor dimensions, leading to an out-of-bounds heap write.

step_by_step_execution.description: Input tensors a and b are received. An attacker provides a matrix 'a' with shape e.g. [1, 4294967297] (2^32+1) and 'b' with shape [4294967297, 1], both valid matrices.
The matrix shape checks pass since both tensors are 2D matrices.

Dimension sizes are computed and narrowed from int64 to int. With transpose_a_=false: m = (int)a.dim_size(0), k = (int)a.dim_size(1). The value 4294967297 (2^32+1) truncates to 1 when cast to 32-bit int.
The check k == k2 passes (both are 1). The check m >= 0 && n >= 0 && k >= 0 passes (all are 1).
Output tensor is allocated with shape {m, n} = {1, 1}, a single float element.

The matrix multiplication proceeds using the actual tensor data. The contract operation or sparse matmul reads and writes based on the real underlying tensor dimensions (which are huge), writing far beyond the 4-byte output buffer.

state.value: potentially up to 4294967297 * 1 floats, far exceeding the 1-float buffer

commit_message: Make line_no with too large value(2**20) invalid. Fixes #124
cve_desc: tinyexr 0.9.5 has a integer overflow over-write in tinyexr::DecodePixelData in tinyexr.h, related to OpenEXR code.

code_snippets: const unsigned char *data_ptr = reinterpret_cast<const unsigned char *>(head + offsets[tile_idx]);
vulnerabilities.description: Integer overflow in the tile offset bounds check. If offsets[tile_idx] is a very large value (close to UINT64_MAX), the addition `offsets[tile_idx] + sizeof(int) * 5` can wrap around to a small value, bypassing the size check. This leads to an out-of-bounds read when `head + offsets[tile_idx]` is computed, and `data_size` is computed incorrectly as well.

code_snippets: const unsigned char *data_ptr = reinterpret_cast<const unsigned char *>(head + offsets[tile_idx]);

step_by_step_execution.description: The function receives offsets vector from the caller, which contains file offsets parsed from the EXR file (attacker-controlled).
The bounds check computes offsets[tile_idx] + sizeof(int) * 5. With offsets[tile_idx] = 0xFFFFFFFFFFFFFFEC, this is 0xFFFFFFFFFFFFFFEC + 20 = 0x0000000000000000 (wraps to 0 on 64-bit).
The check `0 > 1024` is false, so the bounds check is bypassed.
data_size is computed as size - 0 = 1024, which is incorrect. data_ptr is computed as head + 0xFFFFFFFFFFFFFFEC, a pointer far out of bounds.
memcpy reads 16 bytes from the out-of-bounds data_ptr into tile_coordinates, causing an out-of-bounds read.

code_snippets: const unsigned char *data_ptr = reinterpret_cast<const unsigned char *>(head + offsets[y_idx]);
vulnerabilities.description: Same integer overflow vulnerability in the scanline format path. If offsets[y_idx] is close to UINT64_MAX, the addition `offsets[y_idx] + sizeof(int) * 2` wraps around, bypassing the bounds check and leading to out-of-bounds reads.

code_snippets: const unsigned char *data_ptr = reinterpret_cast<const unsigned char *>(head + offsets[y_idx]);

step_by_step_execution.description: The function enters the scanline (non-tiled) path with attacker-controlled offsets.
The bounds check computes offsets[y_idx] + sizeof(int) * 2. With offsets[y_idx] = 0xFFFFFFFFFFFFFFF8, this wraps to 0x0000000000000000.
The check `0 > 512` is false, so the bounds check is bypassed.
data_ptr is computed as head + 0xFFFFFFFFFFFFFFF8, a wildly out-of-bounds pointer. memcpy reads from this pointer.

commit_message:
Fix a null pointer exception in SVDF

This is due to not checking that `GetVariableInput` returns non-null tensor.

Also fix a potential null pointer exception in `GetVariableInput`.

PiperOrigin-RevId: 385160147
Change-Id: Iadf3f0705b036a9014d27caa5a8bbd91f4c4c401
cve_desc: TensorFlow is an end-to-end open source platform for machine learning. In affected versions the implementation of SVDF in TFLite is [vulnerable to a null pointer error](https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorflow/blob/460e000de3a83278fb00b61a16d161b1964f15f4/tensorflow/lite/kernels/svdf.cc#L300-L313). The [`GetVariableInput` function](https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorflow/blob/460e000de3a83278fb00b61a16d161b1964f15f4/tensorflow/lite/kernels/kernel_util.cc#L115-L119) can return a null pointer but `GetTensorData` assumes that the argument is always a valid tensor. Furthermore, because `GetVariableInput` calls [`GetMutableInput`](https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorflow/blob/460e000de3a83278fb00b61a16d161b1964f15f4/tensorflow/lite/kernels/kernel_util.cc#L82-L90) which might return `nullptr`, the `tensor->is_variable` expression can also trigger a null pointer exception. We have patched the issue in GitHub commit 5b048e87e4e55990dae6b547add4dae59f4e1c76. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.6.0. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.5.1, TensorFlow 2.4.3, and TensorFlow 2.3.4, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

code_snippets:
TfLiteTensor* tensor = GetMutableInput(context, node, index);
  return tensor->is_variable ? tensor : nullptr;
vulnerabilities.description: If GetMutableInput returns nullptr (e.g., due to an invalid index or missing input), the subsequent dereference `tensor->is_variable` will cause a null pointer dereference, leading to a crash or potentially exploitable behavior.

step_by_step_execution.description: GetMutableInput is called with the provided context, node, and index.

state.value: any integer, possibly out of range or referencing a missing input

step_by_step_execution.description: GetMutableInput may return nullptr if the index is invalid or the input tensor cannot be resolved.
The code dereferences tensor via `tensor->is_variable` without a null check, causing a null pointer dereference.

commit_message:
Improve to cover scale value greater than one

PiperOrigin-RevId: 433050921
cve_desc: TensorFlow is an open source platform for machine learning. Prior to versions 2.9.0, 2.8.1, 2.7.2, and 2.6.4, certain TFLite models that were created using TFLite model converter would crash when loaded in the TFLite interpreter. The culprit is that during quantization the scale of values could be greater than 1 but code was always assuming sub-unit scaling. Thus, since code was calling `QuantizeMultiplierSmallerThanOneExp`, the `TFLITE_CHECK_LT` assertion would trigger and abort the process. Versions 2.9.0, 2.8.1, 2.7.2, and 2.6.4 contain a patch for this issue.

commit_message:
Fixed recursive async function calls.

Previously, PromiseCapability record was stored (function->context)
directly in function object during a function invocation.  This is
not correct, because PromiseCapability record should be linked to
current execution context.  As a result, function->context is
overwritten with consecutive recursive calls which results in
use-after-free.

This closes #451 issue on Github.
cve_desc: njs through 0.7.0, used in NGINX, was discovered to contain a heap use-after-free in njs_await_fulfilled.

commit_message:
Improve to cover scale value greater than one

PiperOrigin-RevId: 433050921
cve_desc: TensorFlow is an open source platform for machine learning. Prior to versions 2.9.0, 2.8.1, 2.7.2, and 2.6.4, certain TFLite models that were created using TFLite model converter would crash when loaded in the TFLite interpreter. The culprit is that during quantization the scale of values could be greater than 1 but code was always assuming sub-unit scaling. Thus, since code was calling `QuantizeMultiplierSmallerThanOneExp`, the `TFLITE_CHECK_LT` assertion would trigger and abort the process. Versions 2.9.0, 2.8.1, 2.7.2, and 2.6.4 contain a patch for this issue.

commit_message:
fix possible pattern crash

issue #325
cve_desc: A denial of service vulnerability was found in tildearrow Furnace. It has been classified as problematic. This is due to an incomplete fix of CVE-2022-1211. It is possible to initiate the attack remotely but it requires user interaction. The issue got fixed with the patch 0eb02422d5161767e9983bdaa5c429762d3477ce.

vulnerabilities.description: The static buffer `id` is only 32 bytes, but multiple `sprintf` calls write to it without bounds checking. The first sprintf includes the result of `noteName()` (variable length) plus format specifiers with two integers. Other sprintf calls use `%.2X` which specifies a *minimum* width of 2, not a maximum — if the underlying int value is large or negative (and not -1), it will print more hex digits. Combined with three integer format specifiers (`%d_%d_%d`) and fixed string prefixes, these can overflow the 32-byte buffer.

step_by_step_execution.description: The function is called with valid parameters so that the row is visible, ord and i are in range, and the loop over channels is entered.

state.value: static char[32], uninitialized for this call

step_by_step_execution.description: For the note column, sprintf is called with noteName() result. noteName() returns a string representation of a note. If the pattern data contains unexpected values, noteName could return a longer-than-expected string. Even with a normal 3-char note name, the total could approach the limit with large i,j values.

state.value: "%s##PN_%d_%d" -> e.g. "C#5##PN_255_31" = 14 chars, fits; but unusual noteName results or larger values could exceed 32

step_by_step_execution.description: For effect value columns, when pat->data[i][index+1] is not -1, sprintf with %.2X is used. The %.2X format specifier sets minimum width 2, not maximum. If the int value stored in pattern data is a large number (e.g., 0xFFFFFFFF or any value > 0xFF), %.2X will print more than 2 hex characters.

state.value: an int value, e.g. 0x12345678 (not -1, so the %.2X branch is taken)
"12345678##PF0_255_31" = 21+ chars, and with larger int values for k, i, j this grows further

step_by_step_execution.description: The sprintf writes past the end of the 32-byte `id` buffer, causing a stack buffer overflow (or static storage corruption since id is static).

state.value: overflowed past 32 bytes, corrupting adjacent memory

commit_message:
Fixed recursive async function calls.

Previously, PromiseCapability record was stored (function->context)
directly in function object during a function invocation.  This is
not correct, because PromiseCapability record should be linked to
current execution context.  As a result, function->context is
overwritten with consecutive recursive calls which results in
use-after-free.

This closes #451 issue on Github.
cve_desc: njs through 0.7.0, used in NGINX, was discovered to contain a heap use-after-free in njs_await_fulfilled.

commit_message:
Merge pull request from GHSA-vhxv-phmx-g52q

* Prevent OOB read/write when parsing RTCP FB RPSI

* Add log information

* Modification based on comments.
cve_desc: PJSIP is a free and open source multimedia communication library written in C. PJSIP versions 2.12 and prior do not parse incoming RTCP feedback RPSI (Reference Picture Selection Indication) packet, but any app that directly uses pjmedia_rtcp_fb_parse_rpsi() will be affected. A patch is available in the `master` branch of the `pjsip/pjproject` GitHub repository. There are currently no known workarounds.

vulnerabilities.description: An attacker-controlled `padlen` value (read directly from the packet buffer, range 0-255) can cause an unsigned integer underflow in the calculation of `rpsi_bit_len`. If `padlen` is greater than `rpsi_len*8 - 16`, the subtraction wraps around to a very large value. This huge value is then used to compute the string length passed to `pj_strset`, resulting in the rpsi output referencing a buffer region far beyond the actual packet data, enabling an out-of-bounds read.

step_by_step_execution.description: Function receives a crafted RTCP packet buffer. The assertions check that `buf` and `rpsi` are non-null, and `length >= sizeof(pjmedia_rtcp_common)`. Assume length is e.g. 24 bytes.
Header fields are checked: `hdr->pt == RTCP_PSFB` and `hdr->count == 3`. Attacker crafts the packet to satisfy this.
`rpsi_len` is computed from `hdr->length`. Attacker sets `hdr->length` to network-byte-order 3, so `rpsi_len = (3 - 2) * 4 = 4`.
The check `length < rpsi_len + 12` evaluates to `24 < 16` which is false, so execution continues.
`p` is set to point past the common header. `padlen` is read from the first byte of the payload. Attacker sets this byte to 255.

state.value: points to second byte of RPSI payload

step_by_step_execution.description: `rpsi->rpsi_bit_len` is computed as `rpsi_len*8 - 16 - padlen = 4*8 - 16 - 255 = 32 - 16 - 255 = -239`. Since this is an unsigned type (pj_size_t), it wraps to a very large value (e.g., 2^64 - 239 on 64-bit or 2^32 - 239 on 32-bit).
`pj_strset` is called with length `(rpsi->rpsi_bit_len + 7) / 8`, which is an enormous value, setting the rpsi string to reference far beyond the actual buffer boundary.

commit_message: fixed #2194
cve_desc: Use After Free in GitHub repository gpac/gpac prior to v2.1.0-DEV.

vulnerabilities.description: The comment says 'register TWICE' but the code calls gf_node_unregister twice instead of gf_node_register. When gf_bifs_dec_node returns a newly created node, its reference count is typically 1. The first gf_node_unregister call decrements the reference count to 0 and frees the node. The second gf_node_unregister call then operates on freed memory (use-after-free). Furthermore, the freed node pointer is subsequently stored in inf->new_node, codec->scenegraph->global_qp, and codec->ActiveQP, creating dangling pointer references.

step_by_step_execution.description: A node is decoded from the bitstream via gf_bifs_dec_node. The returned node has an initial reference count (typically 1 from creation).

state.value: valid pointer to newly created node

step_by_step_execution.description: The previous global_qp is cleaned up and codec->ActiveQP and codec->scenegraph->global_qp are set to NULL.
The node's tag is checked and confirmed to be TAG_MPEG4_QuantizationParameter, so execution continues past the check.
The node is assigned to codec->ActiveQP and codec->scenegraph->global_qp.
First gf_node_unregister(node, NULL) is called, decrementing the reference count from 1 to 0. This frees the node.
Second gf_node_unregister(node, NULL) is called on the already-freed node, causing a use-after-free / double-free.
The freed node pointer is stored in inf->new_node, codec->ActiveQP, and codec->scenegraph->global_qp, all becoming dangling pointers that will be used later.

commit_message:
i2c: fix stack buffer overflow vulnerability in i2c md command

When running "i2c md 0 0 80000100", the function do_i2c_md parses the
length into an unsigned int variable named length. The value is then
moved to a signed variable:

    int nbytes = length;
    #define DISP_LINE_LEN 16
    int linebytes = (nbytes > DISP_LINE_LEN) ? DISP_LINE_LEN : nbytes;
    ret = dm_i2c_read(dev, addr, linebuf, linebytes);

On systems where integers are 32 bits wide, 0x80000100 is a negative
value to "nbytes > DISP_LINE_LEN" is false and linebytes gets assigned
0x80000100 instead of 16.

The consequence is that the function which reads from the i2c device
(dm_i2c_read or i2c_read) is called with a 16-byte stack buffer to fill
but with a size parameter which is too large. In some cases, this could
trigger a crash. But with some i2c drivers, such as drivers/i2c/nx_i2c.c
(used with "nexell,s5pxx18-i2c" bus), the size is actually truncated to
a 16-bit integer. This is because function i2c_transfer expects an
unsigned short length. In such a case, an attacker who can control the
response of an i2c device can overwrite the return address of a function
and execute arbitrary code through Return-Oriented Programming.

Fix this issue by using unsigned integers types in do_i2c_md. While at
it, make also alen unsigned, as signed sizes can cause vulnerabilities
when people forgot to check that they can be negative.

Signed-off-by: Nicolas Iooss <nicolas.iooss+uboot@ledger.fr>
Reviewed-by: Heiko Schocher <hs@denx.de>
cve_desc: In Das U-Boot through 2022.07-rc5, an integer signedness error and resultant stack-based buffer overflow in the "i2c md" command enables the corruption of the return address pointer of the do_i2c_md function.

commit_message:
Fixed Array.prototype.lastIndexOf() with unicode string as "this".

Previously, when lastIndexOf() was called with unicode string as "this"
argument and a negative "fromIndex" argument null-pointer dererence
might occur because njs_string_offset() was called with invalid index
value whereas njs_string_offset() should always be called with valid
index argument.

The fix is to verify that from index is valid.

This closes #482 issue on Github.
cve_desc: Nginx NJS v0.7.2 was discovered to contain a segmentation violation in the function njs_string_offset at src/njs_string.c.

commit_message:
Prevent use after free.

A very old version of the code used `result` as a simple pointer to a resource. Two years later, the pointer got changed to a `unique_ptr` but author forgot to remove the call to `Unref`. Three years after that, we finally uncover the UAF.

PiperOrigin-RevId: 387924872
Change-Id: I70fb6f199164de49fac20c168132a07b84903f9b
cve_desc: TensorFlow is an end-to-end open source platform for machine learning. In affected versions the implementation for `tf.raw_ops.BoostedTreesCreateEnsemble` can result in a use after free error if an attacker supplies specially crafted arguments. The [implementation](https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorflow/blob/f24faa153ad31a4b51578f8181d3aaab77a1ddeb/tensorflow/core/kernels/boosted_trees/resource_ops.cc#L55) uses a reference counted resource and decrements the refcount if the initialization fails, as it should. However, when the code was written, the resource was represented as a naked pointer but later refactoring has changed it to be a smart pointer. Thus, when the pointer leaves the scope, a subsequent `free`-ing of the resource occurs, but this fails to take into account that the refcount has already reached 0, thus the resource has been already freed. During this double-free process, members of the resource object are accessed for cleanup but they are invalid as the entire resource has been freed. We have patched the issue in GitHub commit 5ecec9c6fbdbc6be03295685190a45e7eee726ab. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.6.0. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.5.1, TensorFlow 2.4.3, and TensorFlow 2.3.4, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

commit_message:
i2c: fix stack buffer overflow vulnerability in i2c md command

When running "i2c md 0 0 80000100", the function do_i2c_md parses the
length into an unsigned int variable named length. The value is then
moved to a signed variable:

    int nbytes = length;
    #define DISP_LINE_LEN 16
    int linebytes = (nbytes > DISP_LINE_LEN) ? DISP_LINE_LEN : nbytes;
    ret = dm_i2c_read(dev, addr, linebuf, linebytes);

On systems where integers are 32 bits wide, 0x80000100 is a negative
value to "nbytes > DISP_LINE_LEN" is false and linebytes gets assigned
0x80000100 instead of 16.

The consequence is that the function which reads from the i2c device
(dm_i2c_read or i2c_read) is called with a 16-byte stack buffer to fill
but with a size parameter which is too large. In some cases, this could
trigger a crash. But with some i2c drivers, such as drivers/i2c/nx_i2c.c
(used with "nexell,s5pxx18-i2c" bus), the size is actually truncated to
a 16-bit integer. This is because function i2c_transfer expects an
unsigned short length. In such a case, an attacker who can control the
response of an i2c device can overwrite the return address of a function
and execute arbitrary code through Return-Oriented Programming.

Fix this issue by using unsigned integers types in do_i2c_md. While at
it, make also alen unsigned, as signed sizes can cause vulnerabilities
when people forgot to check that they can be negative.

Signed-off-by: Nicolas Iooss <nicolas.iooss+uboot@ledger.fr>
Reviewed-by: Heiko Schocher <hs@denx.de>
cve_desc: In Das U-Boot through 2022.07-rc5, an integer signedness error and resultant stack-based buffer overflow in the "i2c md" command enables the corruption of the return address pointer of the do_i2c_md function.

commit_message: fixed #1885
cve_desc: Segmentation fault vulnerability exists in Gpac through 1.0.1 via the gf_odf_size_descriptor function in desc_private.c when using mp4box, which causes a denial of service.

vulnerabilities.description: If gf_isom_get_esd returns NULL for the scene track (sceneT), the code unconditionally dereferences esd when accessing esd->OCRESID outside the sample-count if-block. This leads to a NULL pointer dereference.

code_snippets:
if (remove_ocr) esd->OCRESID = 0;
		else if (esd->OCRESID == esd->ESID) esd->OCRESID = 0;

step_by_step_execution.description: IOD_Profile is GF_SDP_IOD_ISMA (or GF_SDP_IOD_ISMA_STRICT which gets converted), and sceneT is non-zero, so the ISMA branch is entered.
gf_isom_get_esd is called for the scene track and returns NULL (e.g., if the track has no valid ESD).
gf_isom_get_sample_count may return a value != 1, so the if-block is skipped entirely.
The code unconditionally accesses esd->OCRESID, dereferencing the NULL pointer.

code_snippets:
if (remove_ocr) esd->OCRESID = 0;
			else if (esd->OCRESID == esd->ESID) esd->OCRESID = 0;
vulnerabilities.description: If gf_isom_get_esd returns NULL for the OD track (odT), the code unconditionally dereferences esd when accessing esd->OCRESID. This leads to a NULL pointer dereference.

code_snippets:
if (remove_ocr) esd->OCRESID = 0;
			else if (esd->OCRESID == esd->ESID) esd->OCRESID = 0;

step_by_step_execution.description: IOD_Profile is GF_SDP_IOD_ISMA and odT is non-zero, so the OD track branch is entered.
gf_isom_get_esd is called for the OD track and returns NULL.
gf_isom_get_sample_count may return a value != 1, so the if-block is skipped.
The code unconditionally accesses esd->OCRESID, dereferencing the NULL pointer.

vulnerabilities.description: gf_base64_encode is given a max output size of 2000, but buf64 is later used in sprintf to write into sdpLine (5100 bytes). More critically, the final IOD encoding path calls gf_odf_desc_write which may produce a buffer of arbitrary size. If the encoded IOD descriptor is large, gf_base64_encode output (up to 2000 chars) plus the format string prefix for the final sprintf into sdpLine could approach or fit within bounds, but there is no validation that gf_base64_encode actually respected the 2000-byte limit or that the resulting sdpLine string fits. If the base64 output approaches the limit, the final sprintf with its ~60-char prefix and quotes could produce a string close to 5100 bytes, and an off-by-one or slightly oversized encoding could overflow sdpLine.

step_by_step_execution.description: gf_odf_desc_write encodes the IOD into buffer with arbitrary size.
gf_base64_encode is called with output limit 2000, encoding 'size' bytes into buf64. If size is ~1500, base64 output is ~2000 bytes.

state.value: up to 2000 chars of base64 data, null-terminated

step_by_step_execution.description: sprintf writes the format string prefix (~53 chars) plus buf64 (up to 2000 chars) plus closing quote and null into sdpLine (5100 bytes). Total could be ~2054 which fits, but the lack of bounds checking with sprintf means any unexpected increase could overflow.

state.value: stack buffer of 5100 bytes, written with up to ~2054 bytes

commit_message: fixed #1885
cve_desc: Segmentation fault vulnerability exists in Gpac through 1.0.1 via the gf_odf_size_descriptor function in desc_private.c when using mp4box, which causes a denial of service.

vulnerabilities.description: NULL pointer dereference: gf_isom_get_esd() can return NULL for the scene track, but the code unconditionally dereferences esd after the if block to access esd->OCRESID without a NULL check.

code_snippets:
if (remove_ocr) esd->OCRESID = 0;
		else if (esd->OCRESID == esd->ESID) esd->OCRESID = 0;

step_by_step_execution.description: Function is called with IOD_Profile == GF_SDP_IOD_ISMA and a file that has a scene track.
The code enters the IOD_Profile == GF_SDP_IOD_ISMA branch.
gf_isom_get_esd(file, sceneT, 1) is called for the scene track but returns NULL (e.g., the track has no valid ESD).
The sample count check and inner block may or may not execute, but does not change esd.
Code reaches 'if (remove_ocr) esd->OCRESID = 0;' which dereferences esd while it is NULL, causing a NULL pointer dereference.

vulnerabilities.description: NULL pointer dereference: gf_isom_get_esd() can return NULL for the OD track, but the code unconditionally dereferences esd to access esd->OCRESID without a NULL check.

code_snippets:
if (remove_ocr) esd->OCRESID = 0;
			else if (esd->OCRESID == esd->ESID) esd->OCRESID = 0;

step_by_step_execution.description: Function is called with IOD_Profile == GF_SDP_IOD_ISMA and a file that has an OD track (odT != 0).
gf_isom_get_esd(file, odT, 1) is called but returns NULL.
The sample count check block may or may not execute; esd remains NULL throughout.
Code reaches 'if (remove_ocr) esd->OCRESID = 0;' which dereferences NULL esd, causing a crash.

vulnerabilities.description: Potential buffer overflow: gf_base64_encode is called with an output limit of 2000, but buf64 is 5000 bytes. If gf_base64_encode does not properly enforce the output limit and the input data is large, it can write beyond 2000 bytes and potentially beyond the 5000-byte buf64 buffer, or produce a string that overflows sdpLine (5100 bytes) via sprintf.

step_by_step_execution.description: gf_odf_desc_write encodes the IOD descriptor into buffer with an arbitrary size.
gf_base64_encode is called with output limit 2000. Base64 encoding of 3000 bytes produces ~4000 characters. If the function does not truncate, size64 could be ~4000.

buf64[size64] = 0 writes a null terminator at index ~4000, within the 5000-byte buffer. Then sprintf into sdpLine (5100 bytes) with prefix 'a=mpeg4-iod:"data:application/mpeg4-iod;base64,' (~48 chars) + ~4000 chars + '"' = ~4049, which fits. However if size is even larger (e.g. >3700 bytes), base64 output exceeds 5000 and buf64 itself overflows.

state.value: 5000 bytes stack buffer, base64 output could exceed this

commit_message:
Fixed Array.prototype.lastIndexOf() with unicode string as "this".

Previously, when lastIndexOf() was called with unicode string as "this"
argument and a negative "fromIndex" argument null-pointer dererence
might occur because njs_string_offset() was called with invalid index
value whereas njs_string_offset() should always be called with valid
index argument.

The fix is to verify that from index is valid.

This closes #482 issue on Github.
cve_desc: Nginx NJS v0.7.2 was discovered to contain a segmentation violation in the function njs_string_offset at src/njs_string.c.

vulnerabilities.description: In the byte/ASCII string path, the pointer `p` is set to `string_prop.start + from` without checking that `from` is within the string bounds (i.e., `from < length`). If `args->from` is >= `length`, this results in an out-of-bounds read. Similarly, `p` is decremented in a loop down to `to` without checking that `p` stays >= `string_prop.start`, so if `to` is negative, `p` can decrement before the start of the buffer.

step_by_step_execution.description: Function receives args where value is a string (or object string), args->from is set to a value >= the string's character length, and args->to is set to a value < args->from.
The code enters the string handling branch and computes string properties via njs_string_prop.
Since length == string_prop.size, the code enters the ASCII string path. Pointer p is computed as string_prop.start + from, which is beyond the allocated buffer.

state.value: string_prop.start + 100 (out of bounds, buffer is only 5 bytes)

step_by_step_execution.description: The while loop condition (i-- > to) is true (100 > 0). njs_string_new is called with p pointing to out-of-bounds memory, reading 1 byte from an invalid location.

commit_message:
xfrm: policy: check policy direction value

The 'dir' parameter in xfrm_migrate() is a user-controlled byte which is used
as an array index. This can lead to an out-of-bound access, kernel lockup and
DoS. Add a check for the 'dir' value.

This fixes CVE-2017-11600.

References: https://bugzilla.redhat.com/show_bug.cgi?id=1474928
Fixes: 80c9abaabf42 ("[XFRM]: Extension for dynamic update of endpoint address(es)")
Cc: <stable@vger.kernel.org> # v2.6.21-rc1
Reported-by: "bo Zhang" <zhangbo5891001@gmail.com>
Signed-off-by: Vladis Dronov <vdronov@redhat.com>
Signed-off-by: Steffen Klassert <steffen.klassert@secunet.com>
cve_desc: net/xfrm/xfrm_policy.c in the Linux kernel through 4.12.3, when CONFIG_XFRM_MIGRATE is enabled, does not ensure that the dir value of xfrm_userpolicy_id is XFRM_POLICY_MAX or less, which allows local users to cause a denial of service (out-of-bounds access) or possibly have unspecified other impact via an XFRM_MSG_MIGRATE xfrm Netlink message.

commit_message:
[misc] Fix use-after-free in net_bind()

Thanks to Ba Jinsheng for reporting this bug
cve_desc: OwnTone (aka owntone-server) through 28.1 has a use-after-free in net_bind() in misc.c.

vulnerabilities.description: After freeaddrinfo(servinfo) is called, the pointer `ptr` (which points into the freed `servinfo` linked list) is dereferenced to access `ptr->ai_addr` and `ptr->ai_addrlen`. This is a use-after-free vulnerability.

step_by_step_execution.description: The function calls getaddrinfo() which allocates the servinfo linked list.

state.value: allocated linked list of addrinfo structures

step_by_step_execution.description: The for loop iterates over the servinfo list. When a successful bind occurs, the loop breaks with ptr pointing to the successful addrinfo node.

state.value: points to an element within the servinfo linked list

step_by_step_execution.description: freeaddrinfo(servinfo) is called, freeing all nodes in the linked list including the one ptr points to.
The check `if (!ptr)` passes (ptr is non-null but dangling), so execution continues.
getsockname() is called with ptr->ai_addr and &ptr->ai_addrlen, dereferencing the freed memory. This is a use-after-free.

commit_message:
FTP: Fix buffer overflow in PORT parser, reported by Aaron Esau

Signed-off-by: Joachim Nilsson <troglobit@gmail.com>
cve_desc: An unauthenticated stack-based buffer overflow vulnerability in common.c's handle_PORT in uftpd FTP server versions 2.10 and earlier can be abused to cause a crash and could potentially lead to remote code execution.

vulnerabilities.description: The values a, b, c, d are parsed from user input as integers via sscanf with no range validation. Each %d can produce a value like -2147483648 (11 characters). sprintf then formats up to four such values plus dots into `addr`, which is only INET_ADDRSTRLEN (16) bytes. This can overflow the stack buffer `addr`.

step_by_step_execution.description: User-controlled string `str` is passed to sscanf, parsing six integers.
sscanf parses a, b, c, d as large negative integers with no bounds checking.
sprintf writes the formatted string into `addr` (16 bytes). The output "-2147483648.-2147483648.-2147483648.-2147483648" is 47 bytes plus null terminator, far exceeding the 16-byte buffer.

commit_message:
fix possible pattern crash

issue #325
cve_desc: A denial of service vulnerability was found in tildearrow Furnace. It has been classified as problematic. This is due to an incomplete fix of CVE-2022-1211. It is possible to initiate the attack remotely but it requires user interaction. The issue got fixed with the patch 0eb02422d5161767e9983bdaa5c429762d3477ce.

vulnerabilities.description: Out-of-bounds read on the `extFxColors` array. The final `else` branch for effect coloring is reached when `pat->data[i][index]` >= 0xe0, but there is no upper bound check. If the pattern data (loaded from a file) contains an effect value significantly larger than 0xff (e.g., 0x1000), the index `pat->data[i][index] - 0xe0` can be arbitrarily large, reading out of bounds.

step_by_step_execution.description: The function is called with valid `i`, `ord`, and `chans` parameters, and the row is visible, so early returns are not taken.
The loop iterates over channels: `for (int j=0; j<chans; j++)`. The channel is visible and not collapsed.
The inner effects loop runs: `for (int k=0; k<e->song.pat[j].effectRows; k++)`. index is calculated as `4+(k<<1)`.
The code checks `pat->data[i][index]` which is read from pattern cache (potentially loaded from a malicious file). The value is not -1, so the else branch is entered.
The chain of if/else-if conditions is evaluated: the value is not < 0x10, not < 0x20, ..., not < 0xe0. The final `else` branch is taken.

The expression `extFxColors[pat->data[i][index]-0xe0]` is evaluated. The index is 0x1000 - 0xe0 = 0xF20, which is far beyond the bounds of the `extFxColors` array (which likely has only 32 entries for 0xe0-0xff).

commit_message:
Prevent heap OOB in sparse reduction ops.

PiperOrigin-RevId: 387934524
Change-Id: I894aa30f1e454f09b471d565b4a325da49322c1a
cve_desc: TensorFlow is an end-to-end open source platform for machine learning. In affected versions the implementation of sparse reduction operations in TensorFlow can trigger accesses outside of bounds of heap allocated data. The [implementation](https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorflow/blob/a1bc56203f21a5a4995311825ffaba7a670d7747/tensorflow/core/kernels/sparse_reduce_op.cc#L217-L228) fails to validate that each reduction group does not overflow and that each corresponding index does not point to outside the bounds of the input tensor. We have patched the issue in GitHub commit 87158f43f05f2720a374f3e6d22a7aaa3a33f750. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.6.0. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.5.1, TensorFlow 2.4.3, and TensorFlow 2.3.4, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

code_snippets:
const int64_t idx = CoordinatesToFlatIndex(g.group(), output_strides);
      out_flat(idx) = reduced_val();
vulnerabilities.description: The flat index computed from sparse tensor group coordinates is used to write into the output tensor without bounds checking. Since the sparse tensor indices originate from user input (`indices_t`), a malicious user can craft indices that are negative or exceed the declared shape, causing the computed `idx` to fall outside the allocated `out_flat` buffer, leading to an out-of-bounds read/write.

step_by_step_execution.description: User provides `input_indices` tensor with attacker-controlled coordinate values (e.g., negative values or values exceeding `input_shape`).

state.value: attacker-controlled, e.g. contains index value -1 or value >= shape dimension

step_by_step_execution.description: A SparseTensor `sp` is created from deep copies of the user-supplied indices, values, and shape. No validation is performed to ensure all index values are within [0, shape[d]) for each dimension d.

state.value: SparseTensor with potentially out-of-range index coordinates

step_by_step_execution.description: output_strides are computed based on the declared shape dimensions for the group_by_dims. The output tensor `out_values` is allocated with `reduction.reduced_shape`.

state.value: flat view of output tensor with size = product of reduced_shape dimensions

step_by_step_execution.description: The sparse tensor is reordered and grouped. g.group() returns coordinates derived from the sparse tensor indices (user-controlled).

state.value: coordinates from user-supplied indices, could be negative or out-of-range

step_by_step_execution.description: CoordinatesToFlatIndex computes idx = sum(coords[i] * strides[i]). With out-of-range coordinates, idx can be negative or exceed the output tensor size.

state.value: could be negative or >= out_flat.size() due to malicious coordinates

step_by_step_execution.description: out_flat(idx) = reduced_val() writes to an out-of-bounds memory location, causing heap corruption.

commit_message:
Fix tf.raw_ops.QuantizeAndDequantizeV4Grad vulnerability with invalid input_min or input_max.

Check that argument is actually a scalar before treating it as such.

PiperOrigin-RevId: 445198280
cve_desc: TensorFlow is an open source platform for machine learning. Prior to versions 2.9.0, 2.8.1, 2.7.2, and 2.6.4, the implementation of `tf.raw_ops.QuantizeAndDequantizeV4Grad` does not fully validate the input arguments. This results in a `CHECK`-failure which can be used to trigger a denial of service attack. Versions 2.9.0, 2.8.1, 2.7.2, and 2.6.4 contain a patch for this issue.

vulnerabilities.description: When axis_ is 0, the else branch is taken (since axis_ != -1), and `axis_ - 1` evaluates to -1. This negative value is passed to `flat_inner_outer_dims<T, 3>()`, which expects a non-negative dimension index. This can lead to undefined behavior, such as an out-of-bounds read/write or a CHECK failure, depending on the TensorFlow internal implementation.

step_by_step_execution.description: The function reads inputs: gradient (input 0), input (input 1). axis_ is a member variable set at construction time.
The check `axis_ >= -1` passes since 0 >= -1.
The check `axis_ == -1 || axis_ < input.shape().dims()` passes, assuming input has at least 1 dimension (dims > 0), since 0 < dims.
depth is computed as `input.dim_size(axis_)` = `input.dim_size(0)` since axis_ != -1.
Since axis_ (0) != -1, the else branch is taken.
flat_inner_outer_dims<T, 3>(axis_ - 1) is called with axis_ - 1 = -1. This passes a negative index to the reshape function, causing undefined behavior (out-of-bounds memory access or crash).

vulnerabilities.description: When axis_ != -1 and input_min_tensor (or input_max_tensor) is a scalar (dims == 0), the code calls `dim_size(0)` on a 0-dimensional tensor. Since a scalar tensor has no dimensions, accessing dimension index 0 is out-of-bounds, leading to undefined behavior.

step_by_step_execution.description: axis_ is set to a positive value (e.g. 1), and input_min_tensor is provided as a scalar.
The check `input_min_tensor.dims() == 0 || input_min_tensor.dims() == 1` passes because dims == 0.

Since axis_ != -1, the code enters the block that calls `input_min_tensor.dim_size(0)`. However, the tensor is a scalar with 0 dimensions, so accessing dim_size(0) is an out-of-bounds access on the shape's dimension array.

commit_message: Merge pull request from GHSA-26j7-ww69-c4qj
cve_desc: PJSIP is a free and open source multimedia communication library written in C language implementing standard based protocols such as SIP, SDP, RTP, STUN, TURN, and ICE. In versions prior to and including 2.12.1 a stack buffer overflow vulnerability affects PJSIP users that use STUN in their applications, either by: setting a STUN server in their account/media config in PJSUA/PJSUA2 level, or directly using `pjlib-util/stun_simple` API. A patch is available in commit 450baca which should be included in the next release. There are no known workarounds for this issue.

vulnerabilities.description: The while loop increments msg->attr_count without ever checking it against the size of the msg->attr[] array. A crafted STUN message with many small attributes can cause msg->attr_count to exceed the array bounds, leading to an out-of-bounds write when assigning to msg->attr[msg->attr_count].

step_by_step_execution.description: The function receives a buffer and parses the STUN header, extracting msg_len from the header.

state.value: Value from header, e.g. a large value like 1024

step_by_step_execution.description: The msg_len check passes because buf_len - sizeof(pjstun_msg_hdr) equals msg_len.

The while loop begins iterating over attributes. Each attribute has a minimum padded size of 4 bytes (sizeof(pjstun_attr_hdr) rounded up). For a msg_len of 1024, up to 256 attributes of minimum size could be parsed.
On each iteration, the code accesses &msg->attr[msg->attr_count] without checking if msg->attr_count is within the bounds of the attr array in pjstun_msg.

state.value: can exceed the fixed size of msg->attr[]

step_by_step_execution.description: When msg->attr_count exceeds the array dimension, the write *attr = (pjstun_attr_hdr*)p_attr writes beyond the msg->attr[] array, corrupting adjacent memory.

state.value: greater than PJSTUN_MAX_ATTR (or equivalent array size)
points past the end of msg->attr[]

commit_message:
Fix EinsumHelper::ParseEquation to avoid uninitialized accesses.

EinsumHelper::ParseEquation is supposed to return true or false in
input_has_ellipsis and output_has_ellipsis to indicate whether there is
ellipsis in the inputs and output. Previously, when there is no ellipsis in the
inputs or output, the routine doesn't assign false to the variables. This
change initializes the two variables with false to fix the problem.
PiperOrigin-RevId: 391772004
Change-Id: I17b6c88aadef4131470378e48cced054bf252e86
cve_desc: TensorFlow is an open source platform for machine learning. In affeced versions during execution, `EinsumHelper::ParseEquation()` is supposed to set the flags in `input_has_ellipsis` vector and `*output_has_ellipsis` boolean to indicate whether there is ellipsis in the corresponding inputs and output. However, the code only changes these flags to `true` and never assigns `false`. This results in unitialized variable access if callers assume that `EinsumHelper::ParseEquation()` always sets these flags. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.7.0. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.6.1, TensorFlow 2.5.2, and TensorFlow 2.4.4, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

vulnerabilities.description: When num_inputs is 0 (i.e., ParseEinsumEquation returns an empty input list), the code accesses (*input_label_counts)[0] and potentially (*input_label_counts)[1], which are out-of-bounds because input_label_counts was resized to 0 elements. The short-circuit evaluation only guards the [1] access when num_inputs == 1, but does not guard [0] access when num_inputs == 0.

step_by_step_execution.description: ParseEinsumEquation is called, which parses the equation string and populates input_str and output_str. If the equation has no inputs (e.g., an edge case or malformed equation), input_str is empty.
num_inputs is set to input_str.size(), which is 0. input_labels is resized to 0. input_label_counts is resized to 0.
MapToLabels is called on output_str, populating label_mapping with labels from the output string. num_labels is set to label_mapping.size() which can be > 0.
The output label counting loop runs. output_label_counts is resized to num_labels and populated correctly.

The label_types loop iterates for label from 0 to num_labels-1. For each label, it evaluates the 'unique' expression. Since num_inputs == 0, the condition 'num_inputs == 1' is false, so evaluation proceeds to '(*input_label_counts)[0][label]'.
Accessing (*input_label_counts)[0] is an out-of-bounds read on a vector of size 0, leading to undefined behavior.

commit_message:
RDMA/rtrs-clt: Fix possible double free in error case

Callback function rtrs_clt_dev_release() for put_device() calls kfree(clt)
to free memory. We shouldn't call kfree(clt) again, and we can't use the
clt after kfree too.

Replace device_register() with device_initialize() and device_add() so that
dev_set_name can() be used appropriately.

Move mutex_destroy() to the release function so it can be called in
the alloc_clt err path.

Fixes: eab098246625 ("RDMA/rtrs-clt: Refactor the failure cases in alloc_clt")
Link: https://lore.kernel.org/r/20220217030929.323849-1-haris.iqbal@ionos.com
Reported-by: Miaoqian Lin <linmq006@gmail.com>
Signed-off-by: Md Haris Iqbal <haris.iqbal@ionos.com>
Reviewed-by: Jack Wang <jinpu.wang@ionos.com>
Signed-off-by: Jason Gunthorpe <jgg@nvidia.com>
cve_desc: drivers/infiniband/ulp/rtrs/rtrs-clt.c in the Linux kernel before 5.16.12 has a double free related to rtrs_clt_dev_release.

commit_message:
ui/vnc: limit client_cut_text msg payload size

currently a malicious client could define a payload
size of 2^32 - 1 bytes and send up to that size of
data to the vnc server. The server would allocated
that amount of memory which could easily create an
out of memory condition.

This patch limits the payload size to 1MB max.

Please note that client_cut_text messages are currently
silently ignored.

Signed-off-by: Peter Lieven <pl@kamp.de>
Signed-off-by: Gerd Hoffmann <kraxel@redhat.com>
cve_desc: Integer overflow in the VNC display driver in QEMU before 2.1.0 allows attachers to cause a denial of service (process crash) via a CLIENT_CUT_TEXT message, which triggers an infinite loop.

vulnerabilities.description: Integer overflow in VNC_MSG_CLIENT_CUT_TEXT handling. The expression `8 + dlen` where `dlen` is a `uint32_t` can wrap around. For example, if a client sends `dlen = 0xFFFFFFF8`, then `8 + dlen` overflows to `0`, causing the function to return 0 (or a small value). On the next call, the code falls through to `client_cut_text(vs, read_u32(data, 4), data + 8)` with `dlen` being a huge value but the actual data buffer being far too small, leading to a heap buffer over-read.

step_by_step_execution.description: Function is called with len=1 and data[0]=VNC_MSG_CLIENT_CUT_TEXT. It returns 8 requesting more data.
Function is called again with len=8. It reads dlen from data at offset 4. A malicious client sets this to 0xFFFFFFF8.
Since dlen > 0, the code computes `8 + dlen`. Because dlen is uint32_t, 8 + 0xFFFFFFF8 = 0x100000000 which wraps to 0 in 32-bit arithmetic. The return value (int) becomes 0.

Returning 0 signals to the VNC framework that the message is complete. On the next invocation (or in this same path depending on framework behavior), the code reaches client_cut_text with read_u32(data,4) = 0xFFFFFFF8 and data+8 pointing to a small buffer, causing a massive out-of-bounds read.

commit_message:
exec: --cap do not set inheritable capabilities

Closes: CVE-2022-27650

Signed-off-by: Giuseppe Scrivano <gscrivan@redhat.com>
cve_desc: A flaw was found in crun where containers were incorrectly started with non-empty default permissions. A vulnerability was found in Moby (Docker Engine) where containers were started incorrectly with non-empty inheritable Linux process capabilities. This flaw allows an attacker with access to programs with inheritable file capabilities to elevate those capabilities to the permitted set when execve(2) runs.

vulnerabilities.description: Off-by-one error in the loop that populates process->args. The loop iterates `argc - first_arg` times, but each iteration accesses `argv[first_arg + i + 1]`. On the last iteration (i = argc - first_arg - 1), the index becomes `first_arg + (argc - first_arg - 1) + 1 = argc`. Since `argv[argc]` is NULL by the C standard, this results in `xstrdup(NULL)`, which is a NULL pointer dereference. Additionally, `process->args_len` is set to `argc` instead of the actual number of populated arguments, which could cause out-of-bounds reads by callers that trust this length.

step_by_step_execution.description: argp_parse processes the arguments, setting first_arg to the index of the first non-option argument (the container ID).

state.value: index of first non-option arg, e.g., 1
total argument count, e.g., 4 (prog, container_id, cmd, arg)

step_by_step_execution.description: crun_assert_n_args checks argc - first_arg >= 2 (since exec_options.process is NULL). This passes, e.g. 4 - 1 = 3 >= 2.
Execution enters the else branch. process->args is allocated with (argc + 1) entries. The loop iterates with i from 0 to argc - first_arg - 1 (i.e., i = 0, 1, 2).
On iteration i=0: accesses argv[first_arg + 0 + 1] = argv[2] (the command). Valid.
On iteration i=1: accesses argv[first_arg + 1 + 1] = argv[3] (the arg). Valid.
On iteration i=2 (last iteration): accesses argv[first_arg + 2 + 1] = argv[4] = argv[argc]. By C standard, argv[argc] is NULL. xstrdup(NULL) is called, causing a NULL pointer dereference.

commit_message:
rsi: release skb if rsi_prepare_beacon fails

In rsi_send_beacon, if rsi_prepare_beacon fails the allocated skb should
be released.

Signed-off-by: Navid Emamdoost <navid.emamdoost@gmail.com>
Signed-off-by: Kalle Valo <kvalo@codeaurora.org>
cve_desc: A memory leak in the rsi_send_beacon() function in drivers/net/wireless/rsi/rsi_91x_mgmt.c in the Linux kernel through 5.3.11 allows attackers to cause a denial of service (memory consumption) by triggering rsi_prepare_beacon() failures, aka CID-d563131ef23c.

vulnerabilities.description: When rsi_prepare_beacon() fails, the function returns -EINVAL without freeing the allocated skb, causing a memory leak.

step_by_step_execution.description: An skb is allocated via dev_alloc_skb(MAX_MGMT_PKT_SIZE).
Alignment adjustment is performed on skb->data.
rsi_prepare_beacon() is called and returns a non-zero (failure) value.
The function returns -EINVAL without calling dev_kfree_skb(skb), leaking the allocated skb.

commit_message:
exec: --cap do not set inheritable capabilities

Closes: CVE-2022-27650

Signed-off-by: Giuseppe Scrivano <gscrivan@redhat.com>
cve_desc: A flaw was found in crun where containers were incorrectly started with non-empty default permissions. A vulnerability was found in Moby (Docker Engine) where containers were started incorrectly with non-empty inheritable Linux process capabilities. This flaw allows an attacker with access to programs with inheritable file capabilities to elevate those capabilities to the permitted set when execve(2) runs.

vulnerabilities.description: Off-by-one error in the loop that populates process->args. The loop runs for i = 0 to argc - first_arg - 1. On the last iteration (i = argc - first_arg - 1), it accesses argv[first_arg + (argc - first_arg - 1) + 1] = argv[argc], which is NULL by the C standard. Passing NULL to xstrdup (which wraps strdup) causes a NULL pointer dereference. Additionally, process->args_len is set to argc instead of the actual number of arguments copied (argc - first_arg - 1), which can mislead downstream consumers into reading uninitialized/NULL entries as valid arguments.

step_by_step_execution.description: argp_parse processes argc/argv; first_arg is set to the index of the first non-option argument.
crun_assert_n_args checks argc - first_arg >= 2 (since exec_options.process is NULL in this branch). The check passes (4 - 1 = 3 >= 2).
Execution enters the else branch. process->args_len is set to argc (4), and process->args is allocated with (argc + 1) = 5 slots.

state.value: 4 (argc, incorrect — should be number of command args)

step_by_step_execution.description: The for loop iterates i from 0 to argc - first_arg - 1 = 2. On each iteration it accesses argv[first_arg + i + 1].
On the last iteration (i=2), argv[first_arg + 2 + 1] = argv[4] = argv[argc] is accessed. By the C standard, argv[argc] is NULL.
xstrdup(NULL) is called, which internally calls strdup(NULL), resulting in a NULL pointer dereference (undefined behavior / crash).

commit_message:
Fix security vulnerability with LSTMBlockCellOp

PiperOrigin-RevId: 446028341
cve_desc: TensorFlow is an open source platform for machine learning. Prior to versions 2.9.0, 2.8.1, 2.7.2, and 2.6.4, the implementation of `tf.raw_ops.LSTMBlockCell` does not fully validate the input arguments. This results in a `CHECK`-failure which can be used to trigger a denial of service attack. The code does not validate the ranks of any of the arguments to this API call. This results in `CHECK`-failures when the elements of the tensor are accessed. Versions 2.9.0, 2.8.1, 2.7.2, and 2.6.4 contain a patch for this issue.

vulnerabilities.description: The tensors wci, wcf, and wco are used as vec<T>() (1-D vectors expected to have cell_size elements) without any shape validation. An attacker can supply tensors of arbitrary size for these inputs, causing out-of-bounds memory reads/writes inside the LSTMBlockCellFprop functor which assumes they each have cell_size elements.

step_by_step_execution.description: Input tensors are read from the OpKernelContext, including wci_tensor, wcf_tensor, and wco_tensor.

state.value: attacker-controlled tensor, e.g. shape [] (scalar) or shape [1]
attacker-controlled tensor, e.g. shape [] (scalar) or shape [1]
attacker-controlled tensor, e.g. shape [] (scalar) or shape [1]

step_by_step_execution.description: cell_size is derived from cs_prev_tensor->dim_size(1). Various shape checks are performed for cs_prev, h_prev, w, and b tensors, but NO shape checks are performed for wci, wcf, or wco.

The functor LSTMBlockCellFprop is invoked with wci_tensor->vec<T>(), wcf_tensor->vec<T>(), wco_tensor->vec<T>(). The functor indexes these vectors up to cell_size elements, but the actual tensors may have fewer elements.
Inside the functor, accessing index >= 1 on a vector of size 1 results in an out-of-bounds read or write, leading to heap buffer overflow.

commit_message: Clean up handling CAP LS
cve_desc: Directory traversal vulnerability in the client in HexChat 2.11.0 allows remote IRC servers to read or modify arbitrary files via a .. (dot dot) in the server name.

commit_message: Fix handling of APNG with 0 delay_den (#313)
cve_desc: libjxl v0.3.7 is affected by a Divide By Zero in issue in lib/extras/codec_apng.cc jxl::DecodeImageAPNG(). When encoding a malicous APNG file using cjxl, an attacker can trigger a denial of service.

vulnerabilities.description: Integer overflow in the computation of `rowbytes = w * 4` and `imagesize = h * rowbytes`. Both `w`, `h`, `rowbytes`, and `imagesize` are `unsigned int` (32-bit). If `w` is large (e.g., >= 2^30 + 1) yet still passes the `cMaxPNGSize` check, `w * 4` overflows, resulting in a very small allocation. The PNG decoder then writes full image rows into the undersized heap buffer, causing a massive heap buffer overflow.

step_by_step_execution.description: The function reads the PNG signature and IHDR chunk from the input bytes.
Width and height are read from the IHDR chunk as 32-bit unsigned integers.
The check `w > cMaxPNGSize || h > cMaxPNGSize` is evaluated. Assuming cMaxPNGSize is large enough (e.g. 2^31-1), the check passes and execution continues.
`rowbytes = w * 4` is computed. Since both are unsigned int (32-bit), this overflows: 0x40000001 * 4 = 0x100000004, truncated to 0x4.
`imagesize = h * rowbytes` is computed with the overflowed rowbytes value.
`frameRaw.p = new unsigned char[imagesize]` allocates only 8 bytes on the heap.
Row pointers are set: `frameRaw.rows[j] = frameRaw.p + j * rowbytes`. These point within or very close to the tiny 8-byte buffer.

processing_start and processing_data are called. The PNG decoder processes the image data with actual width 0x40000001 and writes w*4 = ~4GB of pixel data per row into the 8-byte buffer, causing a heap buffer overflow.

commit_message:
xfrm: policy: check policy direction value

The 'dir' parameter in xfrm_migrate() is a user-controlled byte which is used
as an array index. This can lead to an out-of-bound access, kernel lockup and
DoS. Add a check for the 'dir' value.

This fixes CVE-2017-11600.

References: https://bugzilla.redhat.com/show_bug.cgi?id=1474928
Fixes: 80c9abaabf42 ("[XFRM]: Extension for dynamic update of endpoint address(es)")
Cc: <stable@vger.kernel.org> # v2.6.21-rc1
Reported-by: "bo Zhang" <zhangbo5891001@gmail.com>
Signed-off-by: Vladis Dronov <vdronov@redhat.com>
Signed-off-by: Steffen Klassert <steffen.klassert@secunet.com>
cve_desc: net/xfrm/xfrm_policy.c in the Linux kernel through 4.12.3, when CONFIG_XFRM_MIGRATE is enabled, does not ensure that the dir value of xfrm_userpolicy_id is XFRM_POLICY_MAX or less, which allows local users to cause a denial of service (out-of-bounds access) or possibly have unspecified other impact via an XFRM_MSG_MIGRATE xfrm Netlink message.

commit_message: Merge pull request from GHSA-7fw8-54cv-r7pm
cve_desc: PJSIP is a free and open source multimedia communication library written in C language implementing standard based protocols such as SIP, SDP, RTP, STUN, TURN, and ICE. In versions 2.11.1 and prior, parsing an incoming SIP message that contains a malformed multipart can potentially cause out-of-bound read access. This issue affects all PJSIP users that accept SIP multipart. The patch is available as commit in the `master` branch. There are no known workarounds.
