commit_message:
Fixed Array.prototype.slice() with slow "this" argument.

Previously, when "this" argument was not a fast array, but the "deleted" array
was a fast array, the "deleted" array may be left in uninitialized state if
"this" argument had gaps.

This fix is to ensure that "deleted" is properly initialized.

This fixes #485 issue on Github.
cve_desc: Nginx NJS v0.7.2 was discovered to contain a segmentation violation in the function njs_value_own_enumerate at src/njs_value.c.

vulnerabilities.description: TOCTOU (Time-of-Check Time-of-Use) vulnerability: The object's length is read early via njs_object_length(), but subsequent calls to njs_value_to_integer() can trigger JavaScript callbacks (e.g., valueOf()) that modify the array (shrinking it or changing its length). The stale 'length' value is then used to compute 'start' and 'delete', which can be out-of-bounds relative to the array's actual (now smaller) size. This leads to out-of-bounds reads in the fast-array copy-to-deleted loop and out-of-bounds writes in the memcpy that inserts new items.

code_snippets:
ret = njs_array_copy_within(vm, this, start + items, start + delete,
                                        array->length - (start + delete), 0);

step_by_step_execution.description: njs_object_length() is called, retrieving the array's current length into the 'length' variable.

state.value: 100 (for example, a fast array with 100 elements)

step_by_step_execution.description: njs_value_to_integer() is called on args[1] (the start argument). If args[1] is an object with a valueOf() callback, that callback executes arbitrary JavaScript. The callback shrinks the array (e.g., sets arr.length = 0 or pops all elements).
start is clamped: start = njs_min(start, length) = njs_min(0, 100) = 0. delete is computed as length - start = 100 (for nargs==2 case).
njs_array_alloc allocates 'deleted' array with 100 elements. The fast array check passes (njs_is_fast_array(this) && deleted->object.fast_array).

The fast-array copy loop executes: for (i = 0, n = 0; i < 100; i++, n++) deleted->start[i] = array->start[n]. Since array->length is 0 (the array was shrunk), reading array->start[n] for n in [0, 99] is an out-of-bounds read from the array's backing buffer.

In the second fast-array block, array->length (0) - (start + delete) = 0 - 100 = -100, causing njs_array_copy_within to operate with incorrect parameters. Then array->length += delta modifies the length incorrectly, and the memcpy writes items at array->start[start], potentially out-of-bounds of the (now empty) allocated buffer.

state.value: -100 (underflow as int64_t, or huge value if unsigned)

commit_message: Fix possible buffer overflow
cve_desc: None

vulnerabilities.description: Integer overflow in the size calculation for o_malloc. The sum rpid_hash_len+client_data_hash_len+credential_id_len+cert_x_len+cert_y_len+2 can wrap around to a small value if the caller-controlled size_t parameters are large enough, resulting in a small heap allocation followed by large memcpy operations that write far beyond the allocated buffer.

step_by_step_execution.description: Function is called with attacker-influenced size parameters, e.g. credential_id_len = SIZE_MAX - 100, and other lengths being small but nonzero.
Code reaches the o_malloc call. The sum rpid_hash_len+client_data_hash_len+credential_id_len+cert_x_len+cert_y_len+2 overflows size_t, wrapping around to a small value (e.g., around 30 bytes).
memcpy copies rpid_hash (32 bytes) into data_signed at offset 1. This already nearly fills the small buffer.
memcpy copies client_data_hash (32 bytes) into data_signed at offset 33, writing well past the ~30 byte buffer boundary.
Subsequent memcpy calls for credential_id, cert_x, and cert_y continue writing far beyond the allocated heap buffer.

state.value: continues growing far past allocation size

commit_message:
Don't do any work when reshaping 0 elements sparse tensor.

If reshaping to 0 elements tensor, check that input has no elements.
If reshaping no elements input, check that output has no elements.

PiperOrigin-RevId: 388296986
Change-Id: Iadc9fe7252e14313ca987e69bf0d7042fd10232a
cve_desc: TensorFlow is an end-to-end open source platform for machine learning. In affected versions the implementation of `tf.raw_ops.SparseReshape` can be made to trigger an integral division by 0 exception. The [implementation](https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorflow/blob/8d72537c6abf5a44103b57b9c2e22c14f5f49698/tensorflow/core/kernels/reshape_util.cc#L176-L181) calls the reshaping functor whenever there is at least an index in the input but does not check that shape of the input or the target shape have both a non-zero number of elements. The [reshape functor](https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorflow/blob/8d72537c6abf5a44103b57b9c2e22c14f5f49698/tensorflow/core/kernels/reshape_util.cc#L40-L78) blindly divides by the dimensions of the target shape. Hence, if this is not checked, code will result in a division by 0. We have patched the issue in GitHub commit 4923de56ec94fff7770df259ab7f2288a74feb41. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.6.0. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.5.1 as this is the other affected version.

vulnerabilities.description: Integer overflow in the computation of `product` when multiplying target shape dimensions. An attacker can supply large dimension values in `target_shape_in` that cause `product` (int64_t) to overflow, wrapping around. This corrupted value can then bypass the subsequent checks (`product > 0`, `product * missing == dense_size`, `output_shape.num_elements() == dense_size`), leading to an incorrectly sized output tensor and potential out-of-bounds memory access in the subsequent `ReshapeSparseTensorFunctor` call.

step_by_step_execution.description: The function receives `target_shape_in` containing attacker-controlled dimension values, e.g., {-1, 2^40, 2^40, 2^40}. `output_rank` is set to the number of elements in target_shape_in.
`dense_size` is computed from `input_shape_in`. For example, a small sparse tensor with `dense_size = 8`.
Loop iteration d=0: `size = -1`, so `unknown_index` is set to 0, `output_shape.AddDim(1)`.
Loop iteration d=1: `size = 2^40` (passes `size >= 0` check). `product *= 2^40`.
Loop iteration d=2: `size = 2^40`. `product *= 2^40`. Now product = 2^80, which overflows int64_t (max ~2^63-1), wrapping to some value.
Loop iteration d=3: `size = 2^40`. `product *= 2^40` again. The overflowed product could wrap to a small positive value, e.g., 8.
After the loop, `unknown_index != -1` so we enter the branch. The check `product > 0` passes since the wrapped value is positive (8). `missing = dense_size / product = 8 / 8 = 1`.
The check `product * missing == dense_size` evaluates to `8 * 1 == 8`, which passes. `output_shape.set_dim(0, 1)` sets the unknown dimension to 1.

The check `output_shape.num_elements() == dense_size` may also be bypassed if TensorShape's internal computation similarly overflows, or this specific combination is crafted to match. The output tensor `result_indices` is allocated with shape {nnz, output_rank}, and `ReshapeSparseTensorFunctor` operates using the corrupted `output_shape`, causing incorrect index computations that can write out-of-bounds values into the result tensor.

commit_message:
Don't do any work when reshaping 0 elements sparse tensor.

If reshaping to 0 elements tensor, check that input has no elements.
If reshaping no elements input, check that output has no elements.

PiperOrigin-RevId: 388296986
Change-Id: Iadc9fe7252e14313ca987e69bf0d7042fd10232a
cve_desc: TensorFlow is an end-to-end open source platform for machine learning. In affected versions the implementation of `tf.raw_ops.SparseReshape` can be made to trigger an integral division by 0 exception. The [implementation](https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorflow/blob/8d72537c6abf5a44103b57b9c2e22c14f5f49698/tensorflow/core/kernels/reshape_util.cc#L176-L181) calls the reshaping functor whenever there is at least an index in the input but does not check that shape of the input or the target shape have both a non-zero number of elements. The [reshape functor](https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorflow/blob/8d72537c6abf5a44103b57b9c2e22c14f5f49698/tensorflow/core/kernels/reshape_util.cc#L40-L78) blindly divides by the dimensions of the target shape. Hence, if this is not checked, code will result in a division by 0. We have patched the issue in GitHub commit 4923de56ec94fff7770df259ab7f2288a74feb41. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.6.0. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.5.1 as this is the other affected version.

vulnerabilities.description: The variable `product` accumulates the multiplication of user-controlled dimension sizes from `target_shape` without any overflow check. An attacker can provide multiple large positive int64 dimension sizes that, when multiplied together, overflow the int64_t `product` variable. This overflowed value can then be used in subsequent calculations (`dense_size / product`, `product * missing == dense_size`) which may also overflow, causing the validation checks to pass with incorrect values. This can lead to an incorrectly shaped output tensor and out-of-bounds memory access in the subsequent `ReshapeSparseTensorFunctor` call.

step_by_step_execution.description: The function reads target_shape_in, which contains attacker-controlled dimension values. output_rank is determined by the number of elements in target_shape_in.

state.value: e.g. 3 (attacker controls number of target dimensions)

step_by_step_execution.description: The loop iterates over target dimensions. Suppose dimensions are chosen such that two are very large (e.g., 2^40 and 2^40) and one is -1 (unknown). The size >= 0 check passes for the large values.

state.value: e.g., 2^40 for the first large dimension

step_by_step_execution.description: On the next large dimension, product *= size overflows int64_t (2^40 * 2^40 = 2^80 which wraps around in int64).

state.value: Overflowed value, e.g. wraps to a small positive number due to signed overflow (undefined behavior in C++)

step_by_step_execution.description: Since unknown_index != -1, the code computes missing = dense_size / product. With the overflowed small product, missing becomes a large value.

state.value: dense_size / (small overflowed product) — a potentially very large value
The number of elements in the original sparse tensor's dense shape

commit_message:
swtpm: Check header size indicator against expected size (CID 375869)

This fix addresses Coverity issue CID 375869.

Check the header size indicated in the header of the state against the
expected size and return an error code in case the header size indicator
is different. There was only one header size so far since blobheader was
introduced, so we don't need to deal with different sizes.

Without this fix a specially craft header could have cause out-of-bounds
accesses on the byte array containing the swtpm's state.

Signed-off-by: Stefan Berger <stefanb@linux.ibm.com>
cve_desc: swtpm is a libtpms-based TPM emulator with socket, character device, and Linux CUSE interface. Versions prior to 0.5.3, 0.6.2, and 0.7.1 are vulnerable to out-of-bounds read. A specially crafted header of swtpm's state, where the blobheader's hdrsize indicator has an invalid value, may cause an out-of-bounds access when the byte array representing the state of the TPM is accessed. This will likely crash swtpm or prevent it from starting since the state cannot be understood. Users should upgrade to swtpm v0.5.3, v0.6.2, or v0.7.1 to receive a patch. There are currently no known workarounds.

vulnerabilities.description: The length check uses `sizeof(bh)` where `bh` is a `blobheader *` (a pointer). This evaluates to the size of a pointer (4 or 8 bytes), not the size of the `blobheader` structure. If `length` is smaller than `sizeof(blobheader)` but larger than or equal to `sizeof(blobheader *)`, the check passes, and subsequent accesses to `bh->totlen`, `bh->min_version`, `bh->version`, `bh->hdrsize`, `bh->flags` read beyond the bounds of the `data` buffer.

step_by_step_execution.description: Function is called with a `data` buffer of a small length, e.g., `length = 8` on a 64-bit system where `sizeof(blobheader)` is larger than 8.

state.value: pointer to a buffer of 8 bytes

step_by_step_execution.description: `bh` is set to `(blobheader *)data`. The guard check compares `length < sizeof(bh)`. Since `bh` is a pointer, `sizeof(bh)` is `sizeof(blobheader *)` which is 8 on a 64-bit system. 8 < 8 is false, so the check passes.

The code accesses `bh->totlen`, `bh->min_version`, `bh->version`, `bh->hdrsize`, and `bh->flags`, which are fields within the `blobheader` struct. Since the buffer is only 8 bytes but the struct is larger, these accesses read beyond the allocated buffer, resulting in out-of-bounds reads.

Without this fix a specially craft header could have cause out-of-bounds
accesses on the byte array containing the swtpm's state.

Signed-off-by: Stefan Berger <stefanb@linux.ibm.com>
cve_desc: swtpm is a libtpms-based TPM emulator with socket, character device, and Linux CUSE interface. Versions prior to 0.5.3, 0.6.2, and 0.7.1 are vulnerable to out-of-bounds read. A specially crafted header of swtpm's state, where the blobheader's hdrsize indicator has an invalid value, may cause an out-of-bounds access when the byte array representing the state of the TPM is accessed. This will likely crash swtpm or prevent it from starting since the state cannot be understood. Users should upgrade to swtpm v0.5.3, v0.6.2, or v0.7.1 to receive a patch. There are currently no known workarounds.

vulnerabilities.description: The initial length check uses `sizeof(bh)` where `bh` is a `blobheader *` (pointer). This evaluates to the size of a pointer (4 or 8 bytes depending on architecture), not the size of the `blobheader` structure. If the `blobheader` struct is larger than a pointer, the check is insufficient and subsequent accesses to `bh->totlen`, `bh->min_version`, `bh->hdrsize`, `bh->version`, `bh->flags` can read beyond the bounds of the `data` buffer.

step_by_step_execution.description: Function is called with a `data` buffer whose `length` is greater than or equal to sizeof(pointer) but smaller than sizeof(blobheader). For example, on a 64-bit system, length could be 8 while blobheader is, say, 20+ bytes.
The guard check `length < sizeof(bh)` evaluates to `8 < 8` which is false, so execution continues past the check.

The code dereferences `bh->totlen`, `bh->min_version`, `bh->hdrsize`, `bh->version`, and `bh->flags`, all of which may reside at offsets beyond the 8 valid bytes, causing out-of-bounds reads on the `data` buffer.

offsets potentially beyond 8 bytes into data buffer

commit_message:
Validate dimensions of input tensor in `FractionalAvgPoolGrad`

PiperOrigin-RevId: 388286227
Change-Id: Ieb7566155e92acc8993a2212c76deacadc0edc8a
cve_desc: TensorFlow is an end-to-end open source platform for machine learning. In affected versions the implementation for `tf.raw_ops.FractionalAvgPoolGrad` can be tricked into accessing data outside of bounds of heap allocated buffers. The [implementation](https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorflow/blob/f24faa153ad31a4b51578f8181d3aaab77a1ddeb/tensorflow/core/kernels/fractional_avg_pool_op.cc#L205) does not validate that the input tensor is non-empty. Thus, code constructs an empty `EigenDoubleMatrixMap` and then accesses this buffer with indices that are outside of the empty area. We have patched the issue in GitHub commit 0f931751fb20f565c4e94aa6df58d54a003cdb30. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.6.0. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.5.1, TensorFlow 2.4.3, and TensorFlow 2.3.4, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

code_snippets: const int64_t in_index = (b * in_rows + in_r) * in_cols + in_c;
vulnerabilities.description: The values from row_seq_tensor_flat and col_seq_tensor_flat are used directly as indices (in_row_start, in_col_start) without any bounds checking against [0, in_rows) or [0, in_cols). A malicious user can supply negative or excessively large sequence values, causing out-of-bounds read/write on the in_backprop_tensor_temp buffer via the computed in_index.

step_by_step_execution.description: User provides orig_input_tensor_shape with valid dimensions, e.g. [1, 4, 4, 1], and out_backprop with shape [1, 2, 2, 1].
User provides row_seq_tensor = [-10, 2, 3] (3 elements > out_rows=2, passing the check). col_seq_tensor = [0, 1, 2].
In the loop with r=0, in_row_start is read from row_seq_tensor_flat(0) which is -10. in_row_end = min(row_seq_tensor_flat(1)-1, 3) = min(1, 3) = 1. Since -10 <= 1, the inner loop executes.
Inner loop iterates in_r from -10 to 1. For in_r=-10, in_c=0: in_index = (0*4 + (-10))*4 + 0 = -40. This is a negative index.
coeffRef(d, -40) accesses memory before the start of the in_backprop_tensor_temp buffer, causing out-of-bounds write.

vulnerabilities.description: There is no check that in_batch == out_batch or in_depth == out_depth. Additionally, when in_row_start equals 0 and in_row_end is clamped to in_max_row_index but in_col_start > in_max_col_index (after clamping in_col_end < in_col_start), num_elements_in_pooling_cell can become zero or negative, leading to division by zero. For example, if row_seq values are crafted so that start > clamped end but a different dimension causes the loop to still execute.

step_by_step_execution.description: User provides orig_input_tensor_shape = [1, 1, 1, 1] so in_rows=1, in_cols=1. out_backprop has shape [1, 1, 1, 1].
User provides row_seq_tensor = [5, 6] and col_seq_tensor = [0, 1]. row_seq_tensor has 2 elements > out_rows=1, passes check.

For r=0: in_row_start = 5, in_row_end = min(5, 0) = 0 (non-overlapping: 6-1=5, then min(5,0)=0). num_elements = (0 - 5 + 1) * (0 - 0 + 1) = -4. The inner loop condition in_r <= 0 with in_r starting at 5 is false, so loop doesn't execute. However with carefully crafted negative start values the division could be reached with zero denominator.

commit_message:
Fix null deref in xnu.kernelcache ##crash

* Reported by @xshad3 via huntr.dev
cve_desc: NULL Pointer Dereference in GitHub repository radareorg/radare2 prior to 5.6.0.

vulnerabilities.description: In the `beach` error-handling label, `obj->cache_buf = NULL` is executed unconditionally. However, `obj` can be NULL at this point: if `prelink_range` is NULL (the first `goto beach`), or if `R_NEW0` fails (the second `goto beach`), `obj` remains its initialized value of `NULL`. Dereferencing a NULL pointer causes undefined behavior / a crash.

step_by_step_execution.description: Function entry: `obj` is not yet declared/initialized.
`fbuf` is created via `r_buf_ref`, `main_mach0` is created successfully.
`rebase_info` is obtained. `obj` is declared and initialized to NULL.
`prelink_range` is obtained via `get_prelink_info_range_from_mach0`. If it returns NULL, the code executes `goto beach`.
At the `beach` label, `r_buf_free(fbuf)` is called, then `obj->cache_buf = NULL` is executed with `obj` being NULL, causing a null pointer dereference.

commit_message:
FTP: Fix buffer overflow in PORT parser, reported by Aaron Esau

Signed-off-by: Joachim Nilsson <troglobit@gmail.com>
cve_desc: An unauthenticated stack-based buffer overflow vulnerability in common.c's handle_PORT in uftpd FTP server versions 2.10 and earlier can be abused to cause a crash and could potentially lead to remote code execution.

vulnerabilities.description: The return value of sscanf() is never checked. If the input string `str` does not contain six comma-separated integers (e.g., it contains fewer fields or malformed input), some of the local variables a, b, c, d, e, f will remain uninitialized. If sscanf parses at least the first four values correctly (a-d), inet_aton() may succeed on the constructed address string, but `e` and/or `f` will be used uninitialized to compute `ctrl->data_port`, leading to use of uninitialized memory.

step_by_step_execution.description: The function is called with a `str` argument that contains only four comma-separated integers, e.g., "127,0,0,1".
sscanf parses the string and successfully reads only 4 integers. Variables e and f are never written to and remain uninitialized. The return value (4) is discarded.
snprintf constructs the address string "127.0.0.1" from a, b, c, d. This is a valid IPv4 address.
inet_aton() succeeds because "127.0.0.1" is a valid address, so the function does not return early.
The uninitialized values of e and f are used to compute ctrl->data_port, resulting in an indeterminate port value derived from whatever was on the stack.

state.value: indeterminate (e * 256 + f with uninitialized e, f)

commit_message:
vm.c: create break object before clearing GC arena.

Otherwise it possibly cause use-after-free.
cve_desc: Use-After-Free in str_escape in mruby/mruby in GitHub repository mruby/mruby prior to 3.2. Possible arbitrary code execution if being exploited.

code_snippets: m = prepare_missing(mrb, recv, mid, &cls, a, &c, blk, 0);
vulnerabilities.description: In the OP_SEND/OP_SENDB handler (L_SENDB_SYM block), the local variable `blk` is declared but never initialized before being used. It is passed to `prepare_missing()` and later checked with `mrb_proc_p(blk)`. Since `blk` contains indeterminate stack data, this can lead to operating on an arbitrary/garbage mrb_value, potentially causing memory corruption or information disclosure when `mrb_proc_ptr(blk)` dereferences the uninitialized value.

code_snippets: m = prepare_missing(mrb, recv, mid, &cls, a, &c, blk, 0);

step_by_step_execution.description: Execution enters the L_SENDB_SYM block (e.g., via OP_SEND, OP_SENDB, OP_SETIDX, or L_SEND_SYM goto).

state.value: set from syms[b] or a built-in symbol

step_by_step_execution.description: Local variables `recv` and `blk` are declared on the stack. `blk` is never assigned a value, so it holds indeterminate/garbage data from the C stack.
ARGUMENT_NORMALIZE is called with (a, &c, insn). `recv` is then assigned from regs[a]. However, `blk` remains uninitialized.
mrb_method_search_vm looks up the method. If MRB_METHOD_UNDEF_P(m) is true (method not found), prepare_missing is called with the uninitialized `blk` as an argument.

If the method is a C function (MRB_METHOD_CFUNC_P(m) is true), after the call completes and no exception occurs, the code checks `mrb_proc_p(blk)`. Since `blk` is uninitialized, this type check operates on garbage data.

If the garbage value in `blk` happens to look like a proc type tag, `mrb_proc_ptr(blk)` extracts a pointer from the garbage value, and then dereferences it to check flags and access the environment. This constitutes a read of arbitrary memory based on uninitialized data.

state.value: pointer derived from uninitialized blk, potentially arbitrary address

commit_message:
vm.c: create break object before clearing GC arena.

Otherwise it possibly cause use-after-free.
cve_desc: Use-After-Free in str_escape in mruby/mruby in GitHub repository mruby/mruby prior to 3.2. Possible arbitrary code execution if being exploited.

vulnerabilities.description: In the L_SENDB_SYM block (reached from OP_SEND, OP_SENDB, OP_SSEND, OP_SSENDB, OP_GETIDX, OP_SETIDX, and math/comparison opcodes), the local variable 'blk' is declared but never initialized before being used. It is passed to 'prepare_missing()' and later used in 'mrb_proc_p(blk)' to check its type and potentially dereference it as a proc pointer. Using an uninitialized stack variable can lead to information disclosure or memory corruption depending on the garbage value on the stack.

code_snippets: m = prepare_missing(mrb, recv, mid, &cls, a, &c, blk, 0);

step_by_step_execution.description: Execution enters the L_SENDB_SYM label, for example via OP_SEND opcode or via a fallback from OP_GETIDX/OP_ADD/etc. through L_SEND_SYM.
Local variables 'recv' and 'blk' are declared on the stack. 'blk' is never assigned a value.

state.value: uninitialized (contains whatever garbage was on the stack)

step_by_step_execution.description: ARGUMENT_NORMALIZE is called, then recv is assigned from regs[a]. blk remains uninitialized.
mrb_method_search_vm is called. If the method is not found (MRB_METHOD_UNDEF_P(m) is true), prepare_missing is called with the uninitialized 'blk' value as an argument.

After a C function method returns, the code checks 'if (mrb_proc_p(blk))' which reads the uninitialized blk to check its type tag. If the garbage value happens to match MRB_TT_PROC, it will call mrb_proc_ptr(blk), dereferencing a garbage pointer.

state.value: uninitialized, type-checked and potentially dereferenced as a pointer

commit_message: fiber.c: should pack 15+ arguments in an array.
cve_desc: NULL Pointer Dereference in GitHub repository mruby/mruby prior to 3.2.

code_snippets: mrb_stack_extend(mrb, len+2); /* for receiver and (optional) block */
vulnerabilities.description: Integer overflow in `len+2` when `len` is a very large `mrb_int` value (e.g., MRB_INT_MAX). The addition `len+2` wraps around to a small or negative number, causing `mrb_stack_extend` to allocate an insufficient buffer. The subsequent loop then writes `len` (a huge number) of elements starting at `c->stbase+1`, resulting in a heap buffer overflow.

step_by_step_execution.description: Function is called with a very large `len` value, e.g. `len = MRB_INT_MAX`.
Fiber status checks pass, and `status` is `MRB_FIBER_CREATED`, so the created-fiber branch is entered.

`mrb_stack_extend(mrb, len+2)` is called, but `len+2` overflows. For 32-bit mrb_int, `2147483647 + 2` wraps to `-2147483647`, a negative value. The function likely treats this as a no-op or very small allocation.
`b` is set to `c->stbase+1` and `e` is set to `b + len`, which points far beyond the allocated stack buffer.
The while loop `while (b<e)` executes, writing `len` elements from `a` into memory starting at `c->stbase+1`, far exceeding the actual allocated stack size, causing a heap buffer overflow.

state.value: Incremented from c->stbase+1 past allocated boundary

commit_message:
Fix security vulnerability with LSTMBlockCellOp

PiperOrigin-RevId: 446028341
cve_desc: TensorFlow is an open source platform for machine learning. Prior to versions 2.9.0, 2.8.1, 2.7.2, and 2.6.4, the implementation of `tf.raw_ops.LSTMBlockCell` does not fully validate the input arguments. This results in a `CHECK`-failure which can be used to trigger a denial of service attack. The code does not validate the ranks of any of the arguments to this API call. This results in `CHECK`-failures when the elements of the tensor are accessed. Versions 2.9.0, 2.8.1, 2.7.2, and 2.6.4 contain a patch for this issue.

vulnerabilities.description: The rank checks (e.g., x_tensor->dims() == 2, cs_prev_tensor->dims() == 2) are performed AFTER dim_size(0) and dim_size(1) are already called on those tensors. If an attacker provides a tensor with fewer than 2 dimensions (e.g., a scalar or rank-1 tensor), the calls to dim_size(1) will access out-of-bounds metadata, leading to undefined behavior before the rank validation occurs.

step_by_step_execution.description: Input tensors are read from the context. An attacker provides x_tensor as a rank-0 (scalar) or rank-1 tensor.
The code immediately accesses x_tensor->dim_size(0) and x_tensor->dim_size(1) without first checking the rank.

state.value: x_tensor->dim_size(1) — out-of-bounds access on tensor dimension metadata since the tensor has fewer than 2 dimensions

step_by_step_execution.description: Similarly, cs_prev_tensor->dim_size(1) is accessed without verifying cs_prev_tensor has rank >= 2.

state.value: cs_prev_tensor->dim_size(1) — out-of-bounds access if cs_prev_tensor has rank < 2

step_by_step_execution.description: The rank checks (x_tensor->dims() == 2, cs_prev_tensor->dims() == 2, etc.) only appear much later in the function, after the dimension values have already been used for further validation and memory allocation.

state.value: Contain values from out-of-bounds reads; already used in allocations and subsequent checks

vulnerabilities.description: The sizes of wci_tensor, wcf_tensor, and wco_tensor are never validated against cell_size. Only their ranks are checked (must be 1). When these tensors are passed to the LSTM kernel as vec<T>() and indexed up to cell_size, an attacker can provide tensors with fewer elements than cell_size, causing an out-of-bounds read/write.

step_by_step_execution.description: An attacker provides wci_tensor, wcf_tensor, or wco_tensor as rank-1 tensors with a size smaller than cell_size (e.g., size 1 while cell_size is large).
The code checks that wci_tensor->dims() == 1, wcf_tensor->dims() == 1, wco_tensor->dims() == 1 — these pass since they are rank 1. No check on their size vs cell_size exists.
The tensors are passed as vec<T>() to LSTMBlockCellFprop, which will index into them using indices up to cell_size-1, causing out-of-bounds memory access.

state.value: 1 element — reads beyond this are out-of-bounds

commit_message:
HID: elo: fix memory leak in elo_probe

When hid_parse() in elo_probe() fails, it forgets to call usb_put_dev to
decrease the refcount.

Fix this by adding usb_put_dev() in the error handling code of elo_probe().

Fixes: fbf42729d0e9 ("HID: elo: update the reference count of the usb device structure")
Reported-by: syzkaller <syzkaller@googlegroups.com>
Signed-off-by: Dongliang Mu <mudongliangabcd@gmail.com>
Signed-off-by: Jiri Kosina <jkosina@suse.cz>
cve_desc: In drivers/hid/hid-elo.c in the Linux kernel before 5.16.11, a memory leak exists for a certain hid_parse error condition.

commit_message: Merge pull request from GHSA-26j7-ww69-c4qj
cve_desc: PJSIP is a free and open source multimedia communication library written in C language implementing standard based protocols such as SIP, SDP, RTP, STUN, TURN, and ICE. In versions prior to and including 2.12.1 a stack buffer overflow vulnerability affects PJSIP users that use STUN in their applications, either by: setting a STUN server in their account/media config in PJSUA/PJSUA2 level, or directly using `pjlib-util/stun_simple` API. A patch is available in commit 450baca which should be included in the next release. There are no known workarounds for this issue.

vulnerabilities.description: The function dereferences `buf` as a `pjstun_msg_hdr*` and reads `msg->hdr->type` and `msg->hdr->length` before verifying that `buf_len` is at least `sizeof(pjstun_msg_hdr)`. If `buf_len` is smaller than the header size, these reads are out-of-bounds. The length validation (`msg_len != buf_len - sizeof(pjstun_msg_hdr)`) happens after the reads, and when `buf_len < sizeof(pjstun_msg_hdr)`, the unsigned subtraction `buf_len - sizeof(pjstun_msg_hdr)` wraps around to a large value, but the out-of-bounds reads have already occurred.

step_by_step_execution.description: Function is called with a buffer `buf` whose size `buf_len` is less than `sizeof(pjstun_msg_hdr)` (e.g., buf_len = 2 while the header struct is typically 20 bytes).
`msg->hdr` is set to point to `buf`, then `msg->hdr->type` is read. Since `buf_len` is smaller than the struct, this reads past the end of the allocated buffer.

state.value: (pjstun_msg_hdr*)buf, pointing to a buffer of only 2 bytes

step_by_step_execution.description: If the msg_type happens to match one of the valid STUN types (no error returned), execution continues to read `msg->hdr->length`, which is another out-of-bounds read from the undersized buffer.

The length check `msg_len != buf_len - sizeof(pjstun_msg_hdr)` is reached, but the damage (OOB reads) has already been done. The subtraction `buf_len - sizeof(pjstun_msg_hdr)` also wraps around since both are unsigned.

state.value: wraps to a very large value (e.g., SIZE_MAX - 17)

commit_message:
ALSA: pcm: oss: Fix race at SNDCTL_DSP_SYNC

There is a small race window at snd_pcm_oss_sync() that is called from
OSS PCM SNDCTL_DSP_SYNC ioctl; namely the function calls
snd_pcm_oss_make_ready() at first, then takes the params_lock mutex
for the rest.  When the stream is set up again by another thread
between them, it leads to inconsistency, and may result in unexpected
results such as NULL dereference of OSS buffer as a fuzzer spotted
recently.

The fix is simply to cover snd_pcm_oss_make_ready() call into the same
params_lock mutex with snd_pcm_oss_make_ready_locked() variant.

Reported-and-tested-by: butt3rflyh4ck <butterflyhuangxx@gmail.com>
Reviewed-by: Jaroslav Kysela <perex@perex.cz>
Cc: <stable@vger.kernel.org>
Link: https://lore.kernel.org/r/CAFcO6XN7JDM4xSXGhtusQfS2mSBcx50VJKwQpCq=WeLt57aaZA@mail.gmail.com
Link: https://lore.kernel.org/r/20220905060714.22549-1-tiwai@suse.de
Signed-off-by: Takashi Iwai <tiwai@suse.de>
cve_desc: A race condition flaw was found in the Linux kernel sound subsystem due to improper locking. It could lead to a NULL pointer dereference while handling the SNDCTL_DSP_SYNC ioctl. A privileged local user (root or member of the audio group) could use this flaw to crash the system, resulting in a denial of service condition

commit_message:
Fix overflow CHECK issue with `tf.raw_ops.UnsortedSegmentJoin`.

PiperOrigin-RevId: 370766155
Change-Id: I33e7c6626224e1060a8a4ab51ad5d861c6d4c63e
cve_desc: TensorFlow is an end-to-end open source platform for machine learning. An attacker can cause a denial of service by controlling the values of `num_segments` tensor argument for `UnsortedSegmentJoin`. This is because the implementation(https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorflow/blob/a2a607db15c7cd01d754d37e5448d72a13491bdb/tensorflow/core/kernels/unsorted_segment_join_op.cc#L92-L93) assumes that the `num_segments` tensor is a valid scalar. Since the tensor is empty the `CHECK` involved in `.scalar<T>()()` that checks that the number of elements is exactly 1 will be invalidated and this would result in process termination. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.5.0. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.4.2, TensorFlow 2.3.3, TensorFlow 2.2.3 and TensorFlow 2.1.4, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

vulnerabilities.description: num_segments is read from user-controlled input but is never validated to be non-negative. If num_segments is negative, it is passed directly to GetOutputShape which constructs a TensorShape with a negative dimension. This can cause an invalid memory allocation and subsequent out-of-bounds memory access when the output tensor is used. The segment_id validation loop (which checks flat_segment_id(i) < num_segments) does not catch this if the segment_id tensor is empty (e.g., shape [0] which has dims=1 passing the rank-0 check but size 0).

step_by_step_execution.description: num_segments is read from the third input tensor as a scalar. The attacker provides a negative value.
segment_id tensor is provided with a valid rank > 0 but zero elements (e.g., shape [0]). The rank check passes (segment_dims=1 != 0).
The segment_dims <= input_dims check passes if input also has compatible shape (e.g., shape [0]).
The dimension compatibility loop iterates 0..segment_dims but since dim_size(0) is 0==0, it passes.
GetOutputShape is called with negative num_segments, producing a TensorShape with a negative dimension, which can lead to integer underflow in size calculations and an improperly sized allocation.
The segment_id validation loop does not execute because flat_segment_id.size() is 0, so the negative num_segments is never caught by the bounds check on segment IDs.
Any subsequent access to output_flat using computed indices can result in out-of-bounds writes on the improperly allocated output tensor.

cve_desc: TensorFlow is an end-to-end open source platform for machine learning. In affected versions the implementation for `tf.raw_ops.FractionalAvgPoolGrad` can be tricked into accessing data outside of bounds of heap allocated buffers. The [implementation](https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorflow/blob/f24faa153ad31a4b51578f8181d3aaab77a1ddeb/tensorflow/core/kernels/fractional_avg_pool_op.cc#L205) does not validate that the input tensor is non-empty. Thus, code constructs an empty `EigenDoubleMatrixMap` and then accesses this buffer with indices that are outside of the empty area. We have patched the issue in GitHub commit 0f931751fb20f565c4e94aa6df58d54a003cdb30. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.6.0. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.5.1, TensorFlow 2.4.3, and TensorFlow 2.3.4, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

code_snippets: const int64_t in_index = (b * in_rows + in_r) * in_cols + in_c;
vulnerabilities.description: The values from row_seq_tensor_flat and col_seq_tensor_flat are user-controlled and used directly as start indices (in_row_start, in_col_start) for iterating and writing into in_backprop_tensor_temp_mat. While end values are clamped to in_max_row_index/in_max_col_index, start values are never validated to be non-negative or within [0, in_rows) / [0, in_cols). A negative or excessively large start value leads to an out-of-bounds read/write on the backing buffer.

step_by_step_execution.description: User provides row_seq_tensor with a negative value, e.g., row_seq_tensor_flat(0) = -10. The check `row_seq_tensor.NumElements() > out_rows` passes as long as there are enough elements.
In the loop, in_row_start is assigned from row_seq_tensor_flat(r) without bounds checking.
in_row_end is computed and clamped to in_max_row_index via std::min, but may still be >= in_row_start if in_rows is large enough, so the inner loop executes.
The inner loop iterates with in_r from in_row_start (-10) to in_row_end. in_index is computed as (b * in_rows + in_r) * in_cols + in_c, which becomes negative.

state.value: negative value, e.g. (0 * in_rows + (-10)) * in_cols + 0 = -10 * in_cols

step_by_step_execution.description: coeffRef(d, in_index) accesses memory before the start of the tensor buffer, causing out-of-bounds read/write.

vulnerabilities.description: If user-controlled sequence tensor values cause in_row_end < in_row_start or in_col_end < in_col_start (e.g., start value is larger than clamped end value), num_elements_in_pooling_cell can be zero or negative. When zero, a division by zero occurs.

step_by_step_execution.description: User provides row_seq_tensor where row_seq_tensor_flat(r) > in_max_row_index, e.g., in_rows=5, row_seq_tensor_flat(0) = 100.
in_row_end is computed and then clamped to in_max_row_index = 4.

num_elements_in_pooling_cell = (4 - 100 + 1) * (...) = -95 * (...). If col produces a similar situation, the product could be 0 or negative. With careful crafting (e.g., making the col factor exactly 0), division by zero occurs.
Division by num_elements_in_pooling_cell causes floating-point division by zero or incorrect results.

code_snippets: const int64_t in_index = (b * in_rows + in_r) * in_cols + in_c;
vulnerabilities.description: There is no validation that out_batch <= in_batch or out_depth <= in_depth. The outer loop iterates up to out_batch and the inner loop up to out_depth, but these index into in_backprop_tensor_temp_mat which is sized based on in_batch/in_depth. If out_batch > in_batch or out_depth > in_depth, this leads to out-of-bounds writes.

step_by_step_execution.description: User provides orig_input_tensor_shape with in_batch=1 and out_backprop with dim_size(0)=10. No check enforces in_batch >= out_batch.
The loop iterates b from 0 to 9. When b >= 1, in_index = (b * in_rows + in_r) * in_cols + in_c exceeds the allocated size of in_backprop_tensor_temp which has only in_rows * in_cols * 1 columns.
coeffRef(d, in_index) writes beyond the allocated tensor buffer.

commit_message:
Add a check for pad width to be a positive value.

PiperOrigin-RevId: 413275853
Change-Id: I261a8db9dabf5ce48a806a9e58129080c9fac619
cve_desc: Tensorflow is an Open Source Machine Learning Framework. The implementation of `StringNGrams` can be used to trigger a denial of service attack by causing an out of memory condition after an integer overflow. We are missing a validation on `pad_witdh` and that result in computing a negative value for `ngram_width` which is later used to allocate parts of the output. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.8.0. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.7.1, TensorFlow 2.6.3, and TensorFlow 2.5.3, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

vulnerabilities.description: When SPLITS_TYPE is int64, the validation logic stores split values in `int prev_split` (32-bit), causing truncation. An attacker can craft an int64 split value (e.g., -2^32 = -4294967296) whose lower 32 bits are 0, passing the `prev_split == 0` check. Later, the un-truncated int64 value is used directly to index into `input_data`, causing an out-of-bounds memory access.

step_by_step_execution.description: Attacker provides `data` tensor with 2 tstring elements and `data_splits` tensor of int64 type with values [-4294967296, 2].

Validation block entered since splits_vec_size > 0. `prev_split` is assigned from `splits_vec(0)` which is -4294967296 (int64). Truncated to int (32-bit), the lower 32 bits are 0x00000000, so prev_split = 0.
Check `prev_split == 0` passes because truncated value is 0.

Loop i=1: `splits_vec(1)` is 2. Check `splits_vec(1) >= prev_split` → 2 >= 0 (prev_split promoted to int64 = 0) → true. Check `splits_vec(1) <= input_data_size` → 2 <= 2 → true. Both validations pass. `prev_split = (int)2 = 2`.
Final check `prev_split == input_data_size` → 2 == 2 → passes. All validation is complete.
`num_batch_items = 2 - 1 = 1`. Not empty, so main processing loop executes.
In main loop, i=0: `data_start = &input_data[splits_vec(0)]` uses the un-truncated int64 value -4294967296 as an index into input_data, resulting in a massive out-of-bounds memory access.

commit_message:
libnsgif: Fix null pointer deref on frameless GIF input

A crafted GIF file with no frame data could cause a null pointer
dereference leading to denial of service (crash). Reported by
@JieyongMa via huntr.dev.
cve_desc: chafa: NULL Pointer Dereference in function gif_internal_decode_frame at libnsgif.c:599 allows attackers to cause a denial of service (crash) via a crafted input file. in GitHub repository hpjansson/chafa prior to 1.10.2. chafa: NULL Pointer Dereference in function gif_internal_decode_frame at libnsgif.c:599 allows attackers to cause a denial of service (crash) via a crafted input file.

vulnerabilities.description: The LZW-decoded `colour` value (an unsigned char, range 0–255) is used directly as an index into `colour_table` without being bounds-checked against `colour_table_size`. The colour table can be as small as 2 entries (when `flags & GIF_COLOUR_TABLE_SIZE_MASK` is 0), so a decoded colour value >= colour_table_size causes an out-of-bounds read from either `gif->local_colour_table` or `gif->global_colour_table`.

step_by_step_execution.description: The function parses the image descriptor flags from the GIF data to determine the local colour table size.

state.value: gif_data[9], e.g. 0x00 (local colour table flag set but size bits = 0)

step_by_step_execution.description: colour_table is set to either the local or global colour table pointer, which has colour_table_size entries.

state.value: pointer to an array of colour_table_size (e.g., 2) entries

step_by_step_execution.description: LZW decoding begins. The decoder produces byte values that are placed on the stack. Each byte is popped as `colour`.

state.value: unsigned char from LZW stream, range 0–255

step_by_step_execution.description: The colour value is used to index colour_table without any check that colour < colour_table_size. If colour >= 2 (e.g., colour = 128), this reads beyond the allocated colour table.

state.value: out-of-bounds read at index 128 in a 2-element array

commit_message:
patch 8.2.5050: using freed memory when searching for pattern in path

Problem:    Using freed memory when searching for pattern in path.
Solution:   Make a copy of the line.
cve_desc: Use After Free in GitHub repository vim/vim prior to 8.2.

vulnerabilities.description: Heap out-of-bounds read/write when the reallocation of the `files` array fails. If `depth + 1 == old_files == max_path_depth` (i.e., the active include stack has grown to fill all slots), the code attempts to reallocate `files` to a larger size. If that allocation fails (`bigger == NULL`), execution continues and accesses `files[depth + 1]` which equals `files[max_path_depth]`, one element past the end of the heap-allocated array of size `max_path_depth`.

step_by_step_execution.description: The `files` array is allocated with `max_path_depth` (50) elements. `old_files` is set to `max_path_depth` (50), and `depth` starts at -1.

Through 50 iterations of finding include directives and successfully opening included files (without any reaching EOF), `depth` increments from -1 up to 49. No files have been popped, so `old_files` remains 50.
On the 51st include directive, the condition `depth + 1 == old_files` (50 == 50) is true, triggering the reallocation attempt for the `files` array.

The reallocation `ALLOC_MULT(SearchedFile, max_path_depth * 2)` fails and returns NULL. The code inside `if (bigger != NULL)` is skipped entirely. `files`, `max_path_depth`, and `old_files` remain unchanged.

Execution proceeds past the reallocation block to `files[depth + 1].fp = mch_fopen(...)`. Here `depth + 1 = 50`, but the `files` array only has 50 elements (indices 0..49). This writes to `files[50]`, which is a heap out-of-bounds access.

state.value: files[50].fp — one element past end of heap buffer

step_by_step_execution.description: If the file open succeeds, `++depth` makes depth=50, and `depth == old_files` (50==50) is true, leading to further OOB accesses: `vim_free(files[old_files].name)` reads `files[50].name` (OOB), and `files[depth].name = new_fname` writes to `files[50].name` (OOB).

state.value: files[50].name — heap out-of-bounds read and write

commit_message: Clean up handling CAP LS
cve_desc: Directory traversal vulnerability in the client in HexChat 2.11.0 allows remote IRC servers to read or modify arbitrary files via a .. (dot dot) in the server name.

vulnerabilities.description: Stack-based buffer overflow via repeated capability extensions. The extensions_str comes from the remote IRC server and is split by spaces. Since the loop iterates over every token and uses strcmp (not tracking whether a capability was already appended), a malicious server can send duplicate extension names (e.g., "multi-prefix" repeated 20+ times). Each duplicate match appends to the fixed 256-byte stack buffer via strcat, easily overflowing it.

step_by_step_execution.description: The function is called with extensions_str from a remote IRC server, e.g. containing "multi-prefix" repeated 25 times.
buffer is initialized on the stack (256 bytes) and filled with "CAP REQ :" (9 bytes + null terminator).
extensions_str is split by spaces into an array of 25 identical tokens, each being "multi-prefix".
The loop iterates 25 times. On each iteration, the token matches "multi-prefix", so strcat appends "multi-prefix " (13 bytes) to buffer.
After approximately 19 iterations, the buffer's 256-byte capacity is exceeded (9 + 19*13 = 256). Iterations 20-25 write past the end of the stack buffer, corrupting the stack.

commit_message:
HID: elo: fix memory leak in elo_probe

When hid_parse() in elo_probe() fails, it forgets to call usb_put_dev to
decrease the refcount.

Fix this by adding usb_put_dev() in the error handling code of elo_probe().

Fixes: fbf42729d0e9 ("HID: elo: update the reference count of the usb device structure")
Reported-by: syzkaller <syzkaller@googlegroups.com>
Signed-off-by: Dongliang Mu <mudongliangabcd@gmail.com>
Signed-off-by: Jiri Kosina <jkosina@suse.cz>
cve_desc: In drivers/hid/hid-elo.c in the Linux kernel before 5.16.11, a memory leak exists for a certain hid_parse error condition.

vulnerabilities.description: In the error path (err_free label), `usb_put_dev` is never called to release the reference acquired by `usb_get_dev(udev)`. When `hid_parse` or `hid_hw_start` fails, the USB device reference count is leaked, preventing the USB device from ever being freed.

step_by_step_execution.description: The function checks hid_is_usb and allocates priv via kzalloc.
A USB device reference is obtained via usb_get_dev and stored in priv->usbdev.
hid_parse(hdev) is called and returns a non-zero error code.
Execution jumps to err_free, which calls kfree(priv) without first calling usb_put_dev(priv->usbdev) to release the reference.

state.value: USB device reference leaked, refcount never decremented

commit_message: https://github.com/ImageMagick/ImageMagick/issues/131
cve_desc: Heap-based buffer overflow in the CalcMinMax function in coders/mat.c in ImageMagick before 6.9.4-0 allows remote attackers to cause a denial of service (out-of-bounds read and application crash) via a crafted mat file.

vulnerabilities.description: Integer overflow in the computation of `ldblk` when multiplying MATLAB_HDR.SizeX (attacker-controlled unsigned int from file) by 2, 4, or 8. The multiplication is performed in unsigned int (32-bit) arithmetic, so large SizeX values cause a wrap-around, resulting in a small `ldblk`. The buffer `BImgBuff` is then allocated based on the small `ldblk`, but `image->columns` is set to the original large `MATLAB_HDR.SizeX`. When `ImportQuantumPixels` processes `image->columns` pixels from `BImgBuff`, it reads far beyond the allocated heap buffer.

step_by_step_execution.description: MATLAB_HDR.SizeX is read from the file as an unsigned 32-bit integer via ReadBlobXXXLong.

CellType is miDOUBLE (or miINT64/miUINT64), so ldblk is computed as (ssize_t)(8 * MATLAB_HDR.SizeX). The multiplication 8 * 0x40000000 = 0x200000000 overflows 32-bit unsigned int arithmetic, wrapping to 0.
image->columns is set to MATLAB_HDR.SizeX (the original large value).
BImgBuff is allocated via AcquireQuantumMemory((size_t)(ldblk), sizeof(double)), which is AcquireQuantumMemory(0, 8). This allocates a very small (or zero-size) buffer.

state.value: pointer to a 0-byte or minimal heap allocation

step_by_step_execution.description: In the scanline reading loop, ReadBlob reads ldblk (0) bytes into BImgBuff, which succeeds trivially.

ImportQuantumPixels is called with the image (which has columns=0x40000000) and BImgBuff. It attempts to process image->columns pixels from BImgBuff, reading far past the allocated buffer, causing a heap buffer over-read.

vulnerabilities.description: When image->depth is set to 64 (for miINT64, miUINT64, or miDOUBLE cell types), `one << image->depth` performs a left shift of a size_t value by 64 bits. On platforms where size_t is 64-bit, shifting by 64 is undefined behavior in C/C++. This can lead to unpredictable results for image->colors.

step_by_step_execution.description: CellType is miDOUBLE (or miINT64/miUINT64), so image->depth is set to 64.
The expression `one << image->depth` is `1UL << 64`. On a 64-bit platform where size_t is 64 bits, shifting by 64 is undefined behavior per the C standard (shift amount >= width of type).

state.value: undefined (UB: shift by 64 on a 64-bit type)

commit_message: Fix #184: Heap overflow in S3M loader
cve_desc: XMFile::read in XMFile.cpp in milkyplay in MilkyTracker 1.02.00 has a heap-based buffer overflow.

vulnerabilities.description: The loop copies order entries from the file into header->ord[] using index j, which can grow up to header->ordnum (a 16-bit value read from the file, up to 65535). There is no bounds check on j against the fixed size of the header->ord array, leading to a heap buffer overflow.

step_by_step_execution.description: header->ordnum is read from the file as a 16-bit word, potentially up to 65535.
An orders array of size header->ordnum is allocated.
orders are read from the file, filled with attacker-controlled data (none equal to 255 to avoid early break).
The loop iterates over all ordnum entries. Since no entry is 255, j increments each iteration and header->ord[j++] is written to, overflowing the fixed-size header->ord array.

state.value: fixed-size array (typically 256 entries), written out of bounds

vulnerabilities.description: header->insnum is read from the file and used to iterate over instruments. Both instr[i] and smp[s] are accessed without checking whether i or s exceed the allocated sizes of module->instr and module->smp arrays, leading to out-of-bounds writes when a crafted file specifies a large number of instruments.

step_by_step_execution.description: header->insnum is read from the file as a 16-bit word.
The loop iterates i from 0 to header->insnum-1, accessing instr[i] and smp[s] which are fixed-size arrays allocated by XModule.

state.value: increments for each valid sample instrument, up to insnum

step_by_step_execution.description: When i exceeds the allocated size of module->instr, or s exceeds the allocated size of module->smp, out-of-bounds heap writes occur.

vulnerabilities.description: header->patnum is read from the file and used to index phead[i] without bounds checking. If patnum exceeds the allocated size of module->phead, this results in an out-of-bounds access.

step_by_step_execution.description: header->patnum is read from the file as a 16-bit word.
The pattern loop iterates i from 0 to header->patnum-1, accessing phead[i].
If patnum exceeds the pre-allocated size of module->phead, phead[i] is an out-of-bounds access.

commit_message: https://github.com/ImageMagick/ImageMagick/issues/131
cve_desc: Heap-based buffer overflow in the CalcMinMax function in coders/mat.c in ImageMagick before 6.9.4-0 allows remote attackers to cause a denial of service (out-of-bounds read and application crash) via a crafted mat file.

vulnerabilities.description: Integer overflow in the calculation of ldblk when multiplying MATLAB_HDR.SizeX (an unsigned 32-bit value read from the file) by 2, 4, or 8. The multiplication is performed in 32-bit unsigned arithmetic before being cast to ssize_t, which can wrap around to a small value. This leads to a small BImgBuff allocation, while image->columns retains the original large SizeX. ImportQuantumPixels then processes image->columns pixels from the undersized BImgBuff, causing a heap buffer over-read/over-write.

step_by_step_execution.description: MATLAB_HDR.SizeX is read from the file as an unsigned 32-bit integer via ReadBlobXXXLong.
For miINT32/miUINT32 cell type, ldblk is computed as (ssize_t)(4 * MATLAB_HDR.SizeX). The multiplication 4 * 0x80000002 = 0x200000008 overflows 32-bit unsigned to 0x8.
image->columns is set to MATLAB_HDR.SizeX directly.
BImgBuff is allocated via AcquireQuantumMemory(ldblk, sizeof(double)) = AcquireQuantumMemory(8, 8) = 64 bytes.
ReadBlob reads ldblk (8) bytes into BImgBuff, which succeeds without overflowing the buffer.
ImportQuantumPixels processes image->columns (0x80000002) pixels from BImgBuff. For 32-bit depth, this requires reading ~8GB of data from a 64-byte buffer, causing a massive heap buffer over-read.

commit_message:
Prevent use after free.

A very old version of the code used `result` as a simple pointer to a resource. Two years later, the pointer got changed to a `unique_ptr` but author forgot to remove the call to `Unref`. Three years after that, we finally uncover the UAF.

PiperOrigin-RevId: 387924872
Change-Id: I70fb6f199164de49fac20c168132a07b84903f9b
cve_desc: TensorFlow is an end-to-end open source platform for machine learning. In affected versions the implementation for `tf.raw_ops.BoostedTreesCreateEnsemble` can result in a use after free error if an attacker supplies specially crafted arguments. The [implementation](https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorflow/blob/f24faa153ad31a4b51578f8181d3aaab77a1ddeb/tensorflow/core/kernels/boosted_trees/resource_ops.cc#L55) uses a reference counted resource and decrements the refcount if the initialization fails, as it should. However, when the code was written, the resource was represented as a naked pointer but later refactoring has changed it to be a smart pointer. Thus, when the pointer leaves the scope, a subsequent `free`-ing of the resource occurs, but this fails to take into account that the refcount has already reached 0, thus the resource has been already freed. During this double-free process, members of the resource object are accessed for cleanup but they are invalid as the entire resource has been freed. We have patched the issue in GitHub commit 5ecec9c6fbdbc6be03295685190a45e7eee726ab. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.6.0. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.5.1, TensorFlow 2.4.3, and TensorFlow 2.3.4, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

vulnerabilities.description: When InitFromSerialized fails, result->Unref() is called on the object still owned by the unique_ptr. If Unref() decrements the reference count to 0 and deletes the object, the unique_ptr destructor will then attempt to delete the same object again when the function returns (triggered by OP_REQUIRES), resulting in a double-free.

step_by_step_execution.description: A new BoostedTreesEnsembleResource is created and held by a unique_ptr.

state.value: unique_ptr owning a newly allocated BoostedTreesEnsembleResource with refcount=1

step_by_step_execution.description: InitFromSerialized is called and returns false (e.g., due to malformed proto input).
result->Unref() is called, decrementing the refcount to 0 and deleting the object.

state.value: unique_ptr still holds a dangling pointer to the now-freed object

step_by_step_execution.description: OP_REQUIRES macro sets an error status and causes the function to return. Upon return, the unique_ptr destructor fires and calls delete on the already-freed pointer, causing a double-free.

state.value: unique_ptr destructor calls delete on dangling pointer

vulnerabilities.description: When CreateResource returns ALREADY_EXISTS, the raw pointer obtained from result.release() is never reclaimed. The unique_ptr no longer owns the resource, and since CreateResource did not take ownership (it failed), the allocated BoostedTreesEnsembleResource is leaked. Repeated invocations can exhaust memory.

step_by_step_execution.description: A new BoostedTreesEnsembleResource is created and owned by unique_ptr result.
InitFromSerialized succeeds, so execution continues past the error check.
result.release() is called, transferring the raw pointer to CreateResource and releasing unique_ptr ownership.
CreateResource returns ALREADY_EXISTS because a resource already exists for this handle.
The ALREADY_EXISTS check passes, so no error is raised, but the raw pointer from release() was never adopted by CreateResource and is now leaked.

commit_message:
Fix overflow CHECK issue with `tf.raw_ops.UnsortedSegmentJoin`.

PiperOrigin-RevId: 370766155
Change-Id: I33e7c6626224e1060a8a4ab51ad5d861c6d4c63e
cve_desc: TensorFlow is an end-to-end open source platform for machine learning. An attacker can cause a denial of service by controlling the values of `num_segments` tensor argument for `UnsortedSegmentJoin`. This is because the implementation(https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorflow/blob/a2a607db15c7cd01d754d37e5448d72a13491bdb/tensorflow/core/kernels/unsorted_segment_join_op.cc#L92-L93) assumes that the `num_segments` tensor is a valid scalar. Since the tensor is empty the `CHECK` involved in `.scalar<T>()()` that checks that the number of elements is exactly 1 will be invalidated and this would result in process termination. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.5.0. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.4.2, TensorFlow 2.3.3, TensorFlow 2.2.3 and TensorFlow 2.1.4, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

code_snippets:
OP_REQUIRES(context, num_segments_tensor.NumElements() != 0,
                errors::InvalidArgument("Number of segments cannot be empty."));
vulnerabilities.description: There is no validation that num_segments is non-negative. If NUM_SEGMENTS_TYPE is a signed integer type, a negative num_segments value can be passed. GetOutputShape would receive a negative dimension, potentially resulting in an undersized output tensor allocation. The segment_id bounds check `flat_segment_id(i) < num_segments` with a negative num_segments would be trivially satisfied by negative segment_id values (which are also checked >= 0, but integer casting or type confusion could bypass this). Even if the segment validation loop passes (e.g., with an empty segment_id tensor), the output tensor shape computation with a negative num_segments can cause integer underflow/overflow leading to incorrect memory allocation and subsequent out-of-bounds access.

step_by_step_execution.description: num_segments is read from the input tensor as a scalar of NUM_SEGMENTS_TYPE (signed integer type). Attacker provides a negative value.
The only check on num_segments is that num_segments_tensor.NumElements() != 0, which passes since the tensor has one element. No check that num_segments >= 0.

GetOutputShape is called with the negative num_segments value, which can produce an output shape with a negative or zero-sized dimension, leading to a very small or zero-element output tensor allocation.

state.value: shape with negative/wrapped dimension from num_segments = -1

step_by_step_execution.description: In the nested loop, output_index is computed as start_offset + flat_segment_id(i) * big_stride. If the output tensor was under-allocated due to the negative num_segments, any write to output_flat(output_index) is an out-of-bounds write.

commit_message:
ncurses 6.2 - patch 20200531

+ correct configure version-check/warnng for g++ to allow for 10.x
+ re-enable "bel" in konsole-base (report by Nia Huang)
+ add linux-s entry (patch by Alexandre Montaron).

  string in infotocap (report/testcase by "puppet-meteor").

commit_message:
selinux,smack: fix subjective/objective credential use mixups

Jann Horn reported a problem with commit eb1231f73c4d ("selinux:
clarify task subjective and objective credentials") where some LSM
hooks were attempting to access the subjective credentials of a task
other than the current task.  Generally speaking, it is not safe to
access another task's subjective credentials and doing so can cause
a number of problems.

Further, while looking into the problem, I realized that Smack was
suffering from a similar problem brought about by a similar commit
1fb057dcde11 ("smack: differentiate between subjective and objective
task credentials").

This patch addresses this problem by restoring the use of the task's
objective credentials in those cases where the task is other than the
current executing task.  Not only does this resolve the problem
reported by Jann, it is arguably the correct thing to do in these
cases.

Cc: stable@vger.kernel.org
Fixes: eb1231f73c4d ("selinux: clarify task subjective and objective credentials")
Fixes: 1fb057dcde11 ("smack: differentiate between subjective and objective task credentials")
Reported-by: Jann Horn <jannh@google.com>
Acked-by: Eric W. Biederman <ebiederm@xmission.com>
Acked-by: Casey Schaufler <casey@schaufler-ca.com>
Signed-off-by: Paul Moore <paul@paul-moore.com>
cve_desc: An issue was discovered in the Linux kernel before 5.14.8. A use-after-free in selinux_ptrace_traceme (aka the SELinux handler for PTRACE_TRACEME) could be used by local attackers to cause memory corruption and escalate privileges, aka CID-a3727a8bac0a. This occurs because of an attempt to access the subjective credentials of another task.

commit_message:
rsi: fix a double free bug in rsi_91x_deinit()

`dev` (struct rsi_91x_usbdev *) field of adapter
(struct rsi_91x_usbdev *) is allocated  and initialized in
`rsi_init_usb_interface`. If any error is detected in information
read from the device side,  `rsi_init_usb_interface` will be
freed. However, in the higher level error handling code in
`rsi_probe`, if error is detected, `rsi_91x_deinit` is called
again, in which `dev` will be freed again, resulting double free.

This patch fixes the double free by removing the free operation on
`dev` in `rsi_init_usb_interface`, because `rsi_91x_deinit` is also
used in `rsi_disconnect`, in that code path, the `dev` field is not
 (and thus needs to be) freed.

This bug was found in v4.19, but is also present in the latest version
of kernel. Fixes CVE-2019-15504.

Reported-by: Hui Peng <benquike@gmail.com>
Reported-by: Mathias Payer <mathias.payer@nebelwelt.net>
Signed-off-by: Hui Peng <benquike@gmail.com>
Reviewed-by: Guenter Roeck <linux@roeck-us.net>
Signed-off-by: Kalle Valo <kvalo@codeaurora.org>
cve_desc: drivers/net/wireless/rsi/rsi_91x_usb.c in the Linux kernel through 5.2.9 has a Double Free via crafted USB device traffic (which may be remote via usbip or usbredir).

commit_message:
misc: fastrpc: prevent memory leak in fastrpc_dma_buf_attach

In fastrpc_dma_buf_attach if dma_get_sgtable fails the allocated memory
for a should be released.

Signed-off-by: Navid Emamdoost <navid.emamdoost@gmail.com>
Link: https://lore.kernel.org/r/20190925152742.16258-1-navid.emamdoost@gmail.com
Signed-off-by: Greg Kroah-Hartman <gregkh@linuxfoundation.org>
cve_desc: A memory leak in the fastrpc_dma_buf_attach() function in drivers/misc/fastrpc.c in the Linux kernel before 5.3.9 allows attackers to cause a denial of service (memory consumption) by triggering dma_get_sgtable() failures, aka CID-fc739a058d99.

vulnerabilities.description: When dma_get_sgtable fails, the function returns -EINVAL without freeing the previously allocated 'a', causing a memory leak.

step_by_step_execution.description: kzalloc allocates memory for 'a' successfully.
dma_get_sgtable is called and returns a negative error code.
The error path is taken, but the function returns -EINVAL without calling kfree(a), leaking the allocation.

commit_message: 0.16.1: fix for dcraw ljpeg_start() vulnerability
cve_desc: Integer overflow in the ljpeg_start function in dcraw 7.00 and earlier allows remote attackers to cause a denial of service (crash) via a crafted image, which triggers a buffer overflow, related to the len variable.

vulnerabilities.description: If the JPEG marker length field is 0 or 1 (i.e., data[2]=0 and data[3] is 0 or 1), `len` becomes negative (-2 or -1). When this negative `int` is passed to `fread` as the `size_t` count parameter, it is implicitly converted to a very large unsigned value, causing `fread` to attempt to write far beyond the 0x10000-byte stack buffer `data`, resulting in a stack buffer overflow.

step_by_step_execution.description: The initial 2-byte JPEG SOI marker is read and validated (data[1] == 0xd8).
The next 4 bytes are read: 2 bytes for tag and 2 bytes for the marker length. An attacker crafts the length bytes as 0x00, 0x00.
`tag` is computed and passes the `tag <= 0xff00` check. `len` is computed as (0 << 8 | 0) - 2 = -2.

`fread(data, 1, len, ifp)` is called. The `int` value -2 is implicitly converted to `size_t`, yielding a very large value (e.g. 0xFFFFFFFE on 32-bit or 0xFFFFFFFFFFFFFFFE on 64-bit). fread attempts to write up to that many bytes from the file into the stack buffer `data`.
fread writes beyond the bounds of the 65536-byte stack-allocated buffer, causing a stack buffer overflow.

commit_message:
libnsgif: Fix null pointer deref on frameless GIF input

A crafted GIF file with no frame data could cause a null pointer
dereference leading to denial of service (crash). Reported by
@JieyongMa via huntr.dev.
cve_desc: chafa: NULL Pointer Dereference in function gif_internal_decode_frame at libnsgif.c:599 allows attackers to cause a denial of service (crash) via a crafted input file. in GitHub repository hpjansson/chafa prior to 1.10.2. chafa: NULL Pointer Dereference in function gif_internal_decode_frame at libnsgif.c:599 allows attackers to cause a denial of service (crash) via a crafted input file.

vulnerabilities.description: The LZW-decoded `colour` value (an unsigned char, range 0–255) is used directly as an index into `colour_table` without validating that it is within the bounds of the colour table. The colour table size can be as small as 2 entries (when `flags & GIF_COLOUR_TABLE_SIZE_MASK` is 0), but the decompressed pixel value can be up to 255, leading to an out-of-bounds read from either `gif->local_colour_table` or `gif->global_colour_table`.

step_by_step_execution.description: The image descriptor is parsed. The flags byte at gif_data[9] determines the local colour table size. If the low 3 bits are 0, colour_table_size = 2 << 0 = 2.

state.value: e.g. 0x00 (no local colour table flag set, so global table is used, which may also be small)

step_by_step_execution.description: The colour_table pointer is set. If no local colour table flag is set, it points to gif->global_colour_table, whose actual allocated size depends on the GIF's global colour table size field and may have far fewer than 256 entries populated with valid data.

state.value: gif->global_colour_table (with only a small number of valid entries)

step_by_step_execution.description: LZW decompression begins. The decompressed stream produces byte values that represent pixel colour indices.

state.value: points into LZW decode stack with decompressed bytes

step_by_step_execution.description: A colour byte is read from the LZW stack. A malicious or malformed GIF can produce any value 0–255 from the LZW stream.

The colour value is used to index into colour_table without any bounds check against colour_table_size. With colour=200 and colour_table_size=2, this reads 198 entries past the valid end of the table.

vulnerabilities.description: In the clear_image (else) branch, `gif->background_index` is used to index into `colour_table` without any bounds checking against the colour table size. A malformed GIF can specify a background_index that exceeds the colour table, causing an out-of-bounds read.

step_by_step_execution.description: The function is called with clear_image=true (via recursive call when previous frame disposal is GIF_FRAME_CLEAR).
The frame's disposal_method is GIF_FRAME_CLEAR and transparency is false, so the else branch of the clear code is taken.
colour_table[gif->background_index] is accessed. If gif->background_index is larger than the colour table size, this is an out-of-bounds read.

commit_message:
Never follow symlinks when setting file flags on Linux

When opening a file descriptor to set file flags on linux, ensure
no symbolic links are followed. This fixes the case when an archive
contains a directory entry followed by a symlink entry with the same
path. The fixup code would modify file flags of the symlink target.
cve_desc: An improper link resolution flaw can occur while extracting an archive leading to changing modes, times, access control lists, and flags of a file outside of the archive. An attacker may provide a malicious archive to a victim user, who would trigger this flaw when trying to extract the archive. A local attacker may use this flaw to gain more privileges in a system.

commit_message:

An attacker may provide a malicious archive to a victim user, who would trigger this flaw when trying to extract the archive. A local attacker may use this flaw to gain more privileges in a system.

commit_message: Fix wrong boundary checks in inflections parser resulting in stack buffer over-read with corrupt input
cve_desc: Buffer Over-read in GitHub repository bfabiszewski/libmobi prior to 0.11. This vulnerability is capable of arbitrary code execution.

vulnerabilities.description: In the delete ('d') branch, when dir is '<', pos is decremented without verifying it remains >= 0. If pos is 0 before the decrement, it becomes -1, leading to out-of-bounds read (decoded[pos]) and out-of-bounds write (memmove to decoded + pos). The existing bounds check `l < 0 || d + l > decoded + INDX_INFLBUF_SIZEMAX` does not catch a negative pos because l = *decoded_size - (-1) is positive, and d + l wraps back to a valid-looking address.

step_by_step_execution.description: Function is called with *decoded_size = 0 (empty decoded buffer). pos is initialized to 0, mod='i', dir='<'.
First rule byte c=3 is read (value <= 4). mod is set to 'd' (since c > 2), olddir='<', dir='<' (c & 2 is true). Since olddir == dir, pos is not reset.
Next rule byte c=65 ('A', which is > 20) is read, entering the else branch. mod=='d', so we enter the delete path. dir=='<', so pos is decremented.

s = decoded + pos + 1 = decoded + 0, d = decoded + pos = decoded - 1, l = *decoded_size - pos = 0 - (-1) = 1. The bounds check `l < 0` is false (l=1). The check `d + l > decoded + INDX_INFLBUF_SIZEMAX` evaluates to `decoded > decoded + INDX_INFLBUF_SIZEMAX` which is false. Both checks pass.
decoded[pos] (i.e., decoded[-1]) is read, causing an out-of-bounds read. If the check passes, memmove(decoded - 1, decoded, 1) performs an out-of-bounds write.

commit_message: Make line_no with too large value(2**20) invalid. Fixes #124
cve_desc: tinyexr 0.9.5 has a integer overflow over-write in tinyexr::DecodePixelData in tinyexr.h, related to OpenEXR code.

vulnerabilities.description: Signed integer overflow in the scanline decoding path. The variable `line_no` is read directly from attacker-controlled file data. When `line_no` is a large positive value (e.g., close to INT_MAX), the expression `line_no + num_scanline_blocks` overflows, which is undefined behavior in C/C++. A compiler may optimize away the subsequent `num_lines <= 0` check (since it can assume no signed overflow occurs, meaning `line_no + num_scanline_blocks >= line_no` always holds). This can result in `DecodePixelData` being called with corrupted `num_lines` and `line_no` values, leading to out-of-bounds memory writes into `exr_image->images`.

code_snippets:
if (!tinyexr::DecodePixelData(
                      exr_image->images, exr_header->requested_pixel_types,
                      data_ptr, static_cast<size_t>(data_len),
                      exr_header->compression_type, exr_header->line_order,
                      data_width, data_height, data_width, y, line_no,
                      num_lines, ...))

step_by_step_execution.description: Function begins, reads num_channels, computes num_scanline_blocks based on compression type (e.g., 32 for PIZ compression).
data_width and data_height are computed and pass validation checks (both positive, both below threshold).
Enters the scanline (non-tiled) code path. Loop iterates over num_blocks.
For a given block y, reads line_no from attacker-controlled data in the file. Attacker sets line_no to a value near INT_MAX, e.g., 2147483630.
Computes `line_no + num_scanline_blocks` which is `2147483630 + 32 = 2147483662`, exceeding INT_MAX (2147483647). This is signed integer overflow — undefined behavior in C++.

Due to UB, the compiler may assume no overflow occurred. Under this assumption, `line_no + num_scanline_blocks >= line_no` always, so `end_line_no >= line_no`, meaning `num_lines >= 0`. The compiler can optimize away the `num_lines <= 0` check entirely.

state.value: unpredictable due to UB, safety check potentially optimized away

step_by_step_execution.description: The overflow check for `lno` may also not catch this: lno = 2147483630 - 0 = 2147483630, which doesn't exceed INT_MAX, so line_no remains 2147483630 and passes the `line_no < 0` check.
DecodePixelData is called with line_no = 2147483630, which is far beyond data_height (100). This causes out-of-bounds writes into the allocated image buffers.
