commit_message:
rsi: fix a double free bug in rsi_91x_deinit()

`dev` (struct rsi_91x_usbdev *) field of adapter
(struct rsi_91x_usbdev *) is allocated  and initialized in
`rsi_init_usb_interface`. If any error is detected in information
read from the device side,  `rsi_init_usb_interface` will be
freed. However, in the higher level error handling code in
`rsi_probe`, if error is detected, `rsi_91x_deinit` is called
again, in which `dev` will be freed again, resulting double free.

This patch fixes the double free by removing the free operation on
`dev` in `rsi_init_usb_interface`, because `rsi_91x_deinit` is also
used in `rsi_disconnect`, in that code path, the `dev` field is not
 (and thus needs to be) freed.

This bug was found in v4.19, but is also present in the latest version
of kernel. Fixes CVE-2019-15504.

Reported-by: Hui Peng <benquike@gmail.com>
Reported-by: Mathias Payer <mathias.payer@nebelwelt.net>
Signed-off-by: Hui Peng <benquike@gmail.com>
Reviewed-by: Guenter Roeck <linux@roeck-us.net>
Signed-off-by: Kalle Valo <kvalo@codeaurora.org>
cve_desc: drivers/net/wireless/rsi/rsi_91x_usb.c in the Linux kernel through 5.2.9 has a Double Free via crafted USB device traffic (which may be remote via usbip or usbredir).

vulnerabilities.description: On the fail_eps error path, the previously allocated rsi_dev (via kzalloc) is never freed, resulting in a memory leak. If rsi_find_bulk_in_and_out_endpoints fails or the tx_buffer allocation fails, execution jumps to fail_eps which returns without calling kfree(rsi_dev).

step_by_step_execution.description: rsi_dev is allocated via kzalloc
adapter->rsi_dev is set to rsi_dev
rsi_find_bulk_in_and_out_endpoints fails, or tx_buffer allocation fails, causing a jump to fail_eps
Execution reaches fail_eps label and returns status without freeing rsi_dev

commit_message:
Merge pull request from GHSA-48w2-rm65-62xx

* Fix HTTP request smuggling vulnerability

See GHSA-48w2-rm65-62xx or CVE-2021-41136 for more info.

* 4.3.9 release note

* 5.5.1 release note

* 5.5.1
cve_desc: Puma is a HTTP 1.1 server for Ruby/Rack applications. Prior to versions 5.5.1 and 4.3.9, using `puma` with a proxy which forwards HTTP header values which contain the LF character could allow HTTP request smugggling. A client could smuggle a request through a proxy, causing the proxy to send a response back to another unknown client. The only proxy which has this behavior, as far as the Puma team is aware of, is Apache Traffic Server. If the proxy uses persistent connections and the client adds another request in via HTTP pipelining, the proxy may mistake it as the first request's body. Puma, however, would see it as two requests, and when processing the second request, send back a response that the proxy does not expect. If the proxy has reused the persistent connection to Puma to send another request for a different client, the second response from the first client will be sent to the second client. This vulnerability was patched in Puma 5.5.1 and 4.3.9. As a workaround, do not use Apache Traffic Server with `puma`.

code_snippets: size_t puma_parser_execute(puma_parser *parser, const char *buffer, size_t len, size_t off)
vulnerabilities.description: The parser modifies the input buffer in-place by casting away the `const` qualifier from `buffer`. In `tr21` and `tr23` transitions, `snake_upcase_char((char *)p)` writes to the buffer that the function signature declares as `const char *buffer`. If the caller provides a pointer to genuinely read-only memory (e.g., a memory-mapped read-only page or a string literal), this results in a write to read-only memory, causing undefined behavior or a crash. Even with writable memory, this is an unexpected mutation of data declared const, which can lead to data integrity issues in callers that rely on the buffer being unmodified.

step_by_step_execution.description:
Function is called with a `const char *buffer` containing a valid HTTP request with headers, e.g. "GET / HTTP/1.1
Host: example.com

". The buffer may reside in read-only memory.

state.value: pointer to const char data (potentially read-only memory)

step_by_step_execution.description: The parser processes the HTTP method "GET" (states 1 -> 2 -> ... -> 27), the space, the URI "/", the space, and the HTTP version "HTTP/1.1", followed by \r\n, reaching state 15.

state.value: points to first character after \r\n (the 'H' of 'Host')

step_by_step_execution.description: At state 15, the character 'H' matches the token character range, triggering transition tr21. This executes MARK(field_start, p) and then snake_upcase_char((char *)p), which casts away const and writes to the buffer.

state.value: points to 'H' in the header name within the const buffer
non-const alias to the same location, used to write

step_by_step_execution.description: snake_upcase_char writes through the cast pointer to modify the character in the buffer. If the buffer resides in read-only memory, this causes a segfault or undefined behavior. If writable, the caller's data is unexpectedly mutated despite being passed as const.

state.value: modified in-place by snake_upcase_char despite const declaration

commit_message:
Bug 701819: fixed ordering in if expression to avoid out-of-bounds access.

Fixes:
    ./sanbin/gs -dBATCH -dNOPAUSE -r965 -sOutputFile=tmp -sDEVICE=pcx16 ../bug-701819.pdf
cve_desc: A buffer overflow vulnerability in pcx_write_rle() in contrib/japanese/gdev10v.c of Artifex Software GhostScript v9.50 allows a remote attacker to cause a denial of service via a crafted PDF file. This is fixed in v9.51.

vulnerabilities.description: After `from += step`, the pointer `from` may point at or beyond `end`. The condition `data != *from` is evaluated before `from == end` due to left-to-right short-circuit evaluation, causing an out-of-bounds read. Additionally, when `step > 1`, `from` can skip past `end` entirely, making the `from == end` check insufficient even if the operand order were reversed.

step_by_step_execution.description: The outer while loop checks `from < end`, which is true, so execution enters the loop body.

state.value: points to last valid element before end (e.g., end - step)
one past the last valid byte in the buffer

step_by_step_execution.description: `data = *from` reads the current byte. This is a valid access since `from < end`.
`from += step` advances the pointer. Now `from` equals `end` (or exceeds it if step > 1 and the buffer size is not a multiple of step).

state.value: end (or end + k where k > 0 if step > 1)

step_by_step_execution.description: The condition `data != *from` is evaluated first (left operand of `||`). Since `from >= end`, this dereferences a pointer that is out of bounds, reading memory past the buffer.

commit_message: Fix #184: Heap overflow in S3M loader
cve_desc: XMFile::read in XMFile.cpp in milkyplay in MilkyTracker 1.02.00 has a heap-based buffer overflow.

code_snippets:
header->ordnum = f.readWord(); // number of positions in order list (songlength)
	mp_ubyte* orders = new mp_ubyte[header->ordnum];
vulnerabilities.description: The order number (header->ordnum) is read directly from the file as a 16-bit word, allowing values up to 65535. The loop then copies order entries into header->ord[], which is a fixed-size array (typically 256 entries in tracker module headers). If the file specifies an ordnum larger than the fixed size of header->ord[], the write via header->ord[j++] will overflow the buffer.

step_by_step_execution.description: header->ordnum is read from the file as a 16-bit word. A malicious file can set this to a large value, e.g. 60000.
An orders array of size header->ordnum is allocated to hold the raw order data from the file.
The file data is read into the orders array (60000 bytes).
The loop iterates up to header->ordnum (60000) times, copying non-255 values into header->ord[j++]. If the attacker fills the orders with non-255 values, j increments up to 60000.
When j exceeds the fixed size of header->ord[], the write header->ord[j++] = orders[i] causes a heap buffer overflow, corrupting adjacent memory in the TXMHeader structure.

vulnerabilities.description: The 'orders' array is allocated before validation checks on insnum and patnum. If either check fails, the function returns immediately without freeing the 'orders' array, causing a memory leak. Repeated loading of malicious files could exhaust memory.

step_by_step_execution.description: orders array is allocated with new[].
header->insnum is read from the file. A malicious file provides insnum > MP_MAXINS.
The check fails and the function returns MP_LOADER_FAILED without calling delete[] orders, leaking the memory.

commit_message:
handshake: check inappropriate fallback against the configured max version

That allows to operate on a server which is explicitly configured to
utilize earlier than TLS 1.2 versions.
cve_desc: The SSL protocol 3.0, as used in OpenSSL through 1.0.1i and other products, uses nondeterministic CBC padding, which makes it easier for man-in-the-middle attackers to obtain cleartext data via a padding-oracle attack, aka the "POODLE" issue.

vulnerabilities.description: The first loop accesses data[i] and data[i+1] for i iterating in steps of 2 up to datalen, but the check for whether datalen is even occurs AFTER the loop. If datalen is odd (e.g., 3), the last iteration accesses data[2] and data[3], where data[3] is a one-byte out-of-bounds read.

step_by_step_execution.description: Function is called with an odd datalen, e.g., datalen = 3, and data points to a 3-byte buffer.
First loop iteration: i = 0, accesses data[0] and data[1]. Both are within bounds.
Second loop iteration: i = 2, which is less than datalen (3), so loop body executes. data[i] = data[2] is within bounds, but data[i+1] = data[3] is one byte past the end of the buffer.
The out-of-bounds read occurs at data[i+1] in the comparisons for GNUTLS_RENEGO_PROTECTION_REQUEST_MINOR and GNUTLS_FALLBACK_SCSV_MINOR.
The datalen % 2 != 0 check that would reject odd lengths happens only after the loop has already completed, too late to prevent the OOB read.

commit_message:
Check configure file permissions even for non-default files if still privileged

(Bug 1044, CVE-2010-4345)
cve_desc: Exim 4.72 and earlier allows local users to gain privileges by leveraging the ability of the exim user account to specify an alternate configuration file with a directive that contains arbitrary commands, as demonstrated by the spool_directory directive.

code_snippets:
uschar *s = expand_string(host_number_string);
  long int n = Ustrtol(s, &end, 0);
vulnerabilities.description: If expand_string(host_number_string) returns NULL (expansion failure), the result is passed directly to Ustrtol without a NULL check, leading to a NULL pointer dereference.

step_by_step_execution.description: The code checks if host_number_string is not NULL and enters the block.

state.value: non-NULL string that will fail expansion

step_by_step_execution.description: expand_string is called on host_number_string, which returns NULL on failure.
Ustrtol(s, &end, 0) is called with s being NULL, causing a NULL pointer dereference.

vulnerabilities.description: When CONFIGURE_FILE_USE_NODE or CONFIGURE_FILE_USE_EUID is defined, suffix points to the end of filename within big_buffer. If the filename is long (close to big_buffer_size), the sprintf calls append up to ~280 additional characters which can write past the end of big_buffer, causing a buffer overflow.

step_by_step_execution.description: string_nextinlist reads a filename from config_main_filelist into big_buffer, which can be up to big_buffer_size - 1 characters long.

state.value: points into big_buffer, length up to big_buffer_size - 1

step_by_step_execution.description: suffix is set to point at the null terminator at the end of filename within big_buffer.

state.value: filename + Ustrlen(filename), near end of big_buffer

step_by_step_execution.description: sprintf writes '.%ld.%.256s' at suffix, which can add up to ~280 bytes (1 dot + up to 20 digits for long + 1 dot + up to 256 chars for nodename). If filename is long enough, this writes past the end of big_buffer.

state.value: writing up to ~280 additional bytes past end of filename, potentially past big_buffer boundary

commit_message:
handshake: check inappropriate fallback against the configured max version

That allows to operate on a server which is explicitly configured to
utilize earlier than TLS 1.2 versions.
cve_desc: The SSL protocol 3.0, as used in OpenSSL through 1.0.1i and other products, uses nondeterministic CBC padding, which makes it easier for man-in-the-middle attackers to obtain cleartext data via a padding-oracle attack, aka the "POODLE" issue.

vulnerabilities.description: The first loop accesses data[i] and data[i+1] for i iterating in steps of 2 up to datalen, but the check for datalen being even occurs AFTER the loop. If datalen is odd, the last iteration accesses data[datalen-1] (valid) and data[datalen] (one byte out of bounds read).

step_by_step_execution.description: Function is called with an odd datalen, e.g. datalen = 5. The data buffer has exactly 5 valid bytes.
First loop begins. Iteration i=0: accesses data[0] and data[1] — both valid.
Iteration i=2: accesses data[2] and data[3] — both valid.
Iteration i=4: condition 4 < 5 is true. Accesses data[4] (valid) and data[4+1] = data[5] which is one byte past the end of the buffer, causing an out-of-bounds read.
The datalen % 2 != 0 check that would reject odd-length input is only performed later in the function, after the out-of-bounds access has already occurred.

commit_message:
FSCTL_GET_SHADOW_COPY_DATA: Don't return 4 extra bytes at end

labels_data_count already accounts for the unicode null character at the
end of the array. There is no need in adding space for it again.

Signed-off-by: Christof Schmitt <christof.schmitt@us.ibm.com>
Reviewed-by: Jeremy Allison <jra@samba.org>
Reviewed-by: Simo Sorce <idra@samba.org>

Autobuild-User(master): Jeremy Allison <jra@samba.org>
Autobuild-Date(master): Tue Aug  6 04:03:17 CEST 2013 on sn-devel-104
cve_desc: Samba 3.6.6 through 3.6.23, 4.0.x before 4.0.18, and 4.1.x before 4.1.8, when a certain vfs shadow copy configuration is enabled, does not properly initialize the SRV_SNAPSHOT_ARRAY response field, which allows remote authenticated users to obtain potentially sensitive information from process memory via a (1) FSCTL_GET_SHADOW_COPY_DATA or (2) FSCTL_SRV_ENUMERATE_SNAPSHOTS request.

vulnerabilities.description: Integer overflow in labels_data_count calculation in FSCTL_GET_SHADOW_COPY_DATA. If shadow_data->num_volumes (returned from VFS) is sufficiently large, the multiplication `num_volumes * 2 * sizeof(SHADOW_COPY_LABEL)` can overflow a uint32, resulting in a small labels_data_count. This causes a small buffer to be allocated via talloc_zero_array, but the subsequent loop writes `num_volumes * 2 * sizeof(SHADOW_COPY_LABEL)` bytes into it, causing a heap buffer overflow.

step_by_step_execution.description: Function enters FSCTL_GET_SHADOW_COPY_DATA case with max_out_len large enough and labels=True.
SMB_VFS_GET_SHADOW_COPY_DATA succeeds and returns shadow_data with a very large num_volumes (e.g., 0x02000000 ~ 33 million).
labels_data_count is computed. With sizeof(SHADOW_COPY_LABEL)=25, the expression is num_volumes * 2 * 25 + 2 = 0x02000000 * 50 + 2 = 0x640000002, which overflows uint32 to a small value.
*out_len is set to 12 + labels_data_count = 14.
The check `max_out_len < *out_len` passes since max_out_len is large enough (> 14).
talloc_zero_array allocates only 14 bytes for cur_pdata.
The loop iterates num_volumes (33554432) times, each iteration writing 2*sizeof(SHADOW_COPY_LABEL)=50 bytes and advancing cur_pdata by 50 bytes, massively overflowing the 14-byte heap buffer.

commit_message:
Check configure file permissions even for non-default files if still privileged

(Bug 1044, CVE-2010-4345)
cve_desc: Exim 4.72 and earlier allows local users to gain privileges by leveraging the ability of the exim user account to specify an alternate configuration file with a directive that contains arbitrary commands, as demonstrated by the spool_directory directive.

vulnerabilities.description: Buffer overflow via sprintf into big_buffer. The variable `filename` points into `big_buffer`, and `suffix` is set to `filename + Ustrlen(filename)`. The sprintf calls append up to ~280 additional bytes (a long int, a dot, and up to 256 characters of nodename) after the end of the filename string within big_buffer. If the filename returned by string_nextinlist is close to big_buffer_size, the sprintf writes past the end of big_buffer.

step_by_step_execution.description: string_nextinlist reads a filename from config_main_filelist into big_buffer, limited by big_buffer_size. The filename can be up to big_buffer_size - 1 bytes long.

state.value: points into big_buffer, length up to big_buffer_size - 1

step_by_step_execution.description: suffix is computed as filename + Ustrlen(filename), pointing to the null terminator of filename within big_buffer.

state.value: big_buffer + Ustrlen(filename), near end of big_buffer if filename is long

step_by_step_execution.description: uname() succeeds, providing uts.nodename. sprintf writes '.%ld.%.256s' at suffix, appending up to ~280 bytes (1 dot + up to ~20 digits for long + 1 dot + up to 256 chars of nodename + null terminator).

state.value: big_buffer_size - Ustrlen(filename), which could be as small as 1 byte

step_by_step_execution.description: The sprintf writes past the end of big_buffer, causing a heap or stack buffer overflow.

state.value: up to ~279 bytes beyond big_buffer boundary

code_snippets:
uschar *s = expand_string(host_number_string);
  long int n = Ustrtol(s, &end, 0);
vulnerabilities.description: NULL pointer dereference when expand_string returns NULL for host_number_string. The code does not check the return value of expand_string before passing it to Ustrtol, which will dereference the NULL pointer.

step_by_step_execution.description: host_number_string is not NULL, so the block is entered.
expand_string(host_number_string) is called. If the expansion fails (e.g., syntax error or unavailable variable), it returns NULL.
Ustrtol(s, &end, 0) is called with s == NULL, causing a NULL pointer dereference.

commit_message:
media: vidtv: Check for null return of vzalloc

As the possible failure of the vzalloc(), e->encoder_buf might be NULL.
Therefore, it should be better to check it in order
to guarantee the success of the initialization.
If fails, we need to free not only 'e' but also 'e->name'.
Also, if the allocation for ctx fails, we need to free 'e->encoder_buf'
else.

Fixes: f90cf6079bf6 ("media: vidtv: add a bridge driver")
Signed-off-by: Jiasheng Jiang <jiasheng@iscas.ac.cn>
Signed-off-by: Hans Verkuil <hverkuil-cisco@xs4all.nl>
Signed-off-by: Mauro Carvalho Chehab <mchehab@kernel.org>
cve_desc: An issue was discovered in the Linux kernel through 5.16-rc6. There is a lack of check after calling vzalloc() and lack of free after allocation in drivers/media/test-drivers/vidtv/vidtv_s302m.c.

vulnerabilities.description: The encoder buffer is allocated with `vzalloc()` but freed with `kfree()` in the error path. Using `kfree()` on memory obtained from `vzalloc()` is undefined behavior and can lead to heap corruption, as `vmalloc`-family and `kmalloc`-family allocations use different underlying allocators.

step_by_step_execution.description: The function allocates `e` with `kzalloc` and then allocates `e->encoder_buf` with `vzalloc`.

state.value: valid vzalloc'd pointer of size VIDTV_S302M_BUF_SZ

step_by_step_execution.description: The function then attempts to allocate `ctx` with `kzalloc`, which fails and returns NULL.
Execution jumps to the `out_kfree_buf` label, which calls `kfree(e->encoder_buf)` on a `vzalloc`-allocated buffer.

state.value: vzalloc'd pointer being passed to kfree — wrong free function

step_by_step_execution.description: Calling `kfree` on `vzalloc`-allocated memory is undefined behavior and can corrupt the kernel heap, potentially leading to privilege escalation or denial of service.

commit_message: Merge pull request from GHSA-f5qg-pqcg-765m
cve_desc: PJSIP is a free and open source multimedia communication library written in C. Versions 2.12 and prior contain a stack buffer overflow vulnerability that affects PJSUA2 users or users that call the API `pjmedia_sdp_print(), pjmedia_sdp_media_print()`. Applications that do not use PJSUA2 and do not directly call `pjmedia_sdp_print()` or `pjmedia_sdp_media_print()` should not be affected. A patch is available on the `master` branch of the `pjsip/pjproject` GitHub repository. There are currently no known workarounds.

vulnerabilities.description: The initial buffer length check does not account for the space needed by the format (fmt) entries written in the loop. The check only validates against media.slen + transport.slen + 36, but the subsequent loop writes ' ' + fmt[i].slen bytes for each of the fmt_count entries without any bounds checking against 'end'. If there are many or large format entries, the writes will overflow the buffer.

step_by_step_execution.description: The function receives a buffer of size 'len' and checks if len >= media.slen + transport.slen + 36. Suppose media.slen=5, transport.slen=7, so the check requires len >= 48. Assume len=48, buf is 48 bytes.

The code writes 'm', '=', media (5 bytes), ' ', port (up to ~10 digits), possibly '/', port_count, ' ', transport (7 bytes). This consumes roughly 17+ bytes so far, within the 48 byte budget for fixed fields.
The fmt loop begins. Suppose fmt_count=10, and each fmt[i].slen=5. The loop needs 10*(1+5)=60 additional bytes. No bounds check is performed against 'end'.
pj_memcpy writes fmt data past the end of the buffer, causing a heap or stack buffer overflow.

commit_message:
Merge pull request from GHSA-48w2-rm65-62xx

* Fix HTTP request smuggling vulnerability

See GHSA-48w2-rm65-62xx or CVE-2021-41136 for more info.

* 4.3.9 release note

* 5.5.1 release note

* 5.5.1
cve_desc: Puma is a HTTP 1.1 server for Ruby/Rack applications. Prior to versions 5.5.1 and 4.3.9, using `puma` with a proxy which forwards HTTP header values which contain the LF character could allow HTTP request smugggling. A client could smuggle a request through a proxy, causing the proxy to send a response back to another unknown client. The only proxy which has this behavior, as far as the Puma team is aware of, is Apache Traffic Server. If the proxy uses persistent connections and the client adds another request in via HTTP pipelining, the proxy may mistake it as the first request's body. Puma, however, would see it as two requests, and when processing the second request, send back a response that the proxy does not expect. If the proxy has reused the persistent connection to Puma to send another request for a different client, the second response from the first client will be sent to the second client. This vulnerability was patched in Puma 5.5.1 and 4.3.9. As a workaround, do not use Apache Traffic Server with `puma`.

vulnerabilities.description: The function parameter `buffer` is declared as `const char *`, but the parser casts away constness via `(char *)p` and writes to the buffer through `snake_upcase_char()` during header field name parsing. This modifies caller-provided data that is declared as read-only, which is undefined behavior if the underlying memory is truly const (e.g., a read-only memory-mapped region or string literal), potentially causing a crash or memory corruption.

step_by_step_execution.description:
Function is called with a const char *buffer containing a valid HTTP request with headers, e.g., "GET / HTTP/1.1
Host: example.com

", and off=0.

state.value: pointer to const char data (possibly in read-only memory)

step_by_step_execution.description: Parser starts in state 1 (cs=1), reads 'G' which matches [A-Z], transitions via tr0 to state 2, then processes 'ET' through states 27, 28, etc.
After parsing method 'GET' and space, parser reaches state 3, then parses the URI '/' through states, then parses 'HTTP/1.1', then CR LF, arriving at state 15.

In state 15, the character 'H' matches the header field name character set, so the parser transitions via tr21 which executes MARK(field_start, p) and then snake_upcase_char((char *)p), writing to the const buffer.

state.value: pointing at 'H' in 'Host:' within the const buffer
cast removes const qualifier, write is performed on read-only data

step_by_step_execution.description: snake_upcase_char writes through the casted non-const pointer to the const buffer. If the buffer resides in read-only memory (e.g., mmap'd with PROT_READ, or a string literal in .rodata), this causes undefined behavior — typically a segfault or memory corruption.

state.value: written to via snake_upcase_char despite being const

commit_message: Add test to demonstrate buffer overrun
cve_desc: An out-of-bounds write vulnerability exists in the drill format T-code tool number functionality of Gerbv 2.7.0, dev (commit b5f1eacd), and the forked version of Gerbv (commit 71493260). A specially-crafted drill file can lead to code execution. An attacker can provide a malicious file to trigger this vulnerability.

vulnerabilities.description: Post-increment on the `fd` pointer (`fd++`) advances the pointer past the original `gerb_file_t` structure. All subsequent uses of `fd` in the function (e.g., `fd->filename`, `gerb_fgetc(fd)`, `gerb_ungetc(fd)`, `gerb_fgetint(fd, NULL)`) will dereference memory beyond the bounds of the intended object, leading to out-of-bounds reads and writes. This likely should have been `fd` alone (or possibly intended to increment an internal member).

code_snippets: gerbv_stats_printf(stats->error_list, GERBV_MESSAGE_NOTE, -1, _("Tool change stop switch found \"%s\" " "at line %ld in file \"%s\""), tmps, file_line, fd->filename);

step_by_step_execution.description: Function is called with a valid `fd` pointer to a `gerb_file_t` structure.
A character is read via `gerb_fgetc(fd)` and stored in `temp`.
The condition `(temp == 'C') && ((fd->ptr + 2) < fd->datalen)` is true, so we enter the first branch.
Next character is read and equals 'S', so we enter the inner branch.
Next character is read and equals 'T', so we enter the innermost branch.
`fd->ptr -= 4` adjusts the internal pointer back by 4.
`get_line(fd++)` is called. The post-increment operator advances `fd` by `sizeof(gerb_file_t)`, so `fd` now points past the original structure into adjacent memory.

state.value: original_fd + sizeof(gerb_file_t) — points to out-of-bounds memory

step_by_step_execution.description: The subsequent call to `gerbv_stats_printf` accesses `fd->filename`, which reads from the out-of-bounds memory location, causing an out-of-bounds read.

commit_message:
ALSA: pcm: oss: Fix race at SNDCTL_DSP_SYNC

There is a small race window at snd_pcm_oss_sync() that is called from
OSS PCM SNDCTL_DSP_SYNC ioctl; namely the function calls
snd_pcm_oss_make_ready() at first, then takes the params_lock mutex
for the rest.  When the stream is set up again by another thread
between them, it leads to inconsistency, and may result in unexpected
results such as NULL dereference of OSS buffer as a fuzzer spotted
recently.

The fix is simply to cover snd_pcm_oss_make_ready() call into the same
params_lock mutex with snd_pcm_oss_make_ready_locked() variant.

Reported-and-tested-by: butt3rflyh4ck <butterflyhuangxx@gmail.com>
Reviewed-by: Jaroslav Kysela <perex@perex.cz>
Cc: <stable@vger.kernel.org>
Link: https://lore.kernel.org/r/CAFcO6XN7JDM4xSXGhtusQfS2mSBcx50VJKwQpCq=WeLt57aaZA@mail.gmail.com
Link: https://lore.kernel.org/r/20220905060714.22549-1-tiwai@suse.de
Signed-off-by: Takashi Iwai <tiwai@suse.de>
cve_desc: A race condition flaw was found in the Linux kernel sound subsystem due to improper locking. It could lead to a NULL pointer dereference while handling the SNDCTL_DSP_SYNC ioctl. A privileged local user (root or member of the audio group) could use this flaw to crash the system, resulting in a denial of service condition

commit_message:
Fix oobread in java parser ##crash

* Reported by @bet4it via @huntrdev
* BountyID c8f4c2de-7d96-4ad4-857a-c099effca2d6
* Reproducer: bootstrap.class
cve_desc: Out-of-bounds Read in r_bin_java_bootstrap_methods_attr_new function in GitHub repository radareorg/radare2 prior to 5.7.0. The bug causes the program reads data past the end 2f the intented buffer. Typically, this can allow attackers to read sensitive information from other memory locations or cause a crash. More details see [CWE-125: Out-of-bounds read](https://cwe.mitre.org/data/definitions/125.html).

vulnerabilities.description: Out-of-bounds read when accessing buffer at offset 6 (and 7) via R_BIN_JAVA_USHORT without first checking that sz >= offset + 2 (i.e., sz >= 8). If the provided buffer size sz is less than 8, this reads beyond the buffer boundary.

step_by_step_execution.description: Function is called with a small buffer, e.g. sz = 6 or 7 (just enough for the default attr header but not the num_bootstrap_methods field).
r_bin_java_default_attr_new is called and returns a non-NULL attr.
offset is incremented by 6.
R_BIN_JAVA_USHORT(buffer, offset) reads 2 bytes at buffer[6] and buffer[7]. Since sz is 7, buffer[7] is out of bounds.

commit_message:
vnc: sanitize bits_per_pixel from the client

bits_per_pixel that are less than 8 could result in accessing
non-initialized buffers later in the code due to the expectation
that bytes_per_pixel value that is used to initialize these buffers is
never zero.

To fix this check that bits_per_pixel from the client is one of the
values that the rfb protocol specification allows.

This is CVE-2014-7815.

Signed-off-by: Petr Matousek <pmatouse@redhat.com>

[ kraxel: apply codestyle fix ]

Signed-off-by: Gerd Hoffmann <kraxel@redhat.com>
cve_desc: The set_pixel_format function in ui/vnc.c in QEMU allows remote attackers to cause a denial of service (crash) via a small bytes_per_pixel value.

commit_message:
Fix oobread and unaligned casts in the NE entrypoint logic ##crash

* Reported by @hmsec via huntr.dev
* Reproducer: nepocaligns
* BountyID: ec538fa4-06c6-4050-a141-f60153ddeaac
cve_desc: Out-of-bounds Read in r_bin_ne_get_entrypoints function in GitHub repository radareorg/radare2 prior to 5.6.8. This vulnerability may allow attackers to read sensitive information or cause a crash.

vulnerabilities.description: In the moveable bundle case (bundle_type == 0xff), the inner loop's bounds check only ensures `off + 4` is within bounds, but the moveable path consumes up to 6 bytes from `off` (1 flag + 2 skip + 1 segnum + 2 segoff). The `r_read_le16(bin->entry_table + off)` for `segoff` can read 1-2 bytes beyond the validated region, causing an out-of-bounds read on `bin->entry_table`.

step_by_step_execution.description: The outer while loop checks `off < bin->ne_header->EntryTableLength` and the inner for-loop checks `tableat + off + 4 >= r_buf_size(bin->buf)`. Suppose the entry_table buffer has exactly `off + 5` valid bytes (so tableat + off + 4 < buf_size passes since off+4 is valid index, but off+5 is out of bounds).

state.value: some value N at start of inner loop iteration

step_by_step_execution.description: off is incremented by 1 to skip flags byte.
off is incremented by 2 (skip bytes for moveable entry).
segnum is read from bin->entry_table + off (index N+3, which is valid).
off is incremented by 1.
r_read_le16 is called at bin->entry_table + off, which reads 2 bytes at indices N+4 and N+5. Index N+4 is the last guaranteed valid byte; index N+5 is out of bounds of the validated buffer region.

state.value: entry_table[N+4] and entry_table[N+5], but only up to entry_table[N+4] was validated

vulnerabilities.description: Memory leak: When breaking out of the inner for-loop due to intermediate bounds checks (e.g., `off > EntryTableLength` in the moveable path, or `off + 2 >= EntryTableLength` in the fixed path), the already-allocated `entry` is never freed, leaking memory.

step_by_step_execution.description: The inner loop allocates entry via R_NEW0.
In the moveable branch, after reading segnum and incrementing off, the check `off > bin->ne_header->EntryTableLength` evaluates to true.
The code breaks out of the for-loop without freeing `entry`. The entry pointer is lost, causing a memory leak.

commit_message:
media: vidtv: Check for null return of vzalloc

As the possible failure of the vzalloc(), e->encoder_buf might be NULL.
Therefore, it should be better to check it in order
to guarantee the success of the initialization.
If fails, we need to free not only 'e' but also 'e->name'.
Also, if the allocation for ctx fails, we need to free 'e->encoder_buf'
else.

Fixes: f90cf6079bf6 ("media: vidtv: add a bridge driver")
Signed-off-by: Jiasheng Jiang <jiasheng@iscas.ac.cn>
Signed-off-by: Hans Verkuil <hverkuil-cisco@xs4all.nl>
Signed-off-by: Mauro Carvalho Chehab <mchehab@kernel.org>
cve_desc: An issue was discovered in the Linux kernel through 5.16-rc6. There is a lack of check after calling vzalloc() and lack of free after allocation in drivers/media/test-drivers/vidtv/vidtv_s302m.c.

vulnerabilities.description: When the allocation of `ctx` (kzalloc) fails, the error path only calls `kfree(e)`, leaking the memory allocated by `kstrdup(args.name)` (stored in `e->name`) and `vzalloc(VIDTV_S302M_BUF_SZ)` (stored in `e->encoder_buf`).

step_by_step_execution.description: kzalloc for `e` succeeds.
args.name is non-NULL, so kstrdup allocates a copy and stores it in e->name.
vzalloc allocates encoder_buf and stores it in e->encoder_buf.
kzalloc for ctx fails, returning NULL.
The error path executes kfree(e) and returns NULL, but never frees e->name or e->encoder_buf, causing a memory leak.

vulnerabilities.description: The return value of vzalloc for encoder_buf is never checked for NULL. If vzalloc fails, e->encoder_buf is NULL while encoder_buf_sz is set to VIDTV_S302M_BUF_SZ, leading to a NULL pointer dereference when the encoder later tries to write to the buffer.

step_by_step_execution.description: kzalloc for `e` succeeds.
vzalloc(VIDTV_S302M_BUF_SZ) fails and returns NULL.
encoder_buf_sz is set to VIDTV_S302M_BUF_SZ despite the buffer being NULL.
The function returns `e` with a NULL encoder_buf. Any subsequent use of encoder_buf (e.g., in vidtv_s302m_encode) will dereference a NULL pointer.

patch 8.2.4901: NULL pointer access when using invalid pattern

Problem:    NULL pointer access when using invalid pattern.
Solution:   Check for failed regexp program.

commit_message:
patch 8.2.4253: using freed memory when substitute with function call

Problem:    Using freed memory when substitute uses a recursive function call.
Solution:   Make a copy of the substitute text.
cve_desc: Use After Free in GitHub repository vim/vim prior to 8.2.

vulnerabilities.description: Integer overflow in the `needed_len` calculation can lead to a heap buffer overflow. `needed_len` is declared as `int`, but its components involve `unsigned` casts of `STRLEN()` results and can sum to a value exceeding `INT_MAX`. When `needed_len` overflows to a negative value, the check `needed_len > (int)new_start_len` becomes false, skipping reallocation. Subsequent `mch_memmove` and `vim_regsub_multi` calls then write past the end of the `new_start` buffer. Similarly, if `needed_len` is a large positive value near `INT_MAX`, `needed_len + 50` overflows during the allocation size calculation, resulting in an undersized allocation.

step_by_step_execution.description: A substitute command is executed on a buffer with very long lines, resulting in large `STRLEN()` values for `p1` (remaining text after match) and `new_start` (accumulated substitution result).

state.value: pointer to a very long line (e.g., STRLEN close to INT_MAX/2)

step_by_step_execution.description: `copy_len` is calculated as `regmatch.startpos[0].col - copycol`. `needed_len` is computed as `copy_len + ((unsigned)STRLEN(p1) - regmatch.endpos[0].col) + sublen + 1`. The intermediate unsigned arithmetic produces a large value, which when added to other terms and stored in `int needed_len`, overflows past INT_MAX.

state.value: overflowed int, potentially negative or still large

step_by_step_execution.description: On the second or later substitution in the same line, `len = (unsigned)STRLEN(new_start)` is computed and added: `needed_len += len`. This can cause `needed_len` to overflow to a negative value.

state.value: large value from STRLEN(new_start) cast to unsigned then stored in int
negative due to signed integer overflow

step_by_step_execution.description: The check `if (needed_len > (int)new_start_len)` evaluates to false because `needed_len` is negative, so no reallocation of `new_start` occurs. The existing buffer `new_start` with size `new_start_len` is too small for the actual data.

state.value: previous allocation size, e.g., a few hundred bytes

step_by_step_execution.description: `mch_memmove(new_end, sub_firstline + copycol, (size_t)copy_len)` and `vim_regsub_multi(... new_end, TRUE ...)` write data beyond the allocated `new_start` buffer, causing a heap buffer overflow.

state.value: points past valid data in new_start
copy_len + sublen bytes, exceeding buffer capacity

commit_message:
patch 8.2.4253: using freed memory when substitute with function call

Problem:    Using freed memory when substitute uses a recursive function call.
Solution:   Make a copy of the substitute text.
cve_desc: Use After Free in GitHub repository vim/vim prior to 8.2.

vulnerabilities.description: Integer overflow in `needed_len` calculation can cause it to wrap to a negative value. Since `needed_len` is a signed `int` and the accumulated lengths of existing `new_start` content (`len`), the copy portion, the substitution length, and remaining text can exceed INT_MAX over repeated substitutions (e.g., with `:s/.../very_long_replacement/g`), the signed addition overflows. A negative `needed_len` makes the check `needed_len > (int)new_start_len` false, so the buffer `new_start` is not reallocated. Subsequent writes (`mch_memmove`, `vim_regsub_multi`) then write past the end of the heap buffer.

step_by_step_execution.description: A substitute command like `:%s/x/AAAAAA...very_long_replacement.../g` is issued on a buffer with many 'x' characters on one line. The function enters the per-line loop and finds the first match.

On the first match, `needed_len` is calculated as `copy_len + remaining_line_len + sublen + 1`. `new_start` is allocated with `new_start_len = needed_len + 50`. The substitution text is written into `new_start`.

state.value: points into new_start after copied/substituted data

step_by_step_execution.description: On subsequent matches in the same line (do_all is set due to 'g' flag), `len = (unsigned)STRLEN(new_start)` accumulates the growing replacement text. `needed_len` starts from `copy_len + remaining + sublen + 1`, then `needed_len += len` is computed. With a large replacement string and many matches, `len + base_needed_len` exceeds INT_MAX.

state.value: large value from accumulated substitutions, e.g., > 1.5 GB
positive int, e.g., a few thousand

step_by_step_execution.description: The check `if (needed_len > (int)new_start_len)` evaluates to false because `needed_len` is now negative, so no reallocation occurs. `new_end` is set to `new_start + len`, which is past the allocated buffer.

state.value: previous allocation size, smaller than actual need
new_start + len, past the allocated buffer boundary

step_by_step_execution.description: `mch_memmove(new_end, sub_firstline + copycol, (size_t)copy_len)` and `vim_regsub_multi(... new_end ...)` write data beyond the end of the heap-allocated `new_start` buffer, causing a heap buffer overflow.

state.value: points past allocated memory of new_start
positive, data is written out of bounds

commit_message:
esp: Fix possible buffer overflow in ESP transformation

The maximum message size that can be send is bigger than
the  maximum site that skb_page_frag_refill can allocate.
So it is possible to write beyond the allocated buffer.

Fix this by doing a fallback to COW in that case.

v2:

Avoid get get_order() costs as suggested by Linus Torvalds.

Fixes: cac2661c53f3 ("esp4: Avoid skb_cow_data whenever possible")
Fixes: 03e2a30f6a27 ("esp6: Avoid skb_cow_data whenever possible")
Reported-by: valis <sec@valis.email>
Signed-off-by: Steffen Klassert <steffen.klassert@secunet.com>
cve_desc: A heap buffer overflow flaw was found in IPsec ESP transformation code in net/ipv4/esp4.c and net/ipv6/esp6.c. This flaw allows a local attacker with a normal user privilege to overwrite kernel heap objects and may cause a local privilege escalation threat.

commit_message: Added missing allocation checks
cve_desc: Array index error in the (1) dtoa implementation in dtoa.c (aka pdtoa.c) and the (2) gdtoa (aka new dtoa) implementation in gdtoa/misc.c in libc, as used in multiple operating systems and products including in FreeBSD 6.4 and 7.2, NetBSD 5.0, OpenBSD 4.5, Mozilla Firefox 3.0.x before 3.0.15 and 3.5.x before 3.5.4, K-Meleon 1.5.3, SeaMonkey 1.1.8, and other products, allows context-dependent attackers to cause a denial of service (application crash) and possibly execute arbitrary code via a large precision value in the format argument to a printf function, which triggers incorrect memory allocation and a heap-based buffer overflow during conversion to a floating-point number.

commit_message:
Fix oobread and unaligned casts in the NE entrypoint logic ##crash

* Reported by @hmsec via huntr.dev
* Reproducer: nepocaligns
* BountyID: ec538fa4-06c6-4050-a141-f60153ddeaac
cve_desc: Out-of-bounds Read in r_bin_ne_get_entrypoints function in GitHub repository radareorg/radare2 prior to 5.6.8. This vulnerability may allow attackers to read sensitive information or cause a crash.

vulnerabilities.description: In the moveable bundle case (bundle_type == 0xff), the bounds check at the start of the for-loop body verifies `tableat + off + 4 < r_buf_size(bin->buf)`, but the moveable path reads up to off+5 bytes from the start (off+1 for flag, off+3 for segnum, off+4..off+5 for segoff as ut16). This leads to an out-of-bounds read of up to 2 bytes beyond what was validated.

step_by_step_execution.description: The while loop begins with `off = 0`, reads bundle_length and bundle_type, incrementing off by 2.
The for-loop checks `tableat + off + 4 >= r_buf_size(bin->buf)`. Suppose this passes, meaning bytes at tableat+off through tableat+off+3 are within bounds (4 bytes accessible from off).

state.value: less than r_buf_size(bin->buf), so off+0 through off+3 relative are accessible

step_by_step_execution.description: off++ increments offset by 1 (flag byte read). Then off += 2 for moveable case. Now off is at original_off + 3.
segnum is read at bin->entry_table + off (original_off+3), which is the last validated byte. off++ makes off = original_off + 4.

segoff is read as ut16 at bin->entry_table + off, which reads bytes at original_off+4 and original_off+5. Only up to original_off+3 was validated, so original_off+4 and original_off+5 are out-of-bounds reads.

state.value: original_off+4 and original_off+5, beyond the validated range of original_off+3

vulnerabilities.description: When bundle_type is 0xff (moveable), `segnum` is read from attacker-controlled entry table data. The only check is `segnum > 0`, but there is no upper-bound check against the actual number of segment entries. If segnum exceeds the segment_entries array size, this results in an out-of-bounds read on `bin->segment_entries`.

step_by_step_execution.description: The loop reads bundle_type == 0xff from the entry table, entering the moveable branch.
segnum is read from attacker-controlled entry table data. It can be any value from 1 to 255.
The check `if (segnum > 0)` passes for any segnum >= 1. No upper-bound check exists.
bin->segment_entries[segnum - 1] is accessed. If the segment_entries array has fewer than segnum elements (e.g., only 5 segments but segnum=200), this is an out-of-bounds read.

state.value: 199, but array may only have 5 entries

vulnerabilities.description: In the fixed bundle case, when bundle_type equals SegCount, the segment is skipped. But when bundle_type is between 1 and SegCount-1 exclusive, the code accesses segment_entries[bundle_type - 1], which is correct. However, if SegCount is 0 and bundle_type is any non-zero/non-0xff value, the condition fails safely. The real issue is that bundle_type could be exactly equal to SegCount (a valid segment index in 1-based counting), which would be incorrectly skipped — but more critically, the check uses strict less-than instead of less-than-or-equal, meaning segment index SegCount (the last segment) is never accessible, which is a logic bug but not a direct memory safety issue. No additional vulnerability here beyond what's already reported.

step_by_step_execution.description: bundle_type is read from entry table. For a fixed entry, if bundle_type >= 1 and bundle_type < SegCount, segment_entries[bundle_type-1] is accessed. This is safe. However, there is no explicit check against the actual allocated size of segment_entries vs SegCount — if segment_entries was allocated with fewer entries than SegCount indicates (due to a malformed binary), this would be OOB.

state.value: non-zero, non-0xff value from entry table
from NE header, potentially inconsistent with actual segment_entries allocation

commit_message:
ncurses 6.2 - patch 20200531

+ correct configure version-check/warnng for g++ to allow for 10.x
+ re-enable "bel" in konsole-base (report by Nia Huang)
+ add linux-s entry (patch by Alexandre Montaron).
+ drop long-obsolete convert_configure.pl
+ add test/test_parm.c, for checking tparm changes.
+ improve parameter-checking for tparm, adding function _nc_tiparm() to
  handle the most-used case, which accepts only numeric parameters
  (report/testcase by "puppet-meteor").
+ use a more conservative estimate of the buffer-size in lib_tparm.c's
  save_text() and save_number(), in case the sprintf() function
  passes-through unexpected characters from a format specifier
  (report/testcase by "puppet-meteor").
+ add a check for end-of-string in cvtchar to handle a malformed
  string in infotocap (report/testcase by "puppet-meteor").
cve_desc: An issue was discovered in ncurses through v6.2-1. _nc_captoinfo in captoinfo.c has a heap-based buffer overflow.

vulnerabilities.description: When the input string is just "^" ('^' followed by a null terminator), the code increments sp to point at '\0', sets c to 0, but still returns len=2. The caller, which uses the return value to advance its pointer through the string, will skip past the null terminator and read out-of-bounds memory.

step_by_step_execution.description: Function is called with sp pointing to a string consisting of just "^" (sp[0]='^', sp[1]='\0').
The switch matches the '^' case. sp is pre-incremented, now pointing at the null terminator.
c is 0, which is not '?', so c &= 0x1f results in c = 0. len is unconditionally set to 2.

Since c == '\0', neither branch writing to dp is taken. The function returns len = 2, telling the caller that 2 characters were consumed, but only 1 valid character ('^') existed before the null terminator.
The caller advances its pointer by 2, going one byte past the null terminator, causing subsequent reads to access out-of-bounds memory.

state.value: original_sp + 2, which is past the end of the string

commit_message: Add test to demonstrate buffer overrun
cve_desc: An out-of-bounds write vulnerability exists in the drill format T-code tool number functionality of Gerbv 2.7.0, dev (commit b5f1eacd), and the forked version of Gerbv (commit 71493260). A specially-crafted drill file can lead to code execution. An attacker can provide a malicious file to trigger this vulnerability.

vulnerabilities.description: When tool_num is outside the valid range (< TOOL_MIN or >= TOOL_MAX), the code logs an error but does NOT return. Execution continues, and tool_num is used as an index into image->aperture[], resulting in an out-of-bounds array access. This can lead to reading/writing arbitrary memory.

step_by_step_execution.description: The function reads a character after 'T' and determines it is a digit, so it proceeds to parse a tool number.
gerb_fgetint parses the tool number from the file. The value can be any integer, e.g. 99999.
The bounds check fires and logs an error message, but does NOT return or otherwise prevent further execution.
Execution continues past the bounds check. tool_num is used to index image->aperture[tool_num], which is an out-of-bounds array access.
Later in the function, if apert is NULL, image->aperture[tool_num] is assigned a newly allocated object, resulting in an out-of-bounds write.

vulnerabilities.description: The post-increment operator on fd (a pointer parameter) increments the pointer after passing it to get_line(). After this line, fd points to memory past the original gerb_file_t structure. All subsequent uses of fd (e.g., fd->filename) access invalid memory, leading to undefined behavior.

step_by_step_execution.description: After reading 'T', the next character is 'C', and fd->ptr + 2 < fd->datalen, so we enter the TCS branch.
The next character is 'S' and the one after is 'T', so we enter the innermost if block.
fd->ptr is decremented by 4, then get_line(fd++) is called. The post-increment advances the fd pointer by sizeof(gerb_file_t) bytes.

state.value: original_fd + 1 (points past the valid gerb_file_t structure)

step_by_step_execution.description: The subsequent call to gerbv_stats_printf uses fd->filename, but fd now points to invalid memory, causing an out-of-bounds read.

commit_message:
Fix possible stack corruption with XM instrument headers claiming a size of less than 4

Closes #275
cve_desc: MilkyTracker v1.03.00 was discovered to contain a stack overflow via the component LoaderXM::load. This vulnerability is triggered when the program is supplied a crafted XM module file.

vulnerabilities.description: The pattern data parsing loop reads from 'buffer' using index 'pc' without ever checking that 'pc' remains within the bounds of the allocated buffer (size phead[y].patdata). A crafted XM file can specify a small patdata size but large rows*channum, causing out-of-bounds heap reads.

step_by_step_execution.description: The pattern header is read, including phead[y].rows and phead[y].patdata from file data.
A buffer of size phead[y].patdata is allocated on the heap.
The nested loop iterates over rows * channum slots (256 * 32 = 8192 iterations). Each iteration reads at least 1 byte from buffer[pc].

state.value: starts at 0, increments by 1-5 each iteration

step_by_step_execution.description: After a few iterations, pc exceeds phead[y].patdata (10), causing buffer[pc] to read out of bounds from heap memory.

vulnerabilities.description: The nbu array contains byte values (0-255) read from file data, used as indices into smpReloc array of size MP_MAXINSSAMPS. If MP_MAXINSSAMPS < 256 (typically 96 for XM format), a crafted file can set nbu values >= MP_MAXINSSAMPS causing out-of-bounds stack read on smpReloc.

step_by_step_execution.description: Instrument data is read from file, including the note-to-sample mapping bytes copied into nbu array.

state.value: array of MP_MAXINSSAMPS bytes from file, values can be 0-255

step_by_step_execution.description: smpReloc is a stack array of size MP_MAXINSSAMPS (e.g. 96). Only indices 0 to instr[y].samp-1 are initialized.

state.value: stack array of MP_MAXINSSAMPS (96) mp_sint32 elements

step_by_step_execution.description: The loop iterates sc from 0 to MP_MAXINSSAMPS-1, accessing smpReloc[nbu[sc]]. A crafted file sets nbu[sc] to e.g. 200.
smpReloc[200] is accessed, reading 104 elements past the end of the stack-allocated array, resulting in an out-of-bounds stack read.

commit_message: Fix mask usage in image-compositor
cve_desc: A flaw was found in cairo's image-compositor.c in all versions prior to 1.17.4. This flaw allows an attacker who can provide a crafted input file to cairo's image-compositor (for example, by convincing a user to open a file in an application using cairo, or if an application uses cairo on untrusted input) to cause a stack buffer overflow -> out-of-bounds WRITE. The highest impact from this vulnerability is to confidentiality, integrity, as well as system availability.

vulnerabilities.description: The function writes span coverage values into the buffer `r->_buf` without any bounds checking. The amount written depends on the span lengths (`spans[1].x - spans[0].x`), which are derived from input data. Consecutive spans that fall into the `else` branch accumulate writes into `m` without ever verifying that `m` remains within the bounds of `r->_buf`. A sufficiently large total span width (or many consecutive non-zero, non-0xff coverage spans) can cause a heap buffer overflow.

step_by_step_execution.description: Function is called with spans array where multiple consecutive spans have coverage values that are neither 0x00 nor 0xff, and whose combined x-range exceeds the allocated size of r->_buf.

state.value: heap buffer of some fixed size N

step_by_step_execution.description: m is initialized to r->_buf and x0 is set to spans[0].x.

First iteration: len = spans[1].x - spans[0].x. Coverage is not 0xff and not 0x00, so the else branch is taken. *m++ writes one byte, then memset writes (len-1) more bytes. m advances by len total bytes.
Subsequent iterations continue to fall into the else branch with additional spans, each time advancing m by the span length without any bounds check against the size of r->_buf.

state.value: r->_buf + cumulative_len (may exceed buffer size N)

step_by_step_execution.description: When cumulative bytes written exceed the allocated size of r->_buf, the memset and pointer writes corrupt heap memory beyond the buffer.

commit_message:
esp: Fix possible buffer overflow in ESP transformation

The maximum message size that can be send is bigger than
the  maximum site that skb_page_frag_refill can allocate.
So it is possible to write beyond the allocated buffer.

Fix this by doing a fallback to COW in that case.

v2:

Avoid get get_order() costs as suggested by Linus Torvalds.

Fixes: cac2661c53f3 ("esp4: Avoid skb_cow_data whenever possible")
Fixes: 03e2a30f6a27 ("esp6: Avoid skb_cow_data whenever possible")
Reported-by: valis <sec@valis.email>
Signed-off-by: Steffen Klassert <steffen.klassert@secunet.com>
cve_desc: A heap buffer overflow flaw was found in IPsec ESP transformation code in net/ipv4/esp4.c and net/ipv6/esp6.c. This flaw allows a local attacker with a normal user privilege to overwrite kernel heap objects and may cause a local privilege escalation threat.

commit_message:
Save stack space while handling errors

Because error handling (luaG_errormsg) uses slots from EXTRA_STACK,
and some errors can recur (e.g., string overflow while creating an
error message in 'luaG_runerror', or a C-stack overflow before calling
the message handler), the code should use stack slots with parsimony.

This commit fixes the bug "Lua-stack overflow when C stack overflows
while handling an error".
cve_desc: An issue in the component luaG_runerror of Lua v5.4.4 and below leads to a heap-buffer overflow when a recursive error occurs.

commit_message: Merge pull request from GHSA-f5qg-pqcg-765m
cve_desc: PJSIP is a free and open source multimedia communication library written in C. Versions 2.12 and prior contain a stack buffer overflow vulnerability that affects PJSUA2 users or users that call the API `pjmedia_sdp_print(), pjmedia_sdp_media_print()`. Applications that do not use PJSUA2 and do not directly call `pjmedia_sdp_print()` or `pjmedia_sdp_media_print()` should not be affected. A patch is available on the `master` branch of the `pjsip/pjproject` GitHub repository. There are currently no known workarounds.

commit_message:
patch 8.2.4974: ":so" command may read after end of buffer

Problem:    ":so" command may read after end of buffer.
Solution:   Compute length of text properly.
cve_desc: Buffer Over-read in GitHub repository vim/vim prior to 8.2.4974.

commit_message:
Improve fix for avoiding huge number of tiny dashes

Some pathological cases were not caught by the previous fix.

Fixes: QTBUG-95239
Pick-to: 6.2 6.1 5.15
Change-Id: I0337ee3923ff93ccb36c4d7b810a9c0667354cc5
Reviewed-by: Robert Löhning <robert.loehning@qt.io>
cve_desc: Qt 5.x before 5.15.6 and 6.x through 6.1.2 has an out-of-bounds write in QOutlineMapper::convertPath (called from QRasterPaintEngine::fill and QPaintEngineEx::stroke).

vulnerabilities.description: When the element type is CurveToElement, the code accesses points[0] through points[5] (6 qreal values) and advances the types pointer by 3. However, the loop condition only checks `points < lastPoint`, which guarantees at least one qreal value remains, not six. If a malformed QVectorPath has a CurveToElement type near the end of the points array without sufficient remaining points, an out-of-bounds read occurs. This pattern appears in both the non-cosmetic and cosmetic pen code paths.

step_by_step_execution.description: The function receives a QVectorPath `path`. Suppose `path.elementCount()` is 2 (so the points buffer has 2*2=4 qreal values), and the element types array is [MoveToElement, CurveToElement].
Pointers are computed: `points` points to the start of the points buffer, `lastPoint = points + (2 << 1) = points + 4`.
First iteration: *types is MoveToElement. moveTo(points[0], points[1]) is called. points advances by 2, types advances by 1.

Second iteration: Loop check `points < lastPoint` is true (points+2 < points+4). *types is CurveToElement. The code accesses points[0]..points[5], i.e., offsets 2,3,4,5,6,7 from the original buffer. But the buffer only has 4 qreals (indices 0-3). Accessing points[2] through points[5] (original indices 4-7) is out-of-bounds.

state.value: points[0] through points[5] — needs 6 qreals but only 2 remain

patch 8.2.4359: crash when repeatedly using :retab

cve_desc: Heap-based Buffer Overflow in GitHub repository vim/vim prior to 8.2.

commit_message: improve fix #72558, while (u>=0) with unsigned int will always be true
cve_desc: Integer overflow in the _gdContributionsAlloc function in gd_interpolation.c in GD Graphics Library (aka libgd) before 2.2.3 allows remote attackers to cause a denial of service (out-of-bounds memory write or memory consumption) via unspecified vectors.

vulnerabilities.description: The variable `u` is declared as `unsigned int`. In the error-handling cleanup loop, the condition `u >= 0` is always true for an unsigned type. When `u` is decremented past 0, it wraps around to UINT_MAX, causing an out-of-bounds read/free on `res->ContribRow[UINT_MAX]` and continuing indefinitely.

step_by_step_execution.description: The function is called. `u` is declared as `unsigned int u = 0`.
Memory for `res` and `res->ContribRow` is successfully allocated. The for-loop begins with u=0.
On the first iteration (u=0), suppose `windows_size * sizeof(double)` overflows (overflow2 returns true), or gdMalloc returns NULL. The error path is entered.
`u--` is executed. Since u is unsigned and was 0, it wraps around to UINT_MAX (4294967295).
The while condition `u >= 0` is checked. Since u is unsigned, this is always true.

`gdFree(res->ContribRow[4294967295].Weights)` is executed, accessing memory far beyond the allocated `res->ContribRow` array. This is a massive out-of-bounds access leading to undefined behavior, crashes, or potential code execution.

commit_message: Fix mask usage in image-compositor
cve_desc: A flaw was found in cairo's image-compositor.c in all versions prior to 1.17.4. This flaw allows an attacker who can provide a crafted input file to cairo's image-compositor (for example, by convincing a user to open a file in an application using cairo, or if an application uses cairo on untrusted input) to cause a stack buffer overflow -> out-of-bounds WRITE. The highest impact from this vulnerability is to confidentiality, integrity, as well as system availability.

vulnerabilities.description: The mask buffer pointer `m` is advanced without any bounds checking against the actual size of the pixman mask image buffer. When consecutive spans have partial coverage (not 0x00 or 0xFF), bytes are written into the mask buffer based on span widths (`spans[1].x - spans[0].x`). If the cumulative width of partial-coverage spans exceeds the allocated mask buffer size, a heap buffer overflow occurs.

step_by_step_execution.description: The function obtains the mask buffer base pointer from the pixman image.

state.value: pointer to pixman mask image data buffer of some fixed size N

step_by_step_execution.description: The loop begins processing spans. A span with partial coverage (not 0x00 or 0xFF) is encountered.

state.value: spans[1].x - spans[0].x (width of this span in pixels)

step_by_step_execution.description: The else branch writes `len` bytes of coverage into the mask buffer starting at `m`, then advances `m` by `len`.
The loop continues. More partial-coverage spans are processed, each time writing their width in bytes and advancing `m` further.

state.value: base + cumulative_width (grows with each partial-coverage span)

step_by_step_execution.description: If the cumulative width of partial-coverage spans exceeds the allocated size N of the mask buffer, the writes via `*m++` and `memset` write beyond the end of the heap-allocated buffer.

state.value: base + cumulative_width, where cumulative_width > N (heap buffer overflow)

commit_message:
Unsquashfs: additional write outside destination directory exploit fix

An issue on github (https://github.com/plougher/squashfs-tools/issues/72)
showed how some specially crafted Squashfs filesystems containing
invalid file names (with '/' and '..') can cause Unsquashfs to write
files outside of the destination directory.

Since then it has been shown that specially crafted Squashfs filesystems
that contain a symbolic link pointing outside of the destination directory,
coupled with an identically named file within the same directory, can
cause Unsquashfs to write files outside of the destination directory.

Specifically the symbolic link produces a pathname pointing outside
of the destination directory, which is then followed when writing the
duplicate identically named file within the directory.

This commit fixes this exploit by explictly checking for duplicate
filenames within a directory.  As directories in v2.1, v3.x, and v4.0
filesystems are sorted, this is achieved by checking for consecutively
identical filenames.  Additionally directories are checked to
ensure they are sorted, to avoid attempts to evade the duplicate
check.

Version 1.x and 2.0 filesystems (where the directories were unsorted)
are sorted and then the above duplicate filename check is applied.

Signed-off-by: Phillip Lougher <phillip@squashfs.org.uk>
cve_desc: squashfs_opendir in unsquash-2.c in Squashfs-Tools 4.5 allows Directory Traversal, a different vulnerability than CVE-2021-40153. A squashfs filesystem that has been crafted to include a symbolic link and then contents under the same filename in a filesystem can cause unsquashfs to first create the symbolic link pointing outside the expected directory, and then the subsequent write operation will cause the unsquashfs process to write through the symbolic link elsewhere in the filesystem.

commit_message:
Bluetooth: virtio_bt: fix memory leak in virtbt_rx_handle()

On the reception of packets with an invalid packet type, the memory of
the allocated socket buffers is never freed. Add a default case that frees
these to avoid a memory leak.

Fixes: afd2daa26c7a ("Bluetooth: Add support for virtio transport driver")
Signed-off-by: Soenke Huster <soenke.huster@eknoes.de>
Signed-off-by: Marcel Holtmann <marcel@holtmann.org>
cve_desc: drivers/bluetooth/virtio_bt.c in the Linux kernel before 5.16.3 has a memory leak (socket buffers have memory allocated but not freed).

commit_message:
atlantic: Fix OOB read and write in hw_atl_utils_fw_rpc_wait

This bug report shows up when running our research tools. The
reports is SOOB read, but it seems SOOB write is also possible
a few lines below.

In details, fw.len and sw.len are inputs coming from io. A len
over the size of self->rpc triggers SOOB. The patch fixes the
bugs by adding sanity checks.

The bugs are triggerable with compromised/malfunctioning devices.
They are potentially exploitable given they first leak up to
0xffff bytes and able to overwrite the region later.

The patch is tested with QEMU emulater.
This is NOT tested with a real device.

Attached is the log we found by fuzzing.

commit_message:
Fix possible stack corruption with XM instrument headers claiming a size of less than 4

Closes #275
cve_desc: MilkyTracker v1.03.00 was discovered to contain a stack overflow via the component LoaderXM::load. This vulnerability is triggered when the program is supplied a crafted XM module file.

vulnerabilities.description: In the v0x104 instrument loading path, when `instr[y].size` is less than 4, the expression `instr[y].size - 4` (both unsigned) wraps around to a very large value (e.g., 0xFFFFFFFC for size=0). This causes `f.read` to attempt writing a massive amount of data into a 29-byte stack buffer, resulting in a stack buffer overflow.

step_by_step_execution.description: The file header is parsed and `header->ver` is set to 0x104.
Execution enters the v0x104 instrument loading loop. `instr[y].size` is read from the file via `f.readDwords`.
The condition `instr[y].size < 29` is true, so we enter the branch with the 29-byte stack buffer.
The unsigned subtraction `instr[y].size - 4` wraps around due to integer underflow (2 - 4 = 0xFFFFFFFE as mp_uint32).
`f.read(buffer, 1, 0xFFFFFFFE)` is called, attempting to write ~4GB of data into a 29-byte stack-allocated buffer, causing a stack buffer overflow.

vulnerabilities.description: When parsing pattern data, the index `pc` into `buffer` (of size `phead[y].patdata`) is never bounds-checked. A crafted XM file can specify a small `patdata` size but a large number of rows/channels, causing `pc` to exceed the buffer size and read out-of-bounds heap memory.

step_by_step_execution.description: Pattern header is read: `phead[y].rows` and `phead[y].patdata` are read from the file. An attacker sets rows to a large value (e.g., 256) and patdata to a small value (e.g., 10).
A buffer of size `phead[y].patdata` (10 bytes) is allocated on the heap and filled from the file.
The nested loop iterates over rows * channels = 256 * 8 = 2048 slot entries, each consuming 1-5 bytes from buffer via `pc` index.
After processing a few slots, `pc` exceeds 10 (the buffer size) but no bounds check prevents further reads from `buffer[pc]`, causing an out-of-bounds heap read.

state.value: 10-byte heap allocation being read past its end

commit_message: improve fix #72558, while (u>=0) with unsigned int will always be true
cve_desc: Integer overflow in the _gdContributionsAlloc function in gd_interpolation.c in GD Graphics Library (aka libgd) before 2.2.3 allows remote attackers to cause a denial of service (out-of-bounds memory write or memory consumption) via unspecified vectors.

vulnerabilities.description: When the first iteration of the loop (u == 0) fails (either due to overflow or allocation failure), `u--` causes an unsigned integer underflow, wrapping `u` to UINT_MAX. The subsequent cleanup loop `for (i=0; i<=u; i++)` then iterates up to UINT_MAX times, reading out-of-bounds memory from `res->ContribRow` and calling `gdFree` on garbage pointers.

step_by_step_execution.description: Function is called with valid `line_length` (e.g., 1) and a `windows_size` that causes overflow or allocation failure.

state.value: a value causing overflow2(windows_size, sizeof(double)) to be true, or a very large value causing gdMalloc to fail

step_by_step_execution.description: Memory for `res` and `res->ContribRow` is successfully allocated.

state.value: valid pointer to array of 1 ContributionType

step_by_step_execution.description: Loop starts with u = 0. Either overflow_error is set to 1, or Weights allocation returns NULL.
The error branch is entered. `u--` is executed. Since u is `unsigned int` with value 0, it wraps to UINT_MAX (4294967295).
The cleanup loop `for (i=0; i<=u; i++)` iterates up to UINT_MAX times, accessing `res->ContribRow[i].Weights` far beyond the allocated array of size 1, reading and freeing arbitrary memory.
