cve_desc: An out-of-bounds read in the BGP daemon of FRRouting FRR before 8.4 may lead to a segmentation fault and denial of service. This occurs in bgp_capability_msg_parse in bgpd/bgp_packet.c.

vulnerabilities.description: The memcpy copies sizeof(struct capability) bytes from the input buffer (pnt + 1) before validating that the buffer actually contains that many bytes. At this point, only 3 bytes from 'pnt' are guaranteed to exist (the initial bounds check ensures pnt + 3 <= end). If sizeof(struct capability) is larger than (end - pnt - 1), the memcpy reads past the end of the valid buffer, causing a heap buffer over-read. The cap.length validation that would catch this happens AFTER the over-read has already occurred.

step_by_step_execution.description: Function is called with a peer-supplied buffer 'pnt' of 'length' bytes. Consider length = 3 (minimum to pass the first bounds check).
The while loop begins: pnt < end is true.
The first bounds check passes: pnt + 3 <= end (3 <= 3), so execution continues past it.
action is read from *pnt (1 byte, safe).

memcpy(&cap, pnt + 1, sizeof(struct capability)) is executed. From pnt + 1, only 2 bytes remain in the valid buffer (end - (pnt+1) = 2). However, sizeof(struct capability) includes code (1 byte), length (1 byte), and a union containing mpc with afi (2 bytes) and safi (1 byte), making it likely >= 5 bytes. This reads at least 3 bytes beyond the end of the buffer.

state.value: >= 4 (likely 5+, includes code, length, and mpc union)

step_by_step_execution.description: The over-read has already occurred. The subsequent check 'pnt + (cap.length + 3) > end' that validates the capability length happens too late to prevent the buffer over-read from the memcpy.

state.value: contains data read from beyond buffer boundary

By crafting a bogus Firejail container that is accepted by the Firejail setuid-root program as a join target, a local attacker can enter an environment in which the Linux user namespace is still the initial user namespace, the NO_NEW_PRIVS prctl is not activated, and the entered mount namespace is under the attacker's control. commit_message:
ip6_gre: fix ip6gre_err() invalid reads

Andrey Konovalov reported out of bound accesses in ip6gre_err()

If GRE flags contains GRE_KEY, the following expression
*(((__be32 *)p) + (grehlen / 4) - 1)

accesses data ~40 bytes after the expected point, since
grehlen includes the size of IPv6 headers.

Let's use a "struct gre_base_hdr *greh" pointer to make this
code more readable.

p[1] becomes greh->protocol.
grhlen is the GRE header length.

Fixes: c12b395a4664 ("gre: Support GRE over IPv6")
Signed-off-by: Eric Dumazet <edumazet@google.com>
Reported-by: Andrey Konovalov <andreyknvl@google.com>
Signed-off-by: David S. Miller <davem@davemloft.net>
cve_desc: The ip6gre_err function in net/ipv6/ip6_gre.c in the Linux kernel allows remote attackers to have unspecified impact via vectors involving GRE flags in an IPv6 packet, which trigger an out-of-bounds access.

vulnerabilities.description: Out-of-bounds read when extracting the GRE key. The variable `p` already points to `skb->data + offset`, but `grehlen` includes `offset` (it is initialized as `offset + 4` and grows). The expression `((__be32 *)p) + (grehlen / 4) - 1` double-counts the offset, reading far beyond the validated data region. For a typical IPv6 header offset of 40, this reads at `skb->data + 40 + 4*((40+8)/4 - 1) = skb->data + 40 + 44 = skb->data + 84`, while `pskb_may_pull` only validated `grehlen = 48` bytes.

step_by_step_execution.description: Function is called with `offset` pointing to the GRE header within the ICMPv6 error payload. For a standard IPv6 header, offset = 40. p is set to skb->data + 40, grehlen is initialized to offset + 4 = 44.
flags are read from p[0]. Suppose GRE_KEY is set but GRE_CSUM is not, and no GRE_VERSION or GRE_ROUTING.
Since GRE_KEY is set and GRE_CSUM is not, grehlen is incremented by 4.
pskb_may_pull(skb, 48) is called, ensuring 48 bytes are accessible from skb->data. After the pull, p and ipv6h are reassigned.

The GRE key extraction computes: ((__be32 *)p) + (grehlen / 4) - 1 = ((__be32 *)(skb->data + 40)) + (48/4) - 1 = ((__be32 *)(skb->data + 40)) + 11. This accesses skb->data + 40 + 44 = skb->data + 84, which is 36 bytes beyond the 48 bytes validated by pskb_may_pull.

commit_message:
CVE-2022-31214: fixing the fix, one more time

the previous commit "CVE-2022-31214: fixing the fix"
made private-etc=fonts,fonts and similar commands
fail with an error

fix that regression by tolerating already existing
directories
cve_desc: A Privilege Context Switching issue was discovered in join.c in Firejail 0.9.68. By crafting a bogus Firejail container that is accepted by the Firejail setuid-root program as a join target, a local attacker can enter an environment in which the Linux user namespace is still the initial user namespace, the NO_NEW_PRIVS prctl is not activated, and the entered mount namespace is under the attacker's control. In this way, the filesystem layout can be adjusted to gain root privileges through execution of available setuid-root binaries such as su or sudo.

vulnerabilities.description: There is a TOCTOU (Time-of-Check-Time-of-Use) race condition between mkdir() and chmod(). After mkdir() creates the directory (or if it already exists due to EEXIST being ignored), an attacker with local access could replace the directory at `dst` with a symlink to an arbitrary file before chmod() is called. This would cause chmod() to follow the symlink and change permissions on an arbitrary file to `s.st_mode`, which is attacker-influenced (derived from stat on `src`, which itself could be a symlink).

step_by_step_execution.description: The loop begins iterating over path components in src and dst simultaneously.
A path separator or null terminator is found in src, triggering the directory creation logic. The src path is temporarily null-terminated and stat() is called.

state.value: success, S_ISDIR is true, s.st_mode contains the source directory's permissions

step_by_step_execution.description: The dst path is null-terminated at q, and the previous null is restored to '/'. mkdir(dst, 0700) is called. If the directory already exists, errno == EEXIST and execution continues.

state.value: null-terminated path to the new directory component

step_by_step_execution.description: Between mkdir() and chmod(), a local attacker replaces the directory at dst with a symlink pointing to a sensitive file (e.g., /etc/shadow). This is possible because mkdir succeeding with EEXIST means the path may already exist and could be swapped.

state.value: now points to a symlink created by the attacker

step_by_step_execution.description: chmod(dst, s.st_mode) is called, which follows the symlink and changes permissions on the attacker-chosen target file to s.st_mode.

state.value: permissions from the source directory, applied to the symlink target

commit_message:
udf: Restore i_lenAlloc when inode expansion fails

When we fail to expand inode from inline format to a normal format, we
restore inode to contain the original inline formatting but we forgot to
set i_lenAlloc back. The mismatch between i_lenAlloc and i_size was then
causing further problems such as warnings and lost data down the line.

Reported-by: butt3rflyh4ck <butterflyhuangxx@gmail.com>
CC: stable@vger.kernel.org
Fixes: 7e49b6f2480c ("udf: Convert UDF to new truncate calling sequence")
Reviewed-by: Christoph Hellwig <hch@lst.de>
Signed-off-by: Jan Kara <jack@suse.cz>
cve_desc: A flaw null pointer dereference in the Linux kernel UDF file system functionality was found in the way user triggers udf_file_write_iter function for the malicious UDF image. A local user could use this flaw to crash the system. Actual from Linux kernel 4.2-rc1 till 5.17-rc2.

commit_message: Fixed bug #22965 (Crash in gd lib's ImageFillToBorder()).
cve_desc: Stack consumption vulnerability in GD in PHP before 5.6.12 allows remote attackers to cause a denial of service via a crafted imagefilltoborder call.

vulnerabilities.description: The function clamps x and y against the upper bounds (im->sx, im->sy) but does not check for negative values. If x or y is negative, gdImageGetPixel and gdImageSetPixel will be called with negative coordinates, leading to out-of-bounds memory access (read and write).

step_by_step_execution.description: Function is called with a negative y value, e.g. y = -5. x is a valid value, e.g. x = 0.
The border check passes (border >= 0).
The upper bound checks: x >= im->sx is false, y >= im->sy is false. Neither check catches negative values.
The leftward seek loop starts with i = 0. gdImageGetPixel(im, 0, -5) is called, performing an out-of-bounds read using the negative y coordinate.
If the pixel read doesn't match border, gdImageSetPixel(im, 0, -5, color) is called, performing an out-of-bounds write using the negative y coordinate.

commit_message: use mkstemp for sdb
cve_desc: In Singular before 4.3.1, a predictable /tmp pathname is used (e.g., by sdb.cc), which allows local users to gain the privileges of other users via a procedure in a file under /tmp. NOTE: this CVE Record is about sdb.cc and similar files in the Singular interface that have predictable /tmp pathnames; this CVE Record is not about the lack of a safe temporary-file creation capability in the Singular language.

vulnerabilities.description: The EDITOR (or VISUAL) environment variable is read from the user's environment and passed directly to system() when it contains a space. An attacker who can control the EDITOR environment variable can inject arbitrary shell commands. For example, setting EDITOR to 'vi; rm -rf / #' would execute 'vi; rm -rf / # /tmp/sdXXXXXX' via the shell.

step_by_step_execution.description: The function begins and creates a temporary file via mkstemp.
pi->language equals LANG_SINGULAR, so the else branch is taken.
getenv("EDITOR") is called and returns an attacker-controlled string containing a space, e.g., "vi; malicious_command #".
The procedure body is written to the temp file and the file is closed.
fork() is called and returns 0 in the child process.
strchr(editor, ' ') finds a space in the editor string, so the else branch is taken.
A buffer is allocated and sprintf formats the editor and filename into it, then system() is called with the resulting string, executing arbitrary shell commands.

commit_message:
ALSA: control: Re-order bounds checking in get_ctl_id_hash()

These two checks are in the reverse order so it might read one element
beyond the end of the array.  First check if the "i" is within bounds
before using it.

Fixes: 6ab55ec0a938 ("ALSA: control: Fix an out-of-bounds bug in get_ctl_id_hash()")
Signed-off-by: Dan Carpenter <dan.carpenter@oracle.com>
Link: https://lore.kernel.org/r/YwjgNh/gkG1hH7po@kili
Signed-off-by: Takashi Iwai <tiwai@suse.de>
cve_desc: An out-of-bounds access issue was found in the Linux kernel sound subsystem. It could occur when the 'id->name' provided by the user did not end with '\0'. A privileged local user could pass a specially crafted name through ioctl() interface and crash the system or potentially escalate their privileges on the system.

commit_message:
Unsquashfs: additional write outside destination directory exploit fix

An issue on github (https://github.com/plougher/squashfs-tools/issues/72)
showed how some specially crafted Squashfs filesystems containing
invalid file names (with '/' and '..') can cause Unsquashfs to write
files outside of the destination directory.

Since then it has been shown that specially crafted Squashfs filesystems
that contain a symbolic link pointing outside of the destination directory,
coupled with an identically named file within the same directory, can
cause Unsquashfs to write files outside of the destination directory.

Specifically the symbolic link produces a pathname pointing outside
of the destination directory, which is then followed when writing the
duplicate identically named file within the directory.

This commit fixes this exploit by explictly checking for duplicate
filenames within a directory.  As directories in v2.1, v3.x, and v4.0
filesystems are sorted, this is achieved by checking for consecutively
identical filenames.  Additionally directories are checked to
ensure they are sorted, to avoid attempts to evade the duplicate
check.

Version 1.x and 2.0 filesystems (where the directories were unsorted)
are sorted and then the above duplicate filename check is applied.

Signed-off-by: Phillip Lougher <phillip@squashfs.org.uk>
cve_desc: squashfs_opendir in unsquash-2.c in Squashfs-Tools 4.5 allows Directory Traversal, a different vulnerability than CVE-2021-40153. A squashfs filesystem that has been crafted to include a symbolic link and then contents under the same filename in a filesystem can cause unsquashfs to first create the symbolic link pointing outside the expected directory, and then the subsequent write operation will cause the unsquashfs process to write through the symbolic link elsewhere in the filesystem.

commit_message: fix #2963 - oob write (1 byte) in pyc/marshal.c
cve_desc: Rizin is a UNIX-like reverse engineering framework and command-line toolset. Versions 0.4.0 and prior are vulnerable to an out-of-bounds write when getting data from PYC(python) files. A user opening a malicious PYC file could be affected by this vulnerability, allowing an attacker to execute code on the user's machine. Commit number 68948017423a12786704e54227b8b2f918c2fd27 contains a patch.

vulnerabilities.description: When the error check for n2 fails, the function returns NULL without freeing `ret` and `s1`, which were previously allocated. This causes a memory leak of both objects.

step_by_step_execution.description: ret is allocated via RZ_NEW0.
n1 is read from buffer successfully. Overflow check passes.
s1 is allocated and filled from buffer successfully.

state.value: valid heap pointer to n1+1 bytes

step_by_step_execution.description: n2 is read from buffer, but either an error occurs or UT32_ADD_OVFCHK(n2, 1) is true.
The function returns NULL without freeing ret or s1, leaking both allocations.

vulnerabilities.description: When the malloc for s1 fails, the function returns NULL without freeing the previously allocated `ret` object, causing a memory leak.

step_by_step_execution.description: ret is allocated via RZ_NEW0.
n1 is read successfully and overflow check passes.
malloc(n1 + 1) fails, returning NULL.
The function returns NULL without freeing ret, leaking the allocation.

vulnerabilities.description: When the malloc for s2 fails, the function returns NULL without freeing the previously allocated `ret` and `s1`, causing memory leaks.

step_by_step_execution.description: ret is allocated via RZ_NEW0 and s1 is allocated and filled successfully.
n2 is read successfully, overflow check passes.
malloc(n2 + 1) fails, returning NULL.
The function returns NULL without freeing ret or s1, leaking both allocations.

commit_message:
media: em28xx: initialize refcount before kref_get

The commit 47677e51e2a4("[media] em28xx: Only deallocate struct
em28xx after finishing all extensions") adds kref_get to many init
functions (e.g., em28xx_audio_init). However, kref_init is called too
late in em28xx_usb_probe, since em28xx_init_dev before will invoke
those init functions and call kref_get function. Then refcount bug
occurs in my local syzkaller instance.

Fix it by moving kref_init before em28xx_init_dev. This issue occurs
not only in dev but also dev->dev_next.

Fixes: 47677e51e2a4 ("[media] em28xx: Only deallocate struct em28xx after finishing all extensions")
Reported-by: syzkaller <syzkaller@googlegroups.com>
Signed-off-by: Dongliang Mu <mudongliangabcd@gmail.com>
Signed-off-by: Hans Verkuil <hverkuil-cisco@xs4all.nl>
cve_desc: A flaw use after free in the Linux kernel video4linux driver was found in the way user triggers em28xx_usb_probe() for the Empia 28xx based TV cards. A local user could use this flaw to crash the system or potentially escalate their privileges on the system.

vulnerabilities.description: After em28xx_init_dev(dev, ...) succeeds and usb_set_intfdata(intf, dev) has stored a pointer to 'dev' in the USB interface, if the second em28xx_init_dev(dev->dev_next, ...) call fails, the code jumps to err_free which frees 'dev' with kfree() without clearing the interface data pointer (usb_set_intfdata(intf, NULL)). This leaves a dangling pointer in the USB interface structure, leading to a use-after-free when the USB subsystem later accesses the interface data. Additionally, resources allocated by the first successful em28xx_init_dev() are never properly released.

step_by_step_execution.description: Device struct 'dev' is allocated with kzalloc and various fields are populated.

state.value: valid pointer to newly allocated em28xx struct

step_by_step_execution.description: usb_set_intfdata(intf, dev) stores the pointer to 'dev' inside the USB interface structure.
em28xx_init_dev(dev, udev, intf, nr) is called and succeeds (returns 0), initializing the device and potentially allocating sub-resources.
dev->board.has_dual_ts is true, and em28xx_duplicate_dev(dev) succeeds, creating dev->dev_next.
em28xx_init_dev(dev->dev_next, udev, intf, dev->dev_next->devno) is called and fails, returning a non-zero error code.

Code jumps to err_free label, which calls kfree(dev->alt_max_pkt_size_isoc) and kfree(dev). The USB interface still holds the stale pointer set by usb_set_intfdata, and resources from the first em28xx_init_dev are leaked. Any subsequent access to usb_get_intfdata(intf) dereferences freed memory.

commit_message: https://github.com/ImageMagick/ImageMagick/issues/4988
cve_desc: ImageMagick 7.1.0-27 is vulnerable to Buffer Overflow.

vulnerabilities.description: The image depth is set directly from an unvalidated single-byte value (bits_per_pixel) read from the file, which can range 0-255. The `length` for reading pixel rows is computed via `GetBytesPerRow` using this unvalidated depth, while the quantum pixel buffer (`pixels`) is allocated based on `AcquireQuantumInfo` which uses the quantum setting of 32 (via `SetQuantumQuantum`). If the attacker-controlled `image->depth` is larger than 32, `GetBytesPerRow` computes a `length` that exceeds the allocated pixel buffer size. When `ReadBlobStream` falls back to copying into the `pixels` buffer, this results in a heap buffer overflow.

step_by_step_execution.description: The CIN image channel info is read from the file, including bits_per_pixel as a single byte.
image->depth is set directly from the file-controlled bits_per_pixel with no bounds validation.
image->columns is set from the file-controlled pixels_per_line.
The sanity check `((MagickSizeType) image->columns*image->rows/8) > GetBlobSize(image)` passes because it divides by 8 and doesn't account for depth or channels properly.
AcquireQuantumInfo allocates the pixel buffer based on image properties. SetQuantumQuantum sets quantum to 32 bits.

state.value: allocated based on 32-bit quantum and image->columns

step_by_step_execution.description: GetQuantumExtent computes the expected extent based on the 32-bit quantum setting.

state.value: based on 32-bit quantum per sample, e.g., columns*3*4 bytes for RGB

step_by_step_execution.description: GetBytesPerRow computes `length` using the unvalidated image->depth of 64, yielding a larger value than the quantum pixel buffer.

state.value: GetBytesPerRow(1000, 3, 64, True) — larger than allocated pixels buffer

step_by_step_execution.description: ReadBlobStream is called with `length` exceeding the `pixels` buffer size. When it falls back to copying data into the `pixels` buffer, a heap buffer overflow occurs.

state.value: buffer overflow — write beyond allocated heap memory

commit_message: fix memory leaks in function cmdopts_parse
cve_desc: A vulnerability found in jasper. This security vulnerability happens because of a memory leak bug in function cmdopts_parse that can cause a crash or segmentation fault.

commit_message:
PCI: rpadlpar: Fix potential drc_name corruption in store functions

Both add_slot_store() and remove_slot_store() try to fix up the
drc_name copied from the store buffer by placing a NUL terminator at
nbyte + 1 or in place of a '\n' if present. However, the static buffer
that we copy the drc_name data into is not zeroed and can contain
anything past the n-th byte.

This is problematic if a '\n' byte appears in that buffer after nbytes
and the string copied into the store buffer was not NUL terminated to
start with as the strchr() search for a '\n' byte will mark this
incorrectly as the end of the drc_name string resulting in a drc_name
string that contains garbage data after the n-th byte.

Additionally it will cause us to overwrite that '\n' byte on the stack
with NUL, potentially corrupting data on the stack.

The following debugging shows an example of the drmgr utility writing
"PHB 4543" to the add_slot sysfs attribute, but add_slot_store()
logging a corrupted string value.

  drmgr: drmgr: -c phb -a -s PHB 4543 -d 1
  add_slot_store: drc_name = PHB 4543°|<82>!, rc = -19

Fix this by using strscpy() instead of memcpy() to ensure the string
is NUL terminated when copied into the static drc_name buffer.
Further, since the string is now NUL terminated the code only needs to
change '\n' to '\0' when present.

Cc: stable@vger.kernel.org
Signed-off-by: Tyrel Datwyler <tyreld@linux.ibm.com>
[mpe: Reformat change log and add mention of possible stack corruption]
Signed-off-by: Michael Ellerman <mpe@ellerman.id.au>
Link: https://lore.kernel.org/r/20210315214821.452959-1-tyreld@linux.ibm.com
cve_desc: In drivers/pci/hotplug/rpadlpar_sysfs.c in the Linux kernel through 5.11.8, the RPA PCI Hotplug driver has a user-tolerable buffer overflow when writing a new device name to the driver from userspace, allowing userspace to write data to the kernel stack frame directly. This occurs because add_slot_store and remove_slot_store mishandle drc_name '\0' termination, aka CID-cc7a0bb058b8.

commit_message: fix memory leaks in function cmdopts_parse
cve_desc: A vulnerability found in jasper. This security vulnerability happens because of a memory leak bug in function cmdopts_parse that can cause a crash or segmentation fault.

commit_message:
SplashOutputDev::tilingPatternFill: Fix crash on broken file

Issue #802
cve_desc: An issue was discovered in Poppler through 0.78.0. There is a divide-by-zero error in the function SplashOutputDev::tilingPatternFill at SplashOutputDev.cc.

vulnerabilities.description: Division by zero when both ctm[0] and ctm[1] are zero (or both matc[0] and matc[1] are zero in the second similar block). The isfinite check allows zero values. When both are zero, the condition `splashAbs(ctm[1]) > splashAbs(ctm[0])` is false (0 > 0), so the else branch executes and divides by ctm[0] which is 0.

step_by_step_execution.description: CTM is saved, then mat and bbox are concatenated via state->concatCTM calls.
The new ctm is retrieved and checked for isfinite. If ctm[0]==0 and ctm[1]==0, both pass isfinite (0 is finite).
The condition `splashAbs(ctm[1]) > splashAbs(ctm[0])` evaluates to false since 0 > 0 is false, so the else branch is taken.
In the else branch, `ky = ctm[3] - (ctm[1] * ctm[2]) / ctm[0]` performs a division by zero.

code_snippets:
result_width = tBitmap->getWidth() * imgData.repeatX;
  result_height = tBitmap->getHeight() * imgData.repeatY;
vulnerabilities.description: Integer overflow in the computation of result_width and result_height near the end of the function. tBitmap dimensions multiplied by repeat counts (which can be x1-x0 and y1-y0, attacker-influenced) are stored in int variables without overflow checks. The overflowed values are then passed to splash->drawImage, potentially causing heap buffer overflows or other memory corruption.

code_snippets: retValue = splash->drawImage(&tilingBitmapSrc, nullptr, &imgData, colorMode, true, result_width, result_height, matc, false, true) == splashOk;

step_by_step_execution.description: In the fabs(kx)<1 && fabs(ky)<1 branch, repeatX and repeatY are set to x1-x0 and y1-y0 respectively, which are attacker-controlled PDF parameters.

surface_width and surface_height are recalculated after scaling by ky=2/kx, but there is no upper bound check on surface_width*surface_height in this branch (unlike the else branch which checks against 0x800000L).

After gfx->display, result_width and result_height are recomputed as tBitmap->getWidth() * repeatX and tBitmap->getHeight() * repeatY. Since these are int multiplications with no overflow check, the product can overflow INT_MAX.
The potentially overflowed result_width and result_height are passed to splash->drawImage, which may use them for buffer sizing or iteration, leading to memory corruption.

state.value: overflowed int value (could be small positive, zero, or negative)
overflowed int value (could be small positive, zero, or negative)

PCI: rpadlpar: Fix potential drc_name corruption in store functions

Both add_slot_store() and remove_slot_store() try to fix up the

nbyte + 1 or in place of a '\n' if present. However, the static buffer
that we copy the drc_name data into is not zeroed and can contain
anything past the n-th byte.

This is problematic if a '\n' byte appears in that buffer after nbytes
and the string copied into the store buffer was not NUL terminated to
start with as the strchr() search for a '\n' byte will mark this
incorrectly as the end of the drc_name string resulting in a drc_name
string that contains garbage data after the n-th byte.

Additionally it will cause us to overwrite that '\n' byte on the stack
with NUL, potentially corrupting data on the stack.

The following debugging shows an example of the drmgr utility writing
"PHB 4543" to the add_slot sysfs attribute, but add_slot_store()
logging a corrupted string value.

  drmgr: drmgr: -c phb -a -s PHB 4543 -d 1
  add_slot_store: drc_name = PHB 4543°|<82>!, rc = -19

Fix this by using strscpy() instead of memcpy() to ensure the string
is NUL terminated when copied into the static drc_name buffer.
Further, since the string is now NUL terminated the code only needs to
change '\n' to '\0' when present.

Signed-off-by: Tyrel Datwyler <tyreld@linux.ibm.com>

commit_message: fix in thread.call
cve_desc: Heap-based buffer overflow in sqbaselib.cpp in SQUIRREL 3.2 due to lack of a certain sq_reservestack call.

commit_message: Fixed bug #22965 (Crash in gd lib's ImageFillToBorder()).
cve_desc: Stack consumption vulnerability in GD in PHP before 5.6.12 allows remote attackers to cause a denial of service via a crafted imagefilltoborder call.

vulnerabilities.description: The function does not validate that the initial `x` and `y` parameters are within the image bounds (0 <= x < im->sx, 0 <= y < im->sy) before passing them to gdImageGetPixel and gdImageSetPixel. If `y` is negative or >= im->sy, or `x` >= im->sx, out-of-bounds memory reads and writes will occur.

step_by_step_execution.description: Function is called with y >= im->sy (e.g., y = im->sy) and a valid x within bounds, border >= 0.
The border < 0 check passes since border >= 0, so the function does not return early.
The first for-loop starts with i = x = 0, which is >= 0, so the loop body executes.
gdImageGetPixel(im, 0, y) is called with y = im->sy, which is out of bounds for the image, causing an out-of-bounds read on the image pixel data.
If the out-of-bounds read does not match border, gdImageSetPixel(im, 0, y, color) is called with the same out-of-bounds y, causing an out-of-bounds write.

commit_message:
Bug 790784 - (CVE-2017-17784) heap overread in gbr parser / load_image.

We were assuming the input name was well formed, hence was
nul-terminated. As any data coming from external input, this has to be
thorougly checked.
Similar to commit 06d24a79af94837d615d0024916bb95a01bf3c59 but adapted
to older gimp-2-8 code.
cve_desc: In GIMP 2.8.22, there is a heap-based buffer over-read in load_image in plug-ins/common/file-gbr.c in the gbr import parser, related to mishandling of UTF-8 data.

vulnerabilities.description: Integer overflow in the allocation size when a pattern follows a 1-byte brush. The initial sanitization checks ensure `bh.width * bh.height * bh.bytes` does not exceed G_MAXSIZE, but when bh.bytes is 1, bh.width*bh.height can be close to G_MAXSIZE. Later, when a valid pattern header is found, the code allocates `4 * bh.width * bh.height` bytes without re-checking for overflow, causing a small allocation followed by writes far exceeding the buffer size.

step_by_step_execution.description: File is opened and BrushHeader is read from the file.
Sanitization check passes: bh.bytes==1, bh.width and bh.height are within GIMP_MAX_IMAGE_SIZE, and G_MAXSIZE / bh.width / bh.height / 1 >= 1.

state.value: 4294967296 (fits in gsize on 64-bit, but 4*this overflows on 32-bit) or chosen to be large enough that 4x overflows

step_by_step_execution.description: Initial brush_buf is allocated as size = bh.width * bh.height * 1 and read successfully.
Code enters bh.bytes==1 case and reads a PatternHeader. Attacker crafts a valid pattern header with matching width/height and bytes==3.
The expression `4 * bh.width * bh.height` overflows (e.g., on 32-bit gsize or with sufficiently large values). g_malloc allocates a much smaller buffer than intended.
The loop iterates ph.width * ph.height times, writing at offsets i*4 through i*4+3 into the undersized brush_buf, causing a massive heap buffer overflow.

state.value: up to 4*bh.width*bh.height - 1, far beyond allocated size

commit_message: fix in thread.call
cve_desc: Heap-based buffer overflow in sqbaselib.cpp in SQUIRREL 3.2 due to lack of a certain sq_reservestack call.

commit_message:
server: reset meta context replies on starttls

Related to CVE-2021-3716, but not as severe.  No compliant client will
send NBD_CMD_BLOCK_STATUS unless it first negotiates
NBD_OPT_SET_META_CONTEXT.  If an attacker injects a premature
SET_META_CONTEXT, either the client will never notice (because it
never uses BLOCK_STATUS), or the client will overwrite the attacker's
attempt with the client's own SET_META_CONTEXT request after
encryption is enabled.  So I don't class this as having the potential
to trigger denial-of-service due to any protocol mismatch between
compliant client and server (I don't care what happens with
non-compliant clients).

Fixes: 26455d45 (server: protocol: Implement Block Status "base:allocation".)
cve_desc: A flaw was found in nbdkit due to to improperly caching plaintext state across the STARTTLS encryption boundary. A MitM attacker could use this flaw to inject a plaintext NBD_OPT_STRUCTURED_REPLY before proxying everything else a client sends to the server, potentially leading the client to terminate the NBD session. The highest threat from this vulnerability is to system availability.

commit_message: Fix oob write for dwarf with abbrev with count 0 (Fix #2083) (#2086)
cve_desc: Rizin is a UNIX-like reverse engineering framework and command-line toolset. In versions up to and including 0.3.1 there is a heap-based out of bounds write in parse_die() when reversing an AMD64 ELF binary with DWARF debug info. When a malicious AMD64 ELF binary is opened by a victim user, Rizin may crash or execute unintended actions. No workaround are known and users are advised to upgrade.

commit_message:
SplashOutputDev::tilingPatternFill: Fix crash on broken file

Issue #802
cve_desc: An issue was discovered in Poppler through 0.78.0. There is a divide-by-zero error in the function SplashOutputDev::tilingPatternFill at SplashOutputDev.cc.

code_snippets:
result_width = tBitmap->getWidth() * imgData.repeatX;
  result_height = tBitmap->getHeight() * imgData.repeatY;
vulnerabilities.description: Integer overflow in the guard check `repeatX * repeatY <= 4` and in the subsequent computation of `result_width` and `result_height`. When `repeatX` and `repeatY` are both large (e.g., from the `x1-x0` and `y1-y0` path), their product overflows a 32-bit int, potentially wrapping to a small or negative value that passes the `<= 4` check. This leads to further overflowed `result_width`/`result_height` values being passed to `splash->drawImage`, which can cause heap buffer over-reads or over-writes.

code_snippets: retValue = splash->drawImage(&tilingBitmapSrc, nullptr, &imgData, colorMode, true, result_width, result_height, matc, false, true) == splashOk;

step_by_step_execution.description: Function entry. The parameters x0, y0, x1, y1 are attacker-influenced values from a PDF tiling pattern. Suppose x1 - x0 = 65536 and y1 - y0 = 65536.
width and height are computed from bbox. xStep == width and yStep == height pass the early check.
CTM is manipulated and validated (all finite). kx and ky are computed based on ctm values.
The code enters the branch where fabs(kx) < 1 && fabs(ky) < 1. Surface dimensions are recomputed, and repeatX/repeatY are set from x1-x0 and y1-y0.

The guard check `repeatX * repeatY <= 4` is evaluated. 65536 * 65536 = 4294967296, which overflows a 32-bit signed int. This is undefined behavior and in practice can wrap to 0, satisfying <= 4 and NOT returning false.

state.value: 0 <= 4 is true, so check passes and code continues (does NOT return false)

step_by_step_execution.description: A SplashBitmap of surface_width x surface_height is allocated (small), pattern is rendered into it, then result_width and result_height are computed.
splash->drawImage is called with potentially overflowed result_width and result_height, or the blit loop iterates repeatX*repeatY = 4 billion times writing out of bounds of the destination bitmap.

commit_message:
red fixes (#468)

* red fixes

* remove magic number
cve_desc: A race condition exists in Eternal Terminal prior to version 6.2.0 that allows an authenticated attacker to hijack other users' SSH authorization socket, enabling the attacker to login to other systems as the targeted users. The bug is in UserTerminalRouter::getInfoForId().

commit_message: fix #2963 - oob write (1 byte) in pyc/marshal.c
cve_desc: Rizin is a UNIX-like reverse engineering framework and command-line toolset. Versions 0.4.0 and prior are vulnerable to an out-of-bounds write when getting data from PYC(python) files. A user opening a malicious PYC file could be affected by this vulnerability, allowing an attacker to execute code on the user's machine. Commit number 68948017423a12786704e54227b8b2f918c2fd27 contains a patch.

vulnerabilities.description: When `get_st32` returns a negative value (e.g., -1), it is stored in `ut32 n1` as a very large value like 0xFFFFFFFF. The expression `n1 + 1` then overflows to 0, causing `malloc(0)` to return a small (or zero-size) allocation. The subsequent `rz_buf_read(buffer, s1, n1)` attempts to read up to 0xFFFFFFFF bytes into this undersized buffer, resulting in a massive heap buffer overflow.

step_by_step_execution.description: Function entry. `pyc->magic_int & 0xffff` is greater than 62061, so the else branch is taken.
`n1 = get_st32(buffer, &error)` reads a signed 32-bit value from buffer. If the buffer contains 0xFFFFFFFF (-1 as st32), it is stored in ut32 n1.
Error check passes since error is false.
`malloc(n1 + 1)` is called. `n1 + 1` = `0xFFFFFFFF + 1` = `0` due to unsigned 32-bit overflow. `malloc(0)` may return a valid non-NULL pointer to a zero-size heap allocation.
`rz_buf_read(buffer, s1, n1)` attempts to read 0xFFFFFFFF bytes into the zero-size buffer pointed to by s1, causing a heap buffer overflow.

vulnerabilities.description: Same integer overflow vulnerability applies to `n2`. When `get_st32` returns a negative value for `n2`, `n2 + 1` overflows to 0, `malloc(0)` returns a small allocation, and `rz_buf_read` writes up to 0xFFFFFFFF bytes into it.

step_by_step_execution.description: Function entry. `pyc->magic_int & 0xffff` is greater than 62061 for both n1 and n2 branches.
n1 is read successfully with a valid small value, s1 is allocated and filled correctly.
`n2 = get_st32(buffer, &error)` reads -1 from buffer, stored as 0xFFFFFFFF in ut32 n2.
`malloc(n2 + 1)` is called. `n2 + 1` overflows to 0. `malloc(0)` may return a valid non-NULL pointer.
`rz_buf_read(buffer, s2, n2)` attempts to read 0xFFFFFFFF bytes into the zero-size buffer, causing a heap buffer overflow.

vulnerabilities.description: After reading n2, if an error occurs, the function returns NULL without freeing `s1` and `ret`, causing a memory leak. While primarily a resource leak, in long-running applications this can lead to denial of service through memory exhaustion.

step_by_step_execution.description: n1 is successfully read and s1 is allocated and populated. ret was allocated at the start.
get_st32 (or get_ut8) for n2 fails, setting error to true.
The error check `if (error) { return NULL; }` executes, returning NULL without freeing s1 or ret.

commit_message:
media: em28xx: initialize refcount before kref_get

The commit 47677e51e2a4("[media] em28xx: Only deallocate struct
em28xx after finishing all extensions") adds kref_get to many init
functions (e.g., em28xx_audio_init). However, kref_init is called too
late in em28xx_usb_probe, since em28xx_init_dev before will invoke
those init functions and call kref_get function. Then refcount bug
occurs in my local syzkaller instance.

Fix it by moving kref_init before em28xx_init_dev. This issue occurs
not only in dev but also dev->dev_next.

Fixes: 47677e51e2a4 ("[media] em28xx: Only deallocate struct em28xx after finishing all extensions")
Reported-by: syzkaller <syzkaller@googlegroups.com>
Signed-off-by: Dongliang Mu <mudongliangabcd@gmail.com>
Signed-off-by: Hans Verkuil <hverkuil-cisco@xs4all.nl>
cve_desc: A flaw use after free in the Linux kernel video4linux driver was found in the way user triggers em28xx_usb_probe() for the Empia 28xx based TV cards. A local user could use this flaw to crash the system or potentially escalate their privileges on the system.

vulnerabilities.description: After `usb_set_intfdata(intf, dev)` stores a pointer to `dev` in the USB interface structure, if `em28xx_init_dev()` (or any subsequent operation) fails and jumps to `err_free`, the device struct `dev` is freed via `kfree(dev)` without clearing the interface data pointer via `usb_set_intfdata(intf, NULL)`. This leaves a dangling pointer in the USB interface, which can lead to a use-after-free if the interface data is later accessed (e.g., during disconnect).

step_by_step_execution.description: Device is allocated with kzalloc and various fields are initialized.

state.value: pointer to newly allocated em28xx struct

step_by_step_execution.description: `usb_set_intfdata(intf, dev)` stores `dev` pointer in the USB interface's private data.
`em28xx_init_dev(dev, udev, intf, nr)` is called. If it fails (returns non-zero), execution jumps to `err_free`.
At `err_free`, `kfree(dev->alt_max_pkt_size_isoc)` and `kfree(dev)` free the device memory, but `usb_set_intfdata(intf, NULL)` is never called.
Any subsequent access to the interface data (e.g., during USB disconnect callback) will dereference the dangling pointer, causing a use-after-free.

vulnerabilities.description: When `em28xx_duplicate_dev(dev)` succeeds and sets up `dev->dev_next`, a second `em28xx_init_dev` call is made for `dev->dev_next`. If this second init fails, execution jumps to `err_free`, which only frees `dev->alt_max_pkt_size_isoc` and `dev`, without cleaning up the already-initialized first device (registered by the first successful `em28xx_init_dev` call) or the duplicated `dev->dev_next` struct. This leads to resource leaks and a use-after-free as the first device's initialization has registered resources referencing the now-freed `dev`.

step_by_step_execution.description: Device `dev` is allocated and fully initialized. `em28xx_init_dev(dev, ...)` succeeds, registering the device and its subsystems which hold references to `dev`.

state.value: initialized em28xx device, registered with subsystems

step_by_step_execution.description: `dev->board.has_dual_ts` is true and `em28xx_duplicate_dev(dev)` succeeds, allocating and partially setting up `dev->dev_next`.

state.value: pointer to newly allocated secondary em28xx struct

step_by_step_execution.description: The second `em28xx_init_dev(dev->dev_next, ...)` is called and fails.

Execution jumps to `err_free`, which frees `dev->alt_max_pkt_size_isoc` and `dev` with kfree. However, the first `em28xx_init_dev` registered subsystems that hold references to `dev`, which is now freed. `dev->dev_next` is also leaked.

state.value: hold dangling pointers to freed dev

commit_message: use mkstemp for sdb
cve_desc: In Singular before 4.3.1, a predictable /tmp pathname is used (e.g., by sdb.cc), which allows local users to gain the privileges of other users via a procedure in a file under /tmp. NOTE: this CVE Record is about sdb.cc and similar files in the Singular interface that have predictable /tmp pathnames; this CVE Record is not about the lack of a safe temporary-file creation capability in the Singular language.

vulnerabilities.description: Heap buffer overflow when writing the PID into the filename buffer. The string "/tmp/sd000000" is 13 characters long, so omStrDup allocates 14 bytes. sprintf writes the PID at offset 7, leaving only 7 bytes (indices 7-13). A PID of 7 digits (e.g., 4194304) requires 8 bytes (7 digits + null terminator), causing a 1-byte heap overflow.

step_by_step_execution.description: filename is allocated as a duplicate of the string literal "/tmp/sd000000".
sprintf writes the decimal PID at offset 7 in the buffer. If the PID is 7 digits (e.g., 4194304), it needs 8 bytes (7 digits + null).
sprintf writes 1 byte past the end of the allocated heap buffer, corrupting heap metadata or adjacent heap objects.

vulnerabilities.description: OS command injection via the EDITOR (or VISUAL) environment variable. When the EDITOR value contains a space, the code concatenates it with the filename and passes it to system(), allowing an attacker who controls the environment variable to inject arbitrary shell commands (e.g., EDITOR="vi;malicious_cmd ").

step_by_step_execution.description: The EDITOR environment variable is read from the environment.
Since editor contains a space, the strchr(editor,' ') check is non-NULL, so we enter the else branch.
The editor string and filename are concatenated with sprintf into buffer p.
system(p) is called, executing the attacker-injected commands via the shell.

vulnerabilities.description: Predictable temporary file name in /tmp allows symlink attacks. An attacker can predict the filename (based on PID) and create a symlink at that path pointing to an arbitrary file, causing the program to overwrite or read arbitrary files.

step_by_step_execution.description: The temporary filename is constructed using the predictable PID.
An attacker creates a symlink at /tmp/sd<pid> pointing to a target file (e.g., /etc/important_file) before the fopen call.
fopen opens the file for writing, following the symlink and truncating/overwriting the target file.

commit_message:
Bug 702582, CVE 2020-15900 Memory Corruption in Ghostscript 9.52

Fix the 'rsearch' calculation for the 'post' size to give the correct
size.  Previous calculation would result in a size that was too large,
and could underflow to max uint32_t. Also fix 'rsearch' to return the
correct 'pre' string with empty string match.

A future change may 'undefine' this undocumented, non-standard operator
during initialization as we do with the many other non-standard internal
PostScript operators and procedures.
cve_desc: A memory corruption issue was found in Artifex Ghostscript 9.50 and 9.52. Use of a non-standard PostScript operator can allow overriding of file access controls. The 'rsearch' calculation for the 'post' size resulted in a size that was too large, and could underflow to max uint32_t. This was fixed in commit 5d499272b95a6b890a1397e11d20937de000d31b.

commit_message:
lib/iov_iter: initialize "flags" in new pipe_buffer

The functions copy_page_to_iter_pipe() and push_pipe() can both
allocate a new pipe_buffer, but the "flags" member initializer is
missing.

Fixes: 241699cd72a8 ("new iov_iter flavour: pipe-backed")
To: Alexander Viro <viro@zeniv.linux.org.uk>
To: linux-fsdevel@vger.kernel.org
To: linux-kernel@vger.kernel.org
Cc: stable@vger.kernel.org
Signed-off-by: Max Kellermann <max.kellermann@ionos.com>
Signed-off-by: Al Viro <viro@zeniv.linux.org.uk>
cve_desc: A flaw was found in the way the "flags" member of the new pipe buffer structure was lacking proper initialization in copy_page_to_iter_pipe and push_pipe functions in the Linux kernel and could thus contain stale values. An unprivileged local user could use this flaw to write to pages in the page cache backed by read only files and as such escalate their privileges on the system.

commit_message:
Fix #73280: Stack Buffer Overflow in GD dynamicGetbuf

We make sure to never pass a negative `rlen` as size to memcpy().

Cf. <https://github.com/libgd/libgd/commit/53110871>.
cve_desc: Integer signedness error in the dynamicGetbuf function in gd_io_dp.c in the GD Graphics Library (aka libgd) through 2.2.3, as used in PHP before 5.6.28 and 7.x before 7.0.13, allows remote attackers to cause a denial of service (stack-based buffer overflow) or possibly have unspecified other impact via a crafted imagecreatefromstring call.

vulnerabilities.description: If the caller passes a negative `len`, the comparison `remain >= len` will almost certainly be true (since remain is typically non-negative), causing `rlen` to be set to the negative `len`. When `rlen` (a negative int) is passed to `memcpy`, it is implicitly converted to `size_t` (an unsigned type), resulting in a very large copy length, causing a massive heap buffer over-read and out-of-bounds write.

step_by_step_execution.description: The function is called with a negative `len` value (e.g., len = -1).
The dynamic buffer state is read; assume logicalSize=100, pos=0, so remain = 100.
The comparison `remain >= len` is evaluated: 100 >= -1 is true, so `rlen` is set to `len`.

`memcpy` is called. `rlen` (-1 as int) is implicitly converted to `size_t`, becoming a very large value (e.g., 0xFFFFFFFF on 32-bit or 0xFFFFFFFFFFFFFFFF on 64-bit), causing a massive out-of-bounds read from `dp->data` and out-of-bounds write into `buf`.

vulnerabilities.description: If `dp->pos` is greater than `dp->logicalSize`, `remain` becomes negative. The check `remain >= len` fails (assuming positive len), and `remain == 0` is false, so `rlen` is assigned the negative `remain`. This negative value is then cast to a huge `size_t` in the `memcpy` call, causing a heap buffer over-read and write.

step_by_step_execution.description: The function is called with a positive len (e.g., 10). dp->pos has been advanced beyond dp->logicalSize due to external manipulation or a bug.
`remain` is computed as `dp->logicalSize - dp->pos` = 100 - 150 = -50.
`remain >= len` evaluates to `-50 >= 10` which is false.
`remain == 0` evaluates to false, so we take the else branch and set `rlen = remain`.
`memcpy` is called with `rlen = -50`, which is cast to a very large `size_t`, causing a massive out-of-bounds read and write.

commit_message: check vertexIndex which might be -1 for broken DXF
cve_desc: A code execution vulnerability exists in the DL_Dxf::handleLWPolylineData functionality of Ribbonsoft dxflib 3.17.0. A specially-crafted .dxf file can lead to a heap buffer overflow. An attacker can provide a malicious file to trigger this vulnerability.

vulnerabilities.description: Integer overflow in the expression `4*maxVertices` when `maxVertices` is a large value (e.g., 0x20000001). The multiplication overflows the `int` range, resulting in a small allocation, while the subsequent initialization loop iterates `maxVertices` times, writing far beyond the allocated buffer.

step_by_step_execution.description: groupCode is 90, so we enter the first branch. maxVertices is set from user-controlled input via toInt(groupValue).
maxVertices > 0 check passes. Old vertices array is freed if non-null.

Allocation: `new double[4*maxVertices]`. The expression `4 * 536870913` overflows a 32-bit int: 4 * 0x20000001 = 0x80000004, which wraps to a small or negative value. On many implementations this results in a very small allocation (e.g., 4 doubles / 32 bytes).

state.value: 4 (after 32-bit integer overflow from 0x80000004 interpreted as signed int, or a small allocation size)

step_by_step_execution.description: The initialization loop runs for i=0 to maxVertices-1 (536870912 iterations), writing to vertices[i*4] through vertices[i*4+3]. Since the buffer only holds ~4 doubles, this writes massively out of bounds on the heap.

state.value: writes beyond allocated buffer starting from i=1

commit_message: 0.16.1: fix for dcraw ljpeg_start() vulnerability
cve_desc: Integer overflow in the ljpeg_start function in dcraw 7.00 and earlier allows remote attackers to cause a denial of service (crash) via a crafted image, which triggers a buffer overflow, related to the len variable.

vulnerabilities.description: Integer underflow in the computation of `len`. When the JPEG marker length field (data[2]<<8|data[3]) is 0 or 1, subtracting 2 produces a negative int value that, when stored in the `ushort len`, wraps to a very large value (65534 or 65535). This causes `fread` to read an excessive amount of data and subsequent processing (e.g., the Huffman table parsing loop in case 0xffc4) to operate over far more data than intended, processing attacker-controlled or uninitialized bytes.

code_snippets:
for (dp = data; dp < data+len && (c = *dp++) < 4; )
	  jh->free[c] = jh->huff[c] = make_decoder_ref (&dp);

step_by_step_execution.description: The outer do-while loop reads the next 4 bytes for a JPEG marker tag and length.
The tag is computed and passes the check `tag <= 0xff00` (e.g., tag = 0xffc4).
The length field is computed. If data[2]=0 and data[3]=0, the expression (0 << 8 | 0) - 2 = -2 as int, which wraps to 65534 when stored in ushort.

fread(data, 1, 65534, ifp) attempts to read 65534 bytes from the file into the stack buffer. The buffer is 0x10000 (65536) bytes, so no immediate stack overflow, but the data now contains attacker-controlled content far exceeding the intended marker payload.

state.value: Up to 65534 bytes read from file

step_by_step_execution.description: In case 0xffc4, the Huffman decoding loop iterates with bound `dp < data+len` (data+65534), processing up to 65534 bytes of attacker-controlled data through make_decoder_ref, potentially creating corrupt Huffman tables and causing further out-of-bounds operations.

state.value: Iterates from data[0] up to data[65533]

vulnerabilities.description: In the 0xffda case, array indices are computed using attacker-controlled data[0] without validation against `len`. If `len` is small (e.g., 3 bytes actually read), but data[0] is large, the expressions `1+data[0]*2` and `3+data[0]*2` (up to 513) access bytes in the stack buffer that were not populated by the current fread, reading uninitialized or stale stack memory.

step_by_step_execution.description: The do-while loop reads a marker with tag 0xffda and a small length, e.g., data[2]=0, data[3]=5 giving len=3.
fread(data, 1, 3, ifp) reads only 3 bytes into the beginning of data[]. The rest of data[] contains stale/uninitialized stack values.
data[0] is attacker-controlled. If data[0] = 100, index 1+100*2 = 201 and 3+100*2 = 203 are well beyond the 3 bytes read.
jh->psv is set from data[201] and jh->bits is modified by data[203], both reading uninitialized stack memory.

state.value: Modified by uninitialized stack data at data[203]

commit_message:
random32: update the net random state on interrupt and activity

This modifies the first 32 bits out of the 128 bits of a random CPU's
net_rand_state on interrupt or CPU activity to complicate remote
observations that could lead to guessing the network RNG's internal
state.

Note that depending on some network devices' interrupt rate moderation
or binding, this re-seeding might happen on every packet or even almost
never.

In addition, with NOHZ some CPUs might not even get timer interrupts,
leaving their local state rarely updated, while they are running
networked processes making use of the random state.  For this reason, we
also perform this update in update_process_times() in order to at least
update the state when there is user or system activity, since it's the
only case we care about.

Reported-by: Amit Klein <aksecurity@gmail.com>
Suggested-by: Linus Torvalds <torvalds@linux-foundation.org>
Cc: Eric Dumazet <edumazet@google.com>
Cc: "Jason A. Donenfeld" <Jason@zx2c4.com>
Cc: Andy Lutomirski <luto@kernel.org>
Cc: Kees Cook <keescook@chromium.org>
Cc: Thomas Gleixner <tglx@linutronix.de>
Cc: Peter Zijlstra <peterz@infradead.org>
Cc: <stable@vger.kernel.org>
Signed-off-by: Willy Tarreau <w@1wt.eu>
Signed-off-by: Linus Torvalds <torvalds@linux-foundation.org>
cve_desc: The Linux kernel through 5.7.11 allows remote attackers to make observations that help to obtain sensitive information about the internal state of the network RNG, aka CID-f227e3ec3b5c. This is related to drivers/char/random.c and kernel/time/timer.c.

commit_message:
red fixes (#468)

* remove magic number
cve_desc: A race condition exists in Eternal Terminal prior to version 6.2.0 that allows an authenticated attacker to hijack other users' SSH authorization socket, enabling the attacker to login to other systems as the targeted users. The bug is in UserTerminalRouter::getInfoForId().

vulnerabilities.description: The pipe path from endpoint.name() is copied into local.sun_path (a fixed-size buffer, typically 108 bytes on Linux) using strcpy without any length check. If pipePath exceeds the size of sun_path, a stack buffer overflow occurs.

step_by_step_execution.description: The function receives an endpoint parameter and extracts pipePath from endpoint.name().

state.value: attacker-controlled string, potentially longer than 107 characters

step_by_step_execution.description: A sockaddr_un struct is declared on the stack. sun_path is a fixed-size char array (typically 108 bytes).
strcpy copies pipePath into local.sun_path without bounds checking, overflowing the stack buffer if pipePath.size() >= 108.

state.value: overflowed with data beyond its 108-byte boundary

This modifies the first 32 bits out of the 128 bits of a random CPU's
net_rand_state on interrupt or CPU activity to complicate remote
observations that could lead to guessing the network RNG's internal
state.

Note that depending on some network devices' interrupt rate moderation
or binding, this re-seeding might happen on every packet or even almost
never.

In addition, with NOHZ some CPUs might not even get timer interrupts,
leaving their local state rarely updated, while they are running
networked processes making use of the random state.  For this reason, we
also perform this update in update_process_times() in order to at least
update the state when there is user or system activity, since it's the
only case we care about.

cve_desc: The Linux kernel through 5.7.11 allows remote attackers to make observations that help to obtain sensitive information about the internal state of the network RNG, aka CID-f227e3ec3b5c. This is related to drivers/char/random.c and kernel/time/timer.c.

commit_message:
random32: update the net random state on interrupt and activity

This modifies the first 32 bits out of the 128 bits of a random CPU's
net_rand_state on interrupt or CPU activity to complicate remote
observations that could lead to guessing the network RNG's internal
state.

Note that depending on some network devices' interrupt rate moderation
or binding, this re-seeding might happen on every packet or even almost
never.

In addition, with NOHZ some CPUs might not even get timer interrupts,
leaving their local state rarely updated, while they are running
networked processes making use of the random state.  For this reason, we
also perform this update in update_process_times() in order to at least
update the state when there is user or system activity, since it's the
only case we care about.

Reported-by: Amit Klein <aksecurity@gmail.com>
Suggested-by: Linus Torvalds <torvalds@linux-foundation.org>
Cc: Eric Dumazet <edumazet@google.com>
Cc: "Jason A. Donenfeld" <Jason@zx2c4.com>
Cc: Andy Lutomirski <luto@kernel.org>

Cc: Thomas Gleixner <tglx@linutronix.de>

cve_desc: The Linux kernel through 5.7.11 allows remote attackers to make observations that help to obtain sensitive information about the internal state of the network RNG, aka CID-f227e3ec3b5c. This is related to drivers/char/random.c and kernel/time/timer.c.

commit_message:
Make ZlibInStream more robust against failures

Move the checks around to avoid missing cases where we might access
memory that is no longer valid. Also avoid touching the underlying
stream implicitly (e.g. via the destructor) as it might also no
longer be valid.

A malicious server could theoretically use this for remote code
execution in the client.

Issue found by Pavel Cheremushkin from Kaspersky Lab
cve_desc: TigerVNC version prior to 1.10.1 is vulnerable to stack use-after-return, which occurs due to incorrect usage of stack memory in ZRLEDecoder. If decoding routine would throw an exception, ZRLEDecoder may try to access stack variable, which has been already freed during the process of stack unwinding. Exploitation of this vulnerability could potentially result into remote code execution. This attack appear to be exploitable via network connectivity.

vulnerabilities.description: The palette array has 128 entries but only `palSize` entries are initialized. In both packed-pixel and palette-RLE modes, the computed index can exceed `palSize - 1`, causing a read of uninitialized stack memory. An attacker-controlled ZRLE stream can set pixel data indices that reference uninitialized palette slots, potentially leaking stack data into the displayed framebuffer.

step_by_step_execution.description: The mode byte is read from the zlib stream. For example, mode = 20 (rle=false, palSize=20).
The palette array of 128 PIXEL_T entries is allocated on the stack. Only entries 0..19 are initialized from the stream.
Since palSize > 16, bppp is set to 8. Packed pixel decoding begins.
A byte is read from the stream. With bppp=8, the full byte value is used as the index, masked with ((1<<8)-1) & 127, yielding a value in range 0-127.
palette[100] is read, but only palette[0..19] were initialized. Uninitialized stack data is written to buf and then to the pixel buffer via pb->imageRect().

state.value: uninitialized stack data leaked to framebuffer

vulnerabilities.description: In the palette RLE branch, the index read from the stream is masked to 0-127 but the palette only contains `palSize` initialized entries. An attacker can supply an index >= palSize to read uninitialized stack memory from the palette array, which is then written into the pixel buffer.

step_by_step_execution.description: The mode byte is read. For example, mode = 130 (rle=true, palSize=2).
Only palette[0] and palette[1] are initialized. palette[2..127] remain uninitialized.
In the palette RLE loop, an index byte is read from the stream, e.g. 50 (no high bit set, so len=1).
index is masked with 127, yielding 50. palette[50] is read — uninitialized memory.
The uninitialized pixel value is written to buf and subsequently to the pixel buffer.
