commit_message: Fixed an issue affecting recursions in JIT
cve_desc: An out-of-bounds read vulnerability was discovered in the PCRE2 library in the get_recurse_data_length() function of the pcre2_jit_compile.c file. This issue affects recursions in JIT-compiled regular expressions caused by duplicate data transfers.

commit_message: Fixed an issue affecting recursions in JIT
cve_desc: An out-of-bounds read vulnerability was discovered in the PCRE2 library in the get_recurse_data_length() function of the pcre2_jit_compile.c file. This issue affects recursions in JIT-compiled regular expressions caused by duplicate data transfers.

commit_message:
Calcualte max nspace declarations correctly for XML writer

(raptor_xml_writer_start_element_common): Calculate max including for
each attribute a potential name and value.

Fixes Issues #0000617 http://bugs.librdf.org/mantis/view.php?id=617
and #0000618 http://bugs.librdf.org/mantis/view.php?id=618
cve_desc: raptor_xml_writer_start_element_common in raptor_xml_writer.c in Raptor RDF Syntax Library 2.0.15 miscalculates the maximum nspace declarations for the XML writer, leading to heap-based buffer overflows (sometimes seen in raptor_qname_format_as_xml).

code_snippets:
for(j = 0; j < nspace_declarations_count; j++)
            if(nspace_declarations[j].nspace == element->attributes[j]->nspace) {
vulnerabilities.description: The inner deduplication loop uses index `j` to access `element->attributes[j]` instead of the outer loop's `element->attributes[i]`. As `nspace_declarations_count` grows (it includes entries for element namespace and attribute values, not just attribute namespaces), `j` can exceed `element->attribute_count`, causing an out-of-bounds read on the `element->attributes` array.

step_by_step_execution.description: Function is called with an element that has `element->name->nspace` set (non-NULL) and `attribute_count = 2`, with both attributes having distinct namespaces not in scope.
nspace_max_count is computed. The nspace_declarations array is allocated.
Element's own namespace is not in scope, so nspace_declarations[0] is filled with the element namespace declaration.

Attribute loop begins with i=0. The attribute's nspace is not in scope and differs from element's nspace. The inner dedup loop runs for j=0: compares nspace_declarations[0].nspace with element->attributes[0]->nspace. No match found (element ns != attr0 ns). declare_me remains 1, so a namespace declaration is added at index 1.
The attribute value for attributes[0] is added at index 2.

Attribute loop continues with i=1. The attribute's nspace is not in scope and differs from element's nspace. The inner dedup loop now runs for j=0,1,2. When j=2, the code accesses element->attributes[2]->nspace, but attribute_count is only 2, so valid indices are 0 and 1. This is an out-of-bounds read.

commit_message:
xfrm: xfrm_policy: fix a possible double xfrm_pols_put() in xfrm_bundle_lookup()

xfrm_policy_lookup() will call xfrm_pol_hold_rcu() to get a refcount of
pols[0]. This refcount can be dropped in xfrm_expand_policies() when
xfrm_expand_policies() return error. pols[0]'s refcount is balanced in
here. But xfrm_bundle_lookup() will also call xfrm_pols_put() with
num_pols == 1 to drop this refcount when xfrm_expand_policies() return
error.

This patch also fix an illegal address access. pols[0] will save a error
point when xfrm_policy_lookup fails. This lead to xfrm_pols_put to resolve
an illegal address in xfrm_bundle_lookup's error path.

Fix these by setting num_pols = 0 in xfrm_expand_policies()'s error path.

Fixes: 80c802f3073e ("xfrm: cache bundles instead of policies for outgoing flows")
Signed-off-by: Hangyu Hua <hbh25y@gmail.com>
Signed-off-by: Steffen Klassert <steffen.klassert@secunet.com>
cve_desc: An issue was discovered in the Linux kernel through 5.18.14. xfrm_expand_policies in net/xfrm/xfrm_policy.c can cause a refcount to be dropped twice.

commit_message:
net: xilinx_emaclite: Do not print real IOMEM pointer

Printing kernel pointers is discouraged because they might leak kernel
memory layout.  This fixes smatch warning:

drivers/net/ethernet/xilinx/xilinx_emaclite.c:1191 xemaclite_of_probe() warn:
 argument 4 to %08lX specifier is cast from pointer

Signed-off-by: YueHaibing <yuehaibing@huawei.com>
Signed-off-by: David S. Miller <davem@davemloft.net>
cve_desc: drivers/net/ethernet/xilinx/xilinx_emaclite.c in the Linux kernel before 5.13.3 makes it easier for attackers to defeat an ASLR protection mechanism because it prints a kernel pointer (i.e., the real IOMEM pointer).

commit_message:
mount: sanitize paths from non-root users

 $ mount /root/.ssh/../../dev/sda2
 mount: only root can mount UUID=17bc65ec-4125-4e7c-8a7d-e2795064c736 on /boot

this is too promiscuous. It seems better to ignore on command line
specified paths which are not resolve-able for non-root users.

Fixed version:

  $ mount /root/.ssh/../../dev/sda2
  mount: /root/.ssh/../../dev/sda2: Permission denied

  $ mount /dev/sda2
  mount: only root can mount UUID=17bc65ec-4125-4e7c-8a7d-e2795064c736 on /boot

Note that this bug has no relation to mount(2) permissions evaluation
in suid mode. The way how non-root user specifies paths on command
line is completely irrelevant for comparison with fstab entries.

Signed-off-by: Karel Zak <kzak@redhat.com>
cve_desc: (a) mount and (b) umount in util-linux 2.14.1, 2.17.2, and probably other versions allow local users to determine the existence of restricted directories by (1) using the --guess-fstype command-line option or (2) attempting to mount a non-existent device, which generates different error messages depending on whether the directory exists.

commit_message:
xfrm: xfrm_policy: fix a possible double xfrm_pols_put() in xfrm_bundle_lookup()

xfrm_policy_lookup() will call xfrm_pol_hold_rcu() to get a refcount of
pols[0]. This refcount can be dropped in xfrm_expand_policies() when
xfrm_expand_policies() return error. pols[0]'s refcount is balanced in
here. But xfrm_bundle_lookup() will also call xfrm_pols_put() with
num_pols == 1 to drop this refcount when xfrm_expand_policies() return
error.

This patch also fix an illegal address access. pols[0] will save a error
point when xfrm_policy_lookup fails. This lead to xfrm_pols_put to resolve
an illegal address in xfrm_bundle_lookup's error path.

Fix these by setting num_pols = 0 in xfrm_expand_policies()'s error path.

Fixes: 80c802f3073e ("xfrm: cache bundles instead of policies for outgoing flows")
Signed-off-by: Hangyu Hua <hbh25y@gmail.com>
Signed-off-by: Steffen Klassert <steffen.klassert@secunet.com>
cve_desc: An issue was discovered in the Linux kernel through 5.18.14. xfrm_expand_policies in net/xfrm/xfrm_policy.c can cause a refcount to be dropped twice.

commit_message:
mount: sanitize paths from non-root users

 $ mount /root/.ssh/../../dev/sda2
 mount: only root can mount UUID=17bc65ec-4125-4e7c-8a7d-e2795064c736 on /boot

this is too promiscuous. It seems better to ignore on command line
specified paths which are not resolve-able for non-root users.

Fixed version:

  $ mount /root/.ssh/../../dev/sda2
  mount: /root/.ssh/../../dev/sda2: Permission denied

  $ mount /dev/sda2
  mount: only root can mount UUID=17bc65ec-4125-4e7c-8a7d-e2795064c736 on /boot

Note that this bug has no relation to mount(2) permissions evaluation
in suid mode. The way how non-root user specifies paths on command
line is completely irrelevant for comparison with fstab entries.

Signed-off-by: Karel Zak <kzak@redhat.com>
cve_desc: (a) mount and (b) umount in util-linux 2.14.1, 2.17.2, and probably other versions allow local users to determine the existence of restricted directories by (1) using the --guess-fstype command-line option or (2) attempting to mount a non-existent device, which generates different error messages depending on whether the directory exists.

commit_message:
Avoid possible segfault when handling networkstatus vote with bad flavor

Fix for 6530; fix on 0.2.2.6-alpha.
cve_desc: Use-after-free vulnerability in dns.c in Tor before 0.2.2.38 might allow remote attackers to cause a denial of service (daemon crash) via vectors related to failed DNS requests.

commit_message:
netfilter: nf_tables: disallow binding to already bound chain

Update nft_data_init() to report EINVAL if chain is already bound.

Fixes: d0e2c7de92c7 ("netfilter: nf_tables: add NFT_CHAIN_BINDING")
Reported-by: Gwangun Jung <exsociety@gmail.com>
Signed-off-by: Pablo Neira Ayuso <pablo@netfilter.org>
cve_desc: An issue was discovered in net/netfilter/nf_tables_api.c in the Linux kernel before 5.19.6. A denial of service can occur upon binding to an already bound chain.

vulnerabilities.description: The chain reference counter `chain->use` is incremented without checking for integer overflow. If an attacker can create a sufficient number of verdicts (NFT_JUMP or NFT_GOTO) referencing the same chain, the `use` counter (typically a u32) can wrap around to zero. After overflow, subsequent operations that decrement `use` (e.g., removing rules) can cause an underflow, potentially leading to a use-after-free when the chain is prematurely freed while still referenced.

step_by_step_execution.description: The function is called with a verdict code of NFT_JUMP or NFT_GOTO, and the netlink attribute NFTA_VERDICT_CHAIN (or NFTA_VERDICT_CHAIN_ID) identifies a valid, non-base, non-bound chain.

The chain is successfully looked up via nft_chain_lookup or nft_chain_lookup_byid, returning a valid pointer. All validation checks (IS_ERR, nft_is_base_chain, nft_chain_is_bound, binding check) pass.
chain->use is incremented unconditionally without any overflow check.
An attacker repeats this operation approximately 2^32 times (for a u32 counter) targeting the same chain, causing the use counter to wrap around to 0.

With chain->use at 0, a subsequent rule deletion that decrements chain->use will underflow the counter or cause the chain to be freed while still being referenced by other verdicts, leading to a use-after-free condition.

commit_message:
red fixes (#468)

* red fixes

* remove magic number
cve_desc: A race condition exists in Eternal Terminal prior to version 6.2.0 that allows an authenticated attacker to hijack other users' SSH authorization socket, enabling the attacker to login to other systems as the targeted users. The bug is in UserTerminalRouter::getInfoForId().

vulnerabilities.description: strncpy does not null-terminate the destination buffer when the source string length is >= sizeof(local.sun_path). Subsequent uses of local.sun_path (unlink, bind, chmod) will read beyond the buffer looking for a null terminator, causing out-of-bounds reads and potentially operating on unintended paths.

step_by_step_execution.description: The function is called with an endpoint whose name() returns a string of length >= sizeof(local.sun_path) (108 bytes on most Linux systems).
A sockaddr_un struct 'local' is declared on the stack. Its sun_path member is uninitialized (contains stack garbage).
strncpy copies exactly sizeof(local.sun_path) bytes from pipePath but does NOT write a null terminator because the source length >= the limit.

state.value: 108 bytes from pipePath, no null terminator

step_by_step_execution.description: unlink(local.sun_path) is called. Since sun_path is not null-terminated, unlink reads past the end of sun_path into adjacent memory on the stack until it finds a null byte, forming an unintended path string.

state.value: non-null-terminated, read goes out of bounds

step_by_step_execution.description: Similarly, bind() and chmod() operate on the non-null-terminated sun_path, reading out of bounds and potentially operating on wrong filesystem paths.

commit_message:
- Fix Integer Overflows in Size Calculations,
  reported by X41 D-Sec.
cve_desc: Unbound before 1.9.5 allows an integer overflow in a size calculation in dnscrypt/dnscrypt.c. NOTE: The vendor disputes that this is a vulnerability. Although the code may be vulnerable, a running Unbound installation cannot be remotely or locally exploited

commit_message:
patch 8.2.5148: invalid memory access when using expression on command line

Problem:    Invalid memory access when using an expression on the command line.
Solution:   Make sure the position does not go negative.
cve_desc: Buffer Over-read in GitHub repository vim/vim prior to 8.2.

commit_message: check vertexIndex which might be -1 for broken DXF
cve_desc: A code execution vulnerability exists in the DL_Dxf::handleLWPolylineData functionality of Ribbonsoft dxflib 3.17.0. A specially-crafted .dxf file can lead to a heap buffer overflow. An attacker can provide a malicious file to trigger this vulnerability.

vulnerabilities.description: Integer overflow in the expression `4*maxVertices`. If `maxVertices` is a large value parsed from user input (e.g., 0x40000001), `4*maxVertices` overflows to a small value (e.g., 4), causing a small heap allocation. The subsequent initialization loop iterates `maxVertices` times, writing far beyond the allocated buffer.

step_by_step_execution.description: groupCode is 90, so we enter the first branch to allocate vertices.
maxVertices is parsed from user-controlled groupValue via toInt(). Attacker provides a large value like 0x40000001.
maxVertices > 0 is true, so we proceed to allocate. The expression `4*maxVertices` overflows: 4 * 0x40000001 = 0x100000004, which truncates to 4 in a 32-bit int.
new double[4] allocates only 32 bytes on the heap.
The initialization loop runs from i=0 to i<0x40000001, writing to vertices[i*4] through vertices[i*4+3], massively overflowing the 32-byte heap buffer.

vulnerabilities.description: When groupCode==42 and vertexIndex is -1 (its initial value set when groupCode==90 was processed, before any groupCode==10 increments it), the check `vertexIndex<maxVertices` passes but there is no `vertexIndex>=0` check. This results in writing to `vertices[-1]`, an out-of-bounds write before the allocated buffer.

step_by_step_execution.description: A previous call with groupCode==90 allocated the vertices array and set vertexIndex to -1.
This call has groupCode==42, entering the else-if branch for group codes 10/20/30/42.
Since groupCode is not 10, vertexIndex is not incremented and remains -1.
groupCode is not <=30, so we enter the else-if: `groupCode==42 && vertexIndex<maxVertices` => `42==42 && -1<maxVertices` => true.
Write to vertices[4*(-1) + 3] = vertices[-1], which is before the start of the allocated buffer.

commit_message:
Calcualte max nspace declarations correctly for XML writer

(raptor_xml_writer_start_element_common): Calculate max including for
each attribute a potential name and value.

Fixes Issues #0000617 http://bugs.librdf.org/mantis/view.php?id=617
and #0000618 http://bugs.librdf.org/mantis/view.php?id=618
cve_desc: raptor_xml_writer_start_element_common in raptor_xml_writer.c in Raptor RDF Syntax Library 2.0.15 miscalculates the maximum nspace declarations for the XML writer, leading to heap-based buffer overflows (sometimes seen in raptor_qname_format_as_xml).

vulnerabilities.description: Heap buffer overflow in nspace_declarations array. The allocation size accounts for only 1 entry per attribute (attribute_count+1), but the attribute loop can add up to 2 entries per attribute (one for the namespace declaration and one for the attribute+value). If many attributes have unique namespaces not in scope, the total writes to nspace_declarations can exceed the allocated nspace_max_count.

step_by_step_execution.description: nstack is non-NULL, so the allocation block is entered. nspace_max_count is computed as attribute_count + 1 (plus declared_nspaces size and xml_language flag).

state.value: N+1 (e.g., 4, assuming no declared_nspaces and no xml_language)

step_by_step_execution.description: nspace_declarations is allocated with nspace_max_count entries (N+1 slots).

state.value: heap buffer of (N+1) * sizeof(struct nsd) bytes

step_by_step_execution.description: Assume element->name->nspace is NULL or already in scope, so nspace_declarations_count stays 0.

Enter the attribute loop. For each of the N attributes, assume each attribute has a unique nspace not in scope and different from element->name->nspace. For attribute i=0: a namespace declaration entry is added (nspace_declarations_count becomes 1), then an attribute+value entry is added (nspace_declarations_count becomes 2).
For attribute i=1: another namespace declaration entry is added (count=3), then another attribute+value entry (count=4).

For attribute i=2 (N=3): namespace declaration entry is added (count=5). This writes to nspace_declarations[4], which is beyond the allocated N+1=4 slots (indices 0-3). This is an out-of-bounds heap write.

code_snippets:
for(j = 0; j < nspace_declarations_count; j++)
            if(nspace_declarations[j].nspace == element->attributes[j]->nspace) {
vulnerabilities.description: Out-of-bounds read on element->attributes array. The inner loop uses index j to access both nspace_declarations[j] and element->attributes[j], but j iterates up to nspace_declarations_count which can exceed element->attribute_count (since multiple entries can be added per attribute). The intended index for element->attributes was likely i (the outer loop variable).

step_by_step_execution.description: nstack is non-NULL, element has attributes. Enter attribute loop.
For attribute i=0: if its nspace is not in scope, a namespace declaration is added (count=1), then an attribute+value entry is added (count=2).

For attribute i=1: its nspace is not in scope and differs from element->name->nspace. Enter the deduplication loop: for j=0..nspace_declarations_count-1. When j reaches attribute_count (j=2), element->attributes[2] is accessed but only indices 0..1 are valid. This is an out-of-bounds read.

commit_message:
patch 8.2.5148: invalid memory access when using expression on command line

Problem:    Invalid memory access when using an expression on the command line.
Solution:   Make sure the position does not go negative.
cve_desc: Buffer Over-read in GitHub repository vim/vim prior to 8.2.

commit_message: fix #2964 - double-free in bin_qnx.c
cve_desc: Rizin is a UNIX-like reverse engineering framework and command-line toolset. Versions 0.4.0 and prior are vulnerable to a double free in bobj.c:rz_bin_reloc_storage_free() when freeing relocations generated from qnx binary plugin. A user opening a malicious qnx binary could be affected by this vulnerability, allowing an attacker to execute code on the user's machine. Commit number a3d50c1ea185f3f642f2d8180715f82d98840784 contains a patch for this issue.

commit_message: fix #2964 - double-free in bin_qnx.c
cve_desc: Rizin is a UNIX-like reverse engineering framework and command-line toolset. Versions 0.4.0 and prior are vulnerable to a double free in bobj.c:rz_bin_reloc_storage_free() when freeing relocations generated from qnx binary plugin. A user opening a malicious qnx binary could be affected by this vulnerability, allowing an attacker to execute code on the user's machine. Commit number a3d50c1ea185f3f642f2d8180715f82d98840784 contains a patch for this issue.

commit_message:
patch 8.2.4418: crash when using special multi-byte character

Problem:    Crash when using special multi-byte character.
Solution:   Don't use isalpha() for an arbitrary character.
cve_desc: Use of Out-of-range Pointer Offset in GitHub repository vim/vim prior to 8.2.4418.

vulnerabilities.description: Heap buffer overflow when a directory entry name (dp->d_name) exceeds BASENAMELEN bytes. The buffer `buf` is allocated with size STRLEN(path) + BASENAMELEN + 5, but directory entry names from readdir() can exceed BASENAMELEN on certain filesystems. The unchecked STRCPY of dp->d_name into buf at offset s can write past the end of the allocated buffer. Additionally, the subsequent concatenation of "/**" and path_end after the directory name further increases the overflow potential.

step_by_step_execution.description: Buffer `buf` is allocated with size STRLEN(path) + BASENAMELEN + 5.

The loop copies characters from `path` into `buf`, setting `s` to point just past the last '/' before the wildcard component, and `path_end` to point to the remainder of the path after the wildcard component.

state.value: pointer into buf, offset corresponds to directory prefix length
pointer into path after the wildcard segment

step_by_step_execution.description: The directory prefix portion of buf is NUL-terminated (*s = NUL) and opendir is called. readdir() returns a directory entry dp.

state.value: length of directory prefix (up to ~STRLEN(path) bytes)
a filename from the filesystem, potentially longer than BASENAMELEN

step_by_step_execution.description: STRCPY(s, dp->d_name) copies the directory entry name into buf starting at offset s. If strlen(dp->d_name) > BASENAMELEN, this write exceeds the allocated buffer size, since (s - buf) + strlen(dp->d_name) > (s - buf) + BASENAMELEN >= allocated size - 5.

After the overflow from STRCPY(s, dp->d_name), the code computes len = STRLEN(buf) and then does STRCPY(buf + len, "/**") and STRCPY(buf + len + 3, path_end), writing even further past the buffer boundary.

state.value: len + 3 + strlen(path_end), well beyond allocated buffer

commit_message: Fixed bug #72479 - same as #72434
cve_desc: ext/snmp/snmp.c in PHP before 5.5.38, 5.6.x before 5.6.24, and 7.x before 7.0.9 improperly interacts with the unserialize implementation and garbage collection, which allows remote attackers to cause a denial of service (use-after-free and application crash) or possibly have unspecified other impact via crafted serialized data, a related issue to CVE-2016-5773.

commit_message: Fix segfault in older versions of OpenSSL (before 0.9.8i)
cve_desc: The openssl_encrypt function in ext/openssl/openssl.c in PHP 5.3.9 through 5.3.13 does not initialize a certain variable, which allows remote attackers to obtain sensitive information from process memory by providing zero bytes of input data.

vulnerabilities.description: Integer overflow in the computation of `outlen` can lead to a heap buffer overflow. When `raw_input` is true, `data_len` comes directly from user input. If `data_len` is close to INT_MAX, adding `EVP_CIPHER_block_size(cipher_type)` (typically 8 or 16) causes an integer overflow, wrapping `outlen` to a small or negative value. This results in a small heap allocation via `emalloc(outlen + 1)`, but `EVP_DecryptUpdate` processes the full `data_len` bytes, writing far beyond the allocated buffer.

step_by_step_execution.description: User provides arguments with `raw_input = true` and a very large `data` string such that `data_len` is close to INT_MAX (e.g., 0x7FFFFFF0).
Since `raw_input` is true, the base64 decoding branch is skipped, so `data_len` retains its original large value.

The output buffer size is computed: `outlen = data_len + EVP_CIPHER_block_size(cipher_type)`. With block_size of 16, this overflows: 0x7FFFFFF0 + 16 = 0x80000000 which wraps to a negative value for a signed int, or a small value depending on interpretation.

state.value: overflowed value (e.g., negative or small positive)

step_by_step_execution.description: `outbuf = emalloc(outlen + 1)` allocates a very small buffer (or emalloc interprets the size in an unexpected way due to the overflow).
`EVP_DecryptUpdate` is called with the original large `data_len`, writing decrypted output into the undersized `outbuf`, causing a heap buffer overflow.

commit_message: Fix segfault in older versions of OpenSSL (before 0.9.8i)
cve_desc: The openssl_encrypt function in ext/openssl/openssl.c in PHP 5.3.9 through 5.3.13 does not initialize a certain variable, which allows remote attackers to obtain sensitive information from process memory by providing zero bytes of input data.

vulnerabilities.description: Integer overflow in the calculation of the output buffer size. If `data_len` is sufficiently large (close to INT_MAX), the addition `data_len + EVP_CIPHER_block_size(cipher_type)` can wrap around to a small or negative value. This leads to a small heap allocation via `emalloc`, while `EVP_EncryptUpdate` writes up to the original (large) amount of data into the undersized buffer, causing a heap buffer overflow.

step_by_step_execution.description: User-supplied parameters are parsed. `data` and `data_len` come from user input. An attacker provides a very large `data_len` (e.g., close to INT_MAX, such as 0x7FFFFFF0).
A valid cipher is looked up, e.g. AES-128-CBC, which has a block size of 16.

The output buffer length is computed: `outlen = data_len + EVP_CIPHER_block_size(cipher_type)`. With data_len = 0x7FFFFFF0 and block_size = 16, this overflows the signed int, wrapping to a small or negative value (0x7FFFFFF0 + 16 = 0x80000000 which is -2147483648 as signed int).

state.value: negative or small value due to integer overflow (e.g. -2147483648 or wraps to small positive after +1)

step_by_step_execution.description: `emalloc(outlen + 1)` is called. Depending on how the overflowed value is cast to size_t, this may allocate a very small buffer or a very large one. On 32-bit systems, the small wrapped value leads to a small allocation.

state.value: pointer to a small heap buffer

step_by_step_execution.description: `EVP_EncryptUpdate` is called with the original large `data_len`, writing encrypted output into the undersized `outbuf`, causing a heap buffer overflow.

state.value: heap buffer overflow — writing ~INT_MAX bytes into small buffer

commit_message:
input-bmp: Increase header buffer in some cases

Signed-off-by: Peter Lemenkov <lemenkov@gmail.com>
cve_desc: AutoTrace v0.40.0 was discovered to contain a heap overflow via the ReadImage function at input-bmp.c:660.

vulnerabilities.description: Stack buffer overflow when reading BMP Version 4 (biSize=108) or Version 5 (biSize=124) headers. The local buffer is 64 bytes, but ReadOK reads biSize-4 bytes (104 or 120) into it, overflowing the stack buffer.

step_by_step_execution.description: A 64-byte stack buffer is declared.
A crafted BMP file is opened and its magic bytes and initial header are read successfully.
The biSize field is read from the file. A crafted BMP sets this to 108 or 124.
The code enters the branch for biSize == 108 || biSize == 124, and calls ReadOK(fd, buffer, Bitmap_File_Head.biSize - 4).
ReadOK writes 104 or 120 bytes into the 64-byte stack buffer, causing a stack buffer overflow that can overwrite the return address and other stack variables.

vulnerabilities.description: If fopen() fails, fd is NULL, and the code jumps to cleanup which calls fclose(NULL). Passing NULL to fclose is undefined behavior.

step_by_step_execution.description: fopen fails and returns NULL.
The NULL check triggers, and the code executes goto cleanup.
At the cleanup label, fclose(fd) is called with fd=NULL, which is undefined behavior.

commit_message:
PCI: rpadlpar: Fix potential drc_name corruption in store functions

Both add_slot_store() and remove_slot_store() try to fix up the
drc_name copied from the store buffer by placing a NUL terminator at
nbyte + 1 or in place of a '\n' if present. However, the static buffer
that we copy the drc_name data into is not zeroed and can contain
anything past the n-th byte.

This is problematic if a '\n' byte appears in that buffer after nbytes
and the string copied into the store buffer was not NUL terminated to
start with as the strchr() search for a '\n' byte will mark this
incorrectly as the end of the drc_name string resulting in a drc_name
string that contains garbage data after the n-th byte.

Additionally it will cause us to overwrite that '\n' byte on the stack
with NUL, potentially corrupting data on the stack.

The following debugging shows an example of the drmgr utility writing
"PHB 4543" to the add_slot sysfs attribute, but add_slot_store()
logging a corrupted string value.

  drmgr: drmgr: -c phb -a -s PHB 4543 -d 1
  add_slot_store: drc_name = PHB 4543°|<82>!, rc = -19

Fix this by using strscpy() instead of memcpy() to ensure the string
is NUL terminated when copied into the static drc_name buffer.
Further, since the string is now NUL terminated the code only needs to
change '\n' to '\0' when present.

Cc: stable@vger.kernel.org
Signed-off-by: Tyrel Datwyler <tyreld@linux.ibm.com>
[mpe: Reformat change log and add mention of possible stack corruption]
Signed-off-by: Michael Ellerman <mpe@ellerman.id.au>
Link: https://lore.kernel.org/r/20210315214821.452959-1-tyreld@linux.ibm.com
cve_desc: In drivers/pci/hotplug/rpadlpar_sysfs.c in the Linux kernel through 5.11.8, the RPA PCI Hotplug driver has a user-tolerable buffer overflow when writing a new device name to the driver from userspace, allowing userspace to write data to the kernel stack frame directly. This occurs because add_slot_store and remove_slot_store mishandle drc_name '\0' termination, aka CID-cc7a0bb058b8.

vulnerabilities.description: The local buffer `drc_name` is not null-terminated after the `memcpy`. `strchr` scans memory looking for '\n', but since the bytes beyond the copied `nbytes` are uninitialized stack data, `strchr` may read past the `nbytes` boundary and potentially past the end of the `drc_name` array itself if no '\n' or '\0' byte is found in the uninitialized region.

step_by_step_execution.description: Function is called with `buf` containing no newline character and `nbytes` less than MAX_DRC_NAME_LEN (e.g., nbytes = 5).
The size check passes since nbytes < MAX_DRC_NAME_LEN.
memcpy copies 5 bytes from buf into drc_name. Bytes drc_name[5] through drc_name[MAX_DRC_NAME_LEN-1] remain uninitialized stack data.

strchr(drc_name, '\n') is called. Since drc_name is not null-terminated after the copied data, strchr scans beyond the 5 valid bytes into uninitialized stack memory. If no '\n' or '\0' is found within the remaining drc_name buffer, it reads beyond the bounds of the drc_name array.

state.value: pointer to uninitialized memory, or NULL, or a location beyond drc_name

step_by_step_execution.description: If strchr returns a pointer beyond the valid copied region but within the buffer, *end = '\0' writes to an unintended location based on uninitialized data. If strchr reads past the buffer entirely, it constitutes a stack buffer over-read.

commit_message:
Bug 790784 - (CVE-2017-17784) heap overread in gbr parser / load_image.

We were assuming the input name was well formed, hence was
nul-terminated. As any data coming from external input, this has to be
thorougly checked.
Similar to commit 06d24a79af94837d615d0024916bb95a01bf3c59 but adapted
to older gimp-2-8 code.
cve_desc: In GIMP 2.8.22, there is a heap-based buffer over-read in load_image in plug-ins/common/file-gbr.c in the gbr import parser, related to mishandling of UTF-8 data.

vulnerabilities.description: Integer overflow in the allocation `g_malloc(4 * bh.width * bh.height)` when a pattern follows a 1-byte brush. The multiplication `4 * bh.width * bh.height` is performed in 32-bit unsigned arithmetic (since `4` is int and `bh.width`/`bh.height` are guint/uint32), which can wrap around even on 64-bit systems. The initial sanitization check only validates `bh.width * bh.height * bh.bytes` where `bh.bytes=1`, so it permits `bh.width * bh.height` up to G_MAXSIZE. If `bh.width * bh.height > 2^30`, the 32-bit expression `4 * bh.width * bh.height` overflows, causing a small heap allocation. The subsequent loop then writes far beyond the allocated buffer.

step_by_step_execution.description: File is opened and the BrushHeader is read and fields are converted from network byte order.

Sanitization check is evaluated. bh.width, bh.height are within GIMP_MAX_IMAGE_SIZE. bh.bytes==1. G_MAXSIZE / 65536 / 65536 / 1 is checked — on a 64-bit system G_MAXSIZE is 2^64-1, so this passes easily.

The initial brush data buffer is allocated: size = bh.width * bh.height * bh.bytes. Due to 32-bit multiplication of width*height, this overflows to 0, then g_malloc(0) allocates a small buffer. The read of `size` bytes reads 0 bytes and succeeds.
Since bh.bytes==1, the code attempts to read a PatternHeader. A crafted file provides a valid PatternHeader with ph.width==bh.width, ph.height==bh.height, ph.bytes==3, matching all conditions.

A new brush_buf is allocated with g_malloc(4 * bh.width * bh.height). The expression 4 * 65536 * 65536 is computed in 32-bit unsigned arithmetic: 4 * 65536 = 262144 (fits in 32-bit), then 262144 * 65536 = 0x1000000000 which truncates to 0 in 32-bit. g_malloc(0) returns a tiny buffer.

The loop iterates ph.width * ph.height times (also overflows to 0 in 32-bit, but if width/height are chosen such that one overflow occurs and the other doesn't — e.g. width=0x8000, height=0x8000 giving width*height=0x40000000 in 32-bit which doesn't overflow, but 4*width*height does). With width=0x8000, height=0x8000: 4*0x8000*0x8000 = 4*0x40000000 = 0x100000000 → 0 in 32-bit. Loop count = 0x40000000. Each iteration writes 4 bytes at brush_buf + i*4, massively overflowing the heap buffer.

state.value: writes up to ~4GB past the tiny allocation

commit_message:
patch 9.0.0025: accessing beyond allocated memory with the cmdline window

Problem:    Accessing beyond allocated memory when using the cmdline window in
            Ex mode.
Solution:   Use "*" instead of "'<,'>" for Visual mode.
cve_desc: Out-of-bounds Write in GitHub repository vim/vim prior to 9.0.

vulnerabilities.description: When has_visual_range is TRUE, use_plus_cmd is TRUE, and modifiers were parsed (eap->cmd > cmd_start), a 1-byte buffer overflow occurs. The original buffer holds "'<,'>" (5 bytes) + content (len bytes) + NUL (1 byte) = len+6 bytes total. The code writes len bytes of content back to orig_cmd, then appends "'<,'>+" (6 chars + NUL = 7 bytes), for a total of len+7 bytes, overflowing the original buffer by 1 byte.

step_by_step_execution.description: Function entry: eap->cmd points to a string starting with "'<,'>" followed by command modifiers (e.g., "'<,'>silent"). orig_cmd is saved.
The STRNCMP matches "'<,'>", so eap->cmd is advanced by 5 and cmd_start is set. has_visual_range = TRUE.
In the for loop, the "silent" modifier is parsed, advancing eap->cmd past it. eap->cmd now points to the NUL terminator.
The empty command check triggers in ex mode: *eap->cmd == NUL, exmode_active is true, cursor is before last line. use_plus_cmd is set to TRUE and the loop breaks.
Post-loop: has_visual_range is TRUE and eap->cmd > cmd_start, so we enter the use_plus_cmd branch. len = STRLEN(cmd_start) = strlen("silent") = 6.
mch_memmove(orig_cmd, cmd_start, 6) copies "silent" to orig_cmd. Buffer is now "silentsilent\0" (only first 6 bytes changed).

STRCPY(orig_cmd + 6, "'<,'>+") writes 7 bytes (6 chars + NUL) starting at orig_cmd+6. Total bytes written from orig_cmd: 6 + 7 = 13. Original buffer was 12 bytes (5 + 6 + 1). This is a 1-byte out-of-bounds write.

commit_message:
net: xilinx_emaclite: Do not print real IOMEM pointer

Printing kernel pointers is discouraged because they might leak kernel
memory layout.  This fixes smatch warning:

drivers/net/ethernet/xilinx/xilinx_emaclite.c:1191 xemaclite_of_probe() warn:
 argument 4 to %08lX specifier is cast from pointer

Signed-off-by: YueHaibing <yuehaibing@huawei.com>
Signed-off-by: David S. Miller <davem@davemloft.net>
cve_desc: drivers/net/ethernet/xilinx/xilinx_emaclite.c in the Linux kernel before 5.13.3 makes it easier for attackers to defeat an ASLR protection mechanism because it prints a kernel pointer (i.e., the real IOMEM pointer).

code_snippets:
res = platform_get_resource(ofdev, IORESOURCE_MEM, 0);
	lp->base_addr = devm_ioremap_resource(&ofdev->dev, res);
vulnerabilities.description: The return value of the second call to platform_get_resource (for IORESOURCE_MEM) is not checked for NULL before being passed to devm_ioremap_resource. If platform_get_resource returns NULL, a NULL pointer is passed to devm_ioremap_resource, which in certain kernel versions will dereference it, leading to a NULL pointer dereference.

step_by_step_execution.description: The function begins probing and allocates a net_device successfully.
The first platform_get_resource call for IORESOURCE_IRQ succeeds and passes the NULL check.
The second platform_get_resource call for IORESOURCE_MEM returns NULL because the memory resource is not available in the device tree.
res (NULL) is passed directly to devm_ioremap_resource without any NULL check. Inside devm_ioremap_resource (in kernel versions that do not guard against NULL), the NULL pointer is dereferenced.

commit_message:
Fix UAF in aaaa on arm/thumb switching ##crash

* Reported by @peacock-doris via huntr.dev
* Reproducer tests_65185
* This is a logic fix, but not the fully safe as changes in the code
  can result on UAF again, to properly protect r2 from crashing we
  need to break the ABI and add refcounting to RRegItem, which can't
  happen in 5.6.x because of abi-compat rules
cve_desc: Use After Free in op_is_set_bp in GitHub repository radareorg/radare2 prior to 5.6.6.

code_snippets: for (i = from; stack_rev ? i >= to : i < to; stack_rev ? i-- : i++) {
vulnerabilities.description: When `stack_rev` is true, the loop variable `i` is of type `size_t` (unsigned). The loop condition `i >= to` where `to` can be 0 means the condition is always true for an unsigned type, because a `size_t` value is always >= 0. When `i` decrements past 0, it wraps around to SIZE_MAX, and the loop continues with an astronomically large index, leading to out-of-bounds reads via `r_type_func_args_type` and `r_type_func_args_name`.

step_by_step_execution.description: The function is called with `*sign == '+'` and `type == R_ANAL_VAR_KIND_SPV`, entering the `if (*sign == '+')` branch.
`isarg` evaluates to true (e.g., `ptr >= fcn->stack`), so the argument-matching logic is entered.
`place` is set from `r_anal_cc_arg` and `stack_rev` becomes true because `place` equals "stack_rev".

`fname` is successfully resolved via `r_type_func_guess`. `cnt` is set to, e.g., 2 from `r_type_func_args_count`. `from` is set to `cnt - 1 = 1`. `to` is set to `r_anal_cc_max_arg(anal, fcn->cc)` which returns 0.
The loop starts: `for (i = 1; i >= 0; i--)`. Since `i` is `size_t` (unsigned), the condition `i >= 0` is always true.
First iteration with i=1 processes normally. `i--` sets i to 0.
Second iteration with i=0: condition `i >= 0` is true (always true for unsigned). The iteration processes. Then `i--` causes unsigned integer underflow, wrapping i to SIZE_MAX.
Third iteration: condition `SIZE_MAX >= 0` is true. `r_type_func_args_type(anal->sdb_types, fname, SIZE_MAX)` is called with a massively out-of-range index, causing out-of-bounds memory access.

commit_message:
Fix oobread in dyldcache ##crash

* Reported by @hdthky via huntr.dev
* Reproducers: poc1
* BountyID: 8ae2c61a-2220-47a5-bfe8-fe6d41ab1f82
cve_desc: heap-buffer-overflow in GitHub repository radareorg/radare2 prior to 5.6.8. This vulnerability is capable of inducing denial of service.

code_snippets:
for (j = 0; j < hdr->imagesCount; j++) {
			bool printing = !deps[j];
vulnerabilities.description: The `deps` array is allocated using `cache->hdr->imagesCount` (i.e., `cache->hdr[0].imagesCount`), but the loop iterates over `hdr->imagesCount` where `hdr = &cache->hdr[i]` for each cache header `i`. If a subsequent header has a larger `imagesCount` than the first header, accesses to `deps[j]` go out of bounds.

step_by_step_execution.description: The `deps` array is allocated with size `cache->hdr->imagesCount`, which is `cache->hdr[0].imagesCount`.
The outer loop iterates `i` from 0 to `cache->n_hdr - 1`. For `i > 0`, `hdr = &cache->hdr[i]`.
The inner loop iterates `j` from 0 to `hdr->imagesCount - 1`. If `cache->hdr[i].imagesCount > cache->hdr[0].imagesCount`, then `j` exceeds the allocated size of `deps`.

state.value: up to cache->hdr[i].imagesCount - 1, which can exceed cache->hdr[0].imagesCount - 1

step_by_step_execution.description: Access to `deps[j]` is out of bounds, causing a heap buffer overflow (write via `deps[j]++`) or out-of-bounds read (`!deps[j]`).

vulnerabilities.description: The `dep_index` value is derived from attacker-influenced data (`depArray[k] & 0x7fff`) and can be up to 0x7FFF (32767). It is used to index both `deps` (allocated with `cache->hdr[0].imagesCount` entries) and `img` (allocated with `hdr->imagesCount` entries) without any bounds check, leading to out-of-bounds read/write.

step_by_step_execution.description: `depArray` is read from the cache buffer at `cache->accel->depListOffset` with `cache->accel->depListCount` entries.

state.value: attacker-controlled ut16 array from cache file

step_by_step_execution.description: The loop iterates `k` starting from `extras[j].dependentsStartArrayIndex` until `depArray[k] == 0xffff`.
`dep_index = depArray[k] & 0x7fff` is computed. This value can be up to 32767.
`deps[dep_index]++` writes out of bounds if `dep_index >= cache->hdr[0].imagesCount`. Similarly, `img[dep_index]` reads out of bounds if `dep_index >= hdr->imagesCount`.

commit_message:
Fix UAF in aaaa on arm/thumb switching ##crash

* Reported by @peacock-doris via huntr.dev
* Reproducer tests_65185
* This is a logic fix, but not the fully safe as changes in the code
  can result on UAF again, to properly protect r2 from crashing we
  need to break the ABI and add refcounting to RRegItem, which can't
  happen in 5.6.x because of abi-compat rules
cve_desc: Use After Free in op_is_set_bp in GitHub repository radareorg/radare2 prior to 5.6.6.

code_snippets: for (i = from; stack_rev ? i >= to : i < to; stack_rev ? i-- : i++) {
vulnerabilities.description: When `stack_rev` is true, the loop variable `i` is of type `size_t` (unsigned). When `i` reaches 0 and is decremented (`i--`), it wraps around to `SIZE_MAX`. Since the loop condition `i >= to` is always true for an unsigned variable when `to` is 0, this creates an effectively infinite loop that passes a huge out-of-range index to `r_type_func_args_type` and `r_type_func_args_name`, potentially causing out-of-bounds reads.

code_snippets: for (i = from; stack_rev ? i >= to : i < to; stack_rev ? i-- : i++) {

step_by_step_execution.description: The function is called with `sign` being `"+"`, so `*sign == '+'` is true, entering the `+` branch.
`isarg` evaluates to true, so the code enters the argument-handling branch.
`place` is resolved via `r_anal_cc_arg` and equals `"stack_rev"`, setting `stack_rev = true`.
`fname` is successfully resolved (non-NULL). `cnt` is obtained from `r_type_func_args_count`. If `cnt` is 1, `from = 0`. `to` is obtained from `r_anal_cc_max_arg`, which can return 0.
The loop starts with `i = from = 0`. The condition `i >= to` evaluates to `0 >= 0`, which is true. The loop body executes.

If the loop body does not break (e.g., `sum_sz != frame_off` and `tp != NULL`), the loop post-increment expression `i--` is evaluated. Since `i` is `size_t` (unsigned) and its value is 0, this wraps around to SIZE_MAX.
The loop condition `i >= to` is checked again: `SIZE_MAX >= 0` is true for unsigned comparison, so the loop continues with a huge index.
`r_type_func_args_type(anal->sdb_types, fname, SIZE_MAX)` is called with a wildly out-of-range index, potentially leading to out-of-bounds memory access or other undefined behavior.

commit_message:
iwlwifi: pcie: fix rb_allocator workqueue allocation

We don't handle failures in the rb_allocator workqueue allocation
correctly. To fix that, move the code earlier so the cleanup is
easier and we don't have to undo all the interrupt allocations in
this case.

Signed-off-by: Johannes Berg <johannes.berg@intel.com>
Signed-off-by: Luca Coelho <luciano.coelho@intel.com>
cve_desc: drivers/net/wireless/intel/iwlwifi/pcie/trans.c in the Linux kernel 5.2.14 does not check the alloc_workqueue return value, leading to a NULL pointer dereference.

commit_message:
Avoid possible segfault when handling networkstatus vote with bad flavor

Fix for 6530; fix on 0.2.2.6-alpha.
cve_desc: Use-after-free vulnerability in dns.c in Tor before 0.2.2.38 might allow remote attackers to cause a denial of service (daemon crash) via vectors related to failed DNS requests.

vulnerabilities.description: When tok->n_args is exactly 2 (i.e., > 1 but not > 2), the code accesses tok->args[2] which is out of bounds. The error message in the log_warn uses tok->args[2] instead of tok->args[1] to report the unknown flavor.

code_snippets:
log_warn(LD_DIR, "Can't parse document with unknown flavor %s",
               escaped(tok->args[2]));

step_by_step_execution.description: The function receives a network status string to parse. It tokenizes the header.
The token K_NETWORK_STATUS_VERSION is found. The token has exactly 2 arguments (e.g., 'network-status-version 3 badflavor').
The condition tok->n_args > 1 is true (2 > 1), so we enter the block and parse the flavor from tok->args[1].
Since flavor < 0, the error logging branch is taken, which accesses tok->args[2]. With n_args == 2, valid indices are 0 and 1, so args[2] is an out-of-bounds read.

commit_message: Fix oob write for dwarf with abbrev with count 0 (Fix #2083) (#2086)
cve_desc: Rizin is a UNIX-like reverse engineering framework and command-line toolset. In versions up to and including 0.3.1 there is a heap-based out of bounds write in parse_die() when reversing an AMD64 ELF binary with DWARF debug info. When a malicious AMD64 ELF binary is opened by a victim user, Rizin may crash or execute unintended actions. No workaround are known and users are advised to upgrade.

code_snippets:
buf = parse_attr_value(buf, buf_end - buf, &abbrev->defs[i],
				&die->attr_values[i], hdr, debug_str, debug_str_len, big_endian);
vulnerabilities.description: After each call to parse_attr_value, the returned `buf` pointer is not validated before it is used again in the next loop iteration. If parse_attr_value returns NULL (on error) or a pointer past buf_end, the expression `buf_end - buf` on the next iteration will either dereference NULL or produce a negative ptrdiff_t value. When this negative value is implicitly converted to an unsigned size_t parameter, it wraps around to a very large value, effectively disabling any length-based bounds checks inside parse_attr_value, leading to an out-of-bounds read.

code_snippets:
buf = parse_attr_value(buf, buf_end - buf, &abbrev->defs[i],
				&die->attr_values[i], hdr, debug_str, debug_str_len, big_endian);

step_by_step_execution.description: The function is called with a valid buf and buf_end, and abbrev->count >= 2, so the loop will iterate at least once.
First iteration: parse_attr_value is called with a valid (buf_end - buf) length. It parses a malformed/truncated attribute and returns NULL or a pointer past buf_end.
No check is performed on the returned buf value. The loop increments i and proceeds to the next iteration.

On the next iteration, the expression (buf_end - buf) is computed. If buf is NULL, this is undefined behavior / crash. If buf > buf_end, the result is negative, but passed as the size_t length parameter, it wraps to a very large value (e.g., 0xFFFFFFFFFFFFFFxx).

state.value: negative value, wraps to ~SIZE_MAX when cast to size_t

step_by_step_execution.description: parse_attr_value is called with this enormous size, allowing it to read far beyond the actual buffer boundary, resulting in an out-of-bounds read.

int overflow pitfalls.

Suggested-by: Al Viro <viro@zeniv.linux.org.uk>

commit_message:
Fix oobread in dyldcache ##crash

* Reported by @hdthky via huntr.dev
* Reproducers: poc1
* BountyID: 8ae2c61a-2220-47a5-bfe8-fe6d41ab1f82
cve_desc: heap-buffer-overflow in GitHub repository radareorg/radare2 prior to 5.6.8. This vulnerability is capable of inducing denial of service.

code_snippets:
for (j = 0; j < hdr->imagesCount; j++) {
				bool printing = !deps[j];
vulnerabilities.description: The `deps` array is allocated based on `cache->hdr->imagesCount` (i.e., `cache->hdr[0].imagesCount`), but the inner loop iterates up to `hdr->imagesCount` where `hdr = &cache->hdr[i]` for each outer loop iteration. If a subsequent header's `imagesCount` is larger than the first header's `imagesCount`, accessing `deps[j]` goes out of bounds.

step_by_step_execution.description: The `deps` array is allocated with `cache->hdr->imagesCount` elements, which is `cache->hdr[0].imagesCount`.
The outer loop iterates over all headers: `for (i = 0; i < cache->n_hdr; i++)`.
For i > 0, `hdr = &cache->hdr[i]` may have `hdr->imagesCount > cache->hdr[0].imagesCount`.

state.value: potentially larger than deps allocation size

step_by_step_execution.description: The inner loop iterates `j` from 0 to `hdr->imagesCount - 1`, and accesses `deps[j]`, causing an out-of-bounds read/write.

state.value: up to hdr->imagesCount - 1, exceeding allocated size of deps

vulnerabilities.description: The loop over `depArray` starting from `extras[j].dependentsStartArrayIndex` only terminates when `depArray[k] == 0xffff`. There is no check that `k < cache->accel->depListCount`, so a malformed or malicious cache file can cause an out-of-bounds read on `depArray`. Additionally, `dep_index = depArray[k] & 0x7fff` is used to index into `deps` and `img` without any bounds check against `imagesCount`.

step_by_step_execution.description: `depArray` is allocated with `cache->accel->depListCount` elements.
The loop starts at `k = extras[j].dependentsStartArrayIndex` and increments `k` until `depArray[k] == 0xffff`.

state.value: starts at extras[j].dependentsStartArrayIndex, increments without upper bound check

step_by_step_execution.description: If no 0xffff sentinel exists within the array bounds, `k` exceeds `depListCount`, causing out-of-bounds read on `depArray`.
`dep_index = depArray[k] & 0x7fff` can be any value up to 0x7fff. This is used to index `deps[dep_index]` (write) and `img[dep_index]` (read) without checking against `hdr->imagesCount`.

commit_message:
patch 8.2.4418: crash when using special multi-byte character

vulnerabilities.description: Heap buffer overflow: the buffer `buf` is allocated with size `STRLEN(path) + BASENAMELEN + 5`, assuming directory entry names will not exceed BASENAMELEN. However, `dp->d_name` from `readdir()` can contain names longer than BASENAMELEN on certain filesystems. When such a long name is copied into `buf` via `STRCPY(s, dp->d_name)`, and then `path_end` is appended (potentially with the extra "/**" prefix in the starstar case), the write exceeds the allocated buffer, causing a heap buffer overflow.

step_by_step_execution.description: Buffer `buf` is allocated with a size based on the input path length plus BASENAMELEN plus 5.
The path is parsed: `s` points into `buf` at the start of the wildcard component, `e` at the end, and `path_end` points to the remainder of the original path after the wildcard component.

state.value: pointer into buf at start of wildcard component
pointer into buf at end of wildcard component
pointer to remaining path after the wildcard segment

step_by_step_execution.description: A directory is opened and entries are read via readdir(). On certain filesystems (e.g., some network filesystems or FUSE filesystems), `dp->d_name` can be longer than BASENAMELEN (typically 255).

`STRCPY(s, dp->d_name)` copies the directory entry name into `buf` starting at position `s`. Since `dp->d_name` can exceed BASENAMELEN characters, this writes beyond the allocated buffer. The buffer only has room for BASENAMELEN extra bytes beyond the original path content.

state.value: approximately (e - s) + BASENAMELEN + 5, not enough if dp->d_name >> BASENAMELEN

step_by_step_execution.description: Subsequently, `STRCPY(buf + len, path_end)` (or the starstar variant with "/**" prepended) appends the remaining path, further extending the out-of-bounds write into heap memory.

state.value: (s - buf) + strlen(dp->d_name) + strlen(path_end) [+ 3 for starstar], exceeding allocated size

commit_message:
lib/iov_iter: initialize "flags" in new pipe_buffer

The functions copy_page_to_iter_pipe() and push_pipe() can both
allocate a new pipe_buffer, but the "flags" member initializer is
missing.

Fixes: 241699cd72a8 ("new iov_iter flavour: pipe-backed")
To: Alexander Viro <viro@zeniv.linux.org.uk>
To: linux-fsdevel@vger.kernel.org
To: linux-kernel@vger.kernel.org
Cc: stable@vger.kernel.org
Signed-off-by: Max Kellermann <max.kellermann@ionos.com>
Signed-off-by: Al Viro <viro@zeniv.linux.org.uk>
cve_desc: A flaw was found in the way the "flags" member of the new pipe buffer structure was lacking proper initialization in copy_page_to_iter_pipe and push_pipe functions in the Linux kernel and could thus contain stale values. An unprivileged local user could use this flaw to write to pages in the page cache backed by read only files and as such escalate their privileges on the system.

vulnerabilities.description: When initializing a new pipe buffer entry, the code sets ops, page, offset, and len, but never clears or initializes buf->flags. If a previous user of this pipe buffer slot (e.g., from a write via user-space data) set PIPE_BUF_FLAG_CAN_MERGE in buf->flags, this flag persists into the new buffer that now references a page cache page. A subsequent pipe write will see the CAN_MERGE flag and splice/write data directly into the page cache page, allowing modification of file contents that should be read-only. This is the 'Dirty Pipe' vulnerability (CVE-2022-0847).

step_by_step_execution.description: An attacker first fills and drains a pipe so that all pipe buffer slots have PIPE_BUF_FLAG_CAN_MERGE set in their flags from prior anonymous-page writes.
A splice/read from a file triggers copy_page_to_iter_pipe with a page cache page. i->iov_offset (off) is 0, so we skip the merge block.

state.value: a page cache page (e.g., from a read-only file)

step_by_step_execution.description: pipe_full check passes, so we proceed to initialize the buffer at pipe->bufs[i_head & p_mask].

state.value: index of a previously-used slot with stale flags

step_by_step_execution.description: buf->ops, buf->page, buf->offset, buf->len are all set, but buf->flags is NOT cleared. The stale PIPE_BUF_FLAG_CAN_MERGE flag remains.

state.value: page cache page from read-only file

step_by_step_execution.description: A subsequent write() to the pipe sees PIPE_BUF_FLAG_CAN_MERGE on a buffer referencing a page cache page and writes attacker-controlled data directly into the page cache, modifying file contents.

state.value: page cache page now containing attacker-written data

commit_message:
Fixed int overflow in PresentationContext_new

Thanks to hac425 CVE-2020-11038
cve_desc: In FreeRDP less than or equal to 2.0.0, an Integer Overflow to Buffer Overflow exists. When using /video redirection, a manipulated server can instruct the client to allocate a buffer with a smaller size than requested due to an integer overflow in size calculation. With later messages, the server can manipulate the client to write data out of bound to the previously allocated buffer. This has been patched in 2.1.0.

vulnerabilities.description: The expression `width * height * 4` is computed using UINT32 arithmetic, which can overflow and wrap around to a small value. An attacker who controls `width` and `height` (e.g., width=65536, height=65536) can cause the multiplication to overflow, resulting in a much smaller buffer being allocated. Subsequent operations that write decoded video data assuming the full width*height*4 size will write beyond the allocated buffer.

step_by_step_execution.description: Function is called with attacker-influenced width and height values, e.g. width=65536, height=65536.
The expression `width * height * 4` is evaluated as UINT32 arithmetic: 65536 * 65536 * 4 = 2^34, which overflows a 32-bit integer (or size_t on 32-bit) to 0.
BufferPool_Take is called with the overflowed size, allocating a buffer of 0 or very small size.

state.value: pointer to a 0-byte or very small buffer

step_by_step_execution.description: Later code (e.g. YUV/H264 decode, createSurface) writes decoded pixel data assuming a buffer of width*height*4 bytes, causing a heap buffer overflow.

state.value: heap buffer overflow when writing decoded frame data

commit_message: ITS#9454 fix issuerAndThisUpdateCheck
cve_desc: In OpenLDAP through 2.4.57 and 2.5.x through 2.5.1alpha, an assertion failure in slapd can occur in the issuerAndThisUpdateCheck function via a crafted packet, resulting in a denial of service (daemon exit) via a short timestamp. This is related to schema_init.c and checkTime.

vulnerabilities.description: If the issuer value contains no closing double-quote, the scanning loop exits with is->bv_len == x.bv_len. Then `x.bv_len -= is->bv_len + 1` causes an unsigned integer underflow (since ber_len_t is unsigned), wrapping x.bv_len to a very large value. Subsequent reads using x.bv_val and x.bv_len will access memory far beyond the original buffer, leading to out-of-bounds reads.

step_by_step_execution.description: Input is crafted with minimum required length, e.g. `{issuer rdnSequence:"XXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXX,thisUpdate "YYMMDDhhmmssZ"}` where the content within the issuer quotes has no closing double-quote before the end of the inner content tracked by x.bv_len.
Initial validation passes: length check, opening '{' and closing '}' confirmed. x is set to inner content.
Leading spaces consumed (none in this case). 'issuer' keyword matched by strncasecmp, x advanced past 'issuer', space, 'rdnSequence:', and opening '"'.

state.value: points to first char after the opening quote of issuer value
remaining length of content (no closing quote within this span)

step_by_step_execution.description: The scanning loop iterates over all remaining characters in x.bv_len range without finding a closing double-quote. Loop exits because is->bv_len reaches x.bv_len.
x.bv_len -= is->bv_len + 1 is computed. Since is->bv_len == x.bv_len (both N), this becomes N - (N+1) = -1, which wraps to a huge value for unsigned ber_len_t.

state.value: wraps to (ber_len_t)-1, a very large value (e.g., 0xFFFFFFFF or 0xFFFFFFFFFFFFFFFF)

step_by_step_execution.description: Subsequent code uses x.bv_val and the corrupted x.bv_len in space-eating loops, strncasecmp calls, and other operations, reading far beyond the allocated buffer.

state.value: points one byte past the original buffer's inner content

vulnerabilities.description: Same as the issuer case: if the thisUpdate value has no closing double-quote within the remaining x.bv_len, the loop exits with tu->bv_len == x.bv_len, and the subsequent subtraction `x.bv_len -= tu->bv_len + 1` causes an unsigned integer underflow, leading to out-of-bounds reads.

step_by_step_execution.description: Input is crafted so that the thisUpdate field is parsed first (or second) and its value has an opening quote but no closing quote within the remaining buffer tracked by x.bv_len.

state.value: points to content after opening '"' of thisUpdate
remaining length with no '"' character in it

step_by_step_execution.description: The loop scans all remaining characters without finding a closing quote. tu->bv_len reaches x.bv_len.
x.bv_len -= tu->bv_len + 1 computes N - (N+1) = -1, wrapping to a huge unsigned value.
Subsequent operations use the corrupted x.bv_len, causing out-of-bounds memory reads.

state.value: extremely large value enabling OOB reads

vulnerabilities.description: When is->bv_len equals x.bv_len - 1 and a double-quote is found at that position, the code reads is->bv_val[is->bv_len+1] which is at offset x.bv_len from is->bv_val. While the closing '}' of the original input may be in memory at that spot, this is a read one byte beyond the tracked bounds of the current parsing window, constituting an off-by-one out-of-bounds read.

step_by_step_execution.description: During issuer value scanning, is->bv_len has reached x.bv_len - 1, and is->bv_val[is->bv_len] is a double-quote character.
The code checks is->bv_val[is->bv_len+1] without verifying is->bv_len+1 < x.bv_len. This reads at offset x.bv_len from is->bv_val, one byte past the end of the parsing window.

state.value: x.bv_len (one past the valid range for the current parsing context)

commit_message:
patch 9.0.0018: going over the end of the typahead

Problem:    Going over the end of the typahead.
Solution:   Put a NUL after the typeahead.
cve_desc: Integer Overflow or Wraparound in GitHub repository vim/vim prior to 9.0.

vulnerabilities.description: When buf is not NULL, tp points to buf + offset and len = *buflen - offset, so tp[len] writes at buf[*buflen]. If *buflen equals bufsize (the allocated size of buf), this writes a NUL byte one position past the end of the buffer, causing an out-of-bounds write. Similarly when buf is NULL, tp[len] writes past the valid data in typebuf.tb_buf if no extra space is reserved.

step_by_step_execution.description: Function is called with a non-NULL buf of allocated size bufsize, with *buflen == bufsize (the buffer is completely full).

state.value: non-NULL pointer to buffer of size bufsize

step_by_step_execution.description: The outer for loop starts with offset = 0. Since offset (0) < max_offset and offset (0) < *buflen (100), execution proceeds.
tp is set to buf + offset, and len is set to *buflen - offset.
The code executes tp[len] = NUL, which writes to buf[100]. Since buf has allocated size bufsize = 100, valid indices are 0..99. This writes one byte past the end of the buffer.

state.value: buf[100] — one byte past allocated buffer end

commit_message:
patch 8.2.4151: reading beyond the end of a line

Problem:    Reading beyond the end of a line.
Solution:   For block insert only use the offset for correcting the length.
cve_desc: Heap-based Buffer Overflow in vim/vim prior to 8.2.

code_snippets:
if (spaces < 0)  // can happen when the cursor was moved
    spaces = 0;
vulnerabilities.description: When in the append (not b_insert) path with bdp->is_short true and !bdp->is_MAX, if oap->end_vcol < bdp->end_vcol, `spaces` is set to a negative value and `count` is set to the same negative value. `spaces` is later clamped to 0, but `count` is NOT updated and remains negative. The allocation size calculation includes the negative `count`, resulting in a smaller buffer than needed. The subsequent STRMOVE copies data past the end of the allocated buffer, causing a heap buffer overflow.

step_by_step_execution.description: Function enters the append path (b_insert is false) and bdp->is_short is true.
Since bdp->is_short and !bdp->is_MAX, spaces and count are calculated. If oap->end_vcol < bdp->end_vcol, spaces becomes negative, e.g., -1.
The check `if (spaces < 0)` clamps spaces to 0, but count is NOT updated and remains -1.

Allocation is computed: STRLEN(oldp) + 0 + s_len + 0 + (-1) + 1. Due to size_t arithmetic, -1 cast to size_t is 0xFFFFFFFFFFFFFFFF, then +1 wraps to 0. Result: STRLEN(oldp) + s_len, which is 1 byte less than needed for the null terminator.

state.value: STRLEN(oldp) + s_len (missing 1 byte for null terminator)

step_by_step_execution.description: offset bytes of oldp are copied to newp, s_len bytes of s are copied. spaces=0 so no padding and no TAB handling. offset becomes original_offset + s_len.

STRMOVE(newp + offset, oldp) copies the remaining portion of oldp (strlen(remaining_oldp) + 1 bytes including null). Total bytes needed = offset + remaining_oldp_len + 1 = s_len + STRLEN(oldp) + 1, but only s_len + STRLEN(oldp) was allocated. This writes 1 byte past the allocated buffer.

commit_message:
string_number_format: Correctly handles return value of snprintf

Summary: `snprintf` can return a value greater than the number of bytes copied. In case the first byte of the string is not a digit (could be '-'), size of `tmpstr` was being updated without checking `tmplen`. This resulted in either an assertion error or a heap overflow depending on whether the assertion is compiled or not.

Reviewed By: mofarrell, qianxuweiren

Differential Revision: D17327899

fbshipit-source-id: ee53875d21e02608c6d870388eecf1464de24ff1
cve_desc: Insufficient boundary checks when formatting numbers in number_format allows read/write access to out-of-bounds memory, potentially leading to remote code execution. This issue affects HHVM versions prior to 3.30.10, all versions between 4.0.0 and 4.8.5, all versions between 4.9.0 and 4.18.2, and versions 4.19.0, 4.19.1, 4.20.0, 4.20.1, 4.20.2, 4.21.0, 4.22.0, 4.23.0.

vulnerabilities.description: The overflow checks for `integral` and `reslen` are performed in unsigned arithmetic (due to implicit promotion to `size_t` from `thousand_sep.size()` and `dec_point.size()`), but the results are stored in signed `int` variables. On a 64-bit platform, the unsigned sum will almost never wrap around 2^64, so the checks never trigger. However, the subsequent `+=` assignments can overflow the 32-bit signed `int`, resulting in a much smaller value than intended. This causes `reslen` to be too small, leading to an undersized heap buffer allocation, and the subsequent `memcpy` and pointer write operations overflow the buffer.

step_by_step_execution.description: Function is called with d=1e99 (a large double), dec=0, dec_point="", and a thousand_sep string of 21,000,000 characters.
d is positive, so is_negative=0. dec=0 remains. d is rounded. snprintf formats d, producing roughly 100 digits (integral representation of 1e99). tmplen ≈ 100.

state.value: ~100 (no decimal point since dec=0, so integral = tmplen)

step_by_step_execution.description: Since dec=0, dp is set to nullptr. integral = tmplen ≈ 100.

thousand_sep is not empty, so the overflow check is evaluated: `integral + thousand_sep.size() * ((integral-1) / 3) < integral`. On 64-bit, all operands are promoted to size_t (uint64). (99/3)=33 separators. 33 * 21,000,000 = 693,000,000. 100 + 693,000,000 = 693,000,100. This is NOT less than 100 (in 64-bit unsigned), so no error is raised.

integral += 693,000,000. The size_t value 693,000,100 is assigned to int integral. This fits in int32, so integral = 693,000,100. But with a larger thousand_sep (e.g., 70,000,000 chars): 33 * 70,000,000 = 2,310,000,000. integral = 100 + 2,310,000,000 = 2,310,000,100 > INT_MAX. Signed integer overflow occurs, wrapping to ~162,516,804.
reslen = integral = 162,516,804. Since dec=0, no further additions. is_negative=0. String resstr is allocated with only 162,516,804 bytes.

The copy loop iterates over all ~100 digits from tmpbuf, inserting a 70-million-character thousand_sep via memcpy every 3 digits. This writes approximately 2.31 billion bytes into a buffer of only ~162 million bytes, causing a massive heap buffer overflow.

commit_message:
Fix integer overflow in string search causing oobread ##crash

* Reported by @greatergoodest via huntrdev
* BountyID: 8a3dc5cb-08b3-4807-82b2-77f08c137a04
* Reproducer bfileovf
cve_desc: Out-of-bounds Read in GitHub repository radareorg/radare2 prior to 5.7.0.

vulnerabilities.description: When `to` is less than 4 (specifically when the scan range `to - from` is 1, 2, or 3 bytes), the unsigned subtraction `to - 4` wraps around to a very large value (near UINT64_MAX) because `to` is `ut64`. This causes the condition `needle < to - 4` to always be true, leading `r_read_le32` to read 4 bytes from a heap buffer that is smaller than 4 bytes, resulting in a heap buffer over-read.

step_by_step_execution.description: Function is called with from=0, to=3 (a 3-byte scan range), and a valid min value (e.g., min=1).
len is computed as to - from = 3. The check `len < 1 || len > ST32_MAX` passes since len=3 is valid.
buf is allocated via calloc(len, 1), creating a 3-byte heap buffer.
The while loop begins with needle = from = 0. The condition `needle < to (3)` is true.
The optimization check evaluates `needle < to - 4`. Since to=3 and is ut64 (unsigned), `to - 4` wraps around to 0xFFFFFFFFFFFFFFFC. The condition `0 < 0xFFFFFFFFFFFFFFFC` is true.
r_read_le32(buf + (needle - from)) = r_read_le32(buf + 0) reads 4 bytes starting at buf[0], but the buffer is only 3 bytes. This reads 1 byte beyond the allocated heap buffer.

commit_message:
Fix bug 697459 Buffer overflow in fill_threshold_buffer

There was an overflow check for ht_buffer size, but none for the larger
threshold_buffer. Note that this file didn't fail on Windows because the
combination of the ht_buffer and the size of the (miscalculated due to
overflow) threshold_buffer would have exceeded the 2Gb limit.
cve_desc: The fill_threshhold_buffer function in base/gxht_thresh.c in Artifex Software, Inc. Ghostscript 9.20 allows remote attackers to cause a denial of service (heap-based buffer overflow and application crash) or possibly have unspecified other impact via a crafted PostScript document.

commit_message:
i2c-ddc: fix oob read

Suggested-by: Michael Hanselmann <public@hansmi.ch>
Signed-off-by: Gerd Hoffmann <kraxel@redhat.com>
Reviewed-by: Michael Hanselmann <public@hansmi.ch>
Reviewed-by: Philippe Mathieu-Daudé <philmd@redhat.com>
Message-id: 20190108102301.1957-1-kraxel@redhat.com
cve_desc: QEMU, through version 2.10 and through version 3.1.0, is vulnerable to an out-of-bounds read of up to 128 bytes in the hw/i2c/i2c-ddc.c:i2c_ddc() function. A local attacker with permission to execute i2c commands could exploit this to read stack memory of the qemu process on the host.

vulnerabilities.description: If s->reg is not bounded before indexing into s->edid_blob, repeated calls to i2c_ddc_rx will increment s->reg beyond the size of the edid_blob array, leading to an out-of-bounds read.

step_by_step_execution.description: i2c_ddc_rx is called, s->reg holds the current index into edid_blob.

state.value: any value from 0 up to and beyond the size of edid_blob

step_by_step_execution.description: The code reads s->edid_blob[s->reg] without checking if s->reg is within the bounds of the edid_blob array.

state.value: could be >= sizeof(s->edid_blob), causing out-of-bounds read

step_by_step_execution.description: s->reg is incremented unconditionally, so subsequent calls will read further out of bounds.

commit_message:
io_uring: reexpand under-reexpanded iters

[   74.211232] BUG: KASAN: stack-out-of-bounds in iov_iter_revert+0x809/0x900
[   74.212778] Read of size 8 at addr ffff888025dc78b8 by task
syz-executor.0/828
[   74.214756] CPU: 0 PID: 828 Comm: syz-executor.0 Not tainted
5.14.0-rc3-next-20210730 #1
[   74.216525] Hardware name: QEMU Standard PC (i440FX + PIIX, 1996),
BIOS rel-1.14.0-0-g155821a1990b-prebuilt.qemu.org 04/01/2014
[   74.219033] Call Trace:
[   74.219683]  dump_stack_lvl+0x8b/0xb3
[   74.220706]  print_address_description.constprop.0+0x1f/0x140
[   74.224226]  kasan_report.cold+0x7f/0x11b
[   74.226085]  iov_iter_revert+0x809/0x900
[   74.227960]  io_write+0x57d/0xe40
[   74.232647]  io_issue_sqe+0x4da/0x6a80
[   74.242578]  __io_queue_sqe+0x1ac/0xe60
[   74.245358]  io_submit_sqes+0x3f6e/0x76a0
[   74.248207]  __do_sys_io_uring_enter+0x90c/0x1a20
[   74.257167]  do_syscall_64+0x3b/0x90
[   74.257984]  entry_SYSCALL_64_after_hwframe+0x44/0xae

old_size = iov_iter_count();
...
iov_iter_revert(old_size - iov_iter_count());

If iov_iter_revert() is done base on the initial size as above, and the
iter is truncated and not reexpanded in the middle, it miscalculates
borders causing problems. This trace is due to no one reexpanding after
generic_write_checks().

Now iters store how many bytes has been truncated, so reexpand them to
the initial state right before reverting.

Cc: stable@vger.kernel.org
Reported-by: Palash Oswal <oswalpalash@gmail.com>
Reported-by: Sudip Mukherjee <sudipm.mukherjee@gmail.com>
Reported-and-tested-by: syzbot+9671693590ef5aad8953@syzkaller.appspotmail.com
Signed-off-by: Pavel Begunkov <asml.silence@gmail.com>
Signed-off-by: Al Viro <viro@zeniv.linux.org.uk>
cve_desc: An out-of-bounds read flaw was found in the Linux kernel’s io_uring module in the way a user triggers the io_read() function with some special parameters. This flaw allows a local user to read some memory out of bounds.

commit_message:
conf: Fix segfault when parsing mdev types

Commit f1b0890 introduced a potential crash due to incorrect operator
precedence when accessing an element from a pointer to an array.

Backtrace below:

  #0  virNodeDeviceGetMdevTypesCaps (sysfspath=0x7fff801661e0 "/sys/devices/pci0000:00/0000:00:02.0", mdev_types=0x7fff801c9b40, nmdev_types=0x7fff801c9b48) at ../src/conf/node_device_conf.c:2676
  #1  0x00007ffff7caf53d in virNodeDeviceGetPCIDynamicCaps (sysfsPath=0x7fff801661e0 "/sys/devices/pci0000:00/0000:00:02.0", pci_dev=0x7fff801c9ac8) at ../src/conf/node_device_conf.c:2705
  #2  0x00007ffff7cae38f in virNodeDeviceUpdateCaps (def=0x7fff80168a10) at ../src/conf/node_device_conf.c:2342

  #5  0x00007ffff7b69146 in virHashForEach (table=0x7fff9814b700 = {...}, iter=0x7ffff7cb149e <virNodeDeviceObjListExportCallback>, opaque=0x7fffe2ffc6a0) at ../src/util/virhash.c:394

          at ../src/conf/virnodedeviceobj.c:943

  #8  0x00007ffff7e703aa in virConnectListAllNodeDevices (conn=0x7fff98013170, devices=0x7fffe2ffc798, flags=0) at ../src/libvirt-nodedev.c:130

          at src/remote/remote_daemon_dispatch_stubs.h:1591
  #11 0x00007ffff7ce9542 in virNetServerProgramDispatchCall (prog=0x555555690c10, server=0x555555627080, client=0x5555556bf050, msg=0x5555556c0000) at ../src/rpc/virnetserverprogram.c:428
  #12 0x00007ffff7ce90bd in virNetServerProgramDispatch (prog=0x555555690c10, server=0x555555627080, client=0x5555556bf050, msg=0x5555556c0000) at ../src/rpc/virnetserverprogram.c:302
  #13 0x00007ffff7cf042b in virNetServerProcessMsg (srv=0x555555627080, client=0x5555556bf050, prog=0x555555690c10, msg=0x5555556c0000) at ../src/rpc/virnetserver.c:137
  #14 0x00007ffff7cf04eb in virNetServerHandleJob (jobOpaque=0x5555556b66b0, opaque=0x555555627080) at ../src/rpc/virnetserver.c:154

Signed-off-by: Jonathon Jongsma <jjongsma@redhat.com>
Reviewed-by: Ján Tomko <jtomko@redhat.com>
Signed-off-by: Ján Tomko <jtomko@redhat.com>
cve_desc: A flaw was found in libvirt in the virConnectListAllNodeDevices API in versions before 7.0.0. It only affects hosts with a PCI device and driver that supports mediated devices (e.g., GRID driver). This flaw could be used by an unprivileged client with a read-only connection to crash the libvirt daemon by executing the 'nodedev-list' virsh command. The highest threat from this vulnerability is to system availability.

vulnerabilities.description: Operator precedence bug causes out-of-bounds memory access. The expression `*mdev_types[i]` is parsed as `*(mdev_types[i])` (i.e., `*(*(mdev_types + i))`) due to `[]` having higher precedence than unary `*`. The intended expression is `(*mdev_types)[i]`. When `i > 0`, `mdev_types[i]` reads memory at offsets beyond the single `mdev_types` pointer parameter on the stack, leading to out-of-bounds read and subsequent dereference of an arbitrary pointer value.

step_by_step_execution.description: Function is called with `mdev_types` pointing to a valid `virMediatedDeviceTypePtr *` variable, and `*nmdev_types` is >= 2 (indicating a refresh with previously stored types).

state.value: pointer to a single virMediatedDeviceTypePtr* variable (e.g., on the caller's stack)

step_by_step_execution.description: The for-loop begins with i = 0. `*mdev_types[0]` is equivalent to `*(mdev_types[0])` which is `*(*mdev_types)`, i.e., the first element of the array. This works correctly for i=0.

Loop increments i to 1. Now `mdev_types[1]` reads memory at `(char*)mdev_types + sizeof(virMediatedDeviceTypePtr*)`, which is past the single pointer parameter, reading out-of-bounds stack/memory content.

state.value: out-of-bounds read — arbitrary memory content beyond the mdev_types pointer

step_by_step_execution.description: The result of the out-of-bounds read is dereferenced (`*mdev_types[1]`), and the resulting arbitrary pointer is passed to `virMediatedDeviceTypeFree`, which will attempt to operate on invalid memory.

state.value: dereference of arbitrary pointer — undefined behavior / potential crash or exploitation

Solution:   Avoid going over the NUL at the end of the line.

commit_message:
input-bmp: Increase header buffer in some cases

Signed-off-by: Peter Lemenkov <lemenkov@gmail.com>
cve_desc: AutoTrace v0.40.0 was discovered to contain a heap overflow via the ReadImage function at input-bmp.c:660.

vulnerabilities.description: When fopen() fails, fd is NULL. The code jumps to the cleanup label which calls fclose(fd) with a NULL pointer, resulting in undefined behavior (likely a crash).

step_by_step_execution.description: fopen is called and returns NULL because the file cannot be opened.
The code enters the if(!fd) block, logs an error, and calls at_exception_fatal.
The code executes goto cleanup, jumping to the cleanup label.
fclose(fd) is called with fd == NULL, which is undefined behavior per the C standard.

vulnerabilities.description: Bitmap_Head.biWidth and biHeight are 32-bit integers that can be large values (e.g., 65537). They are cast to unsigned short (16-bit), truncating them. ReadImage allocates and fills a buffer based on the full 32-bit dimensions, but at_bitmap_init records the truncated 16-bit dimensions. This mismatch means the bitmap metadata describes a much smaller image than the actual buffer, which can lead to out-of-bounds reads or writes when the image is later processed based on the truncated dimensions.

step_by_step_execution.description: A valid BMP file is opened with biWidth = 65537 and biHeight = 1. This passes all sanity checks since 65537 is a valid positive value well within range.
ReadImage is called with the full 32-bit width (65537), allocating a buffer sized for 65537 pixels wide.
at_bitmap_init is called with (unsigned short)65537 = 1, so the bitmap is recorded as 1 pixel wide while containing data for 65537 pixels.

vulnerabilities.description: For BMP Version 4 (biSize=108) or Version 5 (biSize=124), the code reads biSize-4 bytes into buffer[128]. While 108-4=104 and 124-4=120 both fit, the biSize value is read from the file and could be any value that satisfies the condition (biSize==108 || biSize==124). However, a more subtle issue exists: for the branch `Bitmap_File_Head.biSize >= 56 && Bitmap_File_Head.biSize <= 64`, the read is `biSize - 4` which is at most 60 bytes, fitting in buffer. But for biSize==108 or 124, accesses to buffer offsets up to 0x30 (48) are safe within the 104/120 bytes read. No overflow here on closer inspection. Disregarding this.

step_by_step_execution.description: This was a false alarm upon closer analysis - buffer[128] is large enough for all valid biSize values in the conditional branches. Removing this finding.
