commit_message:
Unsquashfs: fix write outside destination directory exploit

An issue on Github (https://github.com/plougher/squashfs-tools/issues/72)
shows how some specially crafted Squashfs filesystems containing
invalid file names (with '/' and ..) can cause Unsquashfs to write
files outside of the destination directory.

This commit fixes this exploit by checking all names for
validity.

In doing so I have also added checks for '.' and for names that
are shorter than they should be (names in the file system should
not have '\0' terminators).

Signed-off-by: Phillip Lougher <phillip@squashfs.org.uk>
cve_desc: squashfs_opendir in unsquash-1.c in Squashfs-Tools 4.5 stores the filename in the directory entry; this is then used by unsquashfs to create the new file during the unsquash. The filename is not validated for traversal outside of the destination directory, and thus allows writing to locations outside of the destination.

vulnerabilities.description: Out-of-bounds read from directory_table. The outer while loop checks `bytes < size`, but inside the inner `while(dir_count--)` loop, `bytes` is incremented multiple times (by sizeof(*dire) and by dire->size + 1) without re-checking against `size` or the actual length of `directory_table`. With a crafted/corrupted filesystem where `dir_count` (up to SQUASHFS_DIR_COUNT) causes reads beyond the valid data, multiple memcpy calls will read past the end of the directory_table buffer.

step_by_step_execution.description: The function reads an inode and computes the starting byte offset and total size for directory iteration.
The outer while loop begins with `bytes < size` being true. A directory header is read and dir_count is set from the header's count field (+1), up to SQUASHFS_DIR_COUNT.

The inner while(dir_count--) loop iterates. On each iteration, it reads a dir entry from directory_table + bytes via memcpy without checking if bytes + sizeof(*dire) exceeds size or the directory_table allocation size.

state.value: incremented by sizeof(*dire) each iteration, may now exceed size

step_by_step_execution.description: Within the same iteration, another memcpy reads dire->size + 1 bytes from directory_table + bytes for the filename. bytes is further incremented. After several iterations of the inner loop, bytes can be well past the valid region of directory_table.

state.value: further incremented by dire->size + 1, potentially far beyond size and directory_table bounds

step_by_step_execution.description: The memcpy operations read from memory beyond the allocated directory_table buffer, causing an out-of-bounds read. A malicious filesystem image can craft dir_count and dire->size values to control how far out of bounds the read goes.

state.value: exceeds directory_table allocation, out-of-bounds access occurs

cve_desc: The ReadTGAImage function in coders\tga.c in ImageMagick 7.0.5-6 has a memory leak vulnerability that can cause memory exhaustion via invalid colors data in the header of a TGA or VST file.

vulnerabilities.description: When a TGA file has colormap_type != 0 with both colormap_index and colormap_length set to 0, image->colors becomes 0. AcquireImageColormap is called with 0 colors, resulting in an empty or minimal colormap allocation. During pixel decoding, ConstrainColormapIndex with image->colors == 0 will produce an index (typically 0) that is used to access a zero-size colormap, causing a heap buffer over-read.

step_by_step_execution.description: A crafted TGA file is opened with image_type = TGAColormap (colormapped) and colormap_type = 1, colormap_index = 0, colormap_length = 0, bits_per_pixel = 8.
Header validation passes because image_type is TGAColormap and colormap_type is not 0.

Since image_type is TGAColormap, image->storage_class is set to PseudoClass. Because colormap_type != 0, image->colors is set to colormap_index + colormap_length = 0 + 0 = 0. No colormap is allocated in this block.
In the colormap reading block (tga_info.colormap_type != 0), the check image->colors < tga_info.colormap_index evaluates to 0 < 0, which is false. image->colors remains 0.
AcquireImageColormap(image, 0, exception) is called with 0 colors. This may allocate a zero-size or minimal buffer for image->colormap.
Both colormap initialization loops are skipped since colormap_index is 0 and image->colors is 0.

During pixel decoding, a byte is read as index. Since colormap_type != 0, the code accesses image->colormap[ConstrainColormapIndex(image, index, ...)]. With image->colors == 0, ConstrainColormapIndex returns 0, and colormap[0] is accessed on a zero-size allocation, resulting in a heap buffer over-read.

commit_message:
Unsquashfs: fix write outside destination directory exploit

An issue on Github (https://github.com/plougher/squashfs-tools/issues/72)
shows how some specially crafted Squashfs filesystems containing
invalid file names (with '/' and ..) can cause Unsquashfs to write
files outside of the destination directory.

This commit fixes this exploit by checking all names for
validity.

In doing so I have also added checks for '.' and for names that
are shorter than they should be (names in the file system should
not have '\0' terminators).

Signed-off-by: Phillip Lougher <phillip@squashfs.org.uk>
cve_desc: squashfs_opendir in unsquash-1.c in Squashfs-Tools 4.5 stores the filename in the directory entry; this is then used by unsquashfs to create the new file during the unsquash. The filename is not validated for traversal outside of the destination directory, and thus allows writing to locations outside of the destination.

vulnerabilities.description: The variable 'bytes' is used as an offset into 'directory_table' without any bounds checking against the actual allocated size of 'directory_table'. The 'size' variable, which controls the while loop termination, is derived from attacker-controlled inode data ((*i)->data). A crafted filesystem image can set (*i)->data to a large value, causing 'bytes' to index far beyond the allocated 'directory_table' buffer, leading to out-of-bounds reads via the memcpy calls.

step_by_step_execution.description: The inode is read from a crafted squashfs image. (*i)->data is set to a very large value by the attacker, and (*i)->start and (*i)->offset are set to valid values.

state.value: valid value pointing into directory table

step_by_step_execution.description: The directory table start and bytes offset are computed. lookup_entry succeeds and returns a valid byte position.
'size' is computed from attacker-controlled (*i)->data, making the while loop bound extremely large, far exceeding the actual directory_table allocation.

The while loop condition 'bytes < size' is true. The code performs memcpy(&dirh, directory_table + bytes, sizeof(dirh)), reading beyond the bounds of directory_table after a few iterations exhaust the valid data.
Subsequent memcpy calls for directory entries and filenames also read out of bounds from directory_table, potentially reading sensitive memory or causing a crash.

state.value: continues to increment, reading further out of bounds

commit_message: [TALOS-CAN-0063] avoid buffer overrun in ntpq
cve_desc: ntpq in NTP 4.2.x before 4.2.8p4, and 4.3.x before 4.3.77 allows remote attackers to cause a denial of service (crash) via crafted mode 6 response packets.

commit_message: https://github.com/ImageMagick/ImageMagick/issues/1540
cve_desc: In ImageMagick 7.0.8-40 Q16, there is a heap-based buffer over-read in the function WritePNMImage of coders/pnm.c, which allows an attacker to cause a denial of service or possibly information disclosure via a crafted image file. This is related to SetGrayscaleImage in MagickCore/quantize.c.

vulnerabilities.description: The colormap_index buffer is allocated with MagickMax(MaxColormapSize+1, MaxMap) elements but only MaxColormapSize elements are initialized via memset to -1. ScaleQuantumToMap can return values up to MaxMap. If MaxMap >= MaxColormapSize, accessing colormap_index[intensity] for intensity == MaxColormapSize reads uninitialized memory. This uninitialized value is then used to decide whether to add a colormap entry and is later used as a pixel index, potentially leading to out-of-bounds reads or writes.

step_by_step_execution.description: Image is not PseudoClass, so colormap_index is allocated with MagickMax(MaxColormapSize+1, MaxMap) elements.
memset initializes only MaxColormapSize elements of colormap_index to -1, leaving element(s) at index MaxColormapSize (and beyond, up to MaxMap-1) uninitialized.
In the pixel loop, intensity = ScaleQuantumToMap(GetPixelRed(image,q)) computes the pixel intensity which can range from 0 to MaxMap.
If intensity >= MaxColormapSize (e.g., intensity == MaxColormapSize when MaxMap >= MaxColormapSize), colormap_index[intensity] reads uninitialized memory.

The uninitialized value is compared against 0. If it happens to not be negative, the colormap entry for this intensity is never created, and the uninitialized value is used as the pixel index via SetPixelIndex.

vulnerabilities.description: When the image is PseudoClass, colormap_index is allocated but never fully initialized (no memset). Only entries at indices corresponding to colormap alpha values (0..colors-1) are written during the dedup phase. In the second pixel loop, ScaleQuantumToMap(GetPixelIndex(image,q)) can yield any value from 0 to MaxMap. If a pixel's index (after ScaleQuantumToMap) maps to a position not written during dedup, uninitialized memory is read and used as a colormap index, potentially causing out-of-bounds access.

step_by_step_execution.description: Image is PseudoClass, so colormap_index is allocated with MagickMax(image->colors+1, MaxMap) elements. No memset is performed on this buffer.

The code skips the non-PseudoClass block. The sorting and dedup phase sets image->colormap[i].alpha = i for i in 0..colors-1, then sorts, then writes colormap_index[(ssize_t) image->colormap[i].alpha] = j for each i.
In the second pixel loop, GetPixelIndex returns the original pixel index. For a malformed PseudoClass image, this could be any Quantum value. ScaleQuantumToMap maps it to 0..MaxMap.

If the mapped index >= image->colors, colormap_index at that offset is uninitialized. The uninitialized value is cast to Quantum and used as the new pixel index via SetPixelIndex, potentially causing further out-of-bounds colormap access.

commit_message:
'mytx' in bug report #1723194 (http://curl.haxx.se/bug/view.cgi?id=1723194)
pointed out that the warnf() function in the curl tool didn't properly deal
with the cases when excessively long words were used in the string to chop
up.
cve_desc: Curl versions 7.14.1 through 7.61.1 are vulnerable to a heap-based buffer over-read in the tool_msgs.c:voutf() function that may result in information exposure and denial of service.

vulnerabilities.description: When the formatted text contains a segment longer than WARN_TEXTWIDTH with no space characters, 'cut' decrements to 0. If ptr[0] is also not a space, the code executes 'len -= cut' which is 'len -= 0', so 'len' never decreases. Meanwhile 'ptr += cut+1' advances ptr by 1 each iteration. This causes an infinite-like loop where 'ptr' advances far beyond the 'print_buffer' stack array, leading to out-of-bounds reads (via fwrite and ISSPACE checks on ptr[cut]).

step_by_step_execution.description: vsnprintf formats the message into the 256-byte stack buffer 'print_buffer' and returns the length 'len'.

state.value: A string with no space characters, e.g. 200 chars of 'A'

step_by_step_execution.description: The while(len > 0) loop begins. len(200) > 0, so we enter. len(200) > WARN_TEXTWIDTH, so we enter the word-wrap branch.
The inner while loop decrements 'cut' looking for a space. Since there are no spaces, cut decrements all the way to 0 and the loop exits because 'cut' is 0 (falsy).
fwrite(ptr, 0+1, 1, ...) writes 1 byte. Then ptr += 0+1 advances ptr by 1. But len -= 0 leaves len unchanged at 200.

The outer while loop continues since len(200) > 0. The condition len > WARN_TEXTWIDTH is still true. cut is set to WARN_TEXTWIDTH-1 again. The inner loop again finds no space and cut becomes 0. len remains 200, ptr advances by 1 again.

This pattern repeats. After ~256 iterations, ptr has advanced past the end of the 256-byte stack buffer 'print_buffer'. Subsequent fwrite calls and ISSPACE(ptr[cut]) checks read memory beyond the stack buffer.

commit_message:
Unsquashfs: fix write outside destination directory exploit

An issue on Github (https://github.com/plougher/squashfs-tools/issues/72)
shows how some specially crafted Squashfs filesystems containing
invalid file names (with '/' and ..) can cause Unsquashfs to write
files outside of the destination directory.

This commit fixes this exploit by checking all names for
validity.

In doing so I have also added checks for '.' and for names that
are shorter than they should be (names in the file system should
not have '\0' terminators).

Signed-off-by: Phillip Lougher <phillip@squashfs.org.uk>
cve_desc: squashfs_opendir in unsquash-1.c in Squashfs-Tools 4.5 stores the filename in the directory entry; this is then used by unsquashfs to create the new file during the unsquash. The filename is not validated for traversal outside of the destination directory, and thus allows writing to locations outside of the destination.

vulnerabilities.description: The loop bound `size` is derived from inode data (`(*i)->data`) which comes from the filesystem image and can be attacker-controlled. There is no validation that `bytes` stays within the bounds of the `directory_table` buffer. A crafted filesystem with a large `data` field in the inode can cause the while loop to read far past the allocated `directory_table` buffer via SQUASHFS_SWAP_DIR_HEADER, SQUASHFS_SWAP_DIR_ENTRY, and memcpy operations, resulting in out-of-bounds heap reads.

step_by_step_execution.description: read_inode is called with attacker-controlled block_start and offset from a crafted squashfs image. The returned inode has a very large `data` field.
bytes is computed from lookup_entry result plus inode offset. size is computed as (*i)->data + bytes - 3, resulting in a very large value.

The while loop condition `bytes < size` is true since size is very large. SQUASHFS_SWAP_DIR_HEADER reads from directory_table + bytes. As the loop iterates, bytes keeps incrementing and eventually exceeds the actual allocated size of directory_table.
Subsequent reads via SQUASHFS_SWAP_DIR_ENTRY and memcpy also access directory_table + bytes out of bounds, reading arbitrary heap memory.

commit_message:
aaef on arm/thumb switches causes uaf ##crash

* Reported by peacock-doris via huntr.dev
* Reproducer: poc_uaf_r_reg_get
cve_desc: Use After Free in r_reg_get_name_idx in GitHub repository radareorg/radare2 prior to 5.6.6.

vulnerabilities.description: After `buf` is allocated via `malloc`, several early return paths fail to `free(buf)`, causing a memory leak. Specifically, if ESIL initialization fails, if `spname` is NULL, or if `pcname` is NULL/empty, the function returns without freeing the allocated buffer. Repeated triggering of these paths can lead to resource exhaustion.

step_by_step_execution.description: Function is called with valid `str` and addresses such that `iend` is a valid positive value.
`buf` is allocated with `malloc((size_t)iend + 2)`. Allocation succeeds.

state.value: valid heap pointer to iend+2 bytes

step_by_step_execution.description: The code checks if `ESIL` is NULL. If it is, it tries to initialize ESIL with `r_core_cmd0(core, "aei")`. If initialization fails and `core->anal->esil` is still NULL, the function returns immediately.
The function returns without calling `free(buf)`, leaking the heap allocation.

state.value: leaked heap memory of size iend+2

commit_message:
bgpd: Make sure hdr length is at a minimum of what is expected

Ensure that if the capability length specified is enough data.

Signed-off-by: Donald Sharp <sharpd@nvidia.com>
cve_desc: An out-of-bounds read in the BGP daemon of FRRouting FRR before 8.4 may lead to a segmentation fault and denial of service. This occurs in bgp_capability_msg_parse in bgpd/bgp_packet.c.

commit_message: Fix wrong boundary checks in inflections parser resulting in stack buffer over-read with corrupt input
cve_desc: Buffer Over-read in GitHub repository bfabiszewski/libmobi prior to 0.11. This vulnerability is capable of arbitrary code execution.

commit_message:
netfilter: nf_tables_offload: incorrect flow offload action array size

immediate verdict expression needs to allocate one slot in the flow offload
action array, however, immediate data expression does not need to do so.

fwd and dup expression need to allocate one slot, this is missing.

Add a new offload_action interface to report if this expression needs to
allocate one slot in the flow offload action array.

Fixes: be2861dc36d7 ("netfilter: nft_{fwd,dup}_netdev: add offload support")
Reported-and-tested-by: Nick Gregory <Nick.Gregory@Sophos.com>
Signed-off-by: Pablo Neira Ayuso <pablo@netfilter.org>
cve_desc: net/netfilter/nf_dup_netdev.c in the Linux kernel 5.4 through 5.6.10 allows local users to gain privileges because of a heap out-of-bounds write. This is related to nf_tables_offload.

commit_message: Fixed bug #68665 (Invalid free)
cve_desc: The apprentice_load function in libmagic/apprentice.c in the Fileinfo component in PHP through 5.6.4 attempts to perform a free operation on a stack-based character array, which allows remote attackers to cause a denial of service (memory corruption or application crash) or possibly have unspecified other impact via unknown vectors.  NOTE: this is disputed by the vendor because the standard erealloc behavior makes the free operation unreachable

commit_message:
Unsquashfs: fix write outside destination directory exploit

An issue on Github (https://github.com/plougher/squashfs-tools/issues/72)
shows how some specially crafted Squashfs filesystems containing
invalid file names (with '/' and ..) can cause Unsquashfs to write
files outside of the destination directory.

This commit fixes this exploit by checking all names for
validity.

In doing so I have also added checks for '.' and for names that
are shorter than they should be (names in the file system should
not have '\0' terminators).

Signed-off-by: Phillip Lougher <phillip@squashfs.org.uk>
cve_desc: squashfs_opendir in unsquash-1.c in Squashfs-Tools 4.5 stores the filename in the directory entry; this is then used by unsquashfs to create the new file during the unsquash. The filename is not validated for traversal outside of the destination directory, and thus allows writing to locations outside of the destination.

vulnerabilities.description: The loop bound `size` is computed from filesystem inode data (`(*i)->data`) which can be attacker-controlled in a malicious squashfs image. There is no validation that `size` (and consequently `bytes`) stays within the bounds of the `directory_table` buffer. An attacker can craft a squashfs image with a large `data` field in a directory inode, causing `size` to be very large, and the loop will read out-of-bounds from `directory_table` via SQUASHFS_SWAP_DIR_HEADER, SQUASHFS_SWAP_DIR_ENTRY, and memcpy.

step_by_step_execution.description: The inode is read from the filesystem image. The attacker has crafted the inode so that (*i)->data is a very large value (e.g., 0x7FFFFFFF).
lookup_entry returns a valid offset into directory_table. bytes is set to that offset plus (*i)->offset.
size is computed as (*i)->data + bytes - 3. With a large (*i)->data, size becomes very large, far exceeding the actual allocated size of directory_table.

state.value: 0x7FFFFFFF + 100 - 3 = ~2147483744 (could overflow int, but even a moderately large data value produces an out-of-bounds size)

step_by_step_execution.description: The while loop condition `bytes < size` is true since bytes (100) is much less than size. The code reads from `directory_table + bytes` via SQUASHFS_SWAP_DIR_HEADER without checking whether bytes is within the allocated bounds of directory_table.

state.value: 100, then incremented each iteration, eventually exceeding directory_table allocation

step_by_step_execution.description: As the loop continues iterating, bytes grows past the actual allocation of directory_table. Each call to SQUASHFS_SWAP_DIR_HEADER, SQUASHFS_SWAP_DIR_ENTRY, and memcpy reads out-of-bounds from the heap.

commit_message:
netfilter: nf_tables_offload: incorrect flow offload action array size

immediate verdict expression needs to allocate one slot in the flow offload
action array, however, immediate data expression does not need to do so.

fwd and dup expression need to allocate one slot, this is missing.

Add a new offload_action interface to report if this expression needs to
allocate one slot in the flow offload action array.

Fixes: be2861dc36d7 ("netfilter: nft_{fwd,dup}_netdev: add offload support")
Reported-and-tested-by: Nick Gregory <Nick.Gregory@Sophos.com>
Signed-off-by: Pablo Neira Ayuso <pablo@netfilter.org>
cve_desc: net/netfilter/nf_dup_netdev.c in the Linux kernel 5.4 through 5.6.10 allows local users to gain privileges because of a heap out-of-bounds write. This is related to nf_tables_offload.

commit_message: Fixed bug #68665 (Invalid free)
cve_desc: The apprentice_load function in libmagic/apprentice.c in the Fileinfo component in PHP through 5.6.4 attempts to perform a free operation on a stack-based character array, which allows remote attackers to cause a denial of service (memory corruption or application crash) or possibly have unspecified other impact via unknown vectors.  NOTE: this is disputed by the vendor because the standard erealloc behavior makes the free operation unreachable

vulnerabilities.description: When erealloc fails (returns NULL), the code calls efree(mfn) where mfn is a stack-allocated array (char mfn[MAXPATHLEN]). Calling efree (heap free) on a stack address leads to heap corruption or a crash.

step_by_step_execution.description: The function enters the directory-reading branch because fn points to a directory.
mfn is declared as a stack-allocated buffer of size MAXPATHLEN.

state.value: stack-allocated char array of size MAXPATHLEN

step_by_step_execution.description: The while loop reads directory entries. snprintf writes into mfn successfully (returns >= 0), and stat succeeds with S_ISREG true.

state.value: contains a valid file path on the stack

step_by_step_execution.description: The code checks if files >= maxfiles and attempts to grow filearr via erealloc. If erealloc returns NULL (allocation failure), the error handling path is entered.
The error path calls efree(mfn), attempting to free a stack-allocated buffer through the heap allocator, causing heap corruption.

state.value: stack address passed to efree (heap free)

commit_message:
kdc: validate sname in TGS-REQ

In tgs_build_reply(), validate the server name in the TGS-REQ is present before
dereferencing.
cve_desc: A null pointer de-reference was found in the way samba kerberos server handled missing sname in TGS-REQ (Ticket Granting Server - Request). An authenticated user could use this flaw to crash the samba server.

vulnerabilities.description: When both S4U2Self and constrained delegation (S4U2Proxy) code paths execute in the same request, the `tp` principal and `tpn` string allocated in the S4U2Self block are overwritten in the constrained delegation block without being freed first. Since S4U2Self followed by S4U2Proxy is the standard constrained delegation workflow, this memory leak can be triggered repeatedly by an authenticated client, potentially leading to denial of service against the KDC.

step_by_step_execution.description: tp and tpn are initialized to point to cp and cpn respectively (no separate allocation).
The S4U2Self PA-DATA is found and processed. _krb5_principalname2krb5_principal allocates a new principal into tp, and krb5_unparse_name allocates a new string into tpn.

The constrained delegation block is entered (client != NULL, additional_tickets present, cname_in_addl_tkt set, enc_tkt_in_skey == 0). _krb5_principalname2krb5_principal overwrites tp with a new principal without freeing new_principal_1.
krb5_unparse_name overwrites tpn with a new string without freeing new_string_1.
In the cleanup (out label), only new_principal_2 and new_string_2 are freed (tp != cp and tpn != cpn checks pass). new_principal_1 and new_string_1 are permanently leaked.

commit_message: lib: Protect against malicious namespace declarations (CVE-2022-25236)
cve_desc: xmlparse.c in Expat (aka libexpat) before 2.4.5 allows attackers to insert namespace-separator characters into namespace URIs.

commit_message:
patch 9.0.0104: going beyond allocated memory when evaluating string constant

Problem:    Going beyond allocated memory when evaluating string constant.
Solution:   Properly skip over <Key> form.
cve_desc: Heap-based Buffer Overflow in GitHub repository vim/vim prior to 9.0.0102.

An issue on Github (https://github.com/plougher/squashfs-tools/issues/72)

invalid file names (with '/' and ..) can cause Unsquashfs to write

The `dir_count` is capped at `SQUASHFS_DIR_COUNT`, but no check ensures that each individual entry read within that inner loop doesn't exceed the remaining valid bytes.

step_by_step_execution.description: The function reads an inode and computes the starting byte offset into the decompressed directory table.

The outer while loop checks `bytes < size` and enters. A directory header is read via memcpy from `directory_table + bytes`. `dir_count` is set to `dirh.count + 1`. If the filesystem is corrupted, `dirh.count` can be up to SQUASHFS_DIR_COUNT - 1 (254), claiming many entries.

The inner `while(dir_count--)` loop begins iterating. Each iteration reads a dir entry struct and then the name from `directory_table + bytes`, incrementing `bytes` each time. There is NO check that `bytes < size` or that `bytes` is within the allocated bounds of `directory_table` before each memcpy.

state.value: continuously growing, potentially beyond size and beyond directory_table allocation

step_by_step_execution.description: After a few iterations, `bytes` exceeds the valid data region of `directory_table`. Subsequent `memcpy(dire, directory_table + bytes, sizeof(*dire))` reads memory out of bounds of the heap-allocated `directory_table` buffer.

state.value: past end of valid directory_table data

commit_message:
bgpd: Make sure hdr length is at a minimum of what is expected

Ensure that if the capability length specified is enough data.

Signed-off-by: Donald Sharp <sharpd@nvidia.com>
cve_desc: An out-of-bounds read in the BGP daemon of FRRouting FRR before 8.4 may lead to a segmentation fault and denial of service. This occurs in bgp_capability_msg_parse in bgpd/bgp_packet.c.

vulnerabilities.description: The memcpy copies a fixed `sizeof(struct capability_mp_data)` bytes from the packet buffer regardless of the actual `hdr->length` value. The bounds check only validates that `hdr->length` bytes are available, but if `hdr->length` is smaller than `sizeof(struct capability_mp_data)`, the memcpy reads past the validated boundary (and potentially past the `end` of the buffer), causing an out-of-bounds read.

step_by_step_execution.description: The function receives a packet buffer `pnt` of `length` bytes and computes `end = pnt + length`.
The loop begins. The check `pnt + 3 > end` passes since 3 < 4, so we read `action` and `hdr` from the buffer.

An attacker crafts the packet so that `hdr->length` is 0 or 1 — any value smaller than sizeof(struct capability_mp_data) (typically 4). The length check `(pnt + hdr->length + 3) > end` is evaluated. With hdr->length=1, this is pnt+4 > pnt+4 which is false, so the check passes.

state.value: pnt + 4, equal to end, check passes

step_by_step_execution.description: memcpy(&mpc, pnt + 3, sizeof(struct capability_mp_data)) is executed. sizeof(struct capability_mp_data) is typically 4 bytes. It reads 4 bytes starting at pnt+3, but only 1 byte (end - (pnt+3) = 1) is within the validated buffer region.
The memcpy reads 3 bytes beyond the end of the validated buffer, resulting in an out-of-bounds read.

commit_message:
openvswitch: fix OOB access in reserve_sfa_size()

Given a sufficiently large number of actions, while copying and
reserving memory for a new action of a new flow, if next_offset is
greater than MAX_ACTIONS_BUFSIZE, the function reserve_sfa_size() does
not return -EMSGSIZE as expected, but it allocates MAX_ACTIONS_BUFSIZE
bytes increasing actions_len by req_size. This can then lead to an OOB
write access, especially when further actions need to be copied.

Fix it by rearranging the flow action size check.

KASAN splat below:

==================================================================
BUG: KASAN: slab-out-of-bounds in reserve_sfa_size+0x1ba/0x380 [openvswitch]
Write of size 65360 at addr ffff888147e4001c by task handler15/836

CPU: 1 PID: 836 Comm: handler15 Not tainted 5.18.0-rc1+ #27

Cc: stable@vger.kernel.org
Fixes: f28cd2af22a0 ("openvswitch: fix flow actions reallocation")
Signed-off-by: Paolo Valerio <pvalerio@redhat.com>
Acked-by: Eelco Chaudron <echaudro@redhat.com>
Signed-off-by: David S. Miller <davem@davemloft.net>
cve_desc: An integer coercion error was found in the openvswitch kernel module. Given a sufficiently large number of actions, while copying and reserving memory for a new action of a new flow, the reserve_sfa_size() function does not return -EMSGSIZE as expected, potentially leading to an out-of-bounds write access. This flaw allows a local user to crash or potentially escalate their privileges on the system.

vulnerabilities.description: The variable `new_acts_size` is declared as `int` but is assigned the result of a `max()` expression involving `size_t` operands (`req_size` and `ksize()` return value). If `attr_len` is large enough that `next_offset + req_size` exceeds INT_MAX, the assignment to `int new_acts_size` causes an integer overflow/truncation. The truncated (potentially negative or small positive) value can bypass the `new_acts_size > MAX_ACTIONS_BUFSIZE` check, leading to an undersized allocation via `nla_alloc_flow_actions()`. The subsequent `memcpy` of `(*sfa)->actions_len` bytes into this undersized buffer results in a heap buffer overflow.

step_by_step_execution.description: Function is called with a large `attr_len` value (e.g., close to INT_MAX). `req_size` is computed as `NLA_ALIGN(attr_len)`, producing a large `size_t` value.
`next_offset` is computed from the existing actions length and struct offset.

state.value: offsetof(sw_flow_actions, actions) + (*sfa)->actions_len, e.g. a positive value like 100

step_by_step_execution.description: The initial space check `req_size <= (ksize(*sfa) - next_offset)` fails because the current buffer is too small, so execution continues to the reallocation path.

state.value: small remaining space, much less than req_size

step_by_step_execution.description: `new_acts_size` (int) is assigned `max(next_offset + req_size, ksize(*sfa) * 2)`. The expression `next_offset + req_size` is computed in `size_t` and can exceed INT_MAX (e.g., 100 + 0x7FFFFFF0 = 0x80000090). When this is stored into `int new_acts_size`, it overflows/truncates.

state.value: negative value due to truncation, e.g. -2147483504

step_by_step_execution.description: The check `new_acts_size > MAX_ACTIONS_BUFSIZE` is evaluated as a signed comparison. Since `new_acts_size` is negative, it is less than `MAX_ACTIONS_BUFSIZE`, so the size limit check is bypassed.

`nla_alloc_flow_actions(new_acts_size)` is called with the overflowed negative int value. Depending on the allocator's handling, this may allocate a very small buffer or a huge one (if converted to size_t). If it allocates a small buffer and succeeds, execution continues.
`memcpy(acts->actions, (*sfa)->actions, (*sfa)->actions_len)` copies the existing actions data into the newly allocated (undersized) buffer, causing a heap buffer overflow.

state.value: accumulated length from previous calls, potentially larger than allocated buffer

commit_message:
LZWDecodeCompat(): fix potential index-out-of-bounds write. Fixes http://bugzilla.maptools.org/show_bug.cgi?id=2780 / CVE-2018-8905

The fix consists in using the similar code LZWDecode() to validate we
don't write outside of the output buffer.
cve_desc: In LibTIFF 4.0.9, a heap-based buffer overflow occurs in the function LZWDecodeCompat in tif_lzw.c via a crafted TIFF file, as demonstrated by tiff2ps.

vulnerabilities.description: After reading a code that is not CODE_EOI or CODE_CLEAR, the code is used as an index into `dec_codetab` without any bounds validation. A crafted LZW stream can provide a `code` value that exceeds the current table size (or even CSIZE), causing an out-of-bounds read when `codep` is subsequently dereferenced (e.g., `codep->length`, `codep->firstchar`, `codep->value`, `codep->next`).

step_by_step_execution.description: The function enters the main `while (occ > 0)` loop and calls `NextCode` to read a code from the bitstream.
The code is not CODE_EOI and not CODE_CLEAR, so control falls through to `codep = sp->dec_codetab + code`.

state.value: e.g., a value >= CSIZE (4096) or any value >= free_entp index, from a corrupted/malicious stream

step_by_step_execution.description: `codep` is set to point beyond the bounds of the `dec_codetab` array since `code` is not validated against the table size or CSIZE.

state.value: sp->dec_codetab + code, pointing out of bounds of the dec_codetab array

step_by_step_execution.description: The bounds check on `free_entp` passes (free_entp is valid), so the code proceeds. `free_entp->next` is set to `oldcodep` which is valid. Then `free_entp->value` is computed using `codep->firstchar` if `codep < free_entp` — this dereferences the out-of-bounds pointer.

state.value: out-of-bounds read from memory beyond dec_codetab

step_by_step_execution.description: If `code >= 256`, `codep->length` is read (out-of-bounds), and if non-zero, it is used to compute `tp = op + len` and to walk the `codep->next` chain, all based on out-of-bounds data. This can lead to further out-of-bounds reads/writes.

state.value: op + len, potentially far beyond output buffer

commit_message:
Fix oob write in dyldcache

When the individual n_slide_infos were too high, the sum would overflow
and too few entries would be allocated.
cve_desc: Rizin is a UNIX-like reverse engineering framework and command-line toolset. Versions 0.4.0 and prior are vulnerable to an out-of-bounds write when getting data from dyld cache files. A user opening a malicious dyld cache file could be affected by this vulnerability, allowing an attacker to execute code on the user's machine. Commit number 556ca2f9eef01ec0f4a76d1fbacfcf3a87a44810 contains a patch.

vulnerabilities.description: Integer overflow in the computation of `total_slide_infos`. Since `total_slide_infos` is a `ut32` and it accumulates attacker-controlled `ut32` values read from the cache buffer, the sum can wrap around to a small value. The subsequent allocation uses the small wrapped value, but the inner loops iterate based on the original (large) per-header `n_slide_infos[i]` counts, leading to an out-of-bounds heap write via `infos[k]`.

step_by_step_execution.description: The function reads `n_slide_infos[i]` for each header from the cache buffer. These are attacker-controlled ut32 values.
The accumulation `total_slide_infos += n_slide_infos[i]` overflows the ut32, wrapping around.
Allocation `RZ_NEWS0(RzDyldRebaseInfosEntry, total_slide_infos)` allocates a buffer for only 4 entries.

state.value: heap buffer sized for 4 RzDyldRebaseInfosEntry elements

step_by_step_execution.description: The nested loops iterate based on the original n_slide_infos[i] values (0x80000002 each). Each iteration where the slideInfoOffset/slideInfoSize condition is met increments k and writes to infos[k], which can far exceed the allocated 4 entries.

state.value: incremented up to 0x80000002+ times, far exceeding allocated size of 4

commit_message:
Unsquashfs: fix write outside destination directory exploit

An issue on Github (https://github.com/plougher/squashfs-tools/issues/72)
shows how some specially crafted Squashfs filesystems containing
invalid file names (with '/' and ..) can cause Unsquashfs to write
files outside of the destination directory.

This commit fixes this exploit by checking all names for
validity.

In doing so I have also added checks for '.' and for names that
are shorter than they should be (names in the file system should
not have '\0' terminators).

Signed-off-by: Phillip Lougher <phillip@squashfs.org.uk>
cve_desc: squashfs_opendir in unsquash-1.c in Squashfs-Tools 4.5 stores the filename in the directory entry; this is then used by unsquashfs to create the new file during the unsquash. The filename is not validated for traversal outside of the destination directory, and thus allows writing to locations outside of the destination.

vulnerabilities.description: Out-of-bounds read from `directory_table` buffer. The `size` variable is computed from attacker-controlled filesystem metadata (`(*i)->data + bytes`), and the loop condition `while(bytes < size)` only checks against this attacker-derived value, not the actual allocated size of `directory_table`. A crafted squashfs image with a large `data` field in the inode can cause `bytes` to exceed the bounds of the `directory_table` buffer, leading to out-of-bounds memory reads via the multiple `memcpy` calls.

step_by_step_execution.description: The function reads an inode from attacker-controlled filesystem data via read_inode.
start is computed from filesystem superblock and inode data, then bytes is obtained from lookup_entry.

state.value: result of lookup_entry (a valid small offset into directory_table)

step_by_step_execution.description: bytes is incremented by (*i)->offset, and size is computed as (*i)->data + bytes. Since (*i)->data is attacker-controlled, size can be made arbitrarily large.

The while loop condition `bytes < size` is satisfied since size is very large. The first memcpy reads sizeof(dirh) bytes from directory_table + bytes without checking against directory_table's actual allocated size.

state.value: incrementing through iterations, eventually exceeding directory_table bounds

step_by_step_execution.description: As the loop iterates, bytes grows past the actual size of the directory_table buffer. Subsequent memcpy calls for directory entries and filenames read out-of-bounds memory from directory_table.

commit_message: commit bash-20190628 snapshot
cve_desc: An issue was discovered in disable_priv_mode in shell.c in GNU Bash through 5.0 patch 11. By default, if Bash is run with its effective UID not equal to its real UID, it will drop privileges by setting its effective UID to its real UID. However, it does so incorrectly. On Linux and other systems that support "saved UID" functionality, the saved UID is not dropped. An attacker with command execution in the shell can use "enable -f" for runtime loading of a new builtin, which can be a shared object that calls setuid() and therefore regains privileges. However, binaries running with an effective UID of 0 are unaffected.

commit_message:
kdc: validate sname in TGS-REQ

In tgs_build_reply(), validate the server name in the TGS-REQ is present before
dereferencing.
cve_desc: A null pointer de-reference was found in the way samba kerberos server handled missing sname in TGS-REQ (Ticket Granting Server - Request). An authenticated user could use this flaw to crash the samba server.

vulnerabilities.description: In the capath traversal error handling block, the return value of krb5_make_principal() is not checked. If it fails (e.g., due to memory allocation failure), sp remains NULL (it was explicitly set to NULL just before the call). The subsequent call to krb5_unparse_name(context, sp, ...) will then dereference the NULL pointer, causing a crash (denial of service).

step_by_step_execution.description: The initial server lookup via _kdc_db_fetch fails, and the server principal is a krbtgt (get_krbtgt_realm returns non-NULL). The code enters the capath traversal logic.
_krb5_find_capath returns a capath with entries. A new realm from capath is selected via capath[--num_capath].
sp is freed and set to NULL in preparation for rebuilding the principal.
krb5_make_principal is called but its return value is NOT checked. If memory allocation fails inside krb5_make_principal, sp remains NULL.

state.value: still the old error code, not updated

step_by_step_execution.description: krb5_unparse_name(context, sp, &priv->sname) is called with sp == NULL, causing a NULL pointer dereference and crash.

vulnerabilities.description: In the need_referral() branch of the server lookup error handling, krb5_make_principal() is called without checking its return value. If it fails, sp remains NULL (set to NULL just before), and the subsequent krb5_unparse_name call dereferences the NULL pointer.

step_by_step_execution.description: Server lookup via _kdc_db_fetch fails and the principal is not a krbtgt, so the code enters the need_referral() branch.
need_referral() returns true and the referral realm differs from the current realm.
sp is freed and explicitly set to NULL.
krb5_make_principal is called but its return value is not checked. If it fails due to memory allocation failure, sp remains NULL.
krb5_unparse_name(context, sp, &priv->sname) is called with sp == NULL, causing a NULL pointer dereference.

cve_desc: Integer underflow in the cli_scanpe function in pe.c in ClamAV before 0.97.8 allows remote attackers to cause a denial of service (crash) via a skewed offset larger than the size of the PE section in a UPX packed executable, which triggers an out-of-bounds read.

commit_message:
Do not call http_hdr_flags() on pseudo-headers

In http_EstimateWS(), all headers are passed to the http_isfiltered()
function to calculate how many bytes is needed to serialize the entire
struct http. http_isfiltered() will check the headers for whether they are
going to be filtered out later and if so skip them.

However http_isfiltered() would attempt to treat all elements of struct
http as regular headers with an implicit structure. That does not hold for
the first three pseudo-header entries, which would lead to asserts in
later steps.

This patch skips the filter step for pseudo-headers.

Fixes: #3830
cve_desc: In Varnish Cache 7.0.0, 7.0.1, 7.0.2, and 7.1.0, it is possible to cause the Varnish Server to assert and automatically restart through forged HTTP/1 backend responses. An attack uses a crafted reason phrase of the backend response status line. This is fixed in 7.0.3 and 7.1.1.

cve_desc: A use-after-free exists in the Linux Kernel in tc_new_tfilter that could allow a local attacker to gain privilege escalation. The exploit requires unprivileged user namespaces. We recommend upgrading past commit 04c2a47ffb13c29778e2a14e414ad4cb5a5db4b5

commit_message: Bug 705156(1): Avoid double free of PCL error buffer
cve_desc: A vulnerability classified as problematic was found in GhostPCL 9.55.0. This vulnerability affects the function chunk_free_object of the file gsmchunk.c. The manipulation with a malicious file leads to a memory corruption. The attack can be initiated remotely but requires user interaction. The exploit has been disclosed to the public as a POC and may be used. It is recommended to apply the patches to fix this issue.

commit_message: libarchive: sanitize filenames before extracting
cve_desc: Directory traversal vulnerability in File Roller 3.6.x before 3.6.4, 3.8.x before 3.8.3, and 3.9.x before 3.9.3, when libarchive is used, allows remote attackers to create arbitrary files via a crafted archive that is not properly handled in a "Keep directory structure" action, related to fr-archive-libarchive.c and fr-window.c.

vulnerabilities.description: When g_file_query_info returns NULL (an error occurred), the code enters the else branch. If the error is not G_IO_ERROR_NOT_FOUND, it calls g_object_unref(info) where info is NULL. g_object_unref does not accept NULL pointers, leading to a NULL pointer dereference and crash. An attacker who can trigger a specific I/O error (other than NOT_FOUND) during extraction can cause a denial of service.

step_by_step_execution.description: The extraction loop reads an archive entry that is requested for extraction.
The skip_older or !overwrite condition is true, so the code attempts to query file info.
g_file_query_info fails with an error that is NOT G_IO_ERROR_NOT_FOUND (e.g., permission denied).
The else branch is entered. The error does not match G_IO_ERROR_NOT_FOUND, so the inner if-block executes.
g_object_unref(info) is called with NULL, causing a NULL pointer dereference.

code_snippets:
case AE_IFLNK:
	if (! g_file_make_symbolic_link (file, archive_entry_symlink (entry), cancellable, &local_error)) {
vulnerabilities.description: A malicious archive can contain a symbolic link entry pointing to an arbitrary location outside the destination directory (e.g., /etc/), followed by a regular file entry whose path resolves through that symlink. The symlink is created with an attacker-controlled target from archive_entry_symlink(entry), and subsequent file writes will follow the symlink, allowing writing to arbitrary filesystem locations (directory traversal / zip-slip via symlinks).

code_snippets: ostream = (GOutputStream *) g_file_replace (file, NULL, FALSE, G_FILE_CREATE_REPLACE_DESTINATION, cancellable, &load_data->error);

step_by_step_execution.description: Archive contains entry 1: a symlink 'evil_link' with target '/etc/cron.d'.
The symlink is created inside the destination directory via g_file_make_symbolic_link, pointing to /etc/cron.d.
Archive contains entry 2: a regular file 'evil_link/malicious_job'.
g_file_get_child resolves to <destination>/evil_link/malicious_job which, via the symlink, resolves to /etc/cron.d/malicious_job.
g_file_replace opens the file for writing, and archive data is written to /etc/cron.d/malicious_job, achieving arbitrary file write outside the destination.

commit_message:
Avoid an integer overflow in the xbm loader

At the same time, reject some silly input, such as negative
width or height.

https://bugzilla.gnome.org/show_bug.cgi?id=672811
cve_desc: Multiple integer overflows in the read_bitmap_file_data function in io-xbm.c in gdk-pixbuf before 2.26.1 allow remote attackers to cause a denial of service (application crash) via a negative (1) height or (2) width in an XBM file, which triggers a heap-based buffer overflow.

vulnerabilities.description: Integer overflow in the calculation of `size = bytes_per_line * hh`. Both `ww` and `hh` are parsed from user-controlled file input with no upper-bound validation. A crafted bitmap file can specify large width/height values that cause the multiplication to overflow (wrapping around on 32-bit or truncating when assigned to `int`), resulting in a small heap allocation. The subsequent loop then writes far more data than allocated, causing a heap buffer overflow.

step_by_step_execution.description: The function reads lines from the file. A crafted file provides `#define foo_width 64` and `#define foo_height 1073741825` (0x40000001).
The function encounters the `static unsigned char foo_bits[] = {` line and proceeds past the `ww`/`hh` zero checks.
bytes_per_line is calculated: (64+7)/8 + 0 = 8. padding is 0 since version10p is 0.
size is calculated: 8 * 1073741825 = 8589934600 (0x200000008). Since `size` is `int` (32-bit), this overflows/wraps to 8.
g_malloc(8) allocates only 8 bytes on the heap.

The loop iterates `bytes < size` where the logical intended size is ~8 billion bytes but the loop condition uses the overflowed `size` value of 8. On a 32-bit system, or depending on how the compiler handles the overflow, the loop writes 8 bytes — matching the allocation. However, if hh is chosen such that the overflow yields a small positive int while the true product is larger (e.g., ww=8, hh=0x20000001 gives bytes_per_line=1, size=0x20000001 truncated to int = 536870913, but allocation was only that), the mismatch between allocated and written causes heap overflow. More precisely with ww=8, hh=0x80000001: bytes_per_line=1, unsigned product = 0x80000001, assigned to int size = negative value, loop doesn't execute. With ww=64, hh=0x20000001: bytes_per_line=8, unsigned product=0x100000008, truncated to int=8, allocates 8 bytes but logical data in file can trick subsequent writes.

state.value: 0 to size-1, writing to heap buffer beyond its 8-byte allocation if the overflow scenario allows loop iteration beyond allocated size

commit_message:
net: sched: fix use-after-free in tc_new_tfilter()

Whenever tc_new_tfilter() jumps back to replay: label,
we need to make sure @q and @chain local variables are cleared again,
or risk use-after-free as in [1]

For consistency, apply the same fix in tc_ctl_chain()

commit_message: [TALOS-CAN-0063] avoid buffer overrun in ntpq
cve_desc: ntpq in NTP 4.2.x before 4.2.8p4, and 4.3.x before 4.3.77 allows remote attackers to cause a denial of service (crash) via crafted mode 6 response packets.

vulnerabilities.description: When output_raw is not '*' (e.g., '?'), the code appends a character to `bv` after `atoascii` has potentially filled it to its full capacity (`sizeof(bv)` which is `2 * MAXVALLEN`). If `atoascii` produces a string of length `2 * MAXVALLEN - 1` (fully utilizing the buffer), then `len` equals `2 * MAXVALLEN - 1`, `bv[len]` overwrites the null terminator at the last valid index, and `bv[len + 1]` writes one byte past the end of the `bv` array, causing a stack-based buffer overflow.

step_by_step_execution.description: The function enters the while loop and `nextvar` returns a name/value pair where the value is long enough to fill the output buffer and the format triggers `output_raw = '?'` (e.g., a malformed TS, HA, NA, LP, OC, AR, FX, or unknown format).

`atoascii(value, MAXVALLEN, bv, sizeof(bv))` is called, converting up to MAXVALLEN bytes of `value` into ASCII representation in `bv`. If characters are escaped/expanded, the output can fill the entire `bv` buffer of size `2 * MAXVALLEN`, producing a string of length `2 * MAXVALLEN - 1` (null-terminated).

state.value: A string of length 2*MAXVALLEN - 1, fully occupying the buffer

step_by_step_execution.description: `output_raw` is not '*', so the branch to append is taken. `len = strlen(bv)` computes the string length.
`bv[len] = output_raw` writes '?' at index `2 * MAXVALLEN - 1`, which is the last valid index of bv (overwrites the existing null terminator).
`bv[len+1] = '\0'` writes a null byte at index `2 * MAXVALLEN`, which is one byte past the end of the `bv` array. This is an out-of-bounds write on the stack.

commit_message:
patch 9.0.0322: crash when no errors and 'quickfixtextfunc' is set

Problem:    Crash when no errors and 'quickfixtextfunc' is set.
Solution:   Do not handle errors if there aren't any.
cve_desc: Use After Free in GitHub repository vim/vim prior to 9.0.0322.

Integer underflow in the cli_scanpe function in pe.c in ClamAV before 0.97.8 allows remote attackers to cause a denial of service (crash) via a skewed offset larger than the size of the PE section in a UPX packed executable, which triggers an out-of-bounds read.

vulnerabilities.description: Off-by-one heap buffer write when `len` is 1. The buffer is allocated with `cli_malloc(len)` (1 byte). The loop runs once with i=0, then i becomes 2. After the loop, `name[i/2]` writes to `name[1]`, which is one byte past the end of the 1-byte heap allocation.

step_by_step_execution.description: Function is called with len=1 (a non-zero value, so the NULL check is bypassed). len is not > 400, so it stays 1.
cli_malloc(len) allocates a 1-byte heap buffer. name points to this buffer with valid index [0].
fseek and fread succeed, reading 1 byte into name.
The for loop starts with i=0. Condition i < len (0 < 1) is true. name[0] = name[0] is executed. Then i += 2, so i becomes 2.
Loop condition i < len (2 < 1) is false, so loop exits with i=2.
name[i/2] = '\0' writes to name[1]. Since the buffer is only 1 byte (valid index 0), this is a 1-byte out-of-bounds heap write.

The manipulation with a malicious file leads to a memory corruption. The attack can be initiated remotely but requires user interaction. The exploit has been disclosed to the public as a POC and may be used. It is recommended to apply the patches to fix this issue.

commit_message:
Fix XkbSelectEvents() integer underflow

CVE-2020-14361 ZDI-CAN 11573

This vulnerability was discovered by:
Jan-Niklas Sohn working with Trend Micro Zero Day Initiative

Signed-off-by: Matthieu Herrb <matthieu@herrb.eu>
cve_desc: A flaw was found in X.Org Server before xorg-x11-server 1.20.9. An Integer underflow leading to heap-buffer overflow may lead to a privilege escalation vulnerability. The highest threat from this vulnerability is to data confidentiality and integrity as well as system availability.

vulnerabilities.description: When an event type with size=1 is processed (XkbBellNotify, XkbActionMessage, XkbCompatMapNotify), the bounds check uses size=1 (checking dataLeft < 2), but then the else branch changes size to 2, causing the subsequent subtraction `dataLeft -= (size * 2)` to subtract 4 instead of 2. If dataLeft was 2 or 3, this causes an unsigned integer underflow, making dataLeft wrap to a very large value. On subsequent loop iterations, the bounds check passes erroneously, and swap operations (swaps/swapl) read and write memory beyond the allocated request buffer.

step_by_step_execution.description: The request arrives and stuff->length is swapped. REQUEST_AT_LEAST_SIZE ensures the request is at least as large as the fixed header.

state.value: swapped 16-bit value, enough to pass REQUEST_AT_LEAST_SIZE

step_by_step_execution.description: dataLeft is computed as the number of bytes of variable-length data following the fixed header. Attacker crafts request so dataLeft = 2.

state.value: pointer to start of variable data after header

step_by_step_execution.description: Attacker sets affectWhich to include bits for XkbCompatMapNotify (ndx=7, size=1) and XkbAccessXNotify (ndx=10, size=2), and neither bit is set in selectAll or clear.
Loop iteration reaches ndx=7 (XkbCompatMapNotify). size is set to 1. The bounds check verifies dataLeft (2) >= size*2 (2), which passes.
Since size is 1, neither the size==2 nor size==4 branches execute. The else branch sets size = 2.
from.c8 is advanced by size*2 = 4 bytes, and dataLeft is decremented by 4. Since dataLeft was 2 (unsigned), 2 - 4 wraps to 0xFFFFFFFE.

state.value: original pointer + 4 (2 bytes past valid buffer)

step_by_step_execution.description: Loop continues to ndx=10 (XkbAccessXNotify). size is set to 2. Bounds check verifies dataLeft (0xFFFFFFFE) >= size*2 (4), which passes due to the underflow.
The size==2 branch executes swaps(&from.c16[0]) and swaps(&from.c16[1]), performing out-of-bounds read and write on heap memory beyond the allocated request buffer.

state.value: pointing 2 bytes past end of valid request data

commit_message: libarchive: sanitize filenames before extracting
cve_desc: Directory traversal vulnerability in File Roller 3.6.x before 3.6.4, 3.8.x before 3.8.3, and 3.9.x before 3.9.3, when libarchive is used, allows remote attackers to create arbitrary files via a crafted archive that is not properly handled in a "Keep directory structure" action, related to fr-archive-libarchive.c and fr-window.c.

vulnerabilities.description: In the else branch of `if (info != NULL)`, the variable `info` is guaranteed to be NULL (since `g_file_query_info` returned NULL). However, when the error is not `G_IO_ERROR_NOT_FOUND`, the code calls `g_object_unref(info)` on the NULL pointer, causing a NULL pointer dereference / crash.

step_by_step_execution.description: The loop processes an archive entry whose extraction is requested, and either `extract_data->skip_older` or `!extract_data->overwrite` is true, so the file-info query block is entered.
`g_file_query_info` is called and returns NULL (e.g., due to a permission error or other I/O error that is not NOT_FOUND), setting `local_error` to a non-NOT_FOUND error.
Execution enters the `else` branch because `info` is NULL.
`g_error_matches(local_error, G_IO_ERROR, G_IO_ERROR_NOT_FOUND)` returns FALSE, so the inner `if` body is entered.
`g_object_unref(info)` is called with `info == NULL`, causing a NULL pointer dereference.

vulnerabilities.description: When extracting a symlink entry (AE_IFLNK), the symlink target (`archive_entry_symlink(entry)`) is taken directly from the archive without any sanitization. An attacker can craft an archive containing a symlink pointing outside the destination directory (e.g., target `../../etc/cron.d`), and then a subsequent regular file entry targeting that symlink's name. The first entry creates the symlink inside the destination, and the second entry follows it, writing arbitrary content outside the extraction directory.

code_snippets: ostream = (GOutputStream *) g_file_replace (file, NULL, FALSE, G_FILE_CREATE_REPLACE_DESTINATION, cancellable, &load_data->error);

step_by_step_execution.description: The archive contains two entries: first a symlink entry with pathname 'evil_link' and symlink target '../../etc/target', then a regular file entry with pathname 'evil_link' containing attacker-controlled data.

The first entry is processed. `_g_path_get_relative_basename_safe` validates the entry's pathname ('evil_link'), which passes because it is a safe relative name. `file` is set to `<destination>/evil_link`.
`g_file_make_symbolic_link` is called with the unsanitized symlink target `../../etc/target`, creating a symlink at `<destination>/evil_link -> ../../etc/target`.
The second entry is processed. Its pathname 'evil_link' also passes `_g_path_get_relative_basename_safe`. `file` is again `<destination>/evil_link`, which is now a symlink.

`g_file_replace` is called on `file`, which follows the symlink and opens `../../etc/target` for writing. Attacker-controlled data from the archive is written to the target file outside the destination directory.

commit_message:
patch 9.0.0104: going beyond allocated memory when evaluating string constant

Problem:    Going beyond allocated memory when evaluating string constant.
Solution:   Properly skip over <Key> form.
cve_desc: Heap-based Buffer Overflow in GitHub repository vim/vim prior to 9.0.0102.

vulnerabilities.description: Integer overflow / truncation when computing the allocation size. `p - *arg` is a `ptrdiff_t` (64-bit on 64-bit platforms), but `len` is `int` (32-bit). For sufficiently large input strings (>2GB), the cast to `int` truncates the value, potentially producing a small positive number. This leads to a drastically undersized heap allocation, and the subsequent second loop writes far beyond the allocated buffer.

step_by_step_execution.description: Function is called with a very large double-quoted string (>2GB), with evaluate=TRUE and interpolate=0.
The first for-loop scans to find the closing '"'. The pointer `p` advances through the entire >2GB string content until it reaches the closing quote.

The allocation size is computed by casting the large ptrdiff_t value to int. For example, if p - *arg = 0x80000100, (int)(0x80000100) wraps/truncates to a small or negative value. If it wraps to a small positive value (e.g., 256), a tiny buffer is allocated.
alloc(len) allocates a 256-byte buffer on the heap.
The second for-loop iterates over the entire >2GB input string, copying characters into the 256-byte buffer via `*end++` and `MB_COPY_CHAR(p, end)`, resulting in a massive heap buffer overflow.

commit_message:
sungem: switch to use qemu_receive_packet() for loopback

This patch switches to use qemu_receive_packet() which can detect
reentrancy and return early.

This is intended to address CVE-2021-3416.

Cc: Prasad J Pandit <ppandit@redhat.com>
Cc: qemu-stable@nongnu.org
Reviewed-by: Mark Cave-Ayland <mark.cave-ayland@ilande.co.uk>
Reviewed-by: Philippe Mathieu-Daudé <philmd@redhat.com>
Reviewed-by: Alistair Francis <alistair.francis@wdc.com>
Signed-off-by: Jason Wang <jasowang@redhat.com>
cve_desc: A potential stack overflow via infinite loop issue was found in various NIC emulators of QEMU in versions up to and including 5.2.0. The issue occurs in loopback mode of a NIC wherein reentrant DMA checks get bypassed. A guest user/process may use this flaw to consume CPU cycles or crash the QEMU process on the host resulting in DoS scenario.

commit_message:
nfc: st21nfca: Fix potential buffer overflows in EVT_TRANSACTION

It appears that there are some buffer overflows in EVT_TRANSACTION.
This happens because the length parameters that are passed to memcpy
come directly from skb->data and are not guarded in any way.

Signed-off-by: Jordy Zomer <jordy@pwning.systems>
Reviewed-by: Krzysztof Kozlowski <krzysztof.kozlowski@canonical.com>
Signed-off-by: David S. Miller <davem@davemloft.net>
cve_desc: st21nfca_connectivity_event_received in drivers/nfc/st21nfca/se.c in the Linux kernel through 5.16.12 has EVT_TRANSACTION buffer overflows because of untrusted length parameters.

vulnerabilities.description: The aid_len and params_len values are read directly from attacker-controlled skb data without validation against the allocated buffer size (skb->len - 2) or the actual skb length. An attacker can set skb->data[1] to a large value, causing heap buffer overflows on the memcpy into transaction->aid and transaction->params, and out-of-bounds reads from the skb buffer. Additionally, the initial length check uses '&&' instead of '||', meaning the length check can be bypassed if skb->data[0] == NFC_EVT_TRANSACTION_AID_TAG.

step_by_step_execution.description: Function is called with event == ST21NFCA_EVT_TRANSACTION. The attacker provides a crafted skb with skb->len = 8 (NFC_MIN_AID_LENGTH+2 assuming NFC_MIN_AID_LENGTH=6, so not less), skb->data[0] = NFC_EVT_TRANSACTION_AID_TAG (0x81), and skb->data[1] = 0xFF.

The length check `skb->len < NFC_MIN_AID_LENGTH + 2 && skb->data[0] != NFC_EVT_TRANSACTION_AID_TAG` evaluates. Even if skb->len < NFC_MIN_AID_LENGTH + 2, the second condition is false (data[0] IS the AID tag), so the whole condition is false due to &&. The check is bypassed.
devm_kzalloc allocates skb->len - 2 = 6 bytes for the transaction struct.
transaction->aid_len is set to skb->data[1] = 255.

memcpy copies 255 bytes from &skb->data[2] into transaction->aid. The destination buffer (transaction) is only 6 bytes total, causing a massive heap buffer overflow. The source read of 255 bytes from skb->data[2] also exceeds the skb data length of 8, causing an out-of-bounds read.

cve_desc: Cyrus IMAP before 3.2.7, and 3.3.x and 3.4.x before 3.4.1, allows remote authenticated users to bypass intended access restrictions on server annotations and consequently cause replication to stall.

commit_message:
Bug 702582, CVE 2020-15900 Memory Corruption in Ghostscript 9.52

Fix the 'rsearch' calculation for the 'post' size to give the correct
size.  Previous calculation would result in a size that was too large,
and could underflow to max uint32_t. Also fix 'rsearch' to return the
correct 'pre' string with empty string match.

A future change may 'undefine' this undocumented, non-standard operator
during initialization as we do with the many other non-standard internal
PostScript operators and procedures.
cve_desc: A memory corruption issue was found in Artifex Ghostscript 9.50 and 9.52. Use of a non-standard PostScript operator can allow overriding of file access controls. The 'rsearch' calculation for the 'post' size resulted in a size that was too large, and could underflow to max uint32_t. This was fixed in commit 5d499272b95a6b890a1397e11d20937de000d31b.

vulnerabilities.description: In the backward search case (!forward), the 'post' string size is computed incorrectly, leading to a buffer over-read. The formula `count + (size - 1)` does not correctly represent the number of bytes remaining after the match. The correct post size should be `original_count - count` (where original_count = r_size(op1) - size), but the code uses `count + (size - 1)`. This causes the resulting string object to have a size larger than the actual remaining data, allowing reads beyond the original string's buffer.

step_by_step_execution.description: Function is called with forward=false (rsearch). Consider source string "xb" (length 2) and pattern "b" (length 1).
size and count are computed. size = 1, count = 2 - 1 = 1.
ptr is initialized to start of source, then adjusted for backward search: ptr += count, so ptr = start + 1 (pointing to 'b').
ch = pat[0] = 'b'. Loop begins. Iteration 0: *ptr = 'b' == ch. Match found! goto found. count is still 1 (has not been decremented yet).

At 'found' label: the post-match string is configured. op1->value.bytes = ptr + size = start + 2 (one past end of source buffer). r_set_size(op1, count + (size - 1)) = 1 + 0 = 1. But the actual remaining data after the match is 0 bytes (nothing follows 'b' in "xb").

The resulting 'post' string object has size 1 starting at start+2, which is entirely outside the original source string buffer. Any subsequent read of this string object will read 1 byte beyond the allocated buffer (out-of-bounds read).

vulnerabilities.description: When size == 0 and forward == false (backward search for empty pattern), the expression `(size - 1)` causes an unsigned integer underflow since size is uint. This results in `count + UINT_MAX`, setting the post string to an enormous size and enabling a massive out-of-bounds read.

step_by_step_execution.description: Function called with forward=false, source string of length 0, pattern of length 0.
size (0) is not > r_size(op1) (0), so the early return is skipped. count = 0 - 0 = 0.
size == 0, so goto found is taken immediately. ptr = op1->value.bytes (start of source). The backward ptr adjustment is never reached.
At 'found': r_set_size(op1, count + (size - 1)). Since size is uint and equals 0, (size - 1) wraps to UINT_MAX (4294967295). count + UINT_MAX = 0 + 4294967295 = 4294967295.
op1 is set to a string starting at ptr + 0 = start, with size ~4GB. Any subsequent access to this string object allows reading arbitrary memory far beyond the original buffer.

commit_message:
string_number_format: Correctly handles return value of snprintf

Summary: `snprintf` can return a value greater than the number of bytes copied. In case the first byte of the string is not a digit (could be '-'), size of `tmpstr` was being updated without checking `tmplen`. This resulted in either an assertion error or a heap overflow depending on whether the assertion is compiled or not.

Reviewed By: mofarrell, qianxuweiren

Differential Revision: D17327899

fbshipit-source-id: ee53875d21e02608c6d870388eecf1464de24ff1
cve_desc: Insufficient boundary checks when formatting numbers in number_format allows read/write access to out-of-bounds memory, potentially leading to remote code execution. This issue affects HHVM versions prior to 3.30.10, all versions between 4.0.0 and 4.8.5, all versions between 4.9.0 and 4.18.2, and versions 4.19.0, 4.19.1, 4.20.0, 4.20.1, 4.20.2, 4.21.0, 4.22.0, 4.23.0.

vulnerabilities.description: The overflow check for `integral` is performed in `size_t` (unsigned 64-bit) arithmetic because `thousand_sep.size()` returns `size_t`, but `integral` is a 32-bit `int`. On a 64-bit system, the check `integral + thousand_sep.size() * ((integral-1) / 3) < integral` evaluates in `size_t` and can pass even when the subsequent `integral += ...` assignment overflows the `int` type. With a sufficiently large `thousand_sep` string (>~21 million bytes), `integral` overflows to a negative or small positive value, causing `reslen` to be much smaller than needed. The subsequent `String resstr(reslen, ReserveString)` allocates an undersized buffer, and the while loop that copies digits and inserts thousand separators writes far beyond the buffer bounds.

step_by_step_execution.description: Function is called with a valid double (e.g., d=1e308), dec=0, empty dec_point, and a very large thousand_sep string (~21 million+ bytes).
snprintf produces the decimal representation of d. For 1e308, this produces ~309 digits. dp is nullptr since dec=0.

The overflow check is evaluated in size_t arithmetic: integral (promoted to size_t: 309) + thousand_sep.size() * ((integral-1)/3) = 309 + 21053759 * 102 = 309 + 2147483418 = 2147483727. This is greater than 309, so the check passes and no error is raised.

integral += ((integral-1) / 3) * thousand_sep.size() stores the result in int32. 309 + 2147483418 = 2147483727, which exceeds INT_MAX (2147483647), causing signed integer overflow. integral wraps to a negative or small value.

state.value: overflowed int32 value (e.g., -2147483569 or a small positive depending on exact thousand_sep size)

step_by_step_execution.description: reslen is set to the overflowed integral value. Since dec=0 and dec_point is empty, reslen stays at this overflowed value. String resstr(reslen, ReserveString) allocates a buffer far smaller than needed (or with corrupted size).

state.value: overflowed value (much smaller than the ~2 billion bytes actually needed)

step_by_step_execution.description: The while loop iterates over all 309 source digits, writing each one and inserting the ~21 million byte thousand_sep every 3 digits via memcpy. This writes ~2 billion bytes total into a buffer that is far too small, causing a heap buffer overflow.
