commit_message:
nfc: nfcmrvl: main: reorder destructive operations in nfcmrvl_nci_unregister_dev to avoid bugs

There are destructive operations such as nfcmrvl_fw_dnld_abort and
gpio_free in nfcmrvl_nci_unregister_dev. The resources such as firmware,
gpio and so on could be destructed while the upper layer functions such as
nfcmrvl_fw_dnld_start and nfcmrvl_nci_recv_frame is executing, which leads
to double-free, use-after-free and null-ptr-deref bugs.

There are three situations that could lead to double-free bugs.

The first situation is shown below:

   (Thread 1)                 |      (Thread 2)
nfcmrvl_fw_dnld_start         |
 ...                          |  nfcmrvl_nci_unregister_dev
 release_firmware()           |   nfcmrvl_fw_dnld_abort
  kfree(fw) //(1)             |    fw_dnld_over
                              |     release_firmware
  ...                         |      kfree(fw) //(2)
                              |     ...

The second situation is shown below:

   (Thread 1)                 |      (Thread 2)
nfcmrvl_fw_dnld_start         |
 ...                          |
 mod_timer                    |
 (wait a time)                |
 fw_dnld_timeout              |  nfcmrvl_nci_unregister_dev
   fw_dnld_over               |   nfcmrvl_fw_dnld_abort
    release_firmware          |    fw_dnld_over
     kfree(fw) //(1)          |     release_firmware
     ...                      |      kfree(fw) //(2)

The third situation is shown below:

       (Thread 1)               |       (Thread 2)
nfcmrvl_nci_recv_frame          |
 if(..->fw_download_in_progress)|
  nfcmrvl_fw_dnld_recv_frame    |
   queue_work                   |
                                |
fw_dnld_rx_work                 | nfcmrvl_nci_unregister_dev
 fw_dnld_over                   |  nfcmrvl_fw_dnld_abort
  release_firmware              |   fw_dnld_over
   kfree(fw) //(1)              |    release_firmware
                                |     kfree(fw) //(2)

The firmware struct is deallocated in position (1) and deallocated
in position (2) again.

The crash trace triggered by POC is like below:

BUG: KASAN: double-free or invalid-free in fw_dnld_over
Call Trace:
  kfree
  fw_dnld_over
  nfcmrvl_nci_unregister_dev
  nci_uart_tty_close
  tty_ldisc_kill
  tty_ldisc_hangup
  __tty_hangup.part.0
  tty_release
  ...

What's more, there are also use-after-free and null-ptr-deref bugs
in nfcmrvl_fw_dnld_start. If we deallocate firmware struct, gpio or
set null to the members of priv->fw_dnld in nfcmrvl_nci_unregister_dev,
then, we dereference firmware, gpio or the members of priv->fw_dnld in
nfcmrvl_fw_dnld_start, the UAF or NPD bugs will happen.

This patch reorders destructive operations after nci_unregister_device
in order to synchronize between cleanup routine and firmware download
routine.

The nci_unregister_device is well synchronized. If the device is
detaching, the firmware download routine will goto error. If firmware
download routine is executing, nci_unregister_device will wait until
firmware download routine is finished.

Fixes: 3194c6870158 ("NFC: nfcmrvl: add firmware download support")
Signed-off-by: Duoming Zhou <duoming@zju.edu.cn>
Signed-off-by: David S. Miller <davem@davemloft.net>
cve_desc: A flaw in Linux Kernel found in nfcmrvl_nci_unregister_dev() in drivers/nfc/nfcmrvl/main.c can lead to use after free both read or write when non synchronized between cleanup routine and firmware download routine.

commit_message:
bpf: Fix integer overflow in prealloc_elems_and_freelist()

In prealloc_elems_and_freelist(), the multiplication to calculate the
size passed to bpf_map_area_alloc() could lead to an integer overflow.
As a result, out-of-bounds write could occur in pcpu_freelist_populate()
as reported by KASAN:

[...]
[   16.968613] BUG: KASAN: slab-out-of-bounds in pcpu_freelist_populate+0xd9/0x100
[   16.969408] Write of size 8 at addr ffff888104fc6ea0 by task crash/78
[   16.970038]
[   16.970195] CPU: 0 PID: 78 Comm: crash Not tainted 5.15.0-rc2+ #1
[   16.970878] Hardware name: QEMU Standard PC (i440FX + PIIX, 1996), BIOS 1.13.0-1ubuntu1.1 04/01/2014
[   16.972026] Call Trace:
[   16.972306]  dump_stack_lvl+0x34/0x44
[   16.972687]  print_address_description.constprop.0+0x21/0x140
[   16.973297]  ? pcpu_freelist_populate+0xd9/0x100
[   16.973777]  ? pcpu_freelist_populate+0xd9/0x100
[   16.974257]  kasan_report.cold+0x7f/0x11b
[   16.974681]  ? pcpu_freelist_populate+0xd9/0x100
[   16.975190]  pcpu_freelist_populate+0xd9/0x100
[   16.975669]  stack_map_alloc+0x209/0x2a0
[   16.976106]  __sys_bpf+0xd83/0x2ce0
[...]

The possibility of this overflow was originally discussed in [0], but
was overlooked.

Fix the integer overflow by changing elem_size to u64 from u32.

  [0] https://lore.kernel.org/bpf/728b238e-a481-eb50-98e9-b0f430ab01e7@gmail.com/

Fixes: 557c0c6e7df8 ("bpf: convert stackmap to pre-allocation")
Signed-off-by: Tatsuhiko Yasumatsu <th.yasumatsu@gmail.com>
Signed-off-by: Daniel Borkmann <daniel@iogearbox.net>
Link: https://lore.kernel.org/bpf/20210930135545.173698-1-th.yasumatsu@gmail.com
cve_desc: prealloc_elems_and_freelist in kernel/bpf/stackmap.c in the Linux kernel before 5.14.12 allows unprivileged users to trigger an eBPF multiplication integer overflow with a resultant out-of-bounds write.

vulnerabilities.description: The multiplication `elem_size * smap->map.max_entries` can overflow. `elem_size` is u64, but `smap->map.max_entries` is u32. While the multiplication itself is done in u64, if `elem_size` and `max_entries` are both large enough, the product can wrap around, resulting in a smaller-than-expected allocation. Subsequent use via `pcpu_freelist_populate` would treat the allocation as having `max_entries` elements of `elem_size` each, writing far beyond the allocated buffer.

step_by_step_execution.description: elem_size is computed as sizeof(struct stack_map_bucket) + value_size. With a large value_size, elem_size can be a large u64 value.

state.value: large value, e.g. 0x100000008 if value_size is crafted

step_by_step_execution.description: The allocation size is computed as elem_size * smap->map.max_entries. If max_entries is large enough, this u64 multiplication wraps around to a small value.

state.value: wraps around to a small value due to u64 overflow, e.g. elem_size=0x100000008, max_entries=0x10 => 0x1000000080 which may be fine, but with carefully chosen values overflow is possible

step_by_step_execution.description: bpf_map_area_alloc allocates a buffer of the (small, wrapped) size and succeeds.
pcpu_freelist_populate iterates over max_entries elements, each of elem_size bytes, within the undersized buffer, writing beyond its bounds (heap buffer overflow).

commit_message: Avoid maxrlvls more than upper bound to cause heap-buffer-overflow
cve_desc: There's a flaw in jasper's jpc encoder in versions prior to 2.0.23. Crafted input provided to jasper by an attacker could cause an arbitrary out-of-bounds write. This could potentially affect data confidentiality, integrity, or application availability.

vulnerabilities.description: User-controlled `maxrlvls` value (set via `OPT_MAXRLVLS` option) is used as a loop bound to write into the fixed-size `tccp->prcwidthexpns` and `tccp->prcheightexpns` arrays *before* the validation check `if (cp->tccp.maxrlvls < 1)` is reached. There is no upper-bound check on `maxrlvls` against the array size, leading to a heap or stack buffer overflow.

step_by_step_execution.description: The function allocates `cp` and initializes `tccp = &cp->tccp`. `tccp->maxrlvls` is set to the default value of 6.
The option string is parsed. When OPT_MAXRLVLS is encountered, `tccp->maxrlvls` is set to `atoi(jas_tvparser_getval(tvp))`. An attacker can supply a large value, e.g. 10000.
After the parsing loop, the code enters the loop `for (rlvlno = 0; rlvlno < tccp->maxrlvls; ++rlvlno)` which writes to `tccp->prcwidthexpns[rlvlno]` and `tccp->prcheightexpns[rlvlno]`.
Since `rlvlno` exceeds the bounds of the `prcwidthexpns` and `prcheightexpns` arrays, out-of-bounds writes occur, corrupting adjacent memory.
The validation check `if (cp->tccp.maxrlvls < 1)` comes later in the function and only checks the lower bound, never preventing the overflow.

commit_message: lib: Protect against malicious namespace declarations (CVE-2022-25236)
cve_desc: xmlparse.c in Expat (aka libexpat) before 2.4.5 allows attackers to insert namespace-separator characters into namespace URIs.

commit_message:
gsocketclient: Fix criticals

This ensures the parent GTask is kept alive as long as an enumeration
is running and trying to connect.

Closes #1646
Closes #1649
cve_desc: gio/gsocketclient.c in GNOME GLib 2.59.2 does not ensure that a parent GTask remains alive during the execution of a connection-attempting enumeration, which allows remote attackers to cause a denial of service (g_socket_client_connected_callback mishandling and application crash) via a crafted web site, as demonstrated by GNOME Web (aka Epiphany).

commit_message:
bpf: Fix integer overflow in prealloc_elems_and_freelist()

In prealloc_elems_and_freelist(), the multiplication to calculate the
size passed to bpf_map_area_alloc() could lead to an integer overflow.
As a result, out-of-bounds write could occur in pcpu_freelist_populate()
as reported by KASAN:

[...]
[   16.968613] BUG: KASAN: slab-out-of-bounds in pcpu_freelist_populate+0xd9/0x100
[   16.969408] Write of size 8 at addr ffff888104fc6ea0 by task crash/78
[   16.970038]
[   16.970195] CPU: 0 PID: 78 Comm: crash Not tainted 5.15.0-rc2+ #1
[   16.970878] Hardware name: QEMU Standard PC (i440FX + PIIX, 1996), BIOS 1.13.0-1ubuntu1.1 04/01/2014
[   16.972026] Call Trace:
[   16.972306]  dump_stack_lvl+0x34/0x44
[   16.972687]  print_address_description.constprop.0+0x21/0x140
[   16.973297]  ? pcpu_freelist_populate+0xd9/0x100
[   16.973777]  ? pcpu_freelist_populate+0xd9/0x100
[   16.974257]  kasan_report.cold+0x7f/0x11b
[   16.974681]  ? pcpu_freelist_populate+0xd9/0x100
[   16.975190]  pcpu_freelist_populate+0xd9/0x100
[   16.975669]  stack_map_alloc+0x209/0x2a0
[   16.976106]  __sys_bpf+0xd83/0x2ce0
[...]

The possibility of this overflow was originally discussed in [0], but
was overlooked.

Fix the integer overflow by changing elem_size to u64 from u32.

  [0] https://lore.kernel.org/bpf/728b238e-a481-eb50-98e9-b0f430ab01e7@gmail.com/

Fixes: 557c0c6e7df8 ("bpf: convert stackmap to pre-allocation")
Signed-off-by: Tatsuhiko Yasumatsu <th.yasumatsu@gmail.com>
Signed-off-by: Daniel Borkmann <daniel@iogearbox.net>
Link: https://lore.kernel.org/bpf/20210930135545.173698-1-th.yasumatsu@gmail.com
cve_desc: prealloc_elems_and_freelist in kernel/bpf/stackmap.c in the Linux kernel before 5.14.12 allows unprivileged users to trigger an eBPF multiplication integer overflow with a resultant out-of-bounds write.

vulnerabilities.description: Integer overflow in the allocation size calculation. `elem_size` (u32) multiplied by `smap->map.max_entries` (u32) can overflow, resulting in a much smaller allocation than intended. The subsequent `pcpu_freelist_populate` then treats the undersized buffer as if it has `max_entries` elements, causing out-of-bounds writes.

step_by_step_execution.description: elem_size is computed as sizeof(struct stack_map_bucket) + smap->map.value_size. Both are u32 values.

The product elem_size * smap->map.max_entries is computed as a u32 (or implicitly truncated). For example, 128 * 0x02000001 = 0x100000080, which truncates to 0x80 (128 bytes) when it overflows 32-bit range. Even if evaluated as size_t on 64-bit, on 32-bit systems the u32*u32 multiplication overflows before widening.

state.value: Wraps around due to u32 overflow, e.g. 128 bytes instead of ~4GB

step_by_step_execution.description: bpf_map_area_alloc allocates the (small) wrapped-around size, e.g. 128 bytes. The allocation succeeds.

pcpu_freelist_populate is called with the original max_entries count and elem_size, iterating over max_entries elements at elem_size stride in the undersized buffer, writing far beyond the allocated region.

state.value: 128-byte buffer being accessed as if it were ~4GB

commit_message:
patch 8.2.4074: going over the end of NameBuff

Problem:    Going over the end of NameBuff.
Solution:   Check length when appending a space.
cve_desc: vim is vulnerable to Heap-based Buffer Overflow

code_snippets:
if (bt_help(wp->w_buffer)
#ifdef FEAT_QUICKFIX
		|| wp->w_p_pvw
#endif
		|| bufIsChanged(wp->w_buffer)
		|| wp->w_buffer->b_p_ro)
	    *(p + len++) = ' ';
vulnerabilities.description: When a terminal buffer is changed (bufIsChanged is true) but bt_terminal is also true, and bt_help is false, w_p_pvw is false, and b_p_ro is false, the null terminator in NameBuff is overwritten with a space character by `*(p + len++) = ' '`, but none of the subsequent vim_snprintf calls execute to restore a null terminator. This leaves the string without proper null termination, causing subsequent operations like mb_string2cells(p, -1) and screen_puts(p, ...) to read beyond the intended string boundary, potentially reading out-of-bounds memory from NameBuff.

step_by_step_execution.description: get_trans_bufname fills NameBuff with the buffer name; p is set to NameBuff and len is set to its string length.

The compound condition is checked: bt_help is false, w_p_pvw is false, bufIsChanged is true (making the overall OR true). The space character overwrites the null terminator at p[len], and len is incremented.

state.value: undefined/leftover data — no null terminator placed here

step_by_step_execution.description: bt_help(wp->w_buffer) is false, so the first vim_snprintf block is skipped.

state.value: still not null-terminated at position len

step_by_step_execution.description: wp->w_p_pvw is false (or FEAT_QUICKFIX not defined), so the Preview snprintf block is skipped.

state.value: still not null-terminated at position len

step_by_step_execution.description: bufIsChanged is true BUT bt_terminal is also true (FEAT_TERMINAL defined), so the [+] snprintf block is skipped due to the && !bt_terminal condition.

state.value: still not null-terminated at position len

step_by_step_execution.description: wp->w_buffer->b_p_ro is false, so the [RO] snprintf block is skipped. No null terminator has been restored.

state.value: NOT null-terminated at expected position; space at p[original_len], garbage/leftover beyond

step_by_step_execution.description: In the has_mbyte branch, mb_string2cells(p, -1) is called which scans until a null byte. Since the null terminator was destroyed, this reads past the intended string end in NameBuff, potentially reading out of bounds.

state.value: incorrect value, based on reading past intended string end

step_by_step_execution.description: screen_puts(p, row, wp->w_wincol, attr) is called with the non-null-terminated string, reading beyond intended bounds.

commit_message:
cgroup: verify that source is a string

The following sequence can be used to trigger a UAF:

    int fscontext_fd = fsopen("cgroup");
    int fd_null = open("/dev/null, O_RDONLY);
    int fsconfig(fscontext_fd, FSCONFIG_SET_FD, "source", fd_null);
    close_range(3, ~0U, 0);

The cgroup v1 specific fs parser expects a string for the "source"
parameter.  However, it is perfectly legitimate to e.g.  specify a file
descriptor for the "source" parameter.  The fs parser doesn't know what
a filesystem allows there.  So it's a bug to assume that "source" is
always of type fs_value_is_string when it can reasonably also be
fs_value_is_file.

This assumption in the cgroup code causes a UAF because struct
fs_parameter uses a union for the actual value.  Access to that union is
guarded by the param->type member.  Since the cgroup paramter parser
didn't check param->type but unconditionally moved param->string into
fc->source a close on the fscontext_fd would trigger a UAF during
put_fs_context() which frees fc->source thereby freeing the file stashed
in param->file causing a UAF during a close of the fd_null.

Fix this by verifying that param->type is actually a string and report
an error if not.

In follow up patches I'll add a new generic helper that can be used here
and by other filesystems instead of this error-prone copy-pasta fix.
But fixing it in here first makes backporting a it to stable a lot
easier.

Fixes: 8d2451f4994f ("cgroup1: switch to option-by-option parsing")
Reported-by: syzbot+283ce5a46486d6acdbaf@syzkaller.appspotmail.com
Cc: Christoph Hellwig <hch@lst.de>
Cc: Alexander Viro <viro@zeniv.linux.org.uk>
Cc: Dmitry Vyukov <dvyukov@google.com>
Cc: <stable@kernel.org>
Cc: syzkaller-bugs <syzkaller-bugs@googlegroups.com>
Signed-off-by: Christian Brauner <christian.brauner@ubuntu.com>
Signed-off-by: Linus Torvalds <torvalds@linux-foundation.org>
cve_desc: A use-after-free flaw was found in cgroup1_parse_param in kernel/cgroup/cgroup-v1.c in the Linux kernel's cgroup v1 parser. A local attacker with a user privilege could cause a privilege escalation by exploiting the fsconfig syscall parameter leading to a container breakout and a denial of service on the system.

commit_message:
tpm2: Initialize a whole OBJECT before using it

Initialize a whole OBJECT before using it. This is necessary since
an OBJECT may also be used as a HASH_OBJECT via the ANY_OBJECT
union and that HASH_OBJECT can leave bad size inidicators in TPM2B
buffer in the OBJECT. To get rid of this problem we reset the whole
OBJECT to 0 before using it. This is as if the memory for the
OBJECT was just initialized.

Signed-off-by: Stefan Berger <stefanb@linux.ibm.com>
cve_desc: A flaw was found in the libtpms code that may cause access beyond the boundary of internal buffers. The vulnerability is triggered by specially-crafted TPM2 command packets that then trigger the issue when the state of the TPM2's volatile state is written. The highest threat from this vulnerability is to system availability. This issue affects libtpms versions before 0.8.5, before 0.7.9 and before 0.6.6.

commit_message: commit bash-20190628 snapshot
cve_desc: An issue was discovered in disable_priv_mode in shell.c in GNU Bash through 5.0 patch 11. By default, if Bash is run with its effective UID not equal to its real UID, it will drop privileges by setting its effective UID to its real UID. However, it does so incorrectly. On Linux and other systems that support "saved UID" functionality, the saved UID is not dropped. An attacker with command execution in the shell can use "enable -f" for runtime loading of a new builtin, which can be a shared object that calls setuid() and therefore regains privileges. However, binaries running with an effective UID of 0 are unaffected.

vulnerabilities.description: If setuid() fails (and EXIT_ON_SETUID_FAILURE is not defined, or errno != EAGAIN), execution continues past the error. The function then unconditionally sets current_user.euid = current_user.uid, making the program believe privileges were successfully dropped when they were not. The program continues running with elevated (e.g., root) privileges while internal state indicates otherwise, leading to subsequent operations being performed with unintended elevated privileges.

step_by_step_execution.description: The function calls setuid(current_user.uid) to attempt to drop the effective UID to the real UID.
setuid() returns a value < 0, indicating failure. The error is logged via sys_error().
If EXIT_ON_SETUID_FAILURE is not defined, or errno is not EAGAIN, the function does NOT exit. Execution falls through past the if block.
setgid() is called, which may also fail. Even if it succeeds, the setuid failure already means we retain root UID privileges.
The function unconditionally updates current_user.euid and current_user.egid to the unprivileged values, misrepresenting the actual privilege state.

state.value: 1000 (set to uid, but actual euid is still 0)

commit_message:
i2c: ismt: prevent memory corruption in ismt_access()

The "data->block[0]" variable comes from the user and is a number
between 0-255.  It needs to be capped to prevent writing beyond the end
of dma_buffer[].

Fixes: 5e9a97b1f449 ("i2c: ismt: Adding support for I2C_SMBUS_BLOCK_PROC_CALL")
Reported-and-tested-by: Zheyu Ma <zheyuma97@gmail.com>
Signed-off-by: Dan Carpenter <dan.carpenter@oracle.com>
Reviewed-by: Mika Westerberg <mika.westerberg@linux.intel.com>
Signed-off-by: Linus Torvalds <torvalds@linux-foundation.org>
cve_desc: A buffer overflow vulnerability was found in the Linux kernel Intel’s iSMT SMBus host controller driver in the way it handled the I2C_SMBUS_BLOCK_PROC_CALL case (via the ioctl I2C_SMBUS) with malicious input data. This flaw could allow a local user to crash the system.

vulnerabilities.description: In the I2C_SMBUS_BLOCK_DATA write case, data->block[0] is used without validation. It can be up to 255, leading to a large memcpy (up to 255 bytes) into dma_buffer, which is derived from priv->buffer (a fixed-size buffer). This is a heap/stack buffer overflow. Note that the I2C_SMBUS_BLOCK_PROC_CALL case correctly checks `data->block[0] > I2C_SMBUS_BLOCK_MAX`, but the BLOCK_DATA write case does not.

step_by_step_execution.description: Function is called with size = I2C_SMBUS_BLOCK_DATA and read_write = I2C_SMBUS_WRITE.
dma_buffer is set to PTR_ALIGN(&priv->buffer[0], 16), pointing into a fixed-size buffer in the priv structure.
The code enters case I2C_SMBUS_BLOCK_DATA, write branch. data->block[0] is attacker-controlled and not validated. Suppose data->block[0] = 255.
dma_size is computed as data->block[0] + 1 = 256. Since dma_size is u8, this wraps to 0. However, the memcpy uses dma_size - 1 which would also wrap. Let's consider data->block[0] = 200 (no wrap).

memcpy(&dma_buffer[1], &data->block[1], 200) copies 200 bytes into dma_buffer starting at offset 1, writing 201 bytes total into the fixed-size priv->buffer which is typically much smaller (e.g., around 34 bytes for SMBus). This overflows the buffer.

state.value: 201 (1 byte for command + 200 bytes from memcpy)

commit_message: Merge pull request from GHSA-2qpg-f6wf-w984
cve_desc: PJSIP is a free and open source multimedia communication library written in C language implementing standard based protocols such as SIP, SDP, RTP, STUN, TURN, and ICE. In affected versions if the incoming STUN message contains an ERROR-CODE attribute, the header length is not checked before performing a subtraction operation, potentially resulting in an integer underflow scenario. This issue affects all users that use STUN. A malicious actor located within the victim’s network may forge and send a specially crafted UDP (STUN) message that could remotely execute arbitrary code on the victim’s machine. Users are advised to upgrade as soon as possible. There are no known workarounds.

vulnerabilities.description: If the attribute header length field (read from untrusted network data) is less than 4, the subtraction `attr->hdr.length - 4` results in an integer underflow. If the length type is unsigned, this wraps to a very large value; if signed, it becomes negative but may be implicitly converted to a large unsigned value during the subsequent `pj_strdup` memory allocation and copy, leading to a heap buffer over-read or heap overflow.

step_by_step_execution.description: The attribute header is parsed from the raw buffer via GETATTRHDR, populating attr->hdr.length from attacker-controlled network data.
The error code is computed from buf[6] and buf[7]. No issue here in isolation.
value.ptr is set to buf + ATTR_HDR_LEN + 4, pointing into the message buffer.
value.slen is computed as attr->hdr.length - 4. With attr->hdr.length < 4, this underflows.

state.value: negative or wrapped-around large value, e.g. if length=0 then slen = -4 (signed) or 0xFFFFFFFC (unsigned)

step_by_step_execution.description: pj_strdup is called with the corrupted value.slen, causing it to allocate and copy a huge or incorrect amount of memory, resulting in a heap buffer over-read from the source and potential heap overflow in the destination.

state.value: very large or negative, passed to pj_strdup as the length to copy

Reported-by: syzbot+283ce5a46486d6acdbaf@syzkaller.appspotmail.com

Cc: <stable@kernel.org>
Cc: syzkaller-bugs <syzkaller-bugs@googlegroups.com>
Signed-off-by: Christian Brauner <christian.brauner@ubuntu.com>
Signed-off-by: Linus Torvalds <torvalds@linux-foundation.org>
cve_desc: A use-after-free flaw was found in cgroup1_parse_param in kernel/cgroup/cgroup-v1.c in the Linux kernel's cgroup v1 parser. A local attacker with a user privilege could cause a privilege escalation by exploiting the fsconfig syscall parameter leading to a container breakout and a denial of service on the system.

commit_message:
glob: fix heap buffer overflow

* lib/glob.c (glob): Fix off-by-one error introduced into
glibc in commit dd7d45e838a42b0ed470c44b55901ea98d0c2bab
dated 1997-10-29 20:33:40.  Problem reported by Tim Rühsen in:
https://sourceware.org/bugzilla/show_bug.cgi?id=22320
Fix suggested by Bruno Haible.
cve_desc: The GNU C Library (aka glibc or libc6) before 2.27 contains an off-by-one error leading to a heap-based buffer overflow in the glob function in glob.c, related to the processing of home directories using the ~ operator followed by a long string.

vulnerabilities.description: Off-by-one heap buffer overflow in ~username expansion. When `end_name` is not NULL and `unescape` is NULL, the buffer `newp` is allocated with size `end_name - dirname`, but `mempcpy` copies `end_name - dirname` bytes starting from `dirname + 1` (filling the entire buffer), then writes a null terminator one byte past the end of the allocated buffer.

step_by_step_execution.description: The pattern contains a tilde username with a slash, e.g. `~user/file`. The `dirname` is set to `~user/file` (or a copy up to the last `/`), and `filename` points past the last `/`.
Code enters the `~` expansion branch since `dirname[0] == '~'`. Since `dirname[1]` is not `\0` or `/`, it enters the `else` branch for ~NAME lookup.

`end_name = strchr(dirname, '/')` finds the slash. For example, if dirname is `~user`, end_name could be NULL, but if dirname contains a `/`, end_name points to it. In the case where dirname is e.g. `~user` from a pattern like `~user/file`, end_name is NULL. But consider a pattern `~user/sub/file` where dirname becomes `~user/sub`. Then `end_name` points to the first `/`.

state.value: pointer to '/' in dirname, at position 5

step_by_step_execution.description: `unescape` is NULL because GLOB_NOESCAPE is set or no backslashes exist in dirname. Code enters the allocation block since `end_name != NULL`.
Buffer `newp` is allocated with `malloc(end_name - dirname)` = `malloc(5)`. This is intended to hold the username "user" (4 chars) plus a null terminator, which would require exactly 5 bytes.

`mempcpy(newp, dirname + 1, end_name - dirname)` copies 5 bytes from `dirname + 1` (i.e. "user/") into the 5-byte buffer, completely filling it. The returned pointer points one byte past the buffer end.

state.value: newp + 5 (one past end of allocated buffer)

step_by_step_execution.description: The null terminator `'\0'` is written at `newp + 5`, which is one byte past the end of the 5-byte allocated buffer, causing a heap buffer overflow.

commit_message:
nfc: nfcmrvl: main: reorder destructive operations in nfcmrvl_nci_unregister_dev to avoid bugs

There are destructive operations such as nfcmrvl_fw_dnld_abort and
gpio_free in nfcmrvl_nci_unregister_dev. The resources such as firmware,
gpio and so on could be destructed while the upper layer functions such as
nfcmrvl_fw_dnld_start and nfcmrvl_nci_recv_frame is executing, which leads
to double-free, use-after-free and null-ptr-deref bugs.

There are three situations that could lead to double-free bugs.

The first situation is shown below:

   (Thread 1)                 |      (Thread 2)
nfcmrvl_fw_dnld_start         |
 ...                          |  nfcmrvl_nci_unregister_dev
 release_firmware()           |   nfcmrvl_fw_dnld_abort
  kfree(fw) //(1)             |    fw_dnld_over
                              |     release_firmware
  ...                         |      kfree(fw) //(2)
                              |     ...

The second situation is shown below:

   (Thread 1)                 |      (Thread 2)
nfcmrvl_fw_dnld_start         |
 ...                          |
 mod_timer                    |
 (wait a time)                |
 fw_dnld_timeout              |  nfcmrvl_nci_unregister_dev
   fw_dnld_over               |   nfcmrvl_fw_dnld_abort
    release_firmware          |    fw_dnld_over
     kfree(fw) //(1)          |     release_firmware
     ...                      |      kfree(fw) //(2)

The third situation is shown below:

       (Thread 1)               |       (Thread 2)
nfcmrvl_nci_recv_frame          |
 if(..->fw_download_in_progress)|
  nfcmrvl_fw_dnld_recv_frame    |
   queue_work                   |
                                |
fw_dnld_rx_work                 | nfcmrvl_nci_unregister_dev
 fw_dnld_over                   |  nfcmrvl_fw_dnld_abort
  release_firmware              |   fw_dnld_over
   kfree(fw) //(1)              |    release_firmware
                                |     kfree(fw) //(2)

The firmware struct is deallocated in position (1) and deallocated
in position (2) again.

The crash trace triggered by POC is like below:

BUG: KASAN: double-free or invalid-free in fw_dnld_over
Call Trace:
  kfree
  fw_dnld_over
  nfcmrvl_nci_unregister_dev
  nci_uart_tty_close
  tty_ldisc_kill
  tty_ldisc_hangup
  __tty_hangup.part.0
  tty_release
  ...

What's more, there are also use-after-free and null-ptr-deref bugs
in nfcmrvl_fw_dnld_start. If we deallocate firmware struct, gpio or
set null to the members of priv->fw_dnld in nfcmrvl_nci_unregister_dev,
then, we dereference firmware, gpio or the members of priv->fw_dnld in
nfcmrvl_fw_dnld_start, the UAF or NPD bugs will happen.

This patch reorders destructive operations after nci_unregister_device
in order to synchronize between cleanup routine and firmware download
routine.

The nci_unregister_device is well synchronized. If the device is
detaching, the firmware download routine will goto error. If firmware
download routine is executing, nci_unregister_device will wait until
firmware download routine is finished.

Fixes: 3194c6870158 ("NFC: nfcmrvl: add firmware download support")
Signed-off-by: Duoming Zhou <duoming@zju.edu.cn>
Signed-off-by: David S. Miller <davem@davemloft.net>
cve_desc: A flaw in Linux Kernel found in nfcmrvl_nci_unregister_dev() in drivers/nfc/nfcmrvl/main.c can lead to use after free both read or write when non synchronized between cleanup routine and firmware download routine.

vulnerabilities.description: nci_unregister_device(ndev) frees the nfc_dev associated with the nci device. After this call, accessing priv->ndev->nfc_dev->fw_download_in_progress dereferences freed memory (use-after-free).

step_by_step_execution.description: ndev is assigned from priv->ndev
nci_unregister_device(ndev) is called, which internally calls nfc_unregister_device and frees ndev->nfc_dev
The code dereferences priv->ndev->nfc_dev->fw_download_in_progress, accessing the already-freed nfc_dev structure

commit_message:
Sanitize inputs to bookmark-cmd (#591)

Newsbeuter didn't properly shell-escape the arguments passed to
bookmarking command, which allows a remote attacker to perform remote
code execution by crafting an RSS item whose title and/or URL contain
something interpretable by the shell (most notably subshell
invocations.)

This has been reported by Jeriko One <jeriko.one@gmx.us>, complete with
PoC and a patch.

This vulnerability was assigned CVE-2017-12904.
cve_desc: Improper Neutralization of Special Elements used in an OS Command in bookmarking function of Newsbeuter versions 0.7 through 2.9 allows remote attackers to perform user-assisted code execution by crafting an RSS item that includes shell code in its title and/or URL.

commit_message:
ax25: Fix UAF bugs in ax25 timers

There are race conditions that may lead to UAF bugs in
ax25_heartbeat_expiry(), ax25_t1timer_expiry(), ax25_t2timer_expiry(),
ax25_t3timer_expiry() and ax25_idletimer_expiry(), when we call
ax25_release() to deallocate ax25_dev.

One of the UAF bugs caused by ax25_release() is shown below:

      (Thread 1)                    |      (Thread 2)
ax25_dev_device_up() //(1)          |
...                                 | ax25_kill_by_device()
ax25_bind()          //(2)          |
ax25_connect()                      | ...
 ax25_std_establish_data_link()     |
  ax25_start_t1timer()              | ax25_dev_device_down() //(3)
   mod_timer(&ax25->t1timer,..)     |
                                    | ax25_release()
   (wait a time)                    |  ...
                                    |  ax25_dev_put(ax25_dev) //(4)FREE
   ax25_t1timer_expiry()            |
    ax25->ax25_dev->values[..] //USE|  ...
     ...                            |

We increase the refcount of ax25_dev in position (1) and (2), and
decrease the refcount of ax25_dev in position (3) and (4).
The ax25_dev will be freed in position (4) and be used in
ax25_t1timer_expiry().

The fail log is shown below:
==============================================================

[  106.116942] BUG: KASAN: use-after-free in ax25_t1timer_expiry+0x1c/0x60
[  106.116942] Read of size 8 at addr ffff88800bda9028 by task swapper/0/0
[  106.116942] CPU: 0 PID: 0 Comm: swapper/0 Not tainted 5.17.0-06123-g0905eec574
[  106.116942] Hardware name: QEMU Standard PC (i440FX + PIIX, 1996), BIOS rel-14
[  106.116942] Call Trace:
...
[  106.116942]  ax25_t1timer_expiry+0x1c/0x60
[  106.116942]  call_timer_fn+0x122/0x3d0
[  106.116942]  __run_timers.part.0+0x3f6/0x520
[  106.116942]  run_timer_softirq+0x4f/0xb0
[  106.116942]  __do_softirq+0x1c2/0x651
...

This patch adds del_timer_sync() in ax25_release(), which could ensure
that all timers stop before we deallocate ax25_dev.

Signed-off-by: Duoming Zhou <duoming@zju.edu.cn>
Signed-off-by: Paolo Abeni <pabeni@redhat.com>
cve_desc: A NULL pointer dereference flaw was found in the Linux kernel’s Amateur Radio AX.25 protocol functionality in the way a user connects with the protocol. This flaw allows a local user to crash the system.

vulnerabilities.description: NULL pointer dereference when ax25->ax25_dev is NULL and the AX.25 state is AX25_STATE_3 or AX25_STATE_4. The code dereferences ax25_dev without a NULL check in this path, even though ax25_dev is checked for NULL later in the function (indicating it can legitimately be NULL).

step_by_step_execution.description: The function retrieves the sock pointer and checks it is non-NULL.
ax25 and ax25_dev are assigned from the socket's AX.25 control block.
The code checks sk->sk_type == SOCK_SEQPACKET, and enters the switch on ax25->state.
In the AX25_STATE_3/AX25_STATE_4 case, the code dereferences ax25->ax25_dev to access values[AX25_VALUES_PROTOCOL] without checking if ax25_dev is NULL.

commit_message:
nfc: st21nfca: Fix potential buffer overflows in EVT_TRANSACTION

It appears that there are some buffer overflows in EVT_TRANSACTION.
This happens because the length parameters that are passed to memcpy
come directly from skb->data and are not guarded in any way.

Signed-off-by: Jordy Zomer <jordy@pwning.systems>
Reviewed-by: Krzysztof Kozlowski <krzysztof.kozlowski@canonical.com>
Signed-off-by: David S. Miller <davem@davemloft.net>
cve_desc: st21nfca_connectivity_event_received in drivers/nfc/st21nfca/se.c in the Linux kernel through 5.16.12 has EVT_TRANSACTION buffer overflows because of untrusted length parameters.

vulnerabilities.description: The initial validation uses logical AND (&&) instead of logical OR (||). This means if skb->len is very small (e.g., 2 bytes) but skb->data[0] happens to equal NFC_EVT_TRANSACTION_AID_TAG, the check passes, allowing subsequent out-of-bounds reads on skb->data (e.g., skb->data[1], skb->data[2], skb->data[transaction->aid_len + 2], etc.) that exceed the actual buffer length.

step_by_step_execution.description: Function is called with event == ST21NFCA_EVT_TRANSACTION and an skb where skb->len is small (e.g., 2) but skb->data[0] == NFC_EVT_TRANSACTION_AID_TAG.

The guard check evaluates: (skb->len < NFC_MIN_AID_LENGTH + 2) is true (2 < 7), AND (skb->data[0] != NFC_EVT_TRANSACTION_AID_TAG) is false. Because of &&, the overall condition is false, so the early return is NOT taken.
devm_kzalloc allocates skb->len - 2 = 0 bytes for the transaction struct. This allocation may succeed returning a zero-size (or minimum-size) buffer.
transaction->aid_len is set from skb->data[1], which could be any value (e.g., 16, which passes the sizeof(transaction->aid) check).
memcpy reads transaction->aid_len bytes starting from skb->data[2], but skb->len is only 2, so this reads beyond the skb data buffer.

state.value: up to 16 bytes past end of skb data

vulnerabilities.description: After copying the AID, the code accesses skb->data[transaction->aid_len + 2] and skb->data[transaction->aid_len + 3] without verifying that (transaction->aid_len + 4) <= skb->len. Even with a correctly-sized skb passing the initial check, if aid_len is large enough relative to skb->len, these accesses can read beyond the skb buffer.

step_by_step_execution.description: Function is called with event == ST21NFCA_EVT_TRANSACTION, skb->len = 8 (passes the >= NFC_MIN_AID_LENGTH+2 check), skb->data[0] = 0x81, skb->data[1] = 5 (aid_len).
The initial check passes (skb->len >= NFC_MIN_AID_LENGTH + 2). transaction is allocated with skb->len - 2 = 6 bytes.
transaction->aid_len = 5, which is <= sizeof(transaction->aid), so the check passes. memcpy copies 5 bytes from skb->data[2..6], valid since skb->len=8.
Code accesses skb->data[5 + 2] = skb->data[7] (valid, last byte) and skb->data[5 + 3] = skb->data[8], which is one byte beyond the skb buffer (skb->len=8, valid indices 0-7).

commit_message:
jp2_dec: fix `numchans` mixup

When iterating over `dec->cdef->data.cdef.ents`, we need to use its
`numchans` variable, not the one in `jp2_dec_t`.

Fixes CVE-2018-19543
Fixes CVE-2017-9782

Closes https://github.com/jasper-maint/jasper/issues/13
Closes https://github.com/jasper-maint/jasper/issues/18
Closes https://github.com/mdadams/jasper/issues/140
Closes https://github.com/mdadams/jasper/issues/182
cve_desc: JasPer 2.0.12 allows remote attackers to cause a denial of service (heap-based buffer over-read and application crash) via a crafted image, related to the jp2_decode function in libjasper/jp2/jp2_dec.c.

vulnerabilities.description: When a CMAP box is present but no CDEF box was found, dec->cdef is NULL. The code dereferences dec->cdef unconditionally inside the `dec->cmap` branch to assign `cdefd`, leading to a null pointer dereference.

step_by_step_execution.description: The decoder starts and dec->cdef is initialized to 0 (NULL) by jp2_dec_create.
During box parsing, a CMAP box and a PCLR box are encountered and stored, but no CDEF box is encountered.
The check `if (dec->cmap && !dec->pclr)` is false (both present), and `if (!dec->cmap && dec->pclr)` is also false. Processing continues normally.
The code enters the `else` branch (dec->cmap is non-NULL) and executes `cdefd = &dec->cdef->data.cdef;`, dereferencing the NULL pointer dec->cdef.

vulnerabilities.description: If jas_alloc2 returns NULL (allocation failure), the code proceeds to use `lutents[i]` without any NULL check, resulting in a null pointer dereference.

step_by_step_execution.description: A CMAP box with a palette-mapped entry is present, so the code enters the JP2_CMAP_PALETTE branch.
jas_alloc2 is called to allocate memory for lutents but returns NULL due to memory exhaustion.
The code immediately enters the for loop and writes to lutents[i], dereferencing the NULL pointer.

commit_message:
rgw: reject control characters in response-header actions

S3 GetObject permits overriding response header values, but those inputs
need to be validated to insure only characters that are valid in an HTTP
header value are present.

Credit: Initial vulnerability discovery by William Bowling (@wcbowling)
Credit: Further vulnerability discovery by Robin H. Johnson <rjohnson@digitalocean.com>
Signed-off-by: Robin H. Johnson <rjohnson@digitalocean.com>
cve_desc: A flaw was found in the Ceph Object Gateway, where it supports request sent by an anonymous user in Amazon S3. This flaw could lead to potential XSS attacks due to the lack of proper neutralization of untrusted input.

Signed-off-by: Duoming Zhou <duoming@zju.edu.cn>
Signed-off-by: Paolo Abeni <pabeni@redhat.com>
cve_desc: A NULL pointer dereference flaw was found in the Linux kernel’s Amateur Radio AX.25 protocol functionality in the way a user connects with the protocol. This flaw allows a local user to crash the system.

vulnerabilities.description: NULL pointer dereference when ax25->ax25_dev is NULL. The variable ax25_dev is assigned from ax25->ax25_dev without a NULL check. In the AX25_STATE_3/AX25_STATE_4 case, ax25->ax25_dev->values[AX25_VALUES_PROTOCOL] is dereferenced unconditionally, but the NULL check for ax25_dev only occurs later (after the switch statement).

step_by_step_execution.description: The function retrieves the sock pointer and checks it is non-NULL.
ax25 and ax25_dev are retrieved from the socket. ax25_dev could be NULL if no device is associated.
sk->sk_type is SOCK_SEQPACKET, so we enter the SEQPACKET branch.
ax25->state is AX25_STATE_3 or AX25_STATE_4, entering that case branch.
The code dereferences ax25->ax25_dev->values[AX25_VALUES_PROTOCOL] in a switch statement without checking if ax25_dev is NULL, causing a NULL pointer dereference.

commit_message:
patch 8.2.4436: crash with weird 'vartabstop' value

Problem:    Crash with weird 'vartabstop' value.
Solution:   Check for running into the end of the line.

commit_message:
KVM: PPC: Book3S: Fix H_RTAS rets buffer overflow

The kvmppc_rtas_hcall() sets the host rtas_args.rets pointer based on
the rtas_args.nargs that was provided by the guest. That guest nargs
value is not range checked, so the guest can cause the host rets pointer
to be pointed outside the args array. The individual rtas function
handlers check the nargs and nrets values to ensure they are correct,
but if they are not, the handlers store a -3 (0xfffffffd) failure
indication in rets[0] which corrupts host memory.

Fix this by testing up front whether the guest supplied nargs and nret
would exceed the array size, and fail the hcall directly without storing
a failure indication to rets[0].

Also expand on a comment about why we kill the guest and try not to
return errors directly if we have a valid rets[0] pointer.

Fixes: 8e591cb72047 ("KVM: PPC: Book3S: Add infrastructure to implement kernel-side RTAS calls")
Cc: stable@vger.kernel.org # v3.10+
Reported-by: Alexey Kardashevskiy <aik@ozlabs.ru>
Signed-off-by: Nicholas Piggin <npiggin@gmail.com>
Signed-off-by: Michael Ellerman <mpe@ellerman.id.au>
cve_desc: arch/powerpc/kvm/book3s_rtas.c in the Linux kernel through 5.13.5 on the powerpc platform allows KVM guest OS users to cause host OS memory corruption via rtas_args.nargs, aka CID-f62f3c20647e.

commit_message: Fix https://github.com/Exiv2/exiv2/issues/55
cve_desc: In Exiv2 0.26, there is a heap-based buffer over-read in the Exiv2::Image::byteSwap4 function in image.cpp. Remote attackers can exploit this vulnerability to disclose memory data or cause a denial of service via a crafted TIFF file.

code_snippets: DataBuf  buf(size*count + pad+20);  // allocate a buffer

for ( size_t k = 0 ; k < kount ; k++ ) {
                            out << sp << byteSwap2(buf,k*size,bSwap);

for ( size_t k = 0 ; k < kount ; k++ ) {
                            out << sp << byteSwap4(buf,k*size,bSwap);
vulnerabilities.description: Integer overflow in `size*count` when computing the buffer allocation size. Since `size` (up to 8) and `count` are both derived from attacker-controlled file data, their product can overflow a 32-bit integer, resulting in a much smaller buffer being allocated. Subsequent reads and loop accesses using the non-overflowed `kount*size` indices access memory beyond the undersized buffer, causing heap buffer over-read or over-write.

code_snippets:
uint32_t a = byteSwap4(buf,k*size+0,bSwap);
                            uint32_t b = byteSwap4(buf,k*size+4,bSwap);

step_by_step_execution.description: The function reads `type` and `count` from the IFD entry (attacker-controlled file data). Suppose type is a rational type (is8ByteType), so size=8, and count=0x60000000.
kount is computed: since count > 5 and it's not a string/XMP/ICC type, kount = 5.
Buffer allocation: size*count = 8 * 0x60000000 = 0x300000000, which overflows uint32_t to 0x00000000. pad=0. So DataBuf is allocated with size 0 + 0 + 20 = 20 bytes.
bOffsetIsPointer = (count*size > 4) evaluates to (0 > 4) = false due to the same overflow, so no large read into the buffer occurs. Only the 4 bytes from dir[8:11] are copied.

state.value: 4 bytes copied from dir, rest uninitialized, total 20 bytes

step_by_step_execution.description: In the isRationalType print loop, the code iterates k from 0 to 4 (kount=5), accessing buf at offsets k*8 and k*8+4. For k=2, offset is 16 and 20. For k=3, offsets are 24 and 28. For k=4, offsets are 32 and 36. These exceed the 20-byte buffer, causing heap buffer over-read.

vulnerabilities.description: When isPrintXMP returns true, `kount = count` and `buf.pData_[count]=0` is executed. If `size*count` overflows during allocation (e.g., count=0xFFFFFFFF, size=1 yields allocation of ~20 bytes), writing at index `count` is a massive out-of-bounds heap write.

step_by_step_execution.description: An IFD entry has a tag matching XMP (isPrintXMP returns true), type is string (size=1, pad=1), and count is 0xFFFFFFFF (attacker-controlled).
Buffer allocation: size*count + pad + 20 = 1*0xFFFFFFFF + 1 + 20 = 0xFFFFFFFF + 21 = 20 (overflow wraps around). A 20-byte buffer is allocated.
After the loop, `buf.pData_[count] = 0` writes a zero byte at offset 0xFFFFFFFF from the buffer base, which is a massive out-of-bounds heap write.

commit_message:
Fix: Worked around buffer overflows in URL auth's cURL interface

This is only a workaround that keeps compatibility with 2.4.x mainline.
A real fix has been implemented in 2.5.x (master).
cve_desc: A buffer overflow was discovered in the URL-authentication backend of the Icecast before 2.4.4. If the backend is enabled, then any malicious HTTP client can send a request for that specific resource including a crafted header, leading to denial of service and potentially remote code execution.

commit_message:
i2c: ismt: prevent memory corruption in ismt_access()

The "data->block[0]" variable comes from the user and is a number
between 0-255.  It needs to be capped to prevent writing beyond the end
of dma_buffer[].

Fixes: 5e9a97b1f449 ("i2c: ismt: Adding support for I2C_SMBUS_BLOCK_PROC_CALL")
Reported-and-tested-by: Zheyu Ma <zheyuma97@gmail.com>
Signed-off-by: Dan Carpenter <dan.carpenter@oracle.com>
Reviewed-by: Mika Westerberg <mika.westerberg@linux.intel.com>
Signed-off-by: Linus Torvalds <torvalds@linux-foundation.org>
cve_desc: A buffer overflow vulnerability was found in the Linux kernel Intel’s iSMT SMBus host controller driver in the way it handled the I2C_SMBUS_BLOCK_PROC_CALL case (via the ioctl I2C_SMBUS) with malicious input data. This flaw could allow a local user to crash the system.

vulnerabilities.description: In the I2C_SMBUS_BLOCK_DATA write case, data->block[0] is not validated. Since dma_size is a u8, if data->block[0] is 255, then dma_size overflows to 0. The subsequent memcpy uses (dma_size - 1) as the size, which underflows to (size_t)-1 (a huge value), causing a massive buffer over-read/over-write. Even without the wraparound, values of data->block[0] exceeding the DMA buffer size (e.g., >32) cause an out-of-bounds write into dma_buffer.

step_by_step_execution.description: Function is called with size=I2C_SMBUS_BLOCK_DATA and read_write=I2C_SMBUS_WRITE. data->block[0] is attacker-controlled (e.g., 255).
dma_size is computed as data->block[0] + 1. Since dma_size is u8, 255 + 1 overflows to 0.

memcpy is called with size (dma_size - 1). dma_size is 0 (u8), but in the expression (dma_size - 1), it is promoted to int giving -1, then implicitly converted to size_t, yielding a very large value (e.g., 0xFFFFFFFFFFFFFFFF on 64-bit).
memcpy(&dma_buffer[1], &data->block[1], (size_t)-1) performs a massive out-of-bounds read from data->block and out-of-bounds write into dma_buffer, corrupting kernel memory.

vulnerabilities.description: In the I2C_SMBUS_BLOCK_PROC_CALL case, data->block[0] is used directly as the memcpy size without any bounds validation. If data->block[0] exceeds the size of the DMA buffer (priv->buffer), this causes an out-of-bounds write.

step_by_step_execution.description: Function is called with size=I2C_SMBUS_BLOCK_PROC_CALL. data->block[0] is attacker-controlled (e.g., 255).
memcpy is called to copy data->block[0] (255) bytes from &data->block[1] into &dma_buffer[1].
255 bytes are written starting at dma_buffer[1], overflowing the priv->buffer and corrupting adjacent kernel memory.

commit_message:
Sanitize inputs to bookmark-cmd (#591)

Newsbeuter didn't properly shell-escape the arguments passed to
bookmarking command, which allows a remote attacker to perform remote
code execution by crafting an RSS item whose title and/or URL contain
something interpretable by the shell (most notably subshell
invocations.)

This has been reported by Jeriko One <jeriko.one@gmx.us>, complete with
PoC and a patch.

This vulnerability was assigned CVE-2017-12904.
cve_desc: Improper Neutralization of Special Elements used in an OS Command in bookmarking function of Newsbeuter versions 0.7 through 2.9 allows remote attackers to perform user-assisted code execution by crafting an RSS item that includes shell code in its title and/or URL.

code_snippets:
std::string cmdline = strprintf::fmt("%s '%s' %s %s %s",
		                                       bookmark_cmd,
		                                       utils::replace_all(url,"'", "%27"),
		                                       quote_empty(stfl::quote(title)),
		                                       quote_empty(stfl::quote(description)),
		                                       quote_empty(stfl::quote(feed_title)));
vulnerabilities.description: The title, description, and feed_title parameters are passed into a shell command line without proper shell escaping. While the URL is single-quoted with internal single quotes replaced, the other three parameters are only processed by stfl::quote (an STFL UI quoting function, not a shell quoting function) and quote_empty, then interpolated directly into the command string via %s without shell quoting. An attacker who controls an RSS feed's title, item title, or description can inject arbitrary shell commands.

step_by_step_execution.description: The function receives parameters including title, description, and feed_title, which originate from RSS feed content controlled by an attacker.
bookmark_cmd is read from configuration and is non-empty, so the main branch is entered.

The command line is constructed. The title goes through stfl::quote (STFL UI quoting, not shell quoting) and quote_empty, but is interpolated into the shell command string without shell-safe quoting (no single quotes wrapping it in the format string).
The constructed command line is executed via /bin/sh -c or utils::run_interactively, which interprets the injected shell metacharacters, executing the attacker's commands.

state.value: the cmdline string containing injected shell commands

commit_message:
net/mlx5: prevent memory leak in mlx5_fpga_conn_create_cq

In mlx5_fpga_conn_create_cq if mlx5_vector2eqn fails the allocated
memory should be released.

Fixes: 537a50574175 ("net/mlx5: FPGA, Add high-speed connection routines")
Signed-off-by: Navid Emamdoost <navid.emamdoost@gmail.com>
Signed-off-by: Saeed Mahameed <saeedm@mellanox.com>
cve_desc: A memory leak in the mlx5_fpga_conn_create_cq() function in drivers/net/ethernet/mellanox/mlx5/core/fpga/conn.c in the Linux kernel before 5.3.11 allows attackers to cause a denial of service (memory consumption) by triggering mlx5_vector2eqn() failures, aka CID-c8c2a057fdc7.

commit_message: Merge pull request from GHSA-2qpg-f6wf-w984
cve_desc: PJSIP is a free and open source multimedia communication library written in C language implementing standard based protocols such as SIP, SDP, RTP, STUN, TURN, and ICE. In affected versions if the incoming STUN message contains an ERROR-CODE attribute, the header length is not checked before performing a subtraction operation, potentially resulting in an integer underflow scenario. This issue affects all users that use STUN. A malicious actor located within the victim’s network may forge and send a specially crafted UDP (STUN) message that could remotely execute arbitrary code on the victim’s machine. Users are advised to upgrade as soon as possible. There are no known workarounds.

vulnerabilities.description: The attribute length field (attr->hdr.length) is read directly from the network buffer via GETATTRHDR and used to compute value.slen without any upper-bound validation against the actual available buffer size. An attacker can craft a STUN message with a very large length value in the attribute header, causing pj_strdup to read far beyond the actual buffer, resulting in a heap/stack buffer over-read.

step_by_step_execution.description: GETATTRHDR reads the attribute header from buf, including the attacker-controlled length field into attr->hdr.length.
value.ptr is set to buf + ATTR_HDR_LEN + 4 (i.e., buf + 8), pointing into the buffer.
value.slen is computed as attr->hdr.length - 4. With a large hdr.length, this becomes a very large positive value.
The check 'if (value.slen < 0)' does not trigger since 65531 is positive.
pj_strdup copies 65531 bytes starting from value.ptr (buf + 8). The actual attribute data in the buffer is likely much smaller, so this reads far out of bounds of the legitimate buffer.

commit_message:
af_key: Do not call xfrm_probe_algs in parallel

When namespace support was added to xfrm/afkey, it caused the
previously single-threaded call to xfrm_probe_algs to become
multi-threaded.  This is buggy and needs to be fixed with a mutex.

Reported-by: Abhishek Shah <abhishek.shah@columbia.edu>
Fixes: 283bc9f35bbb ("xfrm: Namespacify xfrm state/policy locks")
Signed-off-by: Herbert Xu <herbert@gondor.apana.org.au>
Signed-off-by: Steffen Klassert <steffen.klassert@secunet.com>
cve_desc: A race condition was found in the Linux kernel's IP framework for transforming packets (XFRM subsystem) when multiple calls to xfrm_probe_algs occurred simultaneously. This flaw could allow a local attacker to potentially trigger an out-of-bounds write or leak kernel heap memory by performing an out-of-bounds read and copying it into a socket.

commit_message: Fix bug #79037 (global buffer-overflow in `mbfl_filt_conv_big5_wchar`)
cve_desc: When using certain mbstring functions to convert multibyte encodings, in PHP versions 7.2.x below 7.2.27, 7.3.x below 7.3.14 and 7.4.x below 7.4.2 it is possible to supply data that will cause function mbfl_filt_conv_big5_wchar to read past the allocated buffer. This may lead to information disclosure or crash.

vulnerabilities.description: If the loop completes without finding a matching range (no `break`), `k` equals `sizeof(cp950_pua_tbl)/(sizeof(unsigned short)*4)`, which is one past the last valid index. The code then accesses `cp950_pua_tbl[k][2]`, `cp950_pua_tbl[k][0]`, and potentially `cp950_pua_tbl[k][3]` out of bounds.

step_by_step_execution.description: Function is called with encoding cp950 and filter->status == 0. A lead byte c1 in range (0x81..0x8d) is received, setting filter->status=1 and filter->cache=c1.
Function is called again with a trail byte c (e.g. 0x41, which is > 0x39 and < 0x7f). filter->status is 1, so we enter case 1.
w is computed as (c1-0xa1)*157 + (c-0x40) = (0x81-0xa1)*157 + 1 = negative value. big5_ucs_table lookup is skipped (w < 0), so w is set to 0.
Since encoding is cp950 and w <= 0, and c1=0x81 is in range [0x81,0x8d] and c=0x41 is in range (0x39,0x7f), we enter the PUA block. c2 = 0x81 << 8 | 0x41 = 0x8141.
The for loop iterates through all entries of cp950_pua_tbl looking for c2 in a range. If c2=0x8141 does not match any [cp950_pua_tbl[k][2], cp950_pua_tbl[k][3]] range, the loop ends without breaking.

state.value: sizeof(cp950_pua_tbl)/(sizeof(unsigned short)*4) (one past last valid index)

step_by_step_execution.description: Code accesses cp950_pua_tbl[k][2] with k out of bounds, reading memory beyond the array.

commit_message:
cgroup-v1: Require capabilities to set release_agent

The cgroup release_agent is called with call_usermodehelper.  The function
call_usermodehelper starts the release_agent with a full set fo capabilities.
Therefore require capabilities when setting the release_agaent.

Reported-by: Tabitha Sable <tabitha.c.sable@gmail.com>
Tested-by: Tabitha Sable <tabitha.c.sable@gmail.com>
Fixes: 81a6a5cdd2c5 ("Task Control Groups: automatic userspace notification of idle cgroups")
Cc: stable@vger.kernel.org # v2.6.24+
Signed-off-by: "Eric W. Biederman" <ebiederm@xmission.com>
Signed-off-by: Tejun Heo <tj@kernel.org>
cve_desc: A vulnerability was found in the Linux kernel’s cgroup_release_agent_write in the kernel/cgroup/cgroup-v1.c function. This flaw, under certain circumstances, allows the use of the cgroups v1 release_agent feature to escalate privileges and bypass the namespace isolation unexpectedly.

commit_message: Fix https://github.com/Exiv2/exiv2/issues/55
cve_desc: In Exiv2 0.26, there is a heap-based buffer over-read in the Exiv2::Image::byteSwap4 function in image.cpp. Remote attackers can exploit this vulnerability to disclose memory data or cause a denial of service via a crafted TIFF file.

code_snippets:
uint32_t size   = isStringType(type) ? 1
                                : is2ByteType(type)  ? 2
                                : is4ByteType(type)  ? 4
                                : is8ByteType(type)  ? 8
                                : 1
                                ;
vulnerabilities.description: Integer overflow in `size*count` (both uint32_t) before the cast to `long long` causes a small buffer to be allocated. Later, `buf.pData_[count]=0` writes at offset `count` (which can be very large) into the small buffer, causing a heap-based buffer overflow. For example, with `size=2` and `count=0x80000001`, the uint32_t product overflows to 2, `allocate` becomes ~22, a ~22-byte buffer is allocated, but `buf.pData_[0x80000001]=0` writes far out of bounds.

step_by_step_execution.description: The function reads the IFD entry's tag, type, count, and offset from the 12-byte directory entry.

state.value: Value that makes isPrintXMP(tag,option) return true (e.g. XMP tag)
A 2-byte type (e.g. type=3, short), so is2ByteType returns true

step_by_step_execution.description: size and pad are computed based on the type. Since it's a 2-byte type, size=2, pad=0.
kount is computed. Since isPrintXMP is true, kount = count.

The product `size*count` is computed as uint32_t: 2 * 0x80000001 = 0x100000002, which overflows uint32_t to 0x00000002. Then allocate = (long long)(0x00000002 + 0 + 20) = 22. This passes the io.size() check.
A DataBuf of 22 bytes is allocated.
bOffsetIsPointer = (count*size > 4) = (2 > 4) = false, so no read into the buffer beyond the initial 4-byte copy from dir.

After the print loop, isPrintXMP(tag,option) is true, so the code executes `buf.pData_[count]=0`, which writes a zero byte at offset 0x80000001 in a 22-byte heap buffer — a massive heap buffer overflow.

state.value: write 0 at offset 0x80000001 in a 22-byte buffer

vulnerabilities.description: When bOffsetIsPointer is true with certain overflow conditions, the overflowed `count*size` value used in io.read could differ from the allocated buffer size. Additionally, the loop iterates up to `kount` times accessing `buf` at `k*size` offsets which can exceed the allocated buffer, causing out-of-bounds reads. For example, with size=8 (rational type), count=0x20000001, the product overflows to 8, allocate=28, but kount=5 and the loop reads at offsets up to 4*8+4=36, exceeding the buffer.

step_by_step_execution.description: IFD entry is read with a rational type and large count.
kount is computed: count > 5, so kount = 5. pad = 0.
size*count overflows: 8 * 0x20000001 = 0x100000008, truncated to 0x00000008. allocate = (long long)(8 + 0 + 20) = 28.
Buffer of 28 bytes is allocated. bOffsetIsPointer = (8 > 4) = true, so io.read reads 8 bytes into buffer (overflowed size).

In the rational type printing loop, k iterates 0..4. At k=4: byteSwap4(buf, 4*8+0=32, bSwap) and byteSwap4(buf, 4*8+4=36, bSwap) read at offsets 32 and 36 in a 28-byte buffer — out-of-bounds heap read.

commit_message:
jp2_dec: fix `numchans` mixup

When iterating over `dec->cdef->data.cdef.ents`, we need to use its
`numchans` variable, not the one in `jp2_dec_t`.

Fixes CVE-2018-19543
Fixes CVE-2017-9782

Closes https://github.com/jasper-maint/jasper/issues/13
Closes https://github.com/jasper-maint/jasper/issues/18
Closes https://github.com/mdadams/jasper/issues/140
Closes https://github.com/mdadams/jasper/issues/182
cve_desc: JasPer 2.0.12 allows remote attackers to cause a denial of service (heap-based buffer over-read and application crash) via a crafted image, related to the jp2_decode function in libjasper/jp2/jp2_dec.c.

vulnerabilities.description: The return value of jas_alloc2() for lutents is never checked for NULL. If the allocation fails, the subsequent loop dereferences a NULL pointer, leading to a crash or potential code execution.

step_by_step_execution.description: The function parses JP2 boxes and finds a CMAP box and a PCLR box, so dec->cmap and dec->pclr are both non-NULL.
The code enters the else branch (dec->cmap is set) and iterates over CMAP entries.
A CMAP entry with cmapent->map == JP2_CMAP_PALETTE is encountered.
jas_alloc2 is called to allocate lutents, but the system is low on memory and returns NULL.
The loop writes to lutents[i] where lutents is NULL, causing a NULL pointer dereference.

vulnerabilities.description: When dec->cmap is non-NULL, the code unconditionally dereferences dec->cdef to assign cdefd, but dec->cdef can be NULL if no CDEF box was present in the input. This leads to a NULL pointer dereference.

step_by_step_execution.description: The function parses JP2 boxes. A CMAP box and PCLR box are found, but no CDEF box is present.
The code enters the else branch because dec->cmap is set, and executes the line cdefd = &dec->cdef->data.cdef which dereferences the NULL pointer dec->cdef.

commit_message: Fix bug #72750: wddx_deserialize null dereference
cve_desc: The php_wddx_pop_element function in ext/wddx/wddx.c in PHP before 5.6.25 and 7.x before 7.0.10 allows remote attackers to cause a denial of service (NULL pointer dereference and application crash) or possibly have unspecified other impact via an invalid base64 binary value, as demonstrated by a wddx_deserialize call that mishandles a binary element in a wddxPacket XML document.

vulnerabilities.description: php_base64_decode can return NULL on malformed base64 input. The return value is assigned to Z_STRVAL_P(ent1->data) without a NULL check. The old string is freed via STR_FREE, then the zval's string pointer is set to NULL. Subsequent operations on this zval (e.g., inserting it into an array/object, or printing it) will dereference a NULL pointer.

step_by_step_execution.description: The function is called when closing an XML element. The element name is EL_BINARY, and stack->top > 0.
ent1 is popped from the stack. ent1->data is non-NULL and contains a zval with a string that is invalid base64 data.

state.value: non-NULL zval of type IS_STRING with invalid base64 content

step_by_step_execution.description: php_base64_decode is called with the invalid base64 string. It returns NULL because decoding failed.
STR_FREE frees the old string value, then new_str (NULL) is assigned to Z_STRVAL_P(ent1->data).

Since Z_TYPE_P(ent1->data) is IS_STRING (not IS_OBJECT), the __wakeup branch is skipped. If stack->top > 1, ent1->data (with NULL string pointer) is inserted into the parent's hash table via zend_hash_next_index_insert or zend_symtable_update, propagating a zval with a NULL string pointer that will cause a NULL dereference when accessed.

state.value: zval with Z_STRVAL = NULL, Z_STRLEN = 0, Z_TYPE = IS_STRING

commit_message: Fix bug #79037 (global buffer-overflow in `mbfl_filt_conv_big5_wchar`)
cve_desc: When using certain mbstring functions to convert multibyte encodings, in PHP versions 7.2.x below 7.2.27, 7.3.x below 7.3.14 and 7.4.x below 7.4.2 it is possible to supply data that will cause function mbfl_filt_conv_big5_wchar to read past the allocated buffer. This may lead to information disclosure or crash.

vulnerabilities.description: If `is_in_cp950_pua(c1, c)` returns true but no entry in `cp950_pua_tbl` actually matches the `c2` value (the check in the for-loop never breaks), `k` will equal the table size (one past the last valid index). The code then accesses `cp950_pua_tbl[k][2]`, `cp950_pua_tbl[k][3]`, and `cp950_pua_tbl[k][0]` out of bounds, leading to an out-of-bounds read.

step_by_step_execution.description: Function is called with filter->status == 0, filter->from->no_encoding == mbfl_no_encoding_cp950, and a lead byte c that satisfies c > 0x80 && c < 0xff. filter->status is set to 1 and filter->cache = c.

Function is called again with the second byte. filter->status == 1, so we enter case 1. c1 = filter->cache (the lead byte). The second byte c satisfies the condition (c > 0x39 && c < 0x7f) | (c > 0xa0 && c < 0xff).
w is computed from the Big5-to-UCS table. If w <= 0, we check is_in_cp950_pua(c1, c). Suppose is_in_cp950_pua returns true for this (c1, c) pair.

The for-loop iterates over all entries in cp950_pua_tbl looking for one where c2 >= cp950_pua_tbl[k][2] && c2 <= cp950_pua_tbl[k][3]. If is_in_cp950_pua uses a different or less precise check than this loop, it is possible no entry matches, so the loop completes without breaking.

state.value: sizeof(cp950_pua_tbl)/(sizeof(unsigned short)*4) — one past the last valid index

step_by_step_execution.description: After the loop, cp950_pua_tbl[k][2] is accessed without verifying k is in bounds. This reads memory beyond the array.

commit_message: LibVNCClient: really fix #250
cve_desc: LibVNC before commit 7b1ef0ffc4815cab9a96c7278394152bdc89dc4d contains heap out-of-bound write vulnerability inside structure in VNC client code that can result remote code execution

vulnerabilities.description: Integer overflow in the multiplication `hdr.nSubrects * (4 + (BPP / 8))`. Since `hdr.nSubrects` is a 32-bit unsigned integer controlled by the server, a large value can cause the multiplication to wrap around to a small value, bypassing the `> RFB_BUFFER_SIZE` check. ReadFromRFBServer then reads only the small (wrapped) amount into the buffer, but the subsequent loop iterates `hdr.nSubrects` times, reading far beyond the buffer boundary.

step_by_step_execution.description: hdr.nSubrects is read from the network and byte-swapped. An attacker-controlled server provides a very large value.

state.value: A large value, e.g. 0x40000000 when BPP=32 (element size = 8)

step_by_step_execution.description: The overflow check computes hdr.nSubrects * (4 + (BPP / 8)). With BPP=32, element size is 8. 0x40000000 * 8 = 0x200000000 which truncates to 0 in 32-bit arithmetic, passing the > RFB_BUFFER_SIZE check.
ReadFromRFBServer is called with size 0 (or a small wrapped value), reading little or no data into client->buffer.
The for loop iterates hdr.nSubrects (0x40000000) times, dereferencing ptr which advances well past the end of client->buffer, causing out-of-bounds reads.

state.value: advances 8 bytes per iteration, far beyond client->buffer bounds

commit_message:
gsocketclient: Fix criticals

This ensures the parent GTask is kept alive as long as an enumeration
is running and trying to connect.

Closes #1646
Closes #1649
cve_desc: gio/gsocketclient.c in GNOME GLib 2.59.2 does not ensure that a parent GTask remains alive during the execution of a connection-attempting enumeration, which allows remote attackers to cause a denial of service (g_socket_client_connected_callback mishandling and application crash) via a crafted web site, as demonstrated by GNOME Web (aka Epiphany).

vulnerabilities.description: NULL pointer dereference: when `data` (i.e., `attempt->data`) is NULL (which the comment explicitly states can happen once the task is completed), and the connection attempt fails with `g_socket_connection_connect_finish` returning FALSE, and `attempt->cancellable` is not cancelled, the code dereferences `data` without a NULL check to access `data->connectable` and passes it to `set_last_error`.

step_by_step_execution.description: Function entry: `data` is set from `attempt->data`, which can be NULL as stated by the comment 'data is NULL once the task is completed'.
First NULL check: `if (data && g_task_return_error_if_cancelled(data->task))` — since `data` is NULL, this condition is false, so we skip the early return.
Timeout source cleanup runs (no dependency on `data`).
`g_socket_connection_connect_finish` returns FALSE (connection failed), so we enter the error-handling branch.
`g_cancellable_is_cancelled(attempt->cancellable)` returns FALSE (the attempt's own cancellable was not cancelled), so we enter the `if` body.
`clarify_connect_error(error, data->connectable, attempt->address)` is called, dereferencing `data` which is NULL. This causes a NULL pointer dereference / crash.

vulnerabilities.description: NULL pointer dereference on the success path: if `data` is NULL and `g_socket_connection_connect_finish` succeeds, the code unconditionally dereferences `data` to store the socket and connection, leading to a crash.

step_by_step_execution.description: Function entry: `data` is set from `attempt->data`, which is NULL.
First NULL check passes without returning (data is NULL so the condition is false).
`g_socket_connection_connect_finish` succeeds (returns TRUE), so we skip the error branch.
Code unconditionally dereferences `data`: `data->socket = g_steal_pointer(&attempt->socket)`, causing a NULL pointer dereference.

commit_message:
SERVER-59071 Treat '$sample' as unsharded when connecting directly to shards

(cherry picked from commit f3604b901d688c194de5e430c7fbab060c9dc8e0)
cve_desc: An authorized user may trigger an invariant which may result in denial of service or server exit if a relevant aggregation request is sent to a shard. Usually, the requests are sent via mongos and special privileges are required in order to know the address of the shards and to log in to the shards of an auth enabled environment.

commit_message:
net/mlx5: prevent memory leak in mlx5_fpga_conn_create_cq

In mlx5_fpga_conn_create_cq if mlx5_vector2eqn fails the allocated
memory should be released.

Fixes: 537a50574175 ("net/mlx5: FPGA, Add high-speed connection routines")
Signed-off-by: Navid Emamdoost <navid.emamdoost@gmail.com>
Signed-off-by: Saeed Mahameed <saeedm@mellanox.com>
cve_desc: A memory leak in the mlx5_fpga_conn_create_cq() function in drivers/net/ethernet/mellanox/mlx5/core/fpga/conn.c in the Linux kernel before 5.3.11 allows attackers to cause a denial of service (memory consumption) by triggering mlx5_vector2eqn() failures, aka CID-c8c2a057fdc7.

vulnerabilities.description: When mlx5_vector2eqn() fails, the function jumps to err_cqwq which only calls mlx5_wq_destroy, but does not free the memory allocated by kvzalloc for 'in'. This causes a memory leak.

step_by_step_execution.description: mlx5_cqwq_create succeeds, so execution continues past the first error check.
kvzalloc succeeds, allocating memory for 'in'.

state.value: non-NULL pointer to allocated memory of size inlen

step_by_step_execution.description: mlx5_vector2eqn is called and returns a non-zero error code.
The error check triggers a goto to err_cqwq, skipping kvfree(in).
At err_cqwq label, only mlx5_wq_destroy is called. The memory pointed to by 'in' is never freed.

commit_message:
fix(plugin): Add default limits for chunks and message size

Based on a reported DoS vulnerability reported by Team82 (Claroty
Research).
cve_desc: The package open62541/open62541 before 1.2.5, from 1.3-rc1 and before 1.3.1 are vulnerable to Denial of Service (DoS) due to a missing limitation on the number of received chunks - per single session or in total for all concurrent sessions. An attacker can exploit this vulnerability by sending an unlimited number of huge chunks (e.g. 2GB each) without sending the Final closing chunk.

commit_message:
SERVER-59071 Treat '$sample' as unsharded when connecting directly to shards

(cherry picked from commit f3604b901d688c194de5e430c7fbab060c9dc8e0)
cve_desc: An authorized user may trigger an invariant which may result in denial of service or server exit if a relevant aggregation request is sent to a shard. Usually, the requests are sent via mongos and special privileges are required in order to know the address of the shards and to log in to the shards of an auth enabled environment.

commit_message:
cadence_gem: switch to use qemu_receive_packet() for loopback

This patch switches to use qemu_receive_packet() which can detect
reentrancy and return early.

This is intended to address CVE-2021-3416.

Cc: Prasad J Pandit <ppandit@redhat.com>
Cc: qemu-stable@nongnu.org
Reviewed-by: Philippe Mathieu-Daudé <philmd@redhat.com>
Signed-off-by: Alexander Bulekov <alxndr@bu.edu>
Signed-off-by: Jason Wang <jasowang@redhat.com>
cve_desc: A potential stack overflow via infinite loop issue was found in various NIC emulators of QEMU in versions up to and including 5.2.0. The issue occurs in loopback mode of a NIC wherein reentrant DMA checks get bypassed. A guest user/process may use this flaw to consume CPU cycles or crash the QEMU process on the host resulting in DoS scenario.

commit_message:
patch 8.2.4563: "z=" in Visual mode may go beyond the end of the line

Problem:    "z=" in Visual mode may go beyond the end of the line.
Solution:   Adjust "badlen".
cve_desc: Heap-based Buffer Overflow occurs in vim in GitHub repository vim/vim prior to 8.2.4563.

vulnerabilities.description: Stack-based buffer overflow in `wcopy`. The buffer is `MAXWLEN + 2` bytes. The first `vim_strncpy` can write up to `MAXWLEN` characters (plus NUL) into `wcopy`, meaning `stp->st_wordlen` can be up to `MAXWLEN`. The second `vim_strncpy` writes at offset `stp->st_wordlen` with up to `sug.su_badlen - stp->st_orglen` additional bytes. If `st_wordlen` is close to `MAXWLEN` and the remainder `(su_badlen - st_orglen)` is more than 1, the write exceeds the `MAXWLEN + 2` bound of `wcopy`.

step_by_step_execution.description: The function enters the suggestion listing loop because `count` is 0 and suggestions exist.
In the for-loop, `stp` points to a suggestion where `st_wordlen` is large (up to MAXWLEN) and `st_orglen` is small (the suggestion replaces only part of the bad word).
`vim_strncpy(wcopy, stp->st_word, MAXWLEN)` copies up to MAXWLEN characters into `wcopy`, filling it to offset MAXWLEN (plus a NUL at MAXWLEN).

state.value: MAXWLEN chars written, NUL at index MAXWLEN

step_by_step_execution.description: The condition `sug.su_badlen > stp->st_orglen` is true because the visually selected bad word length exceeds the original length the suggestion replaces.

`vim_strncpy(wcopy + stp->st_wordlen, ..., sug.su_badlen - stp->st_orglen)` writes 9 bytes starting at `wcopy[MAXWLEN]`. The buffer `wcopy` is only `MAXWLEN + 2` bytes, so this overflows by 7+ bytes on the stack.

commit_message:
study_chunk: avoid mutating regexp program within GOSUB

gh16947 and gh17743: studying GOSUB may restudy in an inner call
(via a mix of recursion and enframing) something that an outer call
is in the middle of looking at.  Let the outer frame deal with it.

(CVE-2020-12723)

(cherry picked from commit c4033e740bd18d9fbe3456a9db2ec2053cdc5271)
cve_desc: regcomp.c in Perl before 5.30.3 allows a buffer overflow via a crafted regular expression because of recursive S_study_chunk calls.

commit_message: Fix bug #72606: heap-buffer-overflow (write) simplestring_addn simplestring.c
cve_desc: Integer signedness error in the simplestring_addn function in simplestring.c in xmlrpc-epi through 0.54.2, as used in PHP before 5.5.38, 5.6.x before 5.6.24, and 7.x before 7.0.9, allows remote attackers to cause a denial of service (heap-based buffer overflow) or possibly have unspecified other impact via a long first argument to the PHP xmlrpc_encode_request function.

vulnerabilities.description: The pointer `target` is dereferenced to access `target->size` on the very first line of the function, before the null check `if(target && source)` is performed. If `target` is NULL, this is a null pointer dereference.

step_by_step_execution.description: The function is called with `target` set to NULL.
`target->size` is accessed to initialize `newsize`, dereferencing a NULL pointer before the NULL check on the next line.

commit_message:
sctp: Fix kernel panic while process protocol violation parameter

Since call to function sctp_sf_abort_violation() need paramter 'arg' with
'struct sctp_chunk' type, it will read the chunk type and chunk length from
the chunk_hdr member of chunk. But call to sctp_sf_violation_paramlen()
always with 'struct sctp_paramhdr' type's parameter, it will be passed to
sctp_sf_abort_violation(). This may cause kernel panic.

   sctp_sf_violation_paramlen()
     |-- sctp_sf_abort_violation()
        |-- sctp_make_abort_violation()

This patch fixed this problem. This patch also fix two place which called
sctp_sf_violation_paramlen() with wrong paramter type.

Signed-off-by: Wei Yongjun <yjwei@cn.fujitsu.com>
Signed-off-by: Vlad Yasevich <vladislav.yasevich@hp.com>
Signed-off-by: David S. Miller <davem@davemloft.net>
cve_desc: The Stream Control Transmission Protocol (sctp) implementation in the Linux kernel before 2.6.27 does not properly handle a protocol violation in which a parameter has an invalid length, which allows attackers to cause a denial of service (panic) via unspecified vectors, related to sctp_sf_violation_paramlen, sctp_sf_abort_violation, sctp_make_abort_violation, and incorrect data types in function calls.
