commit_message:
KVM: PPC: Book3S: Fix H_RTAS rets buffer overflow

The kvmppc_rtas_hcall() sets the host rtas_args.rets pointer based on
the rtas_args.nargs that was provided by the guest. That guest nargs
value is not range checked, so the guest can cause the host rets pointer
to be pointed outside the args array. The individual rtas function
handlers check the nargs and nrets values to ensure they are correct,
but if they are not, the handlers store a -3 (0xfffffffd) failure
indication in rets[0] which corrupts host memory.

Fix this by testing up front whether the guest supplied nargs and nret
would exceed the array size, and fail the hcall directly without storing
a failure indication to rets[0].

Also expand on a comment about why we kill the guest and try not to
return errors directly if we have a valid rets[0] pointer.

Fixes: 8e591cb72047 ("KVM: PPC: Book3S: Add infrastructure to implement kernel-side RTAS calls")
Cc: stable@vger.kernel.org # v3.10+
Reported-by: Alexey Kardashevskiy <aik@ozlabs.ru>
Signed-off-by: Nicholas Piggin <npiggin@gmail.com>
Signed-off-by: Michael Ellerman <mpe@ellerman.id.au>
cve_desc: arch/powerpc/kvm/book3s_rtas.c in the Linux kernel through 5.13.5 on the powerpc platform allows KVM guest OS users to cause host OS memory corruption via rtas_args.nargs, aka CID-f62f3c20647e.

vulnerabilities.description: The guest-controlled value `args.nargs` is used as an index into the fixed-size `args.args` array without any bounds checking. A malicious guest can set `nargs` to an arbitrarily large value, causing `args.rets` to point beyond the `args` structure on the kernel stack. When the RTAS handler subsequently reads from or writes to `args.rets`, it performs out-of-bounds stack memory access.

step_by_step_execution.description: The guest physical address of the RTAS args is obtained from guest register r4.
The entire `rtas_args` structure is copied from guest memory into the local stack variable `args`. All fields including `nargs` are guest-controlled.
args.rets is set to &args.args[be32_to_cpu(args.nargs)] without validating that nargs is within the bounds of the args.args array.

state.value: e.g. 0xFFFFFFFF or any value > array size
points far beyond the end of args.args, into arbitrary stack memory

step_by_step_execution.description: If a matching token is found, the handler is invoked with the corrupted args.rets pointer. The handler will read/write through args.rets, performing out-of-bounds stack read/write.

commit_message:
tipc: fix size validations for the MSG_CRYPTO type

The function tipc_crypto_key_rcv is used to parse MSG_CRYPTO messages
to receive keys from other nodes in the cluster in order to decrypt any
further messages from them.
This patch verifies that any supplied sizes in the message body are
valid for the received message.

Fixes: 1ef6f7c9390f ("tipc: add automatic session key exchange")
Signed-off-by: Max VA <maxv@sentinelone.com>
Acked-by: Ying Xue <ying.xue@windriver.com>
Signed-off-by: Greg Kroah-Hartman <gregkh@linuxfoundation.org>
Acked-by: Jon Maloy <jmaloy@redhat.com>
Signed-off-by: David S. Miller <davem@davemloft.net>
cve_desc: An issue was discovered in net/tipc/crypto.c in the Linux kernel before 5.14.16. The Transparent Inter-Process Communication (TIPC) functionality allows remote attackers to exploit insufficient validation of user-supplied sizes for the MSG_CRYPTO message type.

vulnerabilities.description: A heap buffer overflow occurs because `skey->keylen` is read from attacker-controlled message data and used as the length for `memcpy` into the `skey->key` field *before* the sanity check (`size != tipc_aead_key_size(skey)`) validates it. An attacker can send a message where `keylen` is much larger than the allocated buffer (`size` bytes), causing an out-of-bounds write on the heap.

step_by_step_execution.description: The function receives a TIPC message header. `size` is set from `msg_data_sz(hdr)` and `data` points to the message payload. An attacker controls the message content.
The lock is acquired and initial checks pass (rx->skey is NULL, key_gen differs or no keys).
`skey` is allocated with `kmalloc(size, GFP_ATOMIC)`, so the buffer is `size` bytes (e.g. 48 bytes).
`skey->keylen` is read from attacker-controlled data at offset `TIPC_AEAD_ALG_NAME`. The attacker sets this to a large value, e.g. 0xFFFF.
`memcpy(skey->alg_name, data, TIPC_AEAD_ALG_NAME)` copies the algorithm name — this fits within the struct.
`memcpy(skey->key, ..., skey->keylen)` copies 65535 bytes into `skey->key`, which starts at an offset within the 48-byte allocation. This writes far beyond the allocated heap buffer.

state.value: ~48 - offsetof(struct tipc_aead_key, key), much less than 65535

step_by_step_execution.description: The sanity check `size != tipc_aead_key_size(skey)` occurs *after* the overflow has already happened. The damage is done before the validation.

commit_message:
patch 8.2.4563: "z=" in Visual mode may go beyond the end of the line

Problem:    "z=" in Visual mode may go beyond the end of the line.
Solution:   Adjust "badlen".
cve_desc: Heap-based Buffer Overflow occurs in vim in GitHub repository vim/vim prior to 8.2.4563.

vulnerabilities.description: Stack-based buffer overflow in `wcopy`. The buffer `wcopy` is `MAXWLEN + 2` bytes. The code first copies `stp->st_word` (up to MAXWLEN bytes) into `wcopy`, then appends the remainder of the bad word at offset `stp->st_wordlen`. When `stp->st_wordlen` is close to MAXWLEN and `sug.su_badlen - stp->st_orglen` is greater than 1, the write exceeds the bounds of `wcopy`. The `badlen` value can be large when derived from a Visual selection.

step_by_step_execution.description: The function is entered. `wcopy` is a stack buffer of size `MAXWLEN + 2`.
VIsual_active is true. `badlen` is computed from the Visual selection span, which can be large (e.g., a long word on a single line).
`spell_find_suggest` is called with this large `badlen`, setting `sug.su_badlen` accordingly.
In the suggestion display loop, `stp->st_word` is copied into `wcopy` with `vim_strncpy(wcopy, stp->st_word, MAXWLEN)`, writing up to MAXWLEN characters.

state.value: contains up to MAXWLEN chars of st_word

step_by_step_execution.description: The condition `sug.su_badlen > stp->st_orglen` is true. The code calls `vim_strncpy(wcopy + stp->st_wordlen, ..., sug.su_badlen - stp->st_orglen)`. With `stp->st_wordlen` near MAXWLEN and the remainder being large, this writes past the end of the MAXWLEN+2-sized buffer.

commit_message: Avoid maxrlvls more than upper bound to cause heap-buffer-overflow
cve_desc: There's a flaw in jasper's jpc encoder in versions prior to 2.0.23. Crafted input provided to jasper by an attacker could cause an arbitrary out-of-bounds write. This could potentially affect data confidentiality, integrity, or application availability.

vulnerabilities.description: Integer overflow in the computation of cp->numtiles. An attacker can specify very small tile dimensions (e.g., tilewidth=1, tileheight=1) via the option string, causing numhtiles and numvtiles to be very large. Their product overflows, yielding a small cp->numtiles value. Callers using cp->numtiles for memory allocation will allocate insufficient memory, leading to heap buffer overflows.

step_by_step_execution.description: User provides option string with tilewidth=1 and tileheight=1
cp->tilewidth and cp->tileheight are set to 1 via atoi in the option parsing loop
After option parsing, cp->refgrdwidth and cp->refgrdheight are computed from image dimensions. For a large image (e.g., width=65536, height=65536), these values are large.
cp->numhtiles = JPC_CEILDIV(65536 - 0, 1) = 65536, cp->numvtiles = JPC_CEILDIV(65536 - 0, 1) = 65536

cp->numtiles = 65536 * 65536 = 4294967296, which overflows a 32-bit uint_fast32_t to 0. With other dimensions the overflow yields an arbitrary small value, causing downstream allocations based on numtiles to be too small.

state.value: 0 or other small value due to overflow

code_snippets:
hsteplcm *= jas_image_cmpthstep(image, cmptno);
vsteplcm *= jas_image_cmptvstep(image, cmptno);
vulnerabilities.description: Integer overflow in the computation of hsteplcm and vsteplcm. These uint_fast32_t variables are multiplied by each component's step size in a loop without overflow checking. If the image has multiple components with step values greater than 1, the product can overflow, corrupting subsequent calculations of cp->imgareatlx, cp->refgrdwidth, tile counts, etc.

step_by_step_execution.description: hsteplcm and vsteplcm are initialized to 1

For each image component, hsteplcm is multiplied by the component's horizontal step. With multiple components having large step values (e.g., 3 components each with hstep=2000), the product overflows uint_fast32_t.
The overflowed hsteplcm is used to multiply cp->imgareatlx if imgareatlx was set via options. This produces a corrupted imgareatlx value.

state.value: corrupted value from multiplication with overflowed hsteplcm

step_by_step_execution.description: cp->refgrdwidth = cp->imgareatlx + jas_image_width(image) may also overflow or produce incorrect dimensions, affecting tile grid calculations and downstream memory allocations.

commit_message: Limit the number of elements in a vector (found by oss-fuzz)
cve_desc: cdf_read_property_info in cdf.c in file through 5.37 does not restrict the number of CDF_VECTOR elements, which allows a heap-based buffer overflow (4-byte out-of-bounds write).

vulnerabilities.description: On 32-bit platforms, the variable `slen` accumulates string lengths via `slen += l >> 1` inside the inner loop for CDF_LENGTH32_STRING/CDF_LENGTH32_WSTRING vector processing. When `l` values from attacker-controlled data are large, `slen` can grow enough that `slen * sizeof(uint32_t)` overflows the 32-bit `size_t`, causing `o4` to wrap around to a small value. Subsequent bounds checks (`o4 + sizeof(uint32_t) > left` and `o4 + l > left`) then pass incorrectly, allowing out-of-bounds reads from `q[o4]` and `CDF_GETUINT32(q, slen)`.

step_by_step_execution.description: Function is called with attacker-controlled CDF stream data. `offs` is valid and passes the initial check.
Section header is read: sh.sh_len is large but within CDF_SHLEN_LIMIT, sh.sh_properties is > 0 and within CDF_PROP_LIMIT. Memory is allocated for property info entries.
In the outer loop, property i=0 is a CDF_VECTOR | CDF_LENGTH32_STRING. nelements is read from attacker-controlled data (e.g., 2). slen is set to 2.

cdf_grow_info is called with nelements, additional space allocated. Inner loop begins. First iteration (j=0, i=0): o4 = slen * 4 = 8. Bounds check passes. l is read from attacker-controlled data as a large value, e.g. 0x7FFFFFF0 (on 32-bit, this is ~2GB which could be < left if sh.sh_len is large).

state.value: large value derived from e - q

step_by_step_execution.description: l passes the check `o4 + l > left` (8 + 0x7FFFFFF0 = 0x7FFFFFF8, valid on 32-bit, and < left). String pointer is set. Then l is adjusted: l is even so no increment. slen += l >> 1 = 2 + 0x3FFFFFF8 = 0x3FFFFFFA.

Second iteration (j=1, i=1): o4 = slen * sizeof(uint32_t) = 0x3FFFFFFA * 4 = 0xFFFFFFE8 on 32-bit. The check `o4 + sizeof(uint32_t) > left` computes 0xFFFFFFE8 + 4 = 0xFFFFFFEC. If left < 0xFFFFFFEC, goto out. But attacker can craft l in the first iteration to make slen * 4 overflow exactly.

state.value: 0x40000002 (if l was chosen to make slen overflow)

step_by_step_execution.description: With a carefully chosen first l (e.g., 0x80000000), slen = 2 + 0x40000000 = 0x40000002. Then o4 = 0x40000002 * 4 = 8 (32-bit overflow). The bounds check `o4 + sizeof(uint32_t) > left` becomes `8 + 4 = 12 > left` which passes (left is large). CDF_GETUINT32(q, slen) reads from q + slen*4 which wraps, reading from an incorrect/out-of-bounds location.

commit_message:
USB: gadget: validate interface OS descriptor requests

Stall the control endpoint in case provided index exceeds array size of
MAX_CONFIG_INTERFACES or when the retrieved function pointer is null.

Signed-off-by: Szymon Heidrich <szymon.heidrich@gmail.com>
Cc: stable@kernel.org
Signed-off-by: Greg Kroah-Hartman <gregkh@linuxfoundation.org>
cve_desc: An issue was discovered in drivers/usb/gadget/composite.c in the Linux kernel before 5.16.10. The USB Gadget subsystem lacks certain validation of interface OS descriptor requests (ones with a large array index and ones associated with NULL function pointer retrieval). Memory corruption might occur.

vulnerabilities.description: If bos_desc() returns a negative error code, the result is cast to (u16) in the min() call, converting it to a large positive number. This means value becomes w_length, and the code proceeds to queue a response of w_length bytes from req->buf, potentially sending uninitialized kernel heap memory to the USB host. Other descriptor cases (USB_DT_CONFIG, USB_DT_STRING) correctly check 'if (value >= 0)' before the min, but USB_DT_BOS does not.

step_by_step_execution.description: A USB GET_DESCRIPTOR request arrives for BOS descriptor (w_value >> 8 == USB_DT_BOS) with w_length set to some value, e.g. 512.
The gadget is superspeed or lpm_capable, so the BOS branch is entered. bos_desc(cdev) is called and returns a negative error, e.g. -ENOMEM (-12).
The negative value is cast to (u16) in min(w_length, (u16) value). (u16)(-12) == 65524. min(512, 65524) == 512.

Since value (512) is >= 0 and not USB_GADGET_DELAYED_STATUS, execution reaches check_value. req->length is set to 512 and composite_ep0_queue is called, sending 512 bytes from req->buf to the USB host. Since bos_desc failed, the buffer may contain stale/uninitialized heap data.

state.value: uninitialized or stale kernel heap data

commit_message: Fixed miscomputation bugs in secp-256r1 modulo functions.
cve_desc: The ecc_256_modp function in ecc-256.c in Nettle before 3.2 does not properly handle carry propagation and produces incorrect output in its implementation of the P-256 NIST elliptic curve, which allows attackers to have unspecified impact via unknown vectors, a different vulnerability than CVE-2015-8805.

commit_message:
Fix oobread segfault in java arith8.class ##crash

* Reported by Cen Zhang via huntr.dev
cve_desc: Heap-based Buffer Overflow in GitHub repository radareorg/radare2 prior to 5.6.2.

vulnerabilities.description: Out-of-bounds read when reading `number_of_classes` from the buffer at offset 6 without first checking that `sz` is large enough (at least 8). If `sz` is less than 8, the `R_BIN_JAVA_USHORT(buffer, offset)` macro reads beyond the allocated buffer.

step_by_step_execution.description: Function is called with a buffer of size `sz`. `sz` could be as small as 6 (enough for `r_bin_java_default_attr_new` to succeed but not more).
`r_bin_java_default_attr_new` is called and succeeds, consuming 6 bytes conceptually. `offset` is set to 6.
The code reads `R_BIN_JAVA_USHORT(buffer, offset)` at offset 6, which accesses `buffer[6]` and `buffer[7]`. There is no check that `offset + 2 <= sz` (i.e., `8 <= sz`) before this read.
This results in reading 1 or 2 bytes past the end of the buffer, causing an out-of-bounds read.

state.value: buffer[6] and buffer[7], but buffer only has `sz` (6 or 7) valid bytes

commit_message: Fix: index entry label not being zero-terminated with corrupt input
cve_desc: Buffer Over-read in GitHub repository bfabiszewski/libmobi prior to 0.11.

vulnerabilities.description: After calling mobi_getstring_ordt or mobi_indx_get_label, the returned label_length is not validated against INDX_LABEL_SIZEMAX. If these functions return a label_length larger than INDX_LABEL_SIZEMAX, the strncpy call will read beyond the stack-allocated text buffer, causing a stack buffer over-read. Furthermore, those functions themselves may write beyond the text buffer, causing a stack buffer overflow.

step_by_step_execution.description: The function is entered with valid indx, and entry_length and entry_number pass their respective checks.

state.value: some valid entry length from IDXT offsets

step_by_step_execution.description: buf->maxlen is restricted and original is saved. label_length is read from buffer via mobi_buffer_get8.
label_length is checked against entry_length and passes (label_length <= entry_length).
A stack buffer text of size INDX_LABEL_SIZEMAX is declared.

mobi_getstring_ordt or mobi_indx_get_label is called, which may perform ORDT character expansion (e.g., multi-byte to wider encoding), potentially expanding the output. The returned label_length can exceed INDX_LABEL_SIZEMAX.
No check is performed to validate the new label_length against INDX_LABEL_SIZEMAX.

malloc(label_length + 1) allocates a heap buffer sized to the new label_length. strncpy reads label_length + 1 bytes from the stack buffer text, which is only INDX_LABEL_SIZEMAX bytes, causing a stack buffer over-read.

commit_message: imptcp: fix Segmentation Fault when octet count is to high
cve_desc: A denial of service vulnerability was found in rsyslog in the imptcp module. An attacker could send a specially crafted message to the imptcp socket, which would cause rsyslog to crash. Versions before 8.27.0 are vulnerable.

vulnerabilities.description: The octet count parser accumulates digits into pThis->iOctetsRemain via repeated multiply-by-10-and-add without any upper-bound check. A malicious sender can supply a very long sequence of digits, causing a signed integer overflow. This can make iOctetsRemain wrap to a negative value. When the octet-counting copy path is later entered, `octatesToCopy` takes this negative value. The subsequent guard checks (`buffLen < octatesToCopy` and `octatesToCopy + pThis->iMsg > iMaxLine`) both evaluate false for a negative `octatesToCopy`. The negative int is then implicitly cast to a very large `size_t` in the `memcpy` call, causing a massive heap buffer overflow.

step_by_step_execution.description: Function is called repeatedly for each byte. The session is in eAtStrtFram state and receives a digit character, entering eInOctetCnt state with iOctetsRemain = 0.

The attacker sends many digit characters (e.g., '9' repeated ~10 times). Each call executes `pThis->iOctetsRemain = pThis->iOctetsRemain * 10 + c - '0'` without any bounds check. Signed integer overflow occurs (undefined behavior in C), and the value wraps to a negative number.

The attacker sends a space character ' ' to terminate the octet count. The check `pThis->iOctetsRemain < 1` fires and logs an error, but does NOT abort or change the framing mode. Execution falls through to set `pThis->inputState = eInMsg`.
On the next call, the code enters the eInMsg / TCP_FRAMING_OCTET_COUNTING branch. `octatesToCopy` is set to the negative `iOctetsRemain`.
Guard `buffLen < octatesToCopy`: buffLen is positive, octatesToCopy is negative. Positive is not less than negative, so this check is false. octatesToCopy remains negative.
Guard `octatesToCopy + pThis->iMsg > iMaxLine`: negative + small positive is still negative, which is not > iMaxLine. Check is false.

memcpy is called. The negative int `octatesToCopy` is implicitly converted to size_t (a very large unsigned value), causing a heap buffer overflow that reads far beyond *buff and writes far beyond pThis->pMsg.

patch 9.0.0011: reading beyond the end of the line with put command

Problem:    Reading beyond the end of the line with put command.

commit_message:
Submitted by: Tomas Hoger <thoger@redhat.com>

Fix for CVE-2010-0433 where some kerberos enabled versions of OpenSSL
could be crashed if the relevant tables were not present (e.g. chrooted).
cve_desc: The kssl_keytab_is_available function in ssl/kssl.c in OpenSSL before 0.9.8n, when Kerberos is enabled but Kerberos configuration files cannot be opened, does not check a certain return value, which allows remote attackers to cause a denial of service (NULL pointer dereference and daemon crash) via SSL cipher negotiation, as demonstrated by a chroot installation of Dovecot or stunnel without Kerberos configuration files inside the chroot.

commit_message:
Fix oobread segfault in java arith8.class ##crash

* Reported by Cen Zhang via huntr.dev
cve_desc: Heap-based Buffer Overflow in GitHub repository radareorg/radare2 prior to 5.6.2.

vulnerabilities.description: Memory leak of `attr`: `r_bin_java_default_attr_new` may successfully allocate `attr`, but the subsequent bounds check (`buf_offset + offset + 8 > sz`) returns NULL without freeing the already-allocated `attr` object.

step_by_step_execution.description: attr is allocated via r_bin_java_default_attr_new and returns a non-NULL pointer.
offset is incremented by 6.
The bounds check `buf_offset + offset + 8 > sz` is evaluated. If buf_offset + 14 > sz, this condition is true.
The function returns NULL without freeing `attr`, leaking the memory allocated by r_bin_java_default_attr_new.

vulnerabilities.description: When both name lookups fail, `icattr` is freed directly with `free()` instead of the proper destructor. This leaks `icattr->flags_str` (allocated by `retrieve_class_method_access_string`) and `icattr->name` (just allocated by `r_str_dup`).

step_by_step_execution.description: icattr is allocated via R_NEW0.
icattr->flags_str is set to a heap-allocated string from retrieve_class_method_access_string.
Both name resolution attempts fail. icattr->name is set via r_str_dup(NULL, "NULL").
free(icattr) frees only the struct itself, but icattr->flags_str and icattr->name are not freed, causing memory leaks.

commit_message:
USB: gadget: validate interface OS descriptor requests

Stall the control endpoint in case provided index exceeds array size of
MAX_CONFIG_INTERFACES or when the retrieved function pointer is null.

Signed-off-by: Szymon Heidrich <szymon.heidrich@gmail.com>
Cc: stable@kernel.org
Signed-off-by: Greg Kroah-Hartman <gregkh@linuxfoundation.org>
cve_desc: An issue was discovered in drivers/usb/gadget/composite.c in the Linux kernel before 5.16.10. The USB Gadget subsystem lacks certain validation of interface OS descriptor requests (ones with a large array index and ones associated with NULL function pointer retrieval). Memory corruption might occur.

vulnerabilities.description: If bos_desc() returns a negative error code, the cast to (u16) in 'min(w_length, (u16) value)' converts it to a large positive number (e.g., -ENOMEM = -12 becomes 65524). The min() then returns w_length, making 'value' a positive number. This causes the code at 'check_value' to queue a response of w_length bytes from req->buf, potentially leaking stale kernel heap data to the USB host. Other descriptor cases (USB_DT_CONFIG, USB_DT_STRING) properly check 'if (value >= 0)' before the min(), but USB_DT_BOS does not.

step_by_step_execution.description: A USB host sends a GET_DESCRIPTOR request for USB_DT_BOS with a non-zero w_length (e.g., 255), which is within USB_COMP_EP0_BUFSIZ.
The gadget is superspeed or lpm_capable, so the USB_DT_BOS case is entered. bos_desc(cdev) is called and returns a negative error code (e.g., -ENOMEM = -12).
The expression 'min(w_length, (u16) value)' is evaluated. Casting -12 to u16 yields 65524. min(255, 65524) = 255. Unlike USB_DT_CONFIG and USB_DT_STRING cases, there is no 'if (value >= 0)' guard.

Execution falls through the switch and reaches 'check_value'. Since value (255) >= 0 and != USB_GADGET_DELAYED_STATUS, req->length is set to 255, and the buffer (which was never populated by bos_desc due to the error) is queued for transmission to the USB host via composite_ep0_queue.

commit_message:
Remove 'umask' calls from 'mkdir'.

Fixes <http://bugs.gnu.org/24659>.

* libguile/filesys.c (SCM_DEFINE): Remove calls to 'umask' when MODE is
unbound; instead, use 0777 as the mode.  Update docstring to clarify
this.
* doc/ref/posix.texi (File System): Adjust accordingly.
* NEWS: Mention it.
cve_desc: The mkdir procedure of GNU Guile temporarily changed the process' umask to zero. During that time window, in a multithreaded application, other threads could end up creating files with insecure permissions. For example, mkdir without the optional mode argument would create directories as 0777. This is fixed in Guile 2.0.13. Prior versions are affected.

commit_message: Fixed miscomputation bugs in secp-256r1 modulo functions.
cve_desc: The ecc_256_modp function in ecc-256.c in Nettle before 3.2 does not properly handle carry propagation and produces incorrect output in its implementation of the P-256 NIST elliptic curve, which allows attackers to have unspecified impact via unknown vectors, a different vulnerability than CVE-2015-8805.

commit_message:
auth: Don't crash expanding %variables when username isn't set.

This continues the auth-policy fix in
c3d3faa4f72a676e183f34be960cff13a5a725ae
cve_desc: The auth component in Dovecot before 2.2.27, when auth-policy is configured, allows a remote attackers to cause a denial of service (crash) by aborting authentication without setting a username.

commit_message:
Prevent over long nonces in ChaCha20-Poly1305

ChaCha20-Poly1305 is an AEAD cipher, and requires a unique nonce input for
every encryption operation. RFC 7539 specifies that the nonce value (IV)
should be 96 bits (12 bytes). OpenSSL allows a variable nonce length and
front pads the nonce with 0 bytes if it is less than 12 bytes. However it
also incorrectly allows a nonce to be set of up to 16 bytes. In this case
only the last 12 bytes are significant and any additional leading bytes are
ignored.

It is a requirement of using this cipher that nonce values are unique.
Messages encrypted using a reused nonce value are susceptible to serious
confidentiality and integrity attacks. If an application changes the
default nonce length to be longer than 12 bytes and then makes a change to
the leading bytes of the nonce expecting the new value to be a new unique
nonce then such an application could inadvertently encrypt messages with a
reused nonce.

Additionally the ignored bytes in a long nonce are not covered by the
integrity guarantee of this cipher. Any application that relies on the
integrity of these ignored leading bytes of a long nonce may be further
affected.

Any OpenSSL internal use of this cipher, including in SSL/TLS, is safe
because no such use sets such a long nonce value. However user
applications that use this cipher directly and set a non-default nonce
length to be longer than 12 bytes may be vulnerable.

CVE-2019-1543

Fixes #8345

Reviewed-by: Paul Dale <paul.dale@oracle.com>
Reviewed-by: Richard Levitte <levitte@openssl.org>
(Merged from https://github.com/openssl/openssl/pull/8406)

(cherry picked from commit 2a3d0ee9d59156c48973592331404471aca886d6)
cve_desc: ChaCha20-Poly1305 is an AEAD cipher, and requires a unique nonce input for every encryption operation. RFC 7539 specifies that the nonce value (IV) should be 96 bits (12 bytes). OpenSSL allows a variable nonce length and front pads the nonce with 0 bytes if it is less than 12 bytes. However it also incorrectly allows a nonce to be set of up to 16 bytes. In this case only the last 12 bytes are significant and any additional leading bytes are ignored. It is a requirement of using this cipher that nonce values are unique. Messages encrypted using a reused nonce value are susceptible to serious confidentiality and integrity attacks. If an application changes the default nonce length to be longer than 12 bytes and then makes a change to the leading bytes of the nonce expecting the new value to be a new unique nonce then such an application could inadvertently encrypt messages with a reused nonce. Additionally the ignored bytes in a long nonce are not covered by the integrity guarantee of this cipher. Any application that relies on the integrity of these ignored leading bytes of a long nonce may be further affected. Any OpenSSL internal use of this cipher, including in SSL/TLS, is safe because no such use sets such a long nonce value. However user applications that use this cipher directly and set a non-default nonce length to be longer than 12 bytes may be vulnerable. OpenSSL versions 1.1.1 and 1.1.0 are affected by this issue. Due to the limited scope of affected deployments this has been assessed as low severity and therefore we are not creating new releases at this time. Fixed in OpenSSL 1.1.1c (Affected 1.1.1-1.1.1b). Fixed in OpenSSL 1.1.0k (Affected 1.1.0-1.1.0j).

commit_message: auth: mech-rpa - Fail on zero len buffer
cve_desc: In Dovecot before 2.3.11.3, sending a specially formatted RPA request will crash the auth service because a length of zero is mishandled.

commit_message:
Fix: Worked around buffer overflows in URL auth's cURL interface

This is only a workaround that keeps compatibility with 2.4.x mainline.
A real fix has been implemented in 2.5.x (master).
cve_desc: A buffer overflow was discovered in the URL-authentication backend of the Icecast before 2.4.4. If the backend is enabled, then any malicious HTTP client can send a request for that specific resource including a crafted header, leading to denial of service and potentially remote code execution.

commit_message: libnsgif: fix oob in lzw_decode
cve_desc: Heap-based Buffer Overflow in GitHub repository hpjansson/chafa prior to 1.12.0.

vulnerabilities.description: The function writes decoded values to a stack buffer starting at ctx->stack_base with no bounds checking. The while loop follows dictionary entry chains (via previous_entry) and writes one byte per entry to the stack buffer. A crafted LZW stream can build deep dictionary chains (up to 1 << LZW_CODE_MAX entries), and unwinding such a chain writes an unbounded number of bytes to the output stack, potentially overflowing the allocated buffer.

step_by_step_execution.description: Function begins, stack_pos is set to ctx->stack_base, which points to a fixed-size buffer.

A series of valid codes are decoded over multiple calls, each adding a new dictionary entry whose previous_entry points to the prior code, building a long chain of depth up to (1 << LZW_CODE_MAX) - 1.
A new code is read that references a code at the head of this deep chain. code_out is set to that code.

state.value: A code with a very deep chain of previous_entry links

step_by_step_execution.description: The while loop follows the previous_entry chain, writing one byte (*stack_pos++) per iteration without any bounds check against the stack buffer size.

state.value: Incremented once per chain link, potentially thousands of times beyond buffer end

step_by_step_execution.description: The writes overflow the stack buffer, corrupting adjacent memory.

state.value: Far past the end of the allocated buffer at ctx->stack_base

commit_message:
Add a test for GENERAL_NAME_cmp

Based on a boringssl test contributed by David Benjamin

Reviewed-by: Tomas Mraz <tmraz@fedoraproject.org>
cve_desc: The X.509 GeneralName type is a generic type for representing different types of names. One of those name types is known as EDIPartyName. OpenSSL provides a function GENERAL_NAME_cmp which compares different instances of a GENERAL_NAME to see if they are equal or not. This function behaves incorrectly when both GENERAL_NAMEs contain an EDIPARTYNAME. A NULL pointer dereference and a crash may occur leading to a possible denial of service attack. OpenSSL itself uses the GENERAL_NAME_cmp function for two purposes: 1) Comparing CRL distribution point names between an available CRL and a CRL distribution point embedded in an X509 certificate 2) When verifying that a timestamp response token signer matches the timestamp authority name (exposed via the API functions TS_RESP_verify_response and TS_RESP_verify_token) If an attacker can control both items being compared then that attacker could trigger a crash. For example if the attacker can trick a client or server into checking a malicious certificate against a malicious CRL then this may occur. Note that some applications automatically download CRLs based on a URL embedded in a certificate. This checking happens prior to the signatures on the certificate and CRL being verified. OpenSSL's s_server, s_client and verify tools have support for the "-crl_download" option which implements automatic CRL downloading and this attack has been demonstrated to work against those tools. Note that an unrelated bug means that affected versions of OpenSSL cannot parse or construct correct encodings of EDIPARTYNAME. However it is possible to construct a malformed EDIPARTYNAME that OpenSSL's parser will accept and hence trigger this attack. All OpenSSL 1.1.1 and 1.0.2 versions are affected by this issue. Other OpenSSL releases are out of support and have not been checked. Fixed in OpenSSL 1.1.1i (Affected 1.1.1-1.1.1h). Fixed in OpenSSL 1.0.2x (Affected 1.0.2-1.0.2w).

commit_message:
Add a test for GENERAL_NAME_cmp

Based on a boringssl test contributed by David Benjamin

Reviewed-by: Tomas Mraz <tmraz@fedoraproject.org>

This function behaves incorrectly when both GENERAL_NAMEs contain an EDIPARTYNAME. A NULL pointer dereference and a crash may occur leading to a possible denial of service attack. OpenSSL itself uses the GENERAL_NAME_cmp function for two purposes: 1) Comparing CRL distribution point names between an available CRL and a CRL distribution point embedded in an X509 certificate 2) When verifying that a timestamp response token signer matches the timestamp authority name (exposed via the API functions TS_RESP_verify_response and TS_RESP_verify_token) If an attacker can control both items being compared then that attacker could trigger a crash. For example if the attacker can trick a client or server into checking a malicious certificate against a malicious CRL then this may occur. Note that some applications automatically download CRLs based on a URL embedded in a certificate. This checking happens prior to the signatures on the certificate and CRL being verified. OpenSSL's s_server, s_client and verify tools have support for the "-crl_download" option which implements automatic CRL downloading and this attack has been demonstrated to work against those tools. Note that an unrelated bug means that affected versions of OpenSSL cannot parse or construct correct encodings of EDIPARTYNAME. However it is possible to construct a malformed EDIPARTYNAME that OpenSSL's parser will accept and hence trigger this attack. All OpenSSL 1.1.1 and 1.0.2 versions are affected by this issue. Other OpenSSL releases are out of support and have not been checked. Fixed in OpenSSL 1.1.1i (Affected 1.1.1-1.1.1h). Fixed in OpenSSL 1.0.2x (Affected 1.0.2-1.0.2w).

commit_message:
grep -F: fix a heap buffer (read) overrun

grep's read buffer is often filled to its full size, except when
reading the final buffer of a file.  In that case, the number of
bytes read may be far less than the size of the buffer.  However, for
certain unusual pattern/text combinations, grep -F would mistakenly
examine bytes in that uninitialized region of memory when searching
for a match.  With carefully chosen inputs, one can cause grep -F to
read beyond the end of that buffer altogether.  This problem arose via
commit v2.18-90-g73893ff with the introduction of a more efficient
heuristic using what is now the memchr_kwset function. The use of
that function in bmexec_trans could leave TP much larger than EP,
and the subsequent call to bm_delta2_search would mistakenly access
beyond end of the main input read buffer.

* src/kwset.c (bmexec_trans): When TP reaches or exceeds EP,
do not call bm_delta2_search.
* tests/kwset-abuse: New file.
* tests/Makefile.am (TESTS): Add it.
* THANKS.in: Update.
* NEWS (Bug fixes): Mention it.

Prior to this patch, this command would trigger a UMR:

  printf %0360db 0 | valgrind src/grep -F $(printf %019dXb 0)

  Use of uninitialised value of size 8
     at 0x4142BE: bmexec_trans (kwset.c:657)
     by 0x4143CA: bmexec (kwset.c:678)
     by 0x414973: kwsexec (kwset.c:848)
     by 0x414DC4: Fexecute (kwsearch.c:128)
     by 0x404E2E: grepbuf (grep.c:1238)
     by 0x4054BF: grep (grep.c:1417)
     by 0x405CEB: grepdesc (grep.c:1645)
     by 0x405EC1: grep_command_line_arg (grep.c:1692)
     by 0x4077D4: main (grep.c:2570)

See the accompanying test for how to trigger the heap buffer overrun.

Thanks to Nima Aghdaii for testing and finding numerous
ways to break early iterations of this patch.
cve_desc: The bmexec_trans function in kwset.c in grep 2.19 through 2.21 allows local users to cause a denial of service (out-of-bounds heap read and crash) via crafted input when using the -F option.

vulnerabilities.description: kwset->mind is assigned to an `int` variable `len`. If kwset->mind is large enough (e.g., greater than INT_MAX), it will overflow the int, potentially resulting in a negative or small positive value. This corrupted `len` is then used for pointer arithmetic (`tp = text + len`, `sp = kwset->target + len`) and size comparisons (`len > size`), which could lead to out-of-bounds memory reads. For instance, a negative `len` would pass the `len > size` check (since size is unsigned, the comparison is implementation-defined or the negative int gets converted), and `text + len` with a negative len would point before the buffer.

step_by_step_execution.description: kwset->mind is assigned to int len. If mind is a large unsigned value exceeding INT_MAX, len becomes a negative or otherwise unexpected value due to integer overflow.

state.value: negative value after truncation to int

step_by_step_execution.description: Check `len == 0` fails since len is negative.

Check `len > size` — comparing negative int with size_t. The negative int is implicitly converted to a large unsigned value, so this check may pass (returning -1) or fail depending on size. If size is also very large (e.g. SIZE_MAX), the check fails and execution continues.

state.value: negative int, but converted to large size_t for comparison

step_by_step_execution.description: tp = text + len computes a pointer before the start of the text buffer (since len is negative).

state.value: text + negative offset = before text buffer

step_by_step_execution.description: d = d1[U(tp[-1])] reads from an out-of-bounds location before the text buffer.

commit_message: libnsgif: fix oob in lzw_decode
cve_desc: Heap-based Buffer Overflow in GitHub repository hpjansson/chafa prior to 1.12.0.

vulnerabilities.description: When code_new equals current_entry and current_entry equals (1 << LZW_CODE_MAX), the code enters the 'code not in table' branch. The dictionary-add is skipped because the condition `current_entry < (1 << LZW_CODE_MAX)` is false, so no entry is written at table[current_entry]. However, the code later reads `table[code_new].first_value` where code_new == current_entry == (1 << LZW_CODE_MAX). If the table is allocated with (1 << LZW_CODE_MAX) entries (indices 0 through (1 << LZW_CODE_MAX) - 1), this is an out-of-bounds read.

step_by_step_execution.description: The function reads current_entry from ctx. Assume current_entry has reached (1 << LZW_CODE_MAX) because the table is full.
lzw__next_code returns a new code. Assume code_new equals current_entry.
The check `code_new > current_entry` is false (they are equal), so LZW_BAD_CODE is not returned.
The check `code_new < current_entry` is false (they are equal), so we enter the else branch ('code not in table'). code_out is set to ctx->previous_code.
The dictionary-add condition `current_entry < (1 << LZW_CODE_MAX)` is false, so no entry is written to table[current_entry]. The table entry at index (1 << LZW_CODE_MAX) is never initialized.
The code reads `table[code_new].first_value` where code_new == (1 << LZW_CODE_MAX). This accesses memory beyond the allocated table (table has valid indices 0 to (1 << LZW_CODE_MAX) - 1).

commit_message:
CPU time limit patch / setrlimit(RLIMIT_CPU, 0) cheat fix

As discovered here today, the change in Kernel 2.6.17 intended to inhibit
users from setting RLIMIT_CPU to 0 (as that is equivalent to unlimited) by
"cheating" and setting it to 1 in such a case, does not make a difference,
as the check is done in the wrong place (too late), and only applies to the
profiling code.

On all systems I checked running kernels above 2.6.17, no matter what the
hard and soft CPU time limits were before, a user could escape them by
issuing in the shell (sh/bash/zsh) "ulimit -t 0", and then the user's
process was not ever killed.

Attached is a trivial patch to fix that.  Simply moving the check to a
slightly earlier location (specifically, before the line that actually
assigns the limit - *old_rlim = new_rlim), does the trick.

Do note that at least the zsh (but not ash, dash, or bash) shell has the
problem of "caching" the limits set by the ulimit command, so when running
zsh the fix will not immediately be evident - after entering "ulimit -t 0",
"ulimit -a" will show "-t: cpu time (seconds) 0", even though the actual
limit as returned by getrlimit(...) will be 1.  It can be verified by
opening a subshell (which will not have the values of the parent shell in
cache) and checking in it, or just by running a CPU intensive command like
"echo '65536^1048576' | bc" and verifying that it dumps core after one
second.

Regardless of whether that is a misfeature in the shell, perhaps it would
be better to return -EINVAL from setrlimit in such a case instead of
cheating and setting to 1, as that does not really reflect the actual state
of the process anymore.  I do not however know what the ground for that
decision was in the original 2.6.17 change, and whether there would be any
"backward" compatibility issues, so I preferred not to touch that right
now.

Signed-off-by: Andrew Morton <akpm@linux-foundation.org>
Signed-off-by: Linus Torvalds <torvalds@linux-foundation.org>
cve_desc: Linux kernel 2.6.17, and other versions before 2.6.22, does not check when a user attempts to set RLIMIT_CPU to 0 until after the change is made, which allows local users to bypass intended resource limits.

vulnerabilities.description: There is a TOCTOU (time-of-check-time-of-use) race condition between the permission check on old_rlim->rlim_max and the actual write of the new rlimit. The check reading old_rlim->rlim_max is performed without holding the task_lock, while the write is performed under the lock. Two threads sharing the same signal struct can race: Thread A lowers rlim_max (passes the check since new max <= old max), and Thread B raises rlim_max (reads the old, higher value and also passes the check). Depending on scheduling, Thread B's write can land after Thread A's, resulting in an unprivileged escalation of the hard resource limit.

step_by_step_execution.description: Two threads (A and B) sharing the same signal struct enter sys_setrlimit for the same resource. old_rlim->rlim_max is currently HIGH_VALUE.

Thread A wants to lower the hard limit (new_rlim.rlim_max = LOW_VALUE). It reads old_rlim->rlim_max (HIGH_VALUE) without holding any lock. The check (LOW_VALUE > HIGH_VALUE) is false, so the permission check passes.
Thread A acquires task_lock and writes *old_rlim = new_rlim, setting rlim_max to LOW_VALUE, then releases the lock.

Thread B wants to raise the hard limit back (new_rlim.rlim_max = HIGH_VALUE). However, Thread B already read old_rlim->rlim_max as HIGH_VALUE before Thread A's write (the read happened concurrently without locking). The check (HIGH_VALUE > HIGH_VALUE) is false, so the permission check passes even though the unprivileged thread should not be able to raise rlim_max above the current LOW_VALUE.
Thread B acquires task_lock and writes *old_rlim = new_rlim, setting rlim_max back to HIGH_VALUE, bypassing the intended privilege check.

commit_message:
MDEV-25636: Bug report: abortion in sql/sql_parse.cc:6294

The asserion failure was caused by this query

  select /*id=1*/ from t1
  where
   col= ( select /*id=2*/ from ... where corr_cond1
          union
          select /*id=4*/ from ... where corr_cond2)

Here,
- select with id=2 was correlated due to corr_cond1.
- select with id=4 was initially correlated due to corr_cond2, but then
  the optimizer optimized away the correlation, making the select with id=4
  uncorrelated.

However, since select with id=2 remained correlated, the execution had to
re-compute the whole UNION. When it tried to execute select with id=4, it
hit an assertion  (join buffer already free'd).

This is because select with id=4 has freed its execution structures after
it has been executed once. The select is uncorrelated, so it did not expect
it would need to be executed for the second time.

Fixed this by adding this logic in
st_select_lex::optimize_unflattened_subqueries():

  If a member of a UNION is correlated, mark all its members as
  correlated, so that they are prepared to be executed multiple times.
cve_desc: MariaDB through 10.5.9 allows an application crash in sub_select_postjoin_aggr for a NULL value of aggr.

commit_message:
sftp: Fix bug in sftp_mkdir not returning on error.

resolves: #84
(cherry picked from commit a92c97b2e17715c1b3cdd693d14af6c3311d8e44)
cve_desc: Multiple double free vulnerabilities in the (1) agent_sign_data function in agent.c, (2) channel_request function in channels.c, (3) ssh_userauth_pubkey function in auth.c, (4) sftp_parse_attr_3 function in sftp.c, and (5) try_publickey_from_file function in keyfiles.c in libssh before 0.5.3 allow remote attackers to cause a denial of service (crash) and possibly execute arbitrary code via unspecified vectors.

vulnerabilities.description: When the compound condition in the if-statement is true (any buffer_add or sftp_packet_write fails), both `buffer` and `path` are freed inside the if-block. Execution then falls through (no return statement) to the unconditional `ssh_buffer_free(buffer)` and `ssh_string_free(path)` calls, resulting in a double-free of both objects.

step_by_step_execution.description: A buffer and path string are successfully allocated.
The compound if-condition evaluates to true (e.g., buffer_add_u32 returns < 0).
Inside the if-block, ssh_buffer_free(buffer) and ssh_string_free(path) are called, freeing both objects.
Execution falls through (no return) to the unconditional ssh_buffer_free(buffer) and ssh_string_free(path), freeing the already-freed pointers a second time.

commit_message:
MDEV-25636: Bug report: abortion in sql/sql_parse.cc:6294

The asserion failure was caused by this query

  select /*id=1*/ from t1
  where
   col= ( select /*id=2*/ from ... where corr_cond1
          union
          select /*id=4*/ from ... where corr_cond2)

Here,
- select with id=2 was correlated due to corr_cond1.
- select with id=4 was initially correlated due to corr_cond2, but then
  the optimizer optimized away the correlation, making the select with id=4

However, since select with id=2 remained correlated, the execution had to

hit an assertion  (join buffer already free'd).

This is because select with id=4 has freed its execution structures after
it has been executed once. The select is uncorrelated, so it did not expect
it would need to be executed for the second time.

Fixed this by adding this logic in
st_select_lex::optimize_unflattened_subqueries():

  If a member of a UNION is correlated, mark all its members as
  correlated, so that they are prepared to be executed multiple times.
cve_desc: MariaDB through 10.5.9 allows an application crash in sub_select_postjoin_aggr for a NULL value of aggr.

commit_message:
grep -F: fix a heap buffer (read) overrun

grep's read buffer is often filled to its full size, except when
reading the final buffer of a file.  In that case, the number of
bytes read may be far less than the size of the buffer.  However, for
certain unusual pattern/text combinations, grep -F would mistakenly
examine bytes in that uninitialized region of memory when searching
for a match.  With carefully chosen inputs, one can cause grep -F to
read beyond the end of that buffer altogether.  This problem arose via
commit v2.18-90-g73893ff with the introduction of a more efficient
heuristic using what is now the memchr_kwset function. The use of
that function in bmexec_trans could leave TP much larger than EP,
and the subsequent call to bm_delta2_search would mistakenly access
beyond end of the main input read buffer.

* src/kwset.c (bmexec_trans): When TP reaches or exceeds EP,
do not call bm_delta2_search.
* tests/kwset-abuse: New file.
* tests/Makefile.am (TESTS): Add it.
* THANKS.in: Update.
* NEWS (Bug fixes): Mention it.

Prior to this patch, this command would trigger a UMR:

  printf %0360db 0 | valgrind src/grep -F $(printf %019dXb 0)

  Use of uninitialised value of size 8
     at 0x4142BE: bmexec_trans (kwset.c:657)
     by 0x4143CA: bmexec (kwset.c:678)
     by 0x414973: kwsexec (kwset.c:848)
     by 0x414DC4: Fexecute (kwsearch.c:128)
     by 0x404E2E: grepbuf (grep.c:1238)
     by 0x4054BF: grep (grep.c:1417)
     by 0x405CEB: grepdesc (grep.c:1645)
     by 0x405EC1: grep_command_line_arg (grep.c:1692)
     by 0x4077D4: main (grep.c:2570)

See the accompanying test for how to trigger the heap buffer overrun.

Thanks to Nima Aghdaii for testing and finding numerous
ways to break early iterations of this patch.
cve_desc: The bmexec_trans function in kwset.c in grep 2.19 through 2.21 allows local users to cause a denial of service (out-of-bounds heap read and crash) via crafted input when using the -F option.

vulnerabilities.description: The variable `len` is declared as `int` but `kwset->mind` could be a large unsigned value (e.g., `size_t`). If `kwset->mind` is larger than INT_MAX, the assignment to `int len` causes signed integer overflow / truncation, which could result in `len` becoming a small or negative value. This would bypass the safety checks (`len == 0`, `len > size`) and lead to out-of-bounds memory accesses when `len` is used to compute pointers like `tp = text + len` and array offsets like `sp = kwset->target + len`.

step_by_step_execution.description: kwset->mind is assigned to int len. If kwset->mind is a size_t value larger than INT_MAX (e.g., 0x80000001), len wraps to a negative value.
The check `len == 0` is false since len is negative.

The check `len > size` compares int to size_t. The negative int is converted to a large unsigned value, so for typical `size` values this may be true. However, if size is also very large (e.g., on 64-bit systems where size_t is 64-bit and int is 32-bit), the promotion of negative int to size_t yields a value like 0xFFFFFFFF80000001 which is > size, returning -1. But on systems where int and size_t are both 32 bits, len (negative) promoted to unsigned 32-bit becomes 0x80000001 which could be <= size if size is large enough.

state.value: large enough to pass the check

step_by_step_execution.description: tp = text + len computes a pointer before the start of the text buffer (since len is negative), leading to out-of-bounds read when tp[-1] is accessed.

commit_message:
Remove 'umask' calls from 'mkdir'.

Fixes <http://bugs.gnu.org/24659>.

* libguile/filesys.c (SCM_DEFINE): Remove calls to 'umask' when MODE is
unbound; instead, use 0777 as the mode.  Update docstring to clarify
this.
* doc/ref/posix.texi (File System): Adjust accordingly.
* NEWS: Mention it.
cve_desc: The mkdir procedure of GNU Guile temporarily changed the process' umask to zero. During that time window, in a multithreaded application, other threads could end up creating files with insecure permissions. For example, mkdir without the optional mode argument would create directories as 0777. This is fixed in Guile 2.0.13. Prior versions are affected.

vulnerabilities.description: There is a TOCTOU race condition on the process-wide umask. The code calls umask(0) to read the current umask, then immediately restores it with umask(mask). Between these two calls, any other thread in the process sees a umask of 0, meaning files or directories created by other threads in that window will have overly permissive permissions.

step_by_step_execution.description: The function is called with only the `path` argument; `mode` is unbound.
SCM_UNBNDP(mode) evaluates to true, entering the if-branch to determine the current umask.
umask(0) is called, which atomically sets the process-wide umask to 0 and returns the old value. At this instant the process umask is 0.

Between umask(0) and umask(mask), another thread in the same process may create a file or directory. Because the process umask is currently 0, that file/directory will be created with no permission bits masked, resulting in overly permissive permissions (e.g. 0777).
umask(mask) restores the original umask, but the damage window has already passed.

commit_message: MDEV-24176 fixup: GCC -Wmaybe-uninitialized
cve_desc: MariaDB Server v10.6.5 and below was discovered to contain an use-after-free in the component Item_args::walk_arg, which is exploited via specially crafted SQL statements.

commit_message:
MDEV-26402: A SEGV in Item_field::used_tables/update_depend_map_for_order...

When doing condition pushdown from HAVING into WHERE,
Item_equal::create_pushable_equalities() calls
item->set_extraction_flag(IMMUTABLE_FL) for constant items.
Then, Item::cleanup_excluding_immutables_processor() checks for this flag
to see if it should call item->cleanup() or leave the item as-is.

The failure happens when a constant item has a non-constant one inside it,
like:

   (tbl.col=0 AND impossible_cond)

item->walk(cleanup_excluding_immutables_processor) works in a bottom-up
way so it
1. will call Item_func_eq(tbl.col=0)->cleanup()
2. will not call Item_cond_and->cleanup (as the AND is constant)

This creates an item tree where a fixed Item has an un-fixed Item inside
it which eventually causes an assertion failure.

Fixed by introducing this rule: instead of just calling

  item->set_extraction_flag(IMMUTABLE_FL);

we call Item::walk() to set the flag for all sub-items of the item.
cve_desc: MariaDB Server v10.7 and below was discovered to contain a segmentation fault via the component Item_field::used_tables/update_depend_map_for_order.

commit_message:
Prevent an invalid memory writes in compileRule

Thanks to Han Zheng for reporting it

Fixes #1214
cve_desc: Liblouis 3.21.0 has an out-of-bounds write in compileRule in compileTranslationTable.c, as demonstrated by lou_trace.

vulnerabilities.description: During macro expansion, when substituting arguments into the macro definition, argument characters are copied into tmpFile.line without any bounds check against MAXSTRING. The MAXSTRING overflow check only applies when copying individual definition characters (in the 'else if (tmpFile.linelen >= MAXSTRING)' branch), but the argument substitution loop writes characters directly without verifying that tmpFile.linelen + arg.length stays within bounds. This can cause a heap or stack buffer overflow of the tmpFile.line buffer.

code_snippets:
for (int j = 0; j < arg.length; j++)
									tmpFile.line[tmpFile.linelen++] = arg.chars[j];

step_by_step_execution.description: The function processes a token that matches a macro name, so execution enters the macro expansion block.
Arguments are read from the file and stored in the arguments array.

state.value: array of CharsString, each potentially up to MAXSTRING length

step_by_step_execution.description: Macro expansion loop begins. Definition characters are copied one by one into tmpFile.line. When a non-newline character is encountered, there is a bounds check against MAXSTRING.

state.value: some value < MAXSTRING after definition chars are copied

step_by_step_execution.description: When a substitution point is reached (subst < m->substitution_count), the corresponding argument is fetched and its characters are copied into tmpFile.line in a loop WITHOUT any bounds check.

state.value: could be close to MAXSTRING (e.g. MAXSTRING - 1)

step_by_step_execution.description: The inner loop 'for (int j = 0; j < arg.length; j++) tmpFile.line[tmpFile.linelen++] = arg.chars[j]' writes past the end of tmpFile.line because tmpFile.linelen + arg.length can exceed the buffer size (MAXSTRING).

vulnerabilities.description: When fewer arguments than expected are provided during macro expansion, the function returns 0 without freeing the 'arguments' array that was allocated with malloc, causing a memory leak.

step_by_step_execution.description: A token matching a macro name is found, and execution enters the macro expansion block.
Memory is allocated for the arguments array.
The loop tries to read arguments via getToken, but getToken fails for one of them (e.g. end of line).
The condition argument_count < m->argument_count is true, so the function calls compileError and returns 0 without calling free(arguments).

commit_message:
MDEV-26402: A SEGV in Item_field::used_tables/update_depend_map_for_order...

When doing condition pushdown from HAVING into WHERE,
Item_equal::create_pushable_equalities() calls
item->set_extraction_flag(IMMUTABLE_FL) for constant items.
Then, Item::cleanup_excluding_immutables_processor() checks for this flag
to see if it should call item->cleanup() or leave the item as-is.

The failure happens when a constant item has a non-constant one inside it,
like:

   (tbl.col=0 AND impossible_cond)

item->walk(cleanup_excluding_immutables_processor) works in a bottom-up
way so it
1. will call Item_func_eq(tbl.col=0)->cleanup()
2. will not call Item_cond_and->cleanup (as the AND is constant)

This creates an item tree where a fixed Item has an un-fixed Item inside
it which eventually causes an assertion failure.

Fixed by introducing this rule: instead of just calling

  item->set_extraction_flag(IMMUTABLE_FL);

we call Item::walk() to set the flag for all sub-items of the item.
cve_desc: MariaDB Server v10.7 and below was discovered to contain a segmentation fault via the component Item_field::used_tables/update_depend_map_for_order.

commit_message:
sftp: Fix bug in sftp_mkdir not returning on error.

resolves: #84
(cherry picked from commit a92c97b2e17715c1b3cdd693d14af6c3311d8e44)
cve_desc: Multiple double free vulnerabilities in the (1) agent_sign_data function in agent.c, (2) channel_request function in channels.c, (3) ssh_userauth_pubkey function in auth.c, (4) sftp_parse_attr_3 function in sftp.c, and (5) try_publickey_from_file function in keyfiles.c in libssh before 0.5.3 allow remote attackers to cause a denial of service (crash) and possibly execute arbitrary code via unspecified vectors.

commit_message: Fix bug #72606: heap-buffer-overflow (write) simplestring_addn simplestring.c
cve_desc: Integer signedness error in the simplestring_addn function in simplestring.c in xmlrpc-epi through 0.54.2, as used in PHP before 5.5.38, 5.6.x before 5.6.24, and 7.x before 7.0.9, allows remote attackers to cause a denial of service (heap-based buffer overflow) or possibly have unspecified other impact via a long first argument to the PHP xmlrpc_encode_request function.

vulnerabilities.description: If add_len is negative (the parameter is a signed int), the size check `target->len + add_len + 1 > target->size` will likely evaluate to false, skipping the realloc. Then memcpy is called with a negative add_len which is implicitly converted to a very large size_t, causing a massive heap buffer overflow (read from source and write into target->str).

step_by_step_execution.description: Function is called with a negative add_len, e.g. add_len = -1. target is valid with target->str already initialized, target->len = 10, target->size = 64.
Both target and source are non-null, and target->str is non-null, so the init branch is skipped.
Size check: target->len + add_len + 1 = 10 + (-1) + 1 = 10. 10 > 64 is false, so the realloc block is skipped entirely.
target->str is non-null. add_len is -1 which is non-zero, so the if(add_len) check passes.

memcpy is called. add_len (-1) is implicitly converted to size_t, becoming SIZE_MAX (e.g. 0xFFFFFFFFFFFFFFFF on 64-bit). This causes a massive out-of-bounds read from source and out-of-bounds write to target->str + target->len.

vulnerabilities.description: When target->len and add_len are both large positive ints, their sum can overflow (signed integer overflow is UB, but practically wraps). This can cause the size check to be bypassed or cause a too-small allocation via realloc, followed by a memcpy that writes far beyond the allocated buffer.

step_by_step_execution.description: Function is called with a very large add_len, e.g. add_len = INT_MAX (2147483647). target has target->len = 100, target->size = 200.
Size check computes target->len + add_len + 1 = 100 + 2147483647 + 1. This overflows signed int, resulting in a negative value (e.g. -2147483548).
The comparison -2147483548 > 200 is false, so the realloc branch is entirely skipped. The buffer remains at its original size of 200 bytes.
target->str is non-null and add_len is non-zero, so memcpy is called with add_len = 2147483647 bytes into a buffer of size 200, causing a massive heap buffer overflow.

explicit GROUP BY is not allowed by the grammar.

commit_message:
Submitted by: Tomas Hoger <thoger@redhat.com>

Fix for CVE-2010-0433 where some kerberos enabled versions of OpenSSL
could be crashed if the relevant tables were not present (e.g. chrooted).
cve_desc: The kssl_keytab_is_available function in ssl/kssl.c in OpenSSL before 0.9.8n, when Kerberos is enabled but Kerberos configuration files cannot be opened, does not check a certain return value, which allows remote attackers to cause a denial of service (NULL pointer dereference and daemon crash) via SSL cipher negotiation, as demonstrated by a chroot installation of Dovecot or stunnel without Kerberos configuration files inside the chroot.

code_snippets:
krb5rc = krb5_sname_to_principal(krb5context, NULL, 
                                     kssl_ctx->service_name ? kssl_ctx->service_name: KRB5SVC,
                                     KRB5_NT_SRV_HST, &princ);

    krb5rc = krb5_kt_get_entry(krb5context, krb5keytab, 
                                princ,
                                0 /* IGNORE_VNO */,
                                0 /* IGNORE_ENCTYPE */,
                                &entry);
vulnerabilities.description: If krb5_sname_to_principal() fails, its error code in krb5rc is never checked before princ (which remains NULL or is in an indeterminate state) is passed directly to krb5_kt_get_entry(). This leads to a NULL pointer dereference or use of an invalid principal.

code_snippets:
krb5rc = krb5_kt_get_entry(krb5context, krb5keytab, 
                                princ,
                                0 /* IGNORE_VNO */,
                                0 /* IGNORE_ENCTYPE */,
                                &entry);

step_by_step_execution.description: krb5_init_context succeeds, krb5context is valid.
Keytab is resolved or defaulted successfully.
krb5_sname_to_principal is called but fails (e.g., due to DNS resolution failure or memory allocation failure). princ remains NULL, but krb5rc is set to a non-zero error code.
The return value of krb5_sname_to_principal (krb5rc) is never checked. Execution falls through directly to krb5_kt_get_entry, which receives princ=NULL.
krb5_kt_get_entry dereferences the NULL princ pointer, causing a NULL pointer dereference / crash.

commit_message:
tipc: fix size validations for the MSG_CRYPTO type

The function tipc_crypto_key_rcv is used to parse MSG_CRYPTO messages
to receive keys from other nodes in the cluster in order to decrypt any
further messages from them.
This patch verifies that any supplied sizes in the message body are
valid for the received message.

Fixes: 1ef6f7c9390f ("tipc: add automatic session key exchange")
Signed-off-by: Max VA <maxv@sentinelone.com>
Acked-by: Ying Xue <ying.xue@windriver.com>
Signed-off-by: Greg Kroah-Hartman <gregkh@linuxfoundation.org>
Acked-by: Jon Maloy <jmaloy@redhat.com>
Signed-off-by: David S. Miller <davem@davemloft.net>
cve_desc: An issue was discovered in net/tipc/crypto.c in the Linux kernel before 5.14.16. The Transparent Inter-Process Communication (TIPC) functionality allows remote attackers to exploit insufficient validation of user-supplied sizes for the MSG_CRYPTO message type.

vulnerabilities.description: The algorithm name is copied from attacker-controlled network data without ensuring null-termination. If the received data contains TIPC_AEAD_ALG_NAME bytes of non-null characters, the resulting skey->alg_name will not be null-terminated. When this field is later used as a C string (e.g., passed to crypto API functions), it will result in an out-of-bounds read past the buffer.

step_by_step_execution.description: The function receives a network message. `size` and `data` are extracted from the message header.
The minimum size check passes because size >= sizeof(struct tipc_aead_key) + TIPC_AEAD_KEYLEN_MIN.
keylen is read from the data at offset TIPC_AEAD_ALG_NAME.
The size validation check passes: size == keylen + sizeof(struct tipc_aead_key) and keylen <= TIPC_AEAD_KEY_SIZE_MAX.
skey is allocated with kmalloc(size, GFP_ATOMIC). The memory is uninitialized.

state.value: pointer to kmalloc'd buffer of `size` bytes

step_by_step_execution.description: The alg_name field is copied from attacker-controlled data. The attacker fills all TIPC_AEAD_ALG_NAME bytes with non-null characters (e.g., 'AAAA...A'). No null terminator is appended.

state.value: TIPC_AEAD_ALG_NAME bytes of non-null data, NOT null-terminated

step_by_step_execution.description: When skey->alg_name is later consumed as a C string (e.g., by crypto_alloc_aead or similar), string functions read past the end of alg_name causing an out-of-bounds read.

state.value: treated as unbounded string, reads beyond buffer

commit_message: Fixed: HTML escape the log file content when viewed via Monit GUI.
cve_desc: Persistent cross-site scripting (XSS) in http/cervlet.c in Tildeslash Monit before 5.25.3 allows a remote unauthenticated attacker to introduce arbitrary JavaScript via manipulation of an unsanitized user field of the Authorization header for HTTP Basic Authentication, which is mishandled during an _viewlog operation.

commit_message: auth: mech-rpa - Fail on zero len buffer
cve_desc: In Dovecot before 2.3.11.3, sending a specially formatted RPA request will crash the auth service because a length of zero is mishandled.

vulnerabilities.description: When `p` equals `end`, the guard `p > end` does not trigger, and the code proceeds to dereference `p` via `len = *p++`. Since `end` conventionally points one past the last valid byte, reading `*p` when `p == end` is an out-of-bounds read.

step_by_step_execution.description: The function is called with `*data` pointing to the same address as `end` (i.e., no bytes remaining in the buffer).

state.value: one past the last valid byte of the input buffer

step_by_step_execution.description: The guard `p > end` is evaluated. Since `p == end`, the condition is false, so execution continues.
`len = *p++` dereferences `p`, which equals `end` — one past the last valid byte. This is an out-of-bounds read.

commit_message:
Prevent an invalid memory writes in compileRule

Thanks to Han Zheng for reporting it

Fixes #1214
cve_desc: Liblouis 3.21.0 has an out-of-bounds write in compileRule in compileTranslationTable.c, as demonstrated by lou_trace.

vulnerabilities.description: During macro expansion, when substituting arguments into the definition, the code writes argument characters into `tmpFile.line` without checking whether `tmpFile.linelen` exceeds `MAXSTRING`. While individual definition characters are bounds-checked against `MAXSTRING`, argument substitution copies are not, leading to a heap or stack buffer overflow.

code_snippets:
for (int j = 0; j < arg.length; j++)
									tmpFile.line[tmpFile.linelen++] = arg.chars[j];

step_by_step_execution.description: The function reads a token that matches a defined macro name, entering the macro expansion code path.

state.value: matches m->name for some macro m

step_by_step_execution.description: Arguments are read from the file for the macro. Each argument is a CharsString which can be up to MAXSTRING characters long.

state.value: up to MAXSTRING for each argument

step_by_step_execution.description: The macro expansion loop iterates over the macro definition. For each definition character, it checks `tmpFile.linelen >= MAXSTRING` before writing.

state.value: incremented for each definition character, bounded by MAXSTRING check

step_by_step_execution.description: When a substitution point is reached, the code copies the entire argument into tmpFile.line WITHOUT any bounds check on tmpFile.linelen.

state.value: could be close to MAXSTRING before substitution

step_by_step_execution.description: If tmpFile.linelen was, e.g., MAXSTRING-1 after writing definition chars, and arg.length is large, the loop writes well past the end of tmpFile.line, causing a buffer overflow.

vulnerabilities.description: The `arguments` array allocated with malloc() during macro expansion is never freed on any code path (success or failure), resulting in a memory leak. While not directly exploitable for code execution, repeated invocations can exhaust memory.

step_by_step_execution.description: A token matching a macro name is found, entering the macro expansion block.
Memory is allocated for the arguments array.
Arguments are parsed and the macro is expanded. On the success path, the function returns 1.
On the error path (e.g. compileError for insufficient arguments), the function returns 0 without freeing arguments.

allocation attempts in TIFFReadDirEntryArray() on short files.
Effective for mmap'ed case. And non-mmap'ed case, but restricted
