Fixes http://bugzilla.maptools.org/show_bug.cgi?id=2675
cve_desc: The TIFFReadDirEntryArray function in tif_read.c in LibTIFF 4.0.8 mishandles memory allocation for short files, which allows remote attackers to cause a denial of service (allocation failure and application crash) in the TIFFFetchStripThing function in tif_dirread.c during a tiff2pdf invocation.

commit_message:
sctp: correct bounds check in sctp_setsockopt_auth_key

The bonds check to prevent buffer overlflow was not exactly
right.  It still allowed overflow of up to 8 bytes which is
sizeof(struct sctp_authkey).

Since optlen is already checked against the size of that struct,
we are guaranteed not to cause interger overflow either.

Signed-off-by: Vlad Yasevich <vladislav.yasevich@hp.com>
Signed-off-by: David S. Miller <davem@davemloft.net>
cve_desc: Integer overflow in the sctp_setsockopt_auth_key function in net/sctp/socket.c in the Stream Control Transmission Protocol (sctp) implementation in the Linux kernel 2.6.24-rc1 through 2.6.26.3 allows remote attackers to cause a denial of service (panic) or possibly have unspecified other impact via a crafted sca_keylength field associated with the SCTP_AUTH_KEY option.

vulnerabilities.description: The validation of sca_keylength checks it against the total optlen, but sca_key is a flexible array member at the end of struct sctp_authkey. The key data starts at an offset of sizeof(struct sctp_authkey) within the allocated buffer. By setting sca_keylength to a value between (optlen - sizeof(struct sctp_authkey)) and optlen, the subsequent sctp_auth_set_key() call will read sca_keylength bytes starting from authkey->sca_key, overrunning the heap buffer by up to sizeof(struct sctp_authkey) bytes. The correct check should be sca_keylength > optlen - sizeof(struct sctp_authkey).

step_by_step_execution.description: The function checks that optlen > sizeof(struct sctp_authkey). Suppose optlen = sizeof(struct sctp_authkey) + 1.
A buffer of optlen bytes is allocated via kmalloc and filled from userspace via copy_from_user.

state.value: heap buffer of (sizeof(struct sctp_authkey) + 1) bytes, fully user-controlled content

step_by_step_execution.description: User sets authkey->sca_keylength to optlen (sizeof(struct sctp_authkey) + 1). The check 'sca_keylength > optlen' is false, so validation passes.

sctp_auth_set_key is called and will process sca_keylength bytes starting from authkey->sca_key. The sca_key field is at offset sizeof(struct sctp_authkey) within the buffer, so it reads from offset sizeof(struct sctp_authkey) to offset sizeof(struct sctp_authkey) + sca_keylength - 1, which is 2*sizeof(struct sctp_authkey) bytes from the start of the buffer.

state.value: up to sizeof(struct sctp_authkey) bytes beyond the allocated heap buffer

commit_message: fix oob write bug inside libmobi
cve_desc: libmobi is vulnerable to Out-of-bounds Write

vulnerabilities.description: When offset is negative, the code only checks that `buf->offset >= aoffset` (source doesn't underflow), but does NOT check that `buf->offset + len <= buf->maxlen` (destination doesn't overflow) or that the source read region `[buf->offset - aoffset, buf->offset - aoffset + len)` stays within the buffer. An attacker can supply a negative offset and a large `len` to trigger an out-of-bounds read and write via `memmove`.

step_by_step_execution.description: Function is called with a negative offset (e.g., offset = -1) and a large len. Suppose buf->offset = 10, buf->maxlen = 100, offset = -1, len = 200.
aoffset is computed as abs(-1) = 1. source is set to buf->data + 10.
offset < 0, so we enter the else branch. Check: buf->offset (10) < aoffset (1) is false, so we proceed.
source is decremented by aoffset: source = buf->data + 10 - 1 = buf->data + 9.

memmove is called: memmove(buf->data + 10, buf->data + 9, 200). This reads 200 bytes from buf->data + 9 and writes 200 bytes to buf->data + 10, both far exceeding buf->maxlen of 100. This is a heap-based buffer overflow (write) and out-of-bounds read.

vulnerabilities.description: If `offset` equals `INT_MIN`, `abs(INT_MIN)` is undefined behavior in C because the result cannot be represented as an `int`. This can lead to `aoffset` being an unexpected value (e.g., INT_MIN cast to size_t becomes a very large number), potentially bypassing subsequent bounds checks.

step_by_step_execution.description: Function is called with offset = INT_MIN (-2147483648 on 32-bit int).
abs(INT_MIN) is undefined behavior. On many implementations, abs(INT_MIN) returns INT_MIN. Cast to size_t yields a very large value (2147483648 on 32-bit, or larger on 64-bit).

offset >= 0 is false, so we enter else branch. Check: buf->offset < aoffset — with aoffset being extremely large, this is almost certainly true, so we'd return with MOBI_BUFFER_END. However, the undefined behavior itself means the compiler may optimize this code in unexpected ways, potentially skipping the bounds check entirely.

state.value: very large or unpredictable due to UB

commit_message:
lib-smtp: smtp-command-parser - Fix infinite loop occurring when command stream ends in UTF-8 character.

Broken by 8f08f1944be438a2422b604c08e5060b5c7bd72f.
cve_desc: lib-smtp in submission-login and lmtp in Dovecot 2.3.9 before 2.3.9.3 mishandles truncated UTF-8 data in command parameters, as demonstrated by the unauthenticated triggering of a submission-login infinite loop.

vulnerabilities.description: The length argument to uni_utf8_get_char_n is computed as `(size_t)(p - parser->end)`. Since the while loop guarantees `p < parser->end`, the expression `p - parser->end` is negative. Casting a negative value to `size_t` (an unsigned type) results in integer wraparound, producing a very large value. This tells uni_utf8_get_char_n that a huge buffer is available, potentially causing it to read far beyond the actual end of the buffer.

step_by_step_execution.description: The function begins and computes `p = parser->cur + parser->state.poff`. The while loop condition `p < parser->end` is checked and is true.

state.value: pointer to end of valid buffer data

step_by_step_execution.description: Since `parser->auth_response` is false, the else branch is taken, calling `uni_utf8_get_char_n(p, (size_t)(p - parser->end), &ch)`.

state.value: very large unsigned value due to integer wraparound (e.g., 0xFFFFFFFFFFFFFFF6 on 64-bit)

step_by_step_execution.description: uni_utf8_get_char_n receives an enormous size parameter, believing the buffer extends far beyond parser->end. If the UTF-8 sequence starting at `p` indicates a multi-byte character, the function may read bytes past parser->end to decode it.

state.value: reads beyond parser->end, out-of-bounds heap/buffer read

commit_message:
Fix race condition in NewSessionTicket

If a NewSessionTicket is received by a multi-threaded client when
attempting to reuse a previous ticket then a race condition can occur
potentially leading to a double free of the ticket data.

CVE-2015-1791

This also fixes RT#3808 where a session ID is changed for a session already
in the client session cache. Since the session ID is the key to the cache
this breaks the cache access.

Parts of this patch were inspired by this Akamai change:
https://github.com/akamai/openssl/commit/c0bf69a791239ceec64509f9f19fcafb2461b0d3

Reviewed-by: Rich Salz <rsalz@openssl.org>
(cherry picked from commit 27c76b9b8010b536687318739c6f631ce4194688)

Conflicts:
	ssl/ssl.h
	ssl/ssl_err.c
cve_desc: Race condition in the ssl3_get_new_session_ticket function in ssl/s3_clnt.c in OpenSSL before 0.9.8zg, 1.0.0 before 1.0.0s, 1.0.1 before 1.0.1n, and 1.0.2 before 1.0.2b, when used for a multi-threaded client, allows remote attackers to cause a denial of service (double free and application crash) or possibly have unspecified other impact by providing a NewSessionTicket during an attempt to reuse a ticket that had been obtained earlier.

commit_message:
gzip -d: do not clobber stack for valid input on x86_64

* unlzw.c (unlzw): Avoid integer overflow.
Aki Helin reported the segfault along with an input to trigger the bug.
* NEWS (Bug fixes): Mention it.
cve_desc: Integer underflow in the unlzw function in unlzw.c in gzip before 1.4 on 64-bit platforms, as used in ncompress and probably others, allows remote attackers to cause a denial of service (application crash) or possibly execute arbitrary code via a crafted archive that uses LZW compression, leading to an array index error.

vulnerabilities.description: The while loop that traces back through the prefix table has no bounds check on `stackp`. If a crafted/corrupt compressed input creates a cycle in the `tab_prefix` table (where prefix entries reference each other in a loop), `stackp` will be decremented indefinitely past the beginning of the `de_stack` buffer, causing an out-of-bounds write. The only check before this loop is `code > free_ent`, which rejects codes strictly above `free_ent` but allows codes equal to `free_ent` and all valid-looking codes below it, so a circular chain among previously-inserted entries is not detected.

step_by_step_execution.description: stackp is initialized to point to the top of de_stack buffer.

A crafted input provides valid-looking LZW codes that populate tab_prefix entries such that a cycle is formed, e.g., tab_prefixof(A) = B and tab_prefixof(B) = A, where A >= 256 and B >= 256, and both A, B < free_ent.
When a code referencing one of the cycle members is decoded, the code passes the `code > free_ent` check (since code < free_ent) and enters the while loop.
The while loop follows tab_prefix chains: code bounces between A and B indefinitely since both are >= 256, and stackp is decremented each iteration with no bounds check.

state.value: Decreasing continuously past de_stack buffer boundary

step_by_step_execution.description: Writes via `*--stackp = tab_suffixof(code)` go to memory before the de_stack buffer, corrupting adjacent memory (out-of-bounds write).

state.value: Far below de_stack start — arbitrary memory before buffer

commit_message: MDEV-26323 use-after-poison issue of MariaDB server
cve_desc: MariaDB Server v10.6 and below was discovered to contain an use-after-free in the component my_strcasecmp_8bit, which is exploited via specially crafted SQL statements.

code_snippets:
char *s= (char*)alloc_root(root, dl->length + so_ext_len + 1);
    memcpy(s, dl->str, dl->length);
vulnerabilities.description: If alloc_root() fails and returns NULL, the subsequent memcpy and strcpy will dereference a NULL pointer, leading to a crash (denial of service). There is no NULL check on the return value of alloc_root().

step_by_step_execution.description: The function is called with a valid dl that does not end with SO_EXT, so the if-condition is true.
alloc_root is called to allocate memory. Under memory pressure, it returns NULL.
memcpy(s, dl->str, dl->length) is called with s == NULL, causing a NULL pointer dereference.

commit_message:
hfsplus: check read_mapping_page() return value

While testing more corrupted images with hfsplus, i came across
one which triggered the following bug:

[15840.675016] BUG: unable to handle kernel paging request at fffffffb
[15840.675016] IP: [<c0116a4f>] kmap+0x15/0x56
[15840.675016] *pde = 00008067 *pte = 00000000
[15840.675016] Oops: 0000 [#1] PREEMPT DEBUG_PAGEALLOC

[15840.675016]

[15840.675016]        000007dd cab0bcf4 cab0bd04 c022cb3a cab0bcf4 cab15a6c ca9290c0 00000000
[15840.675016] Call Trace:
[15840.675016]  [<c0231cfb>] ? hfsplus_block_allocate+0x6f/0x2d3
