[15840.675016]  =======================
[15840.675016] Code: 00 00 75 10 83 3d 88 2f ec c0 02 75 07 89 d0 e8 12 56 05 00 5d c3 55 ba 06 00 00 00 89 e5 53 89 c3 b8 3d eb 7e c0 e8 16 74 00 00 <8b> 03 c1 e8 1e 69 c0 d8 02 00 00 05 b8 69 8e c0 2b 80 c4 02 00
[15840.675016] EIP: [<c0116a4f>] kmap+0x15/0x56 SS:ESP 0068:cab0bc94
[15840.675016] ---[ end trace 4fea40dad6b70e5f ]---

This happens because the return value of read_mapping_page() is passed on
to kmap unchecked.  The bug is triggered after the first
read_mapping_page() in hfsplus_block_allocate(), this patch fixes all
three usages in this functions but leaves the ones further down in the
file unchanged.

Signed-off-by: Eric Sesterhenn <snakebyte@gmx.de>
Cc: Roman Zippel <zippel@linux-m68k.org>
Signed-off-by: Andrew Morton <akpm@linux-foundation.org>
Signed-off-by: Linus Torvalds <torvalds@linux-foundation.org>
cve_desc: The hfsplus_block_allocate function in fs/hfsplus/bitmap.c in the Linux kernel before 2.6.28-rc1 does not check a certain return value from the read_mapping_page function before calling kmap, which allows attackers to cause a denial of service (system crash) via a crafted hfsplus filesystem image.

vulnerabilities.description: In the block allocation loop (after the 'found' label, in the 'do full u32s' section), when a new page is mapped, the 'end' pointer is always set to the full page size (pptr + PAGE_CACHE_BITS / 32) without checking whether the bitmap size ('size') ends within this page. In contrast, the search loop correctly checks '(size ^ offset) / PAGE_CACHE_BITS' to cap 'end' when the bitmap ends mid-page. This means the allocation loop can read and write u32 values beyond the valid bitmap region on the last page, corrupting filesystem metadata.

step_by_step_execution.description: The function is called with a 'size' that does not align to PAGE_CACHE_BITS, so the bitmap occupies a partial last page.

state.value: e.g. PAGE_CACHE_BITS + 64 (bitmap extends only 64 bits into the second page)

step_by_step_execution.description: A free bit is found near the end of the first page or the start of the second page via the search loop. The 'found' label is reached with 'len' still large.
In the 'do full u32s' allocation section, curr reaches end of the current page, so the code unmaps the page and reads a new page.

The new page is mapped and 'end' is set to pptr + PAGE_CACHE_BITS / 32 (full page), without checking if 'size' falls within this page. Since size = PAGE_CACHE_BITS + 64, only 2 u32s are valid bitmap data on this page, but end points to the full page boundary.
The inner while loop iterates curr from pptr to end, writing 0xffffffff to u32s well beyond the 2 valid bitmap entries, corrupting data stored after the bitmap on that page.

commit_message:
flxdec: Don't unref() parent in the chain function

We don't own the reference here, it is owned by the caller and given to
us for the scope of this function. Leftover mistake from 0.10 porting.

https://bugzilla.gnome.org/show_bug.cgi?id=774897
cve_desc: The gst_decode_chain_free_internal function in the flxdex decoder in gst-plugins-good in GStreamer before 1.10.2 allows remote attackers to cause a denial of service (invalid memory read and crash) via an invalid file, which triggers an incorrect unref call.

vulnerabilities.description: Integer overflow in computing flxdec->size from attacker-controlled width and height header fields. If width * height overflows a guint (32-bit), flxdec->size becomes much smaller than intended. Subsequent g_malloc allocations are undersized, and operations like flx_decode_chunks, memcpy, and flx_colorspace_convert write beyond the allocated buffers. Additionally, flxdec->size * 4 can itself overflow, leading to an undersized output buffer.

step_by_step_execution.description: A crafted FLX file is fed to the decoder. The adapter accumulates enough data for the header.
The header is read and endianness-fixed. The type field passes validation (e.g., FLX_MAGICHDR_FLC).
flxdec->size is computed as (guint)width * (guint)height. 65536 * 65536 = 0x100000000, which overflows a 32-bit guint to 0.
g_malloc(flxdec->size) is called for frame_data and delta_data, allocating 0-byte (or minimal) buffers.
The colorspace converter is created with the original (large) width and height values.
During playback, flx_decode_chunks writes decoded frame data into flxdec->frame_data based on the original dimensions, causing a heap buffer overflow on the undersized buffer.

state.value: 0-byte buffer being written with up to 65536*65536 bytes

vulnerabilities.description: Integer underflow when computing the chunk data size. If a crafted file provides flxfh.size < FlxFrameChunkSize, the subtraction wraps around to a very large value (unsigned), causing gst_adapter_take to attempt reading a huge amount of data or producing a massive allocation.

step_by_step_execution.description: The decoder is in GST_FLXDEC_PLAYING state and the adapter has sufficient data for the frame chunk header.
The frame chunk header is read. The attacker sets flxfh.size to a small value (e.g. 4) and flxfh.id to FLX_FRAME_TYPE.
The check 'avail < flxfh.size' passes since avail >= FlxFrameChunkSize > 4.
The adapter flushes FlxFrameChunkSize bytes, then computes flxfh.size - FlxFrameChunkSize. Since both are unsigned and flxfh.size < FlxFrameChunkSize, this wraps to a huge value (e.g. 0xFFFFFFFA).
gst_adapter_take is called with this huge size, potentially causing a massive heap allocation or out-of-bounds read from the adapter's internal buffers.

state.value: result of gst_adapter_take with ~4GB size request

commit_message:
CPU time limit patch / setrlimit(RLIMIT_CPU, 0) cheat fix

As discovered here today, the change in Kernel 2.6.17 intended to inhibit
users from setting RLIMIT_CPU to 0 (as that is equivalent to unlimited) by
"cheating" and setting it to 1 in such a case, does not make a difference,
as the check is done in the wrong place (too late), and only applies to the
profiling code.

On all systems I checked running kernels above 2.6.17, no matter what the
hard and soft CPU time limits were before, a user could escape them by
issuing in the shell (sh/bash/zsh) "ulimit -t 0", and then the user's
process was not ever killed.

Attached is a trivial patch to fix that.  Simply moving the check to a
slightly earlier location (specifically, before the line that actually
assigns the limit - *old_rlim = new_rlim), does the trick.

Do note that at least the zsh (but not ash, dash, or bash) shell has the
problem of "caching" the limits set by the ulimit command, so when running
zsh the fix will not immediately be evident - after entering "ulimit -t 0",
"ulimit -a" will show "-t: cpu time (seconds) 0", even though the actual
limit as returned by getrlimit(...) will be 1.  It can be verified by
opening a subshell (which will not have the values of the parent shell in
cache) and checking in it, or just by running a CPU intensive command like
"echo '65536^1048576' | bc" and verifying that it dumps core after one
second.

Regardless of whether that is a misfeature in the shell, perhaps it would
be better to return -EINVAL from setrlimit in such a case instead of
cheating and setting to 1, as that does not really reflect the actual state
of the process anymore.  I do not however know what the ground for that
decision was in the original 2.6.17 change, and whether there would be any
"backward" compatibility issues, so I preferred not to touch that right
now.

Signed-off-by: Andrew Morton <akpm@linux-foundation.org>
Signed-off-by: Linus Torvalds <torvalds@linux-foundation.org>
cve_desc: Linux kernel 2.6.17, and other versions before 2.6.22, does not check when a user attempts to set RLIMIT_CPU to 0 until after the change is made, which allows local users to bypass intended resource limits.

vulnerabilities.description: A TOCTOU race condition exists because old_rlim->rlim_max is read without holding task_lock for the permission check, but the actual write happens later under task_lock. A concurrent thread sharing the same signal struct (e.g., another thread in the same thread group) can modify rlim_max between the check and the write. An unprivileged thread can exploit a privileged thread's temporary raising of rlim_max to bypass the capability check and set an arbitrarily high rlim_max.

step_by_step_execution.description: Initial state: rlim_max for a resource is set to 100. Two threads (A and B) share the same signal struct. Thread A has CAP_SYS_RESOURCE, Thread B does not.
Thread A (privileged) enters sys_setrlimit wanting to raise rlim_max to 200. It reads old_rlim->rlim_max = 100, and since it has CAP_SYS_RESOURCE, the check passes.
Thread A acquires task_lock and writes *old_rlim = new_rlim, setting rlim_max to 200, then releases task_lock.

Thread B (unprivileged) enters sys_setrlimit wanting to set rlim_max to 200. It reads old_rlim->rlim_max without holding any lock and sees 200. Since new_rlim.rlim_max (200) <= old_rlim->rlim_max (200), the permission check passes without needing CAP_SYS_RESOURCE.
Meanwhile, Thread A (or another privileged thread) lowers rlim_max back to 100. This write completes under task_lock.

Thread B now acquires task_lock and writes *old_rlim = new_rlim, setting rlim_max back to 200. The unprivileged thread has successfully raised rlim_max beyond the value the administrator intended (100), because its permission check used a stale value.

commit_message:
lib-smtp: smtp-command-parser - Fix infinite loop occurring when command stream ends in UTF-8 character.

Broken by 8f08f1944be438a2422b604c08e5060b5c7bd72f.
cve_desc: lib-smtp in submission-login and lmtp in Dovecot 2.3.9 before 2.3.9.3 mishandles truncated UTF-8 data in command parameters, as demonstrated by the unauthenticated triggering of a submission-login infinite loop.

vulnerabilities.description: The size argument passed to uni_utf8_get_char_n is computed as `(size_t)(p - parser->end)`. Since the loop condition guarantees `p < parser->end`, the expression `p - parser->end` is always negative. Casting a negative value to `size_t` (an unsigned type) wraps it to a very large positive value, causing uni_utf8_get_char_n to believe there is a massive buffer available. This allows reading past the actual end of the buffer (parser->end), resulting in an out-of-bounds read. The correct expression should be `parser->end - p`.

step_by_step_execution.description: The function enters and `parser->auth_response` is false, so the non-auth-response code path will be taken inside the loop.
The loop begins with `p = parser->cur + parser->state.poff`. The loop condition `p < parser->end` is satisfied, so we enter the loop body.

state.value: pointer past the last valid byte in the buffer

step_by_step_execution.description: Since auth_response is false, the else branch is taken. The size argument is computed as `(size_t)(p - parser->end)`. Because p < parser->end, `p - parser->end` is a negative number (e.g., -5 if there are 5 bytes remaining).

state.value: wraps to a very large unsigned value, e.g. 0xFFFFFFFFFFFFFFFB on 64-bit

step_by_step_execution.description: uni_utf8_get_char_n is called with this enormous size parameter. If the data at `p` starts a multi-byte UTF-8 sequence, the function may read bytes beyond parser->end, as it believes the buffer is enormous.
Out-of-bounds read occurs: bytes past parser->end are accessed by uni_utf8_get_char_n, potentially reading unmapped memory (crash) or sensitive data from adjacent memory.

commit_message: libclamav/mspack.c: fix Quantum decompressor (bb#1771)
cve_desc: The qtm_decompress function in libclamav/mspack.c in ClamAV before 0.96 allows remote attackers to cause a denial of service (memory corruption and application crash) via a crafted CAB archive that uses the Quantum (aka .Q) compression format.  NOTE: some of these details are obtained from third party information.

vulnerabilities.description: Out-of-bounds write on the window buffer. In both match copy paths, the number of bytes actually copied can be less than match_length (due to clamping in the non-wrapping case, or no clamping in the wrapping case), but window_posn is always advanced by the full match_length. This causes window_posn to exceed window_size, and on the next loop iteration, writes via window[window_posn++] go out of bounds. Additionally, in the wrapping match case (match_offset > window_posn), there is no check that window_posn + match_length <= window_size, so the copy itself can write past the end of the window buffer.

step_by_step_execution.description: Function enters with valid qtm stream and positive out_bytes. Local state is restored including window_posn and window (buffer of size qtm->window_size).
frame_end is computed. Suppose it is set to a value greater than window_posn, allowing the inner loop to proceed.

state.value: e.g. window_size + 100 (no clamp to window_size is performed)

step_by_step_execution.description: Inner loop iterates: selector >= 4 is decoded (e.g. selector=6 for variable length match), yielding a match_length of, say, 20 and a valid match_offset <= window_posn.

rundest is set to &window[4094]. i = 20. Since match_offset (10) <= window_posn (4094), the else branch is taken. i is clamped: qtm->window_size - window_posn = 2, so i becomes 2. Only 2 bytes are copied within bounds.
After the copy loop, window_posn += match_length adds the original unclamped match_length (20), not the clamped i (2).

On the next iteration of while (window_posn < frame_end), if frame_end > 4114 or in the wrapping case on re-entry, window[window_posn++] writes to window[4114] which is beyond the allocated buffer, causing a heap buffer overflow.

state.value: write to heap memory beyond allocated window buffer

commit_message: Fix: index entry label not being zero-terminated with corrupt input
cve_desc: Buffer Over-read in GitHub repository bfabiszewski/libmobi prior to 0.11.

vulnerabilities.description: Integer overflow in the multiplication `tagx->tags_count * sizeof(MOBIPtagx)` (and similarly for `sizeof(MOBIIndexTag)`) can wrap around on 32-bit systems if `tagx->tags_count` is large enough, resulting in a small heap allocation. The subsequent loop iterates up to `tagx->tags_count` times, writing into `ptagx[ptagx_count]` (or `indx->entries[entry_number].tags[i]`) and causing a heap buffer overflow.

step_by_step_execution.description: The function receives a tagx structure whose tags_count field is attacker-controlled from a parsed MOBI file.

state.value: Large value, e.g. 0x18000000 on a 32-bit system

step_by_step_execution.description: The code checks `tagx->tags_count > 0` and enters the allocation branch.

malloc is called with `tagx->tags_count * sizeof(MOBIPtagx)`. sizeof(MOBIPtagx) is at least 10 bytes (likely 12 with padding). On a 32-bit system where size_t is 32 bits, 0x18000000 * 12 = 0x120000000, which wraps to 0x20000000 (or even smaller depending on exact struct size). With sizeof 11, 0x18000000 * 11 = 0x108000000 wrapping to 0x08000000.

state.value: Wrapped-around small value due to integer overflow, e.g. a few hundred MB or less

step_by_step_execution.description: The while loop iterates with `i < tagx->tags_count`. For each iteration where the tag's value is non-zero, ptagx_count is incremented and a write to ptagx[ptagx_count] occurs.

Since ptagx_count can grow up to the original tagx->tags_count which is much larger than the actual allocated array size (due to the integer overflow), the writes to ptagx[ptagx_count] go out of bounds of the heap allocation, causing a heap buffer overflow.

commit_message:
flxdec: Don't unref() parent in the chain function

We don't own the reference here, it is owned by the caller and given to
us for the scope of this function. Leftover mistake from 0.10 porting.

https://bugzilla.gnome.org/show_bug.cgi?id=774897
cve_desc: The gst_decode_chain_free_internal function in the flxdex decoder in gst-plugins-good in GStreamer before 1.10.2 allows remote attackers to cause a denial of service (invalid memory read and crash) via an invalid file, which triggers an incorrect unref call.

vulnerabilities.description: Integer overflow in computing flxdec->size from attacker-controlled width and height header fields. If width*height overflows a guint (32-bit), a small buffer is allocated but subsequent operations (flx_decode_chunks, memcpy, flx_colorspace_convert) write based on the actual dimensions, causing heap buffer overflows. Additionally, flxdec->size * 4 can overflow, allocating a tiny output buffer.

step_by_step_execution.description: Function enters GST_FLXDEC_READ_HEADER state with enough data available. Header is read from adapter.
Header fields are parsed from attacker-controlled input. Width and height are set to large values, e.g. 65535 each.
Size is computed as (guint)width * (guint)height. 65535 * 65535 = 0xFFFE0001 which fits in 32-bit, but with slightly different values (e.g. 65536*65536) or when multiplied by 4 it overflows.

state.value: Overflowed/wrapped 32-bit value, e.g. for size*4 overflow: width=32768, height=32768 -> size=0x40000000, size*4=0x100000000 wraps to 0

step_by_step_execution.description: g_malloc(flxdec->size) allocates based on the (possibly valid but large) size, but gst_buffer_new_and_alloc(flxdec->size * 4) allocates 0 bytes due to overflow of the *4 multiplication.
flx_colorspace_convert writes width*height*4 bytes into the output buffer that was allocated with size 0, causing a heap buffer overflow.

vulnerabilities.description: Integer underflow when computing chunk size. If attacker sets flxfh.size to a value smaller than FlxFrameChunkSize, the subtraction wraps to a huge value, causing gst_adapter_take to attempt reading a massive amount of memory or returning a buffer with unexpected size.

step_by_step_execution.description: Decoder is in GST_FLXDEC_PLAYING state. A frame chunk header is read from the adapter.
The chunk header has id == FLX_FRAME_TYPE and a small size field (e.g. 2), which is less than FlxFrameChunkSize.
The check 'avail < flxfh.size' passes since avail >= FlxFrameChunkSize > 2. The adapter flushes FlxFrameChunkSize bytes.
gst_adapter_take is called with flxfh.size - FlxFrameChunkSize. Since flxfh.size (2) < FlxFrameChunkSize (e.g. 6), unsigned subtraction wraps to a very large value like 0xFFFFFFFC.
This causes gst_adapter_take to read far beyond available data or allocate a huge buffer, leading to undefined behavior or heap corruption.

vulnerabilities.description: When flxh->depth > 8, flxdec->converter is never initialized (remains NULL or uninitialized), but flx_colorspace_convert is called unconditionally during frame decoding, leading to a NULL pointer dereference.

step_by_step_execution.description: Header is parsed with depth > 8, so the converter is not created.
State transitions to GST_FLXDEC_PLAYING. A FLX_FRAME_TYPE frame is processed.
flx_colorspace_convert is called with flxdec->converter which is NULL, causing a NULL pointer dereference.

commit_message: squash heap-buffer-overflow, PoC TIFF from Hardik
cve_desc: A heap buffer overflow issue was found in ImageMagick. When an application processes a malformed TIFF file, it could lead to undefined behavior or a crash causing a denial of service.

vulnerabilities.description: Integer overflow in the `extent` calculation for the ReadStripMethod. The multiplication `4*((image->depth+7)/8)*(samples_per_pixel+1)*TIFFStripSize(tiff)` can overflow a `size_t` (especially on 32-bit platforms), resulting in a small allocation. Subsequently, `TIFFReadEncodedStrip` writes up to `TIFFStripSize(tiff)` bytes into this undersized buffer, causing a heap buffer overflow.

step_by_step_execution.description: TIFF fields are read: bits_per_sample can be up to 32, samples_per_pixel can be up to 32 (MaxPixelChannels). These pass the validation checks.
The method is determined to be ReadStripMethod because the TIFF has ROWSPERSTRIP set.

The extent is calculated: 4 * ((32+7)/8) * (32+1) * TIFFStripSize(tiff) = 4 * 4 * 33 * TIFFStripSize = 528 * TIFFStripSize. On a 32-bit platform with TIFFStripSize returning e.g. 16MB (0x1000000), the result is 528 * 16777216 = 8,858,370,048 which overflows a 32-bit size_t, wrapping to a small value.
AcquireQuantumMemory allocates a buffer of the (small, overflowed) extent size.
TIFFReadEncodedStrip is called requesting TIFFStripSize(tiff) bytes (16MB) to be read into the undersized strip_pixels buffer, causing a heap buffer overflow.

state.value: small overflowed value, much less than 16MB

vulnerabilities.description: Integer overflow in the `extent` calculation for the ReadTileMethod. The expression `rows*TIFFTileRowSize(tiff)` can overflow, and the outer multiplication `4*(samples_per_pixel+1)*...` can further overflow, leading to a small allocation. TIFFReadTile then writes tile data exceeding the buffer size, causing a heap buffer overflow.

step_by_step_execution.description: TIFF tile dimensions and sample parameters are read. samples_per_pixel can be up to 32.
The method is determined to be ReadTileMethod because TIFFIsTiled returns true.

The extent is calculated: 4*(samples_per_pixel+1)*MagickMax(rows*TIFFTileRowSize(tiff), TIFFTileSize(tiff)). The inner multiplication `rows*TIFFTileRowSize(tiff)` or the outer multiplication can overflow a 32-bit size_t, resulting in a small value.
AcquireQuantumMemory allocates an undersized buffer based on the overflowed extent.
TIFFReadTile reads a full tile into the undersized tile_pixels buffer, writing more data than allocated and causing a heap buffer overflow.

commit_message: fix oob write bug inside libmobi
cve_desc: libmobi is vulnerable to Out-of-bounds Write

vulnerabilities.description: On 32-bit platforms, the addition `buf->offset + aoffset + len` can overflow `size_t`, wrapping around to a small value that passes the bounds check. This allows the subsequent `memmove` to read from and write to out-of-bounds memory. For example, if `buf->offset` is 2GB, `aoffset` is 1GB, and `len` is 2GB, the sum wraps to approximately 1GB, which could be less than `buf->maxlen`, bypassing the guard.

step_by_step_execution.description: Function is called with a non-negative `offset` and a large `len` value on a 32-bit platform.
`aoffset` is computed as `(size_t) abs(offset)`.
The bounds check `buf->offset + aoffset + len > buf->maxlen` is evaluated. The sum `0x80000000 + 100 + 0xC0000000` overflows 32-bit `size_t`, wrapping to a small value (around 0x40000064).
The wrapped sum 0x40000064 is NOT greater than buf->maxlen 0xFFFFFFFF, so the bounds check passes (incorrectly).
`source` is set to `buf->data + buf->offset + aoffset`, and `memmove` is called with the huge `len`, reading and writing far beyond the buffer's actual allocated memory.

vulnerabilities.description: Similarly, in the negative-offset branch, `buf->offset + len` can overflow `size_t` on 32-bit platforms, causing the second part of the bounds check to be bypassed and allowing an out-of-bounds memmove.

step_by_step_execution.description: Function is called with a negative `offset` and a large `len` on a 32-bit platform.
`aoffset` is computed as 1. First check `buf->offset < aoffset` (0x80000000 < 1) is false, so we proceed to second check.
Second check `buf->offset + len > buf->maxlen` is evaluated. `0x80000000 + 0xC0000000` overflows to `0x40000000`.
Wrapped value 0x40000000 is NOT greater than buf->maxlen 0x80000010, so the check passes (incorrectly).
`memmove` is called with `len = 0xC0000000`, reading/writing far past the buffer boundaries.

commit_message:
server: reset meta context replies on starttls

Related to CVE-2021-3716, but not as severe.  No compliant client will
send NBD_CMD_BLOCK_STATUS unless it first negotiates
NBD_OPT_SET_META_CONTEXT.  If an attacker injects a premature
SET_META_CONTEXT, either the client will never notice (because it
never uses BLOCK_STATUS), or the client will overwrite the attacker's
attempt with the client's own SET_META_CONTEXT request after
encryption is enabled.  So I don't class this as having the potential
to trigger denial-of-service due to any protocol mismatch between
compliant client and server (I don't care what happens with
non-compliant clients).

Fixes: 26455d45 (server: protocol: Implement Block Status "base:allocation".)
cve_desc: A flaw was found in nbdkit due to to improperly caching plaintext state across the STARTTLS encryption boundary. A MitM attacker could use this flaw to inject a plaintext NBD_OPT_STRUCTURED_REPLY before proxying everything else a client sends to the server, potentially leading the client to terminate the NBD session. The highest threat from this vulnerability is to system availability.

commit_message: MDEV-26323 use-after-poison issue of MariaDB server
cve_desc: MariaDB Server v10.6 and below was discovered to contain an use-after-free in the component my_strcasecmp_8bit, which is exploited via specially crafted SQL statements.

vulnerabilities.description: If dl->length is smaller than so_ext_len (sizeof(SO_EXT) - 1), the expression `dl->length - so_ext_len` underflows because both operands are of unsigned type `size_t`. This results in a very large positive value, causing `dl->str + dl->length - so_ext_len` to point far out of bounds, leading to an out-of-bounds read in my_strcasecmp.

step_by_step_execution.description: The function is called with a LEX_STRING dl where dl->length is, for example, 0 or 1 (shorter than SO_EXT, which is typically ".so" or ".dll", so so_ext_len is 3 or 4).
so_ext_len is computed as sizeof(SO_EXT) - 1.
The expression dl->length - so_ext_len is evaluated. Since both are size_t (unsigned), 0 - 3 wraps to a very large value (e.g., 0xFFFFFFFFFFFFFFFD on 64-bit).
dl->str + (wrapped value) produces a pointer far outside the allocated buffer, and my_strcasecmp reads from this invalid memory location, causing an out-of-bounds read.

vulnerabilities.description: The return value of alloc_root is not checked for NULL. If the memory allocation fails, s will be NULL, and the subsequent memcpy and strcpy calls will dereference a NULL pointer, leading to a crash.

step_by_step_execution.description: alloc_root is called to allocate dl->length + so_ext_len + 1 bytes. If memory is exhausted, it returns NULL.
memcpy(s, dl->str, dl->length) is called with s == NULL, resulting in a NULL pointer dereference.

commit_message: Fixed: HTML escape the log file content when viewed via Monit GUI.
cve_desc: Persistent cross-site scripting (XSS) in http/cervlet.c in Tildeslash Monit before 5.25.3 allows a remote unauthenticated attacker to introduce arbitrary JavaScript via manipulation of an unsanitized user field of the Authorization header for HTTP Basic Authentication, which is mishandled during an _viewlog operation.

vulnerabilities.description: Log file content is rendered inside an HTML <textarea> without any HTML entity encoding. If an attacker can influence the content of the log file (e.g., by sending crafted requests that get logged), they can inject a closing </textarea> tag followed by arbitrary HTML/JavaScript, resulting in a stored Cross-Site Scripting (XSS) vulnerability.

step_by_step_execution.description: The function checks for readonly access and proceeds if the user has privileges.
The code checks that logging is enabled and syslog is not used, then opens the log file.
The function outputs the opening HTML including an opening <textarea> tag.

The function reads up to 511 bytes from the log file into buf. Suppose the log file contains attacker-controlled data such as '</textarea><script>alert(document.cookie)</script>' (e.g., from a logged malicious HTTP request or username).
buf is null-terminated and appended directly to the HTML output buffer via StringBuffer_append with '%s' format, without any HTML entity escaping.

state.value: now contains the injected closing </textarea> tag and a <script> block, which will be interpreted as active HTML/JS by the browser

step_by_step_execution.description: The response is sent to the user's browser, which parses the injected </textarea>, exits the textarea context, and executes the attacker's JavaScript.

state.value: attacker's script executes in the context of the authenticated user's session

commit_message:
* libtiff/tif_read.c: add protection against excessive memory
allocation attempts in TIFFReadDirEntryArray() on short files.
Effective for mmap'ed case. And non-mmap'ed case, but restricted
to 64bit builds.
Fixes http://bugzilla.maptools.org/show_bug.cgi?id=2675
cve_desc: The TIFFReadDirEntryArray function in tif_read.c in LibTIFF 4.0.8 mishandles memory allocation for short files, which allows remote attackers to cause a denial of service (allocation failure and application crash) in the TIFFFetchStripThing function in tif_dirread.c during a tiff2pdf invocation.

commit_message: ZipFile:  Add path checks to uncompressEntry()
cve_desc: This affects the package juce-framework/JUCE before 6.1.5. This vulnerability is triggered when a malicious archive is crafted with an entry containing a symbolic link. When extracted, the symbolic link is followed outside of the target dir allowing writing arbitrary files on the target host. In some cases, this can allow an attacker to execute arbitrary code. The vulnerable code is in the ZipFile::uncompressEntry function in juce_ZipFile.cpp and is executed when the archive is extracted upon calling uncompressTo() on a ZipFile object.

vulnerabilities.description: The entry filename from the zip archive is used directly with getChildFile without sanitizing path traversal sequences like '../'. A malicious zip file can contain entries with filenames such as '../../etc/crontab' which would cause files to be written outside the intended target directory (Zip Slip attack).

step_by_step_execution.description: The function retrieves the zip entry at the given index.
On non-Windows, backslashes are replaced with forward slashes; on Windows the name is used as-is. The path traversal sequences remain intact.
entryPath is not empty, so execution continues.
targetFile is computed by joining targetDirectory with the attacker-controlled entryPath, resulting in a path outside targetDirectory.
The entry does not end with '/' or '\', so it is treated as a file.
An InputStream is created for the entry.
If the file does not exist or shouldOverwriteFiles is true, execution proceeds to write the file.
Parent directory is created if needed, and a FileOutputStream is opened to the traversed path. The zip entry content is written to the arbitrary file location outside the target directory.

vulnerabilities.description: When a zip entry is marked as a symbolic link, the symlink target path is read from the zip entry content without validation. An attacker can craft a zip with a symlink entry pointing to an arbitrary location (e.g., /etc/passwd), then a subsequent entry targeting that symlink to read or overwrite arbitrary files.

step_by_step_execution.description: A zip entry is retrieved with isSymbolicLink set to true.
targetFile is resolved within targetDirectory.
The entry content (symlink target) is read from the stream. This can be any arbitrary path like /etc/passwd.
A symbolic link is created at targetFile pointing to /etc/passwd. A subsequent zip entry writing to 'innocuous_name' would follow the symlink and overwrite /etc/passwd.

commit_message:
Fix EC_GROUP_new_from_ecparameters to check the base length

Check that there's at least one byte in params->base before trying to
read it.

CVE-2021-3712

Reviewed-by: Viktor Dukhovni <viktor@openssl.org>
Reviewed-by: Paul Dale <pauli@openssl.org>
cve_desc: ASN.1 strings are represented internally within OpenSSL as an ASN1_STRING structure which contains a buffer holding the string data and a field holding the buffer length. This contrasts with normal C strings which are repesented as a buffer for the string data which is terminated with a NUL (0) byte. Although not a strict requirement, ASN.1 strings that are parsed using OpenSSL's own "d2i" functions (and other similar parsing functions) as well as any string whose value has been set with the ASN1_STRING_set() function will additionally NUL terminate the byte array in the ASN1_STRING structure. However, it is possible for applications to directly construct valid ASN1_STRING structures which do not NUL terminate the byte array by directly setting the "data" and "length" fields in the ASN1_STRING array. This can also happen by using the ASN1_STRING_set0() function. Numerous OpenSSL functions that print ASN.1 data have been found to assume that the ASN1_STRING byte array will be NUL terminated, even though this is not guaranteed for strings that have been directly constructed. Where an application requests an ASN.1 structure to be printed, and where that ASN.1 structure contains ASN1_STRINGs that have been directly constructed by the application without NUL terminating the "data" field, then a read buffer overrun can occur. The same thing can also occur during name constraints processing of certificates (for example if a certificate has been directly constructed by the application instead of loading it via the OpenSSL parsing functions, and the certificate contains non NUL terminated ASN1_STRING structures). It can also occur in the X509_get1_email(), X509_REQ_get1_email() and X509_get1_ocsp() functions. If a malicious actor can cause an application to directly construct an ASN1_STRING and then process it through one of the affected OpenSSL functions then this issue could be hit. This might result in a crash (causing a Denial of Service attack). It could also result in the disclosure of private memory contents (such as private keys, or sensitive plaintext). Fixed in OpenSSL 1.1.1l (Affected 1.1.1-1.1.1k). Fixed in OpenSSL 1.0.2za (Affected 1.0.2-1.0.2y).

vulnerabilities.description: Out-of-bounds read when params->base->data is non-NULL but params->base->length is 0. The code checks `!params->base->data` but never verifies that `params->base->length > 0` before accessing `params->base->data[0]`. An ASN1_OCTET_STRING can have a non-NULL data pointer with length 0, leading to a read of one byte past the allocated (empty) buffer.

step_by_step_execution.description: The function receives params where params->base is a valid ASN1_OCTET_STRING with data != NULL but length == 0.
The null checks on params->order, params->base, and params->base->data all pass because they are non-NULL.
A new EC_POINT is created successfully.
The code accesses params->base->data[0] to extract the point conversion form. Since length is 0, this is a read beyond the allocated buffer.

commit_message:
glob: fix heap buffer overflow

* lib/glob.c (glob): Fix off-by-one error introduced into
glibc in commit dd7d45e838a42b0ed470c44b55901ea98d0c2bab
dated 1997-10-29 20:33:40.  Problem reported by Tim Rühsen in:
https://sourceware.org/bugzilla/show_bug.cgi?id=22320
Fix suggested by Bruno Haible.
cve_desc: The GNU C Library (aka glibc or libc6) before 2.27 contains an off-by-one error leading to a heap-based buffer overflow in the glob function in glob.c, related to the processing of home directories using the ~ operator followed by a long string.

vulnerabilities.description: Integer overflow in the tilde (~) home directory expansion path. When constructing the full directory path by concatenating home_dir and the rest of dirname, the expression `home_len + dirlen` can overflow on 32-bit systems (or theoretically on 64-bit with extreme values). If it wraps around to a small value, a small buffer is allocated (via malloc or alloca), but then `home_len + dirlen` total bytes are written via mempcpy, causing a heap or stack buffer overflow.

step_by_step_execution.description: The glob function is called with a pattern like '~/...' (very long path) and flags including GLOB_TILDE. The pattern contains a '/' so filename is set to the last '/' position.
dirname is extracted from pattern up to the last slash. dirlen is set to the distance between pattern start and the last slash.

The tilde expansion branch is entered because dirname[0] == '~'. The condition dirname[1] == '/' or dirname[1] == '\0' is not met since dirname[1] could be any character. Actually for '~/...', dirname[1]=='/' so we enter the home directory lookup for current user.
home_dir is retrieved from getenv('HOME'). An attacker can set HOME to a very long string.
The expression home_len + dirlen is computed. On a 32-bit system, both values are close to 2^31, so their sum overflows size_t and wraps to a small value.

state.value: overflows to a small value, e.g. 0 or a few bytes

step_by_step_execution.description: malloc(home_len + dirlen) is called with the overflowed small value, allocating a tiny buffer.
mempcpy writes home_len bytes of home_dir followed by dirlen bytes of &dirname[1] into newp, writing far beyond the allocated buffer.

commit_message: Removed a potential buffer overflow crash in GmfOpenMesh and debugged the test_libmeshb_block_pipeline.f that crashed
cve_desc: A buffer overflow in the GmfOpenMesh() function of libMeshb v7.61 allows attackers to cause a Denial of Service (DoS) via a crafted MESH file.

vulnerabilities.description: When reading an ASCII mesh file, fscanf with "%s" is used to scan tokens into a fixed-size stack buffer `str[GmfStrSiz]` without any width limit. A maliciously crafted mesh file containing a token longer than GmfStrSiz characters (with no whitespace) will cause a stack buffer overflow.

step_by_step_execution.description: The function is called with a filename ending in ".mesh" (ASCII) and mod == GmfRead.
A stack buffer `str` of size GmfStrSiz is declared.

state.value: uninitialized char array of size GmfStrSiz

step_by_step_execution.description: The file is opened for reading in the ASCII branch via fopen.

state.value: valid FILE pointer to attacker-controlled file

step_by_step_execution.description: The first do-while loop calls fscanf(msh->hdl, "%s", str) to scan whitespace-delimited tokens looking for "MeshVersionFormatted". The %s format specifier has no width limit.

state.value: token read from file, potentially longer than GmfStrSiz

step_by_step_execution.description: If the attacker-controlled file contains a single token (no whitespace) longer than GmfStrSiz bytes, fscanf writes past the end of str, causing a stack buffer overflow.

state.value: overflowed beyond GmfStrSiz bytes on the stack

vulnerabilities.description: When ScaKwdTab returns 0 (failure) in the GmfRead path, the function returns 0 without freeing `msh`, resulting in a memory leak. While not directly exploitable for code execution, repeated calls can exhaust memory.

step_by_step_execution.description: The function allocates msh via calloc.
File is successfully opened and header parsed. Control reaches ScaKwdTab call.
ScaKwdTab(msh) returns 0 (failure), and the function returns 0 without calling free(msh) or closing the file.

state.value: leaked heap allocation, file handle also leaked

commit_message: https://github.com/ImageMagick/ImageMagick/issues/1337
cve_desc: In ImageMagick 7.0.8-13 Q16, there is an infinite loop in the ReadBMPImage function of the coders/bmp.c file. Remote attackers could leverage this vulnerability to cause a denial of service via a crafted bmp file.

vulnerabilities.description: Integer overflow in the computation of `bytes_per_line` and `length`. On 32-bit systems, `image->columns * bmp_info.bits_per_pixel` can overflow, producing a small `bytes_per_line`. Then `bytes_per_line * image->rows` can also overflow to a small `length`. The sanity check `(length/8) > GetBlobSize(image)` is bypassed because `length` is small. The buffer is allocated via `AcquireVirtualMemory` (which does safe overflow-checking multiplication using `image->columns+256UL` as a fallback), so the buffer may be larger than `length`. However, `ReadBlob` only reads the small overflowed `length` bytes, leaving the rest of the buffer uninitialized. The pixel-processing loops then recompute `bytes_per_line` correctly (e.g., `4*(image->columns)` for 32bpp) and access offsets well beyond the initialized portion of the buffer, reading uninitialized heap memory.

step_by_step_execution.description: A crafted BMP file is read with large width (e.g., 0x20000001 on a 32-bit system) and bits_per_pixel=32. image->columns is set from the width.
bytes_per_line is computed: image->columns * bits_per_pixel = 0x20000001 * 32. On 32-bit size_t, this overflows: 0x400000020 truncated to 0x00000020. Then 4*((0x20+31)/32) = 4*1 = 4.
length = bytes_per_line * image->rows = 4 * 2 = 8. The sanity check (8/8) = 1 > GetBlobSize() is likely false, so the check passes.

AcquireVirtualMemory is called with (image->rows, MagickMax(4, image->columns+256)). MagickMax chooses image->columns+256 = 0x20000101. AcquireVirtualMemory does safe multiplication: 2 * 0x20000101 = 0x40000202, which succeeds. Buffer of ~1GB is allocated.
ReadBlob reads only 8 bytes (the overflowed length) into the pixel buffer. The rest of the buffer is uninitialized.

In the 32-bit pixel processing case, bytes_per_line is recomputed as 4*(image->columns) = 4*0x20000001. On 64-bit this is correct; on 32-bit this also overflows but the pixel access loop iterates over image->columns pixels per row, reading 4 bytes each. p = pixels + (rows-y-1)*bytes_per_line accesses well beyond the 8 initialized bytes, reading uninitialized heap data as pixel values.

state.value: points into uninitialized heap memory beyond first 8 bytes

vulnerabilities.description: Integer overflow in the size parameter passed to DecodeImage for RLE-compressed BMPs. The expression `image->columns * image->rows` can overflow on 32-bit systems when both dimensions are large, passing a much smaller size limit to DecodeImage than the actual buffer size, potentially leading to incorrect bounds enforcement within the RLE decoder.

step_by_step_execution.description: A crafted RLE-compressed BMP file specifies large width and height values such that their product overflows size_t.

The pixel buffer is allocated via AcquireVirtualMemory(image->rows, MagickMax(bytes_per_line, image->columns+256)), which performs safe overflow-checked multiplication and allocates a correct-sized buffer.

DecodeImage is called with pixels_size = image->columns * image->rows, which overflows to 0x10000 on 32-bit. DecodeImage may use this truncated value for bounds checking, potentially allowing writes beyond what it believes is the buffer end or stopping early.

commit_message: Removed a potential buffer overflow crash in GmfOpenMesh and debugged the test_libmeshb_block_pipeline.f that crashed
cve_desc: A buffer overflow in the GmfOpenMesh() function of libMeshb v7.61 allows attackers to cause a Denial of Service (DoS) via a crafted MESH file.

vulnerabilities.description: When ScaKwdTab() fails and returns 0, the function returns 0 without freeing the allocated `msh` structure or closing the open file handle (msh->hdl) or file descriptor (msh->FilDes). This leads to a memory leak and file descriptor leak that can be triggered repeatedly by providing malformed mesh files, potentially causing resource exhaustion.

step_by_step_execution.description: A GmfMshSct structure is allocated via calloc and its address stored in msh.
The function enters the GmfRead branch after passing filename, extension, and format checks.
The file is successfully opened (either via open() storing to msh->FilDes, or fopen() storing to msh->hdl), and the mesh version, dimension, and endian code are successfully parsed.

state.value: open FILE* (or msh->FilDes is a valid fd)

step_by_step_execution.description: ScaKwdTab(msh) is called to read keywords from the file. If it returns 0 (e.g., due to a malformed keyword table in the input file), execution falls into the if-block.
The function returns 0 immediately without freeing msh or closing the file handle/descriptor. The allocated memory and open file resource are leaked.

commit_message: squash heap-buffer-overflow, PoC TIFF from Hardik
cve_desc: A heap buffer overflow issue was found in ImageMagick. When an application processes a malformed TIFF file, it could lead to undefined behavior or a crash causing a denial of service.

vulnerabilities.description: In the ReadGenericMethod case, the multiplication `image->columns * image->rows` is performed as a `size_t * size_t` operation before being assigned to `MagickSizeType` (64-bit). On 32-bit systems, this wraps around, resulting in a small `number_pixels` value. The HeapOverflowSanityCheck only validates `image->rows * sizeof(*pixels)`, ignoring `image->columns`. The subsequent allocation is too small, and `TIFFReadRGBAImage` writes the full `columns * rows * 4` bytes, causing a heap buffer overflow.

step_by_step_execution.description: A crafted TIFF image is opened. Width and height are read from the TIFF header. For example, width=65536 and height=65536 on a 32-bit system.
HeapOverflowSanityCheck(image->rows, sizeof(*pixels)) checks image->rows * 4 = 65536 * 4 = 262144, which does not overflow, so the check passes.
number_pixels is computed as image->columns * image->rows. On a 32-bit system, 65536 * 65536 = 2^32 which overflows size_t (32-bit) to 0. The cast to MagickSizeType happens after the overflow.
AcquireVirtualMemory(0, sizeof(*pixels)) allocates a minimal (or zero-size) buffer.
TIFFReadRGBAImage writes width*height*sizeof(uint32) = 65536*65536*4 bytes into the undersized buffer, causing a massive heap buffer overflow.

state.value: up to 17179869184 bytes into a ~0 byte buffer

vulnerabilities.description: In the ReadStripMethod case, the `extent` calculation `4*(samples_per_pixel+1)*TIFFStripSize(tiff)` can overflow. The sub-expression `4*(samples_per_pixel+1)` is computed as 32-bit int. When multiplied with TIFFStripSize result, on 32-bit systems (where size_t is 32-bit), the result can wrap around, leading to an undersized allocation. TIFFReadEncodedStrip then writes up to TIFFStripSize bytes into this undersized buffer, causing a heap buffer overflow.

step_by_step_execution.description: A crafted TIFF with strip layout is opened. samples_per_pixel is read (e.g., 32, maximum allowed by MaxPixelChannels check). TIFFStripSize returns a large value.
extent is computed: 4*(32+1)*40000000 = 4*33*40000000 = 5280000000. On a 32-bit system, size_t is 32-bit, max ~4.29 billion, so this wraps around to 985032704.
AcquireQuantumMemory allocates extent bytes (~985MB or less, depending on available memory). The allocation may succeed.

TIFFReadEncodedStrip reads up to TIFFStripSize (40000000) bytes into strip_pixels. While this fits in the wrapped allocation in this example, with different parameters the wrap could produce a much smaller value leading to overflow.

vulnerabilities.description: In the ReadTileMethod case, the `extent` calculation involves `rows*TIFFTileRowSize(tiff)` where `rows` is uint32. This multiplication can overflow, and then the outer multiplication `4*(samples_per_pixel+1)*result` can also overflow on 32-bit systems. This leads to an undersized allocation, and TIFFReadTile writes tile data beyond the allocated buffer.

step_by_step_execution.description: A crafted tiled TIFF is opened. Tile rows and TIFFTileRowSize are read. For example, rows=65536, TIFFTileRowSize=65536 on a 32-bit system.
The inner product rows*TIFFTileRowSize(tiff) = 65536*65536 overflows to 0 on 32-bit (size_t wrap).
MagickMax(0, TIFFTileSize(tiff)) picks TIFFTileSize. Then 4*(samples_per_pixel+1)*TIFFTileSize could also overflow or produce a value smaller than the actual tile data size.
AcquireQuantumMemory allocates the small extent bytes.
TIFFReadTile reads actual tile data into the undersized buffer, causing heap buffer overflow.

state.value: tile data written beyond buffer boundary

commit_message:
patch 8.2.4436: crash with weird 'vartabstop' value

Problem:    Crash with weird 'vartabstop' value.
Solution:   Check for running into the end of the line.
cve_desc: Heap-based Buffer Overflow in GitHub repository vim/vim prior to 8.2.4436.

vulnerabilities.description: In VREPLACE mode, the code truncates `new_line` at `curwin->w_cursor.col` without verifying that this column index is within the bounds of the allocated `new_line` buffer. The `new_line` buffer is allocated via `vim_strsave(ml_get_curline())` and has a size of `strlen(current_line) + 1`. The `curwin->w_cursor.col` value is derived from `new_cursor_col`, which in the `new_cursor_col >= 0` path is computed as an offset from the original first non-blank plus the new first non-blank column. If the cursor was positioned beyond the actual line content (e.g., with virtual editing), `new_cursor_col` can exceed the length of the current line, causing a heap buffer overflow when writing the NUL terminator.

step_by_step_execution.description: Function is entered with State having VREPLACE_FLAG set. orig_line is saved and orig_col is recorded.

The cursor column is saved into new_cursor_col. Assume the cursor is at a column beyond the actual text content (possible with virtualedit or stale cursor position), e.g., col=10 on a line '  x' (length 3).
beginline(BL_WHITE) moves cursor to first non-blank (col 2 for '  x'). new_cursor_col is adjusted to be the offset from first non-blank: 10 - 2 = 8.
Since new_cursor_col (8) > 0, start_col is set to -1. The indent is then changed (e.g., set_indent keeps it the same or changes it). Cursor ends up on first non-blank of modified line.
Since new_cursor_col >= 0, the code executes: new_cursor_col += curwin->w_cursor.col. Result: 8 + 2 = 10. This exceeds the line length of 3.
curwin->w_cursor.col is set to new_cursor_col (10), which is beyond the line's content.
In the VREPLACE block, new_line = vim_strsave(ml_get_curline()) allocates a buffer of strlen(current_line)+1 bytes (4 bytes for '  x' + NUL).

state.value: heap buffer of 4 bytes containing '  x\0'

step_by_step_execution.description: new_line[curwin->w_cursor.col] = NUL writes NUL at index 10 into a 4-byte buffer, causing a heap buffer overflow.

state.value: NUL written out-of-bounds (6 bytes past end of allocation)

commit_message:
sctp: correct bounds check in sctp_setsockopt_auth_key

The bonds check to prevent buffer overlflow was not exactly
right.  It still allowed overflow of up to 8 bytes which is
sizeof(struct sctp_authkey).

Since optlen is already checked against the size of that struct,
we are guaranteed not to cause interger overflow either.

Signed-off-by: Vlad Yasevich <vladislav.yasevich@hp.com>
Signed-off-by: David S. Miller <davem@davemloft.net>
cve_desc: Integer overflow in the sctp_setsockopt_auth_key function in net/sctp/socket.c in the Stream Control Transmission Protocol (sctp) implementation in the Linux kernel 2.6.24-rc1 through 2.6.26.3 allows remote attackers to cause a denial of service (panic) or possibly have unspecified other impact via a crafted sca_keylength field associated with the SCTP_AUTH_KEY option.

commit_message:
gzip -d: do not clobber stack for valid input on x86_64

* unlzw.c (unlzw): Avoid integer overflow.
Aki Helin reported the segfault along with an input to trigger the bug.
* NEWS (Bug fixes): Mention it.
cve_desc: Integer underflow in the unlzw function in unlzw.c in gzip before 1.4 on 64-bit platforms, as used in ncompress and probably others, allows remote attackers to cause a denial of service (application crash) or possibly execute arbitrary code via a crafted archive that uses LZW compression, leading to an array index error.

vulnerabilities.description: The while loop that traverses prefix chains to generate output characters in reverse order has no bounds check on `stackp`. If a crafted/corrupt LZW compressed stream produces table entries whose prefix chains are excessively long or form a cycle (due to corrupted prefix table entries), `stackp` will be decremented past the beginning of the `de_stack` buffer, causing a stack-based buffer underflow/out-of-bounds write.

step_by_step_execution.description: Function reads `maxbits` from the compressed input stream and initializes LZW decompression tables. `free_ent` is set to FIRST (256 or 257).

state.value: attacker-controlled value from input, up to BITS

step_by_step_execution.description: The decompression loop reads codes from the input stream. As each code is decoded, new entries are added to the prefix/suffix tables via `tab_prefixof(code) = (unsigned short)oldcode` and `tab_suffixof(code) = (char_type)finchar`, with `free_ent` incrementing.

state.value: incrementing with each new table entry
set to oldcode values from attacker-controlled input stream

step_by_step_execution.description: An attacker crafts the compressed stream such that decoded codes build prefix table entries forming a very long chain (or a cycle if a CLEAR code partially resets the table while stale entries remain). When a code is decoded whose prefix chain length exceeds the size of `de_stack`, the loop begins.

state.value: a valid code whose prefix chain is excessively long

step_by_step_execution.description: The while loop follows the prefix chain: each iteration decrements `stackp` and writes a byte. There is no check that `stackp` remains within the bounds of `de_stack`.

state.value: decremented below de_stack base after many iterations

step_by_step_execution.description: The write `*--stackp = tab_suffixof(code)` writes to memory before the `de_stack` buffer, causing an out-of-bounds write. This can corrupt adjacent memory (heap metadata, other global variables) leading to code execution.

state.value: corrupted by writes to addresses before de_stack

commit_message:
Fix race condition in NewSessionTicket

If a NewSessionTicket is received by a multi-threaded client when
attempting to reuse a previous ticket then a race condition can occur
potentially leading to a double free of the ticket data.

CVE-2015-1791

This also fixes RT#3808 where a session ID is changed for a session already
in the client session cache. Since the session ID is the key to the cache
this breaks the cache access.

Parts of this patch were inspired by this Akamai change:
https://github.com/akamai/openssl/commit/c0bf69a791239ceec64509f9f19fcafb2461b0d3

Reviewed-by: Rich Salz <rsalz@openssl.org>
(cherry picked from commit 27c76b9b8010b536687318739c6f631ce4194688)

Conflicts:
	ssl/ssl.h
	ssl/ssl_err.c
cve_desc: Race condition in the ssl3_get_new_session_ticket function in ssl/s3_clnt.c in OpenSSL before 0.9.8zg, 1.0.0 before 1.0.0s, 1.0.1 before 1.0.1n, and 1.0.2 before 1.0.2b, when used for a multi-threaded client, allows remote attackers to cause a denial of service (double free and application crash) or possibly have unspecified other impact by providing a NewSessionTicket during an attempt to reuse a ticket that had been obtained earlier.

commit_message: libclamav/mspack.c: fix Quantum decompressor (bb#1771)
cve_desc: The qtm_decompress function in libclamav/mspack.c in ClamAV before 0.96 allows remote attackers to cause a denial of service (memory corruption and application crash) via a crafted CAB archive that uses the Quantum (aka .Q) compression format.  NOTE: some of these details are obtained from third party information.

vulnerabilities.description: When match_offset > window_posn, the difference is computed as unsigned int arithmetic and stored into signed int j. If match_offset is crafted to be very large (e.g., > INT_MAX + window_posn), the unsigned result overflows when stored in the signed int j, making j negative. The subsequent bounds check `j > (int) qtm->window_size` fails to catch this (negative < positive), and `qtm->window_size - j` with negative j results in a very large index, causing an out-of-bounds read from the window buffer.

step_by_step_execution.description: Function is called with a valid qtm stream and positive out_bytes. Local state is restored including window_posn (e.g., 0).
The inner decompression loop executes. QTM_GET_SYMBOL returns selector >= 4 (e.g., 4), entering the match copy path.
For selector 4, sym and extra are decoded from the bitstream. match_offset is computed as position_base[sym] + extra + 1. A malicious stream can produce a very large match_offset, e.g., 0x80000001.
The check `window_posn + match_length > qtm->window_size` passes (0 + 3 <= window_size), so no error is returned.
Since match_offset (0x80000001) > window_posn (0), we enter the wrapping branch. j = match_offset - window_posn = 0x80000001 as unsigned, stored in signed int j.
The check `j > (int) qtm->window_size` evaluates as -2147483647 > window_size, which is false. The bounds check is bypassed.
runsrc = &window[qtm->window_size - j]. Since j is negative, qtm->window_size - j (with j promoted to unsigned) becomes window_size + 2147483647, a huge out-of-bounds index into the window buffer.
The subsequent copy loops read from the out-of-bounds runsrc pointer, causing an out-of-bounds read from heap memory.

state.value: points far beyond allocated window buffer

commit_message: https://github.com/ImageMagick/ImageMagick/issues/1337
cve_desc: In ImageMagick 7.0.8-13 Q16, there is an infinite loop in the ReadBMPImage function of the coders/bmp.c file. Remote attackers could leverage this vulnerability to cause a denial of service via a crafted bmp file.

vulnerabilities.description: In the 1-bit per pixel case, pixel indexes are set to 0 or 1 without validating against the colormap size. If a BMP file has bits_per_pixel=1 and number_colors=1, the colormap only has 1 entry (index 0), but index 1 will be written for pixels with set bits. When SyncImage resolves colormap entries, it accesses image->colormap[1], which is an out-of-bounds read.

step_by_step_execution.description: A crafted BMP file is read with bmp_info.bits_per_pixel=1 and bmp_info.number_colors=1.
The check `bmp_info.number_colors > (1U << bmp_info.bits_per_pixel)` evaluates to `1 > 2` which is false, so execution continues.
Since bits_per_pixel < 16, the image is set to PseudoClass. image->colors is set to bmp_info.number_colors which is 1.
AcquireImageColormap(image, 1) allocates a colormap with exactly 1 entry (valid index: 0 only).
In the case 1 (1-bit) pixel processing loop, for any pixel data byte with a set bit, index is set to 1 via SetPixelIndex without calling IsValidColormapIndex.

SyncImage is called, which resolves pixel indexes to colormap entries. For pixels with index=1, it reads image->colormap[1] which is beyond the allocated colormap of size 1, causing an out-of-bounds read.

cve_desc: The ReadTGAImage function in coders\tga.c in ImageMagick 7.0.5-6 has a memory leak vulnerability that can cause memory exhaustion via invalid colors data in the header of a TGA or VST file.

vulnerabilities.description: Heap buffer over-read (or undefined behavior) when accessing image->colormap with zero entries. If colormap_type is non-zero but image->colors ends up as 0 (e.g., TGARGB with colormap_type=1, colormap_index=0, bits_per_pixel=8; or TGAColormap with colormap_index=0 and colormap_length=0), AcquireImageColormap is called with size 0. During pixel reading in the 8bpp case, ConstrainColormapIndex is invoked with image->colors=0, leading to either a division by zero or returning index 0, which then accesses image->colormap[0] on a zero-length allocation.

step_by_step_execution.description: TGA header is read. image_type is TGARGB (valid), colormap_type is 1, colormap_index is 0, colormap_length is 0, bits_per_pixel is 8.
Initial validation passes: TGARGB is a valid image type, bits_per_pixel=8 is in the allowed range 2-16, and the colormap_type=0 rejection only applies to TGAColormap/TGARLEColormap types.

Since TGARGB is not TGAColormap/TGAMonochrome/TGARLEColormap/TGARLEMonochrome, storage_class remains DirectClass (not PseudoClass). The PseudoClass block is skipped, so image->colors is never set to colormap_index+colormap_length.
Since colormap_type != 0, the colormap reading section is entered. The check 'image->colors < tga_info.colormap_index' evaluates as '0 < 0' which is false, so image->colors remains 0.
AcquireImageColormap(image, 0, exception) is called, allocating a colormap with 0 entries (or a minimal allocation of 0 bytes via malloc(0)).
The colormap fill loops both immediately terminate since colormap_index=0 and image->colors=0.
Pixel reading begins. For x=0, skip=MagickFalse (TGARGB is not RLE). The switch matches bits_per_pixel=8 (default case). A byte is read as index.

Since colormap_type != 0, the code executes: pixel = image->colormap[ConstrainColormapIndex(image, index, exception)]. With image->colors=0, ConstrainColormapIndex either performs modulo by zero (undefined behavior) or returns 0, leading to an out-of-bounds read of image->colormap[0] on a zero-length allocation.

state.value: out-of-bounds read at index 0 of 0-size allocation

commit_message:
Fix EC_GROUP_new_from_ecparameters to check the base length

Check that there's at least one byte in params->base before trying to
read it.

CVE-2021-3712

Reviewed-by: Viktor Dukhovni <viktor@openssl.org>
Reviewed-by: Paul Dale <pauli@openssl.org>
cve_desc: ASN.1 strings are represented internally within OpenSSL as an ASN1_STRING structure which contains a buffer holding the string data and a field holding the buffer length. This contrasts with normal C strings which are repesented as a buffer for the string data which is terminated with a NUL (0) byte. Although not a strict requirement, ASN.1 strings that are parsed using OpenSSL's own "d2i" functions (and other similar parsing functions) as well as any string whose value has been set with the ASN1_STRING_set() function will additionally NUL terminate the byte array in the ASN1_STRING structure. However, it is possible for applications to directly construct valid ASN1_STRING structures which do not NUL terminate the byte array by directly setting the "data" and "length" fields in the ASN1_STRING array. This can also happen by using the ASN1_STRING_set0() function. Numerous OpenSSL functions that print ASN.1 data have been found to assume that the ASN1_STRING byte array will be NUL terminated, even though this is not guaranteed for strings that have been directly constructed. Where an application requests an ASN.1 structure to be printed, and where that ASN.1 structure contains ASN1_STRINGs that have been directly constructed by the application without NUL terminating the "data" field, then a read buffer overrun can occur. The same thing can also occur during name constraints processing of certificates (for example if a certificate has been directly constructed by the application instead of loading it via the OpenSSL parsing functions, and the certificate contains non NUL terminated ASN1_STRING structures). It can also occur in the X509_get1_email(), X509_REQ_get1_email() and X509_get1_ocsp() functions. If a malicious actor can cause an application to directly construct an ASN1_STRING and then process it through one of the affected OpenSSL functions then this issue could be hit. This might result in a crash (causing a Denial of Service attack). It could also result in the disclosure of private memory contents (such as private keys, or sensitive plaintext). Fixed in OpenSSL 1.1.1l (Affected 1.1.1-1.1.1k). Fixed in OpenSSL 1.0.2za (Affected 1.0.2-1.0.2y).

commit_message: ZipFile:  Add path checks to uncompressEntry()
cve_desc: This affects the package juce-framework/JUCE before 6.1.5. This vulnerability is triggered when a malicious archive is crafted with an entry containing a symbolic link. When extracted, the symbolic link is followed outside of the target dir allowing writing arbitrary files on the target host. In some cases, this can allow an attacker to execute arbitrary code. The vulnerable code is in the ZipFile::uncompressEntry function in juce_ZipFile.cpp and is executed when the archive is extracted upon calling uncompressTo() on a ZipFile object.

vulnerabilities.description: A malicious zip archive can contain a symbolic link entry whose target path is arbitrary (e.g., pointing to "/etc" or "/"). The symlink target read from the zip stream (`originalFilePath`) is never validated to be within the target directory. An attacker can craft a zip with two entries: (1) a symlink entry "foo" -> "/etc", and (2) a regular file entry "foo/shadow". The first extraction creates a symlink inside targetDirectory pointing outside it; the second extraction passes the `isAChildOf` check (the logical path is still a child) but follows the symlink and writes to "/etc/shadow". This is a variant of Zip Slip via symlinks.

step_by_step_execution.description: The function retrieves the zip entry at the given index without bounds checking, and reads the entry filename.
entryPath is set from the filename. It is not empty, so execution continues.
targetFile is computed as targetDirectory.getChildFile("foo"), resolving to e.g. "/tmp/extract/foo".
The isAChildOf check passes because "/tmp/extract/foo" is logically a child of "/tmp/extract".
The entry path does not end with '/' or '\\', so it is not treated as a directory.
An InputStream is created for the entry. The stream content is the symlink target controlled by the attacker, e.g. "/etc".
The symlink check (hasSymbolicPart) may pass because no symlink exists yet in the parent directory at this point.
Since zei->entry.isSymbolicLink is true, the symlink branch is taken. originalFilePath is read from the stream and is "/etc" — an arbitrary attacker-controlled path with no validation.
File::createSymbolicLink creates a symlink at "/tmp/extract/foo" pointing to "/etc". A subsequent extraction of entry "foo/shadow" would traverse this symlink and write to "/etc/shadow".

vulnerabilities.description: The `index` parameter is used with `getUnchecked`, which performs no bounds validation. If a caller passes an out-of-range index, this results in an out-of-bounds read returning an invalid pointer, leading to undefined behavior when dereferenced.

step_by_step_execution.description: The function is called with an index value that is negative or >= entries.size().
entries.getUnchecked(index) accesses the internal array without bounds checking, reading out-of-bounds memory and returning a garbage pointer.
The garbage pointer is immediately dereferenced to access zei->entry.filename, causing undefined behavior (potential crash or information disclosure).

commit_message: Fix #1 about out-of-bounds
cve_desc: elfspirit is an ELF static analysis and injection framework that parses, manipulates, and camouflages ELF files. When analyzing the ELF file format in versions prior to 1.1, there is an out-of-bounds read bug, which can lead to application crashes or information leakage. By constructing a special format ELF file, the information of any address can be leaked. elfspirit version 1.1 contains a patch for this issue.

vulnerabilities.description: ELF header fields (e_shoff, e_phoff, e_shstrndx, e_shnum, e_phnum) are read from the attacker-controlled file and used directly as offsets and indices into the memory-mapped buffer without any bounds checking against st.st_size. A crafted ELF file can cause out-of-bounds reads.

step_by_step_execution.description: The file is opened and memory-mapped. The size of the mapped region is st.st_size.
The ELF header is cast from the start of the mapped region. Fields like e_shoff, e_phoff, and e_shstrndx are attacker-controlled.
shdr is computed as &elf_map[ehdr->e_shoff], which points outside the mapped region.
shstrtab = shdr[ehdr->e_shstrndx] dereferences the out-of-bounds pointer, causing an out-of-bounds read.

vulnerabilities.description: Variables 'dynstr' and 'dynamic' are declared but not initialized. If the ELF file does not contain sections named '.dynstr' or '.dynamic', these variables remain uninitialized and are subsequently used as array indices into shdr[], leading to use of uninitialized values and likely out-of-bounds access.

step_by_step_execution.description: Variables dynstr and dynamic are declared without initialization.
The loop iterates over all sections looking for '.dynstr' and '.dynamic'. If a crafted ELF has no such sections, neither variable is assigned.
The uninitialized 'dynamic' is used as an index: shdr[dynamic].sh_offset. This accesses an arbitrary/garbage index into the shdr array, causing out-of-bounds read.

vulnerabilities.description: The 'name' pointer points into the mmap'd ELF file buffer. When a section name is longer than 15 characters, the code writes '[...]' (6 bytes) into the mapped buffer at offset 9 from name. This corrupts other data in the string table, potentially affecting subsequent lookups (e.g., the .dynstr/.dynamic search). More critically, if shstrtab.sh_offset + shdr[i].sh_name + 9 + 5 exceeds the mapped region, this is an out-of-bounds write.

step_by_step_execution.description: name is set to point into the memory-mapped ELF data at the section string table.
strlen(name) is computed. If the section name is longer than 15 characters, the condition is true.

strcpy writes '[...]\0' (6 bytes) starting at name[9]. If name[9] is near the end of the mapped region (shstrtab.sh_offset + shdr[i].sh_name + 9 + 5 > st.st_size), this writes beyond the mapped area. Even within bounds, it corrupts shared string table data used by later lookups.

state.value: name + 9, which is elf_map + shstrtab.sh_offset + shdr[i].sh_name + 9

commit_message:
LZWDecodeCompat(): fix potential index-out-of-bounds write. Fixes http://bugzilla.maptools.org/show_bug.cgi?id=2780 / CVE-2018-8905

The fix consists in using the similar code LZWDecode() to validate we
don't write outside of the output buffer.
cve_desc: In LibTIFF 4.0.9, a heap-based buffer overflow occurs in the function LZWDecodeCompat in tif_lzw.c via a crafted TIFF file, as demonstrated by tiff2ps.

commit_message:
Avoid an integer overflow in the xbm loader

At the same time, reject some silly input, such as negative
width or height.

https://bugzilla.gnome.org/show_bug.cgi?id=672811
cve_desc: Multiple integer overflows in the read_bitmap_file_data function in io-xbm.c in gdk-pixbuf before 2.26.1 allow remote attackers to cause a denial of service (application crash) via a negative (1) height or (2) width in an XBM file, which triggers a heap-based buffer overflow.

commit_message: Fix #1 about out-of-bounds
cve_desc: elfspirit is an ELF static analysis and injection framework that parses, manipulates, and camouflages ELF files. When analyzing the ELF file format in versions prior to 1.1, there is an out-of-bounds read bug, which can lead to application crashes or information leakage. By constructing a special format ELF file, the information of any address can be leaked. elfspirit version 1.1 contains a patch for this issue.

vulnerabilities.description: Out-of-bounds write via truncation of section name. The 'name' pointer points into the mmap'd file buffer. The validated_offset check only validates the start of 'name', not whether name+9 through name+14 are within bounds. If a section name is longer than 15 bytes but its start is near the end of the mapped region, writing 6 bytes ("[...]" + null) at name+9 can write past the mapped region boundary. Additionally, this writes into the mmap'd data which may corrupt other section names read later.

step_by_step_execution.description: A crafted ELF file is opened and mmap'd into memory.

state.value: pointer to start of mmap'd region

step_by_step_execution.description: Section headers and string table offset are read from the ELF header without full bounds validation.

state.value: attacker-controlled value from crafted section header

step_by_step_execution.description: In the section loop, name is computed as elf_map + shstrtab.sh_offset + shdr[i].sh_name. validated_offset checks that 'name' itself is within [elf_map, elf_map+st.st_size), but does not check name+14.

state.value: points to a location near end of mmap'd region, e.g. elf_map + st.st_size - 12
>15 (string not null-terminated within valid region, or spans near boundary)

step_by_step_execution.description: Since strlen(name) > 15, strcpy(&name[9], "[...]") writes 6 bytes starting at name+9, which extends to name+14, potentially past elf_map + st.st_size.

state.value: name+9 through name+14, which can exceed mapped region

vulnerabilities.description: The variables 'dynstr' and 'dynamic' are used uninitialized if the ELF file does not contain sections named '.dynstr' or '.dynamic'. They are declared without initialization and only set inside conditional blocks in the loop. If no matching section is found, their garbage values are used as indices into shdr[], causing out-of-bounds read and potentially arbitrary memory access.

step_by_step_execution.description: A valid ELF file is parsed that does not contain '.dynstr' or '.dynamic' sections (e.g., a statically linked binary or a minimal object file).
The loop iterates over all section headers looking for '.dynstr' and '.dynamic' by name comparison. Neither is found, so dynstr and dynamic remain uninitialized.
shdr[dynamic].sh_offset is accessed using the uninitialized 'dynamic' as an array index, causing an out-of-bounds read from the shdr array.

vulnerabilities.description: ELF header fields e_shoff, e_phoff, and e_shstrndx are read from the attacker-controlled file and used directly as offsets/indices into the mmap'd buffer without any bounds validation against st.st_size. A crafted ELF with e_shoff or e_phoff larger than the file size leads to out-of-bounds reads.

step_by_step_execution.description: A crafted ELF file is opened with e_shoff set to a value larger than the file size.
shdr is set to elf_map + e_shoff, pointing outside the mmap'd region.
Any access to shdr[...] reads memory outside the mapped region, causing out-of-bounds read.

commit_message: imptcp: fix Segmentation Fault when octet count is to high
cve_desc: A denial of service vulnerability was found in rsyslog in the imptcp module. An attacker could send a specially crafted message to the imptcp socket, which would cause rsyslog to crash. Versions before 8.27.0 are vulnerable.

vulnerabilities.description: In the eInOctetCnt state, each digit character of the octet count is written to pThis->pMsg at offset pThis->iMsg without any bounds check against iMaxLine or the actual buffer size. An attacker can send a frame with an arbitrarily long octet count prefix using leading zeros (e.g., "00000000...001 msg"), causing iMsg to increment far beyond the pMsg buffer size, resulting in a heap buffer overflow.

step_by_step_execution.description: Function is called with a new session in eAtStrtFram state, and the incoming character c is a digit '0'. Since bSuppOctetFram is set and c is a digit, state transitions to eInOctetCnt.
The code enters the eInOctetCnt branch. isdigit(c) is true since c is '0'. The check pThis->iOctetsRemain <= 200000000 passes (0 <= 200000000), so iOctetsRemain = 0 * 10 + '0' - '0' = 0.
The digit is written to pMsg without any bounds check: *(pThis->pMsg + pThis->iMsg++) = c. iMsg increments from 0 to 1.

The attacker continues to send '0' digits. On each call, iOctetsRemain remains 0 (always passes the <= 200000000 check), and each digit is written to pMsg at the current iMsg offset. After N calls with '0' digits, iMsg equals N.
When N exceeds iMaxLine (the allocated size of pMsg), the write *(pThis->pMsg + pThis->iMsg++) = c overflows the heap buffer pMsg, writing attacker-controlled data past the buffer boundary.

commit_message:
hfsplus: check read_mapping_page() return value

While testing more corrupted images with hfsplus, i came across
one which triggered the following bug:

This happens because the return value of read_mapping_page() is passed on
to kmap unchecked.  The bug is triggered after the first
read_mapping_page() in hfsplus_block_allocate(), this patch fixes all
three usages in this functions but leaves the ones further down in the
file unchanged.

Signed-off-by: Eric Sesterhenn <snakebyte@gmx.de>
Cc: Roman Zippel <zippel@linux-m68k.org>
Signed-off-by: Andrew Morton <akpm@linux-foundation.org>
Signed-off-by: Linus Torvalds <torvalds@linux-foundation.org>
cve_desc: The hfsplus_block_allocate function in fs/hfsplus/bitmap.c in the Linux kernel before 2.6.28-rc1 does not check a certain return value from the read_mapping_page function before calling kmap, which allows attackers to cause a denial of service (system crash) via a crafted hfsplus filesystem image.

code_snippets:
page = read_mapping_page(mapping, offset / PAGE_CACHE_BITS, NULL);
	pptr = kmap(page);
vulnerabilities.description: read_mapping_page() can return an ERR_PTR (error-encoded pointer) on failure (e.g., I/O error, corrupted filesystem). The return value is never checked before being passed to kmap(), which would dereference an invalid pointer, causing a kernel NULL pointer dereference / oops. This can be triggered by a maliciously crafted HFS+ filesystem image.

step_by_step_execution.description: Function is called with a valid superblock for a (potentially malicious) HFS+ filesystem, with len > 0.

read_mapping_page() is called to read the bitmap page. If the underlying storage is corrupted or returns an I/O error, read_mapping_page returns an ERR_PTR value (e.g., ERR_PTR(-EIO) = 0xFFFFFFFFFFFFFFFA on 64-bit).

state.value: ERR_PTR(-EIO), an invalid pointer such as 0xFFFF...FFFA

step_by_step_execution.description: kmap(page) is called on the error pointer without any IS_ERR() check. kmap attempts to dereference the invalid page pointer to access page metadata, triggering a kernel oops / NULL pointer dereference.

state.value: result of dereferencing invalid pointer — kernel crash

commit_message: Fix ultra rare but potential memory-leak
cve_desc: ImageMagick before 7.0.8-50 has a memory leak vulnerability in the function ReadBMPImage in coders/bmp.c.
